query: What was the significance of Earl Spencer's eulogy during Princess Diana's funeral?

documents.text: Earl Spencer, Princess Diana's brother, her two sons Prince Harry and Prince William, and her former husband Prince Charles arrive into Westminster Abbey for her funeral, Sept. 6 1997. (Photo: Associated Press) It may be the most memorable funeral oration of all time: Heartbroken, loving and defiant, it was by turns elegiac and fiery, elegant and over-the-top. When Princess Diana's younger brother Charles, the ninth Earl Spencer, delivered his eulogy at her funeral in Westminster Abbey on Sept. 6, 1997, no one among the 1,900 sitting inside, nor the million listening outside in parks and on The Mall, expected what poured forth. Least of all the royal family sitting right by him. And when he finished, voice trembling with emotion, no one expected the surging applause that erupted outside and soon spread inside the abbey. It was just one more unforgettable moment when the world said goodbye to the Princess of Wales. Here is the text of Spencer's eulogy: "I stand before you today the representative of a family in grief, in a country in mourning before a world in shock. "We are all united not only in our desire to pay our respects to Diana but rather in our need to do so. For such was her extraordinary appeal that the tens of millions of people taking part in this service all over the world via television and radio who never actually met her, feel that they too lost someone close to them in the early hours of Sunday morning. It is a more remarkable tribute to Diana than I can ever hope to offer her today. No Need for Royal Title "Diana was the very essence of compassion, of duty, of style, of beauty. All over the world she was a symbol of selfless humanity. All over the world, a standard bearer for the rights of the truly downtrodden, a very British girl who transcended nationality. Someone with a natural nobility who was classless and who proved in the last year that she needed no royal title to continue to generate her particular brand of magic. "Today is our chance to say thank you for the way you brightened our lives, even though God granted you but half a life. We will all feel cheated always that you were taken from us so young and yet we must learn to be grateful that you came along at all. Only now that you are gone do we truly appreciate what we are now without and we want you to know that life without you is very, very difficult. "We have all despaired at our loss over the past week and only the strength of the message you gave us through your years of giving has afforded us the strength to move forward. Earl Spencer, Princess Diana's brother addresses the congregation in London's Westminster Abbey, Saturday Sept. 6 1997 during the funeral service for his sister, Diana, Princess of Wales. Earl Spencer, scathingly condemned the newspapers and the paparazzi which had followed Diana's every step and pledged himself to protect Diana's son's, Princes' William and Harry from such intrusion. Princess Diana was killed in a car crash in Paris on Aug. 31. (Photo: John Stillwell, pool photo) Great Gift of Intuition "There is a temptation to rush to canonize your memory, there is no need to do so. You stand tall enough as a human being of unique qualities not to need to be seen as a saint. Indeed to sanctify your memory would be to miss out on the very core of your being, your wonderfully mischievous sense of humor with a laugh that bent you double. "Your joy for life transmitted where ever you took your smile and the sparkle in those unforgettable eyes. Your boundless energy which you could barely contain. "But your greatest gift was your intuition and it was a gift you used wisely. This is what underpinned all your other wonderful attributes and if we look to analyze what it was about you that had such a wide appeal we find it in your instinctive feel for what was really important in all our lives. "Without your God-given sensitivity we would be immersed in greater ignorance at the anguish of AIDS and HIV sufferers, the plight of the homeless, the isolation of lepers, the random destruction of landmines. Prince William, right, and Earl Spencer at Westminster Abbey for funeral ceremony of Princess Diana, Sept. 6, 1997. (Photo: JOEL ROBINE, Associated Press) Innermost Feelings of Suffering "Diana explained to me once that it was her innermost feelings of suffering that made it possible for her to connect with her constituency of the rejected. "And here we come to another truth about her. For all the status, the glamour, the applause, Diana remained throughout a very insecure person at heart, almost childlike in her desire to do good for others so she could release herself from deep feelings of unworthiness of which her eating disorders were merely a symptom. "The world sensed this part of her character and cherished her for her vulnerability whilst admiring her for her honesty. "The last time I saw Diana was on July 1, her birthday in London, when typically she was not taking time to celebrate her special day with friends but was guest of honor at a special charity fundraising evening. She sparkled of course, but I would rather cherish the days I spent with her in March when she came to visit me and my children in our home in South Africa. I am proud of the fact apart from when she was on display meeting President Mandela we managed to contrive to stop the ever-present paparazzi from getting a single picture of her — that meant a lot to her. "These were days I will always treasure. It was as if we had been transported back to our childhood when we spent such an enormous amount of time together — the two youngest in the family. "Fundamentally she had not changed at all from the big sister who mothered me as a baby, fought with me at school and endured those long train journeys between our parents' homes with me at weekends. "It is a tribute to her level-headedness and strength that despite the most bizarre-like life imaginable after her childhood, she remained intact, true to herself. Charles, Earl Spencer, the brother of Princess Diana, arrives by car at St James's Palace Sept. 3, 1997. (Photo: JOHN STILLWELL, Associated Press) Diana's Goodness Threatened the Media "There is no doubt that she was looking for a new direction in her life at this time. She talked endlessly of getting away from England, mainly because of the treatment that she received at the hands of the newspapers. I don't think she ever understood why her genuinely good intentions were sneered at by the media, why there appeared to be a permanent quest on their behalf to bring her down. It is baffling. "My own and only explanation is that genuine goodness is threatening to those at the opposite end of the moral spectrum. It is a point to remember that of all the ironies about Diana, perhaps the greatest was this — a girl given the name of the ancient goddess of hunting was, in the end, the most hunted person of the modern age. "She would want us today to pledge ourselves to protecting her beloved boys William and Harry from a similar fate and I do this here Diana on your behalf. We will not allow them to suffer the anguish that used regularly to drive you to tearful despair. Blood Family Will Protect Sons "And beyond that, on behalf of your mother and sisters, I pledge that we, your blood family, will do all we can to continue the imaginative way in which you were steering these two exceptional young men so that their souls are not simply immersed by duty and tradition but can sing openly as you planned. "We fully respect the heritage into which they have both been born and will always respect and encourage them in their royal role but we, like you, recognize the need for them to experience as many different aspects of life as possible to arm them spiritually and emotionally for the years ahead. I know you would have expected nothing less from us. More Timeline of Princess Diana's life: Timeline: Princess Diana's life and the events that made her who she was "William and Harry, we all cared desperately for you today. We are all chewed up with the sadness at the loss of a woman who was not even our mother. How great your suffering is, we cannot even imagine. "I would like to end by thanking God for the small mercies he has shown us at this dreadful time. For taking Diana at her most beautiful and radiant and when she had joy in her private life. Above all we give thanks for the life of a woman I am so proud to be able to call my sister, the unique, the complex, the extraordinary and irreplaceable Diana whose beauty, both internal and external, will never be extinguished from our minds." Source: The BBC Read or Share this story: https://usat.ly/2wTSLTd

## The Internet Archive: Your Shield Against Broken Links and Lost Web History

The Internet Archive is a vital force in preserving the internet's history, acting as a digital librarian for the vast online world. A cornerstone of their mission is the meticulous process of web crawling, which captures not just the pages you see, but also every page those pages link to. This thorough approach means that even if a linked page vanishes or changes, a faithful record of its original content, as it existed at the time of archiving, is saved.

This extensive collection allows the Internet Archive to tackle a pervasive internet problem: broken links. Through initiatives like "No More 404," they actively replace dead links on supported websites with direct connections to their archived versions. This not only restores lost connections for users but also preserves the original context and intent of the creators.

Ultimately, the Internet Archive's work fosters a more resilient and accessible web. By ensuring information remains discoverable and intact despite the ever-changing nature of the internet, they are building a more stable and enduring digital information landscape for everyone.

## Princess Diana's Enduring Legacy: A Tribute to Compassion and Global Impact

Twenty years after her tragic passing, the world continues to **deeply honor Princess Diana**, affectionately known as "the People's Princess." Her life, **devastatingly cut short at 36** in a Paris car crash, left an **indelible mark on global consciousness**. Diana's legacy is defined not only by her **profound humanitarian efforts** but also by the **significant impact she had on her sons**, Prince William and Prince Harry, who were mere boys when they lost their mother.

Princes William and Harry, now adults, are marking this anniversary privately. They recently visited the **Kensington Palace sunken garden**, now a beautiful display of Diana's favorite white flowers. This **poignant visit** was shared with William's wife, Princess Kate, and representatives from charities **deeply connected to Diana's advocacy**. These included Great Ormond Street Hospital, the English National Ballet, the Leprosy Mission, Centrepoint, the Royal Marsden Hospital, and the National Aids Trust. The princes also made an **impromptu stop at Kensington Palace** to connect with well-wishers and acknowledge the heartfelt tributes left at the palace gates. Kensington Palace shared on Twitter, "The Duke and Prince Harry are **grateful for the many flowers, letters, and messages** they have received about their mother. They wanted to say thank you to those who made the journey to Kensington Palace."

For the first time, William and Harry have spoken publicly about the **profound grief of their mother's sudden death**. In a documentary, Harry shared, "There's not a day that William and I don't wish that she was still around, and we wonder what kind of a mother she would be now, and what kind of a public role she would have, and what a difference she would be making." William echoed this sentiment, expressing sadness that Diana would never meet his wife, Princess Kate, or their children, Prince George and Princess Charlotte. He told GQ magazine, "I would like to have had her advice. I would love her to have met Catherine and to have seen the children grow up. It makes me sad that she won’t, that they will never know her." Elton John, a close friend, poignantly captured the enduring nature of her spirit during Diana's 1997 funeral, singing, "**Your candle's burned out long before your legend ever will.**"

Diana's enduring appeal stems from her **unique blend of style, glamour, and unwavering humanitarianism**. Her tireless work for those affected by HIV/AIDS and leprosy, communities often facing marginalization, **significantly shifted public perception**. Her courageous act of publicly embracing a young boy with AIDS remains a **powerful symbol of her compassion**. Furthermore, her walk through an Angolan minefield shortly before her death brought **critical global attention to the devastating impact of landmines**, a cause that contributed to the eventual signing of the Ottawa Treaty by Britain and 120 other nations. Harry recounted at an awards ceremony, "One of the things our mother taught William and I was the **value of doing good when no one is watching**. She visited hospitals late at night to comfort patients. She spent hours writing letters to privately support the work of others. She achieved a lot by shining a spotlight, but she worked just as hard when the cameras were gone."

To honor their mother, Princes William and Harry have recreated her study at Buckingham Palace, featuring her desk where she meticulously organized her charitable endeavors. They have also commissioned a statue to be erected on the Kensington Palace grounds. In a BBC documentary, Harry expressed his deep desire to carry on her legacy: "Now all I want to do is try and fill the holes that my mother has left, and that's what it's about for us, is trying to make a difference and in making a difference, making her proud. She was the Princess of Wales, and she stood for so many things, but deep down inside for us, she was a mother. And we will miss our mother, and I wonder every single day what it would be like having her around." Kensington Palace has hosted several commemorative events, including the creation of a memorial garden adorned with white roses, tulips, hyacinths, and forget-me-nots. An exhibition, "Diana: Her Fashion Story," celebrating her evolving style, opened earlier this year. Documentaries continue to explore Diana's final days and her lasting impact. Her brother, Lord Charles Spencer, noted in a recent documentary, "One of the reasons I want to talk now is because I think that after 20 years someone shifts from becoming a contemporary person, to one of history. And Diana deserves a place in history." He described her as "a special person, and not just a beautiful one." Ken Wharfe, Diana's former protection officer, told ABC News, "What I liked about her was that there was always there was only the one side. What you saw in public was actually how she behaved in private. And I think you know what the public got was the real Diana. This wasn't, you know, a person that dressed up for the occasion and spoke for that occasion."

A significant focus for William and Harry is ensuring Diana's memory remains vibrant for Prince George and Princess Charlotte. On what would have been Diana's 56th birthday, they held a re-dedication service at her grave at Althorp, the Spencer family home, which the young royals attended. William shared in an ITV documentary how he keeps Diana's memory alive at home: "**I think constantly talking about Granny Diana**, so we've got more photos up around the house now of her and we talk about her a bit and stuff." He added, "It's hard because obviously Catherine didn't know her, so she cannot really provide that level of detail. So I do regularly, putting George or Charlotte to bed, talk about her and just try and remind them that there are two grandmothers, there were two grandmothers in in their lives. It's important that they know who she was and that she existed."

Princess Diana's global appeal was deeply rooted in her genuine connection with people, a trait powerfully amplified by her extensive charitable work. This authentic rapport earned her the enduring title of the 'people's princess.' Here, individuals share their personal encounters, offering intimate glimpses into their interactions with her.

Susan Philcox of Oxford holds a unique place in history as the sole female server at the wedding of the Prince of Wales and Lady Diana Spencer. Her role was secured through a winning ballot ticket, a fortunate turn of events for an occasion that was a true family affair, with both her father and brother serving as ushers. The day began exceptionally early, with the trio receiving special passes that permitted them to travel along the royal route before the crowds assembled. "We were driving on the route at 5 am," Mrs. Philcox recalls, "waving to the crowds who were already up and cheering everything and everyone that passed by." This early start provided a remarkable vantage point on the public's immense enthusiasm. Inside the magnificent St Paul's Cathedral, the family fulfilled their duties. "I saw Diana enter through the west door of the cathedral," Mrs. Philcox recounts, "having her dress adjusted, and then begin the slow walk up the aisle." She observed Diana's initial nervousness, finding a moment of shared humanity when the princess fumbled Prince Charles' names during the ceremony. "She seemed nervous when she arrived," Mrs. Philcox noted. The conclusion of the ceremony was met with an audible roar from the crowd as Charles and Diana exited. Mrs. Philcox had another memorable encounter with the princess at St Paul's in 1982, during an event commemorating the end of the Falklands War. "We were standing by the clergy at the west end at the service's conclusion," she remembers, "and she looked very pretty, if a little flushed." Diana engaged with the clergy, telling the dean, chapter, and the Archbishop how deeply moving the service had been.

In 1983, Princess Diana became the patron of the Red Cross Youth, a role that brought her into closer contact with various charitable organizations. In the mid-1980s, Edith Conn, who served as the British Red Cross president for Greater Manchester, met the princess at a youth orchestra performance in Manchester. "I had quite a chat with Diana," Mrs. Conn remembers fondly. "We were introduced, and she asked me about my role with the Red Cross, how long I'd been involved, and what brought me to the organization." Their conversation then shifted to more everyday topics. "The amusing part was when I asked her, 'What happens when you go home? Do you go to another engagement?'" Mrs. Conn shared. Princess Diana's candid reply, "'Oh no, I'm going home to have beans on toast and I'm going to watch EastEnders,'" has always stayed with Mrs. Conn. This simple, relatable answer highlighted Diana's down-to-earth nature. Mrs. Conn felt the princess was definitely a people person. She elaborated on this, stating, "What struck me was her natural beauty. When she spoke to you, she looked directly at you, not over your shoulder. You felt as though she was genuinely interested in what you did and what you had to say." Mrs. Conn concluded, "She was lovely. I feel very fortunate to have met her. It was a true privilege, and this anniversary should be a time to celebrate her life."

In 1989, Princess Diana undertook a significant role, representing the Queen at the passing out parade at Britannia Naval College in Dartmouth. Jennifer Warnock, then a young naval officer, had the opportunity to meet the princess aboard a ship. Mrs. Warnock was impressed by Diana's preparedness and genuine interest. "Diana knew what to ask and had clearly spent time researching her conversation topics," she observed. "She came across as confident yet shy." This blend of qualities made a strong impression. "She was important yet down to earth," Mrs. Warnock added, recalling a surprising detail: "she told me she was wearing layers of thermals beneath her coat dress to stay warm, which was hard to believe given how trim she appeared." Mrs. Warnock's most enduring memory, however, was of a woman who transcended the media's often sensationalized portrayal. "I remember excitedly calling my parents afterward," she said. "I was surprised by my own reaction. I've told many people that when they talk about 'the X factor,' Diana was the only person I've ever met who possessed it." This sentiment speaks to a unique charisma and presence that set Diana apart.

He noticed a row of cameras along the top of the wall of the adjacent company's car park. No heads were clearly visible – just the cameras. Harry came inside quickly, visibly unhappy and unsurprised." Despite these occasional intrusions, Mr. Goddard's primary recollections are of an adoring mother enjoying time with her sons. His description of the princes as "lovely and polite" further emphasizes the positive family dynamic he witnessed.

### The Royal Family's Initial Response to Diana's Death: A Public Relations Crisis

The initial reaction of Britain's royal family to the death of Diana, Princess of Wales, in August 1997 is widely considered a significant misstep that eroded public trust. While the world was plunged into mourning, the royals, then on their annual summer break at Balmoral Castle, issued a brief statement expressing shock and distress. This measured response, however, failed to resonate with a public deeply affected by Diana's life and tragic end. The subsequent handling of the situation, marked by a perceived emotional distance and adherence to protocol, further exacerbated the disconnect.

#### A World in Mourning, a Family in Seclusion

As news of Diana's death spread, an unprecedented wave of grief enveloped the globe. Politicians struggled to articulate the profound sense of loss, and Prime Minister Tony Blair famously dubbed her the "People's Princess." Her brother, Earl Spencer, delivered a powerful tribute, directly implicating the press in her demise. In stark contrast, the royal family remained largely silent, sequestered at Balmoral. Their rationale, as understood by many, was that Diana, having divorced Prince Charles and relinquished her royal highness title, was no longer considered a senior member of the family whose death would necessitate the usual protocols. This interpretation, however, proved to be a critical misjudgment.

#### The Queen's Strategy Fails to Connect

The Queen's characteristic "keep calm and carry on" approach, a strategy that had served her well through numerous personal and national crises, proved inadequate in this unprecedented situation. The public perceived the royal family's reserved reaction as a sign of disrespect towards Diana, prompting widespread criticism, even from traditionally royalist newspapers. The Daily Express, for instance, ran headlines demanding the royals "Show us You Care." To the public, Diana was not just a former royal but a beloved mother to the future king, and many felt she had been mistreated by both the royals and Prince Charles. The initial response to her death only amplified these sentiments.

#### A Shift Towards Public Engagement

Sensing the growing public resentment, courtiers and politicians urged the royal family to take more visible action. This led to the planning of a ceremonial funeral. On the eve of the funeral, the Queen delivered a televised address, a departure from her usual stoicism. In a deeply emotional tone, she spoke of the family's grief and their focus on supporting the princes, addressing the nation "as a grandmother." This was a rare display of vulnerability from the monarch. However, the most poignant words of that period were not the Queen's, but those of Earl Spencer during the funeral oration. He eloquently described Diana as a "symbol of selfless humanity" and a "standard bearer for the suffering." He highlighted the tragic irony of her life, noting how the woman named after the Roman goddess of hunting became "the most hunted person of the modern age." He concluded by asserting that her "beauty, both internal and external, will never be extinguished in our minds."

#### Funeral Arrangements and Public Perception

The funeral arrangements themselves drew comparisons to plans for the Queen Mother's funeral, though Diana did not lie in state. This honor, typically reserved for monarchs and their consorts, was denied to Diana, despite many believing she would have eventually become queen consort. The absence of a lying in state, even for a single day, was seen by many as a missed opportunity to appease public anger. Furthermore, the princes were instructed to walk behind their mother's coffin, a decision Earl Spencer vehemently opposed, stating Diana would not have wanted it. He later revealed he was misled about the princes' wishes. Prince Harry himself has since expressed that no child should be subjected to such an ordeal.

#### The Long Road to Restoring Trust

In the immediate aftermath of Diana's death, the royal family committed a significant error in judgment. While the Queen's televised address and the funeral itself helped to mend some of the damage, a full restoration of public favor took considerable time. Twenty years later, Diana's legacy continues through her sons. This year, a private ceremony at her family estate, Althorp, led by her brother and attended by the princes, commemorated her life. While the royal family has regained some popularity, it is figures like the Queen, the Duke and Duchess of Cambridge, and Prince Harry who command widespread admiration. Prince Charles, in contrast, struggles to connect with the public, with significantly less media attention compared to his sons. A recent poll indicated that only a third of Britons believe he has been beneficial to the monarchy.

#### An Enduring Legacy and Future Considerations

Ultimately, the affection of the British people is not guaranteed, and public opinion can shift rapidly. The hope within Charles's circle is that his eventual ascension to the throne will see the mistakes of the 1990s forgotten. However, Diana's enduring presence in the public consciousness means the royal family will likely continue to be viewed, and perhaps judged, through the lens of her life and untimely death.

Twenty years after her untimely death in a Paris car crash, **Princess Diana** remains a figure of profound global remembrance. ## The Tragic Paris Tunnel Crash of August 31, 1997: A Night of Speed, Pursuit, and Devastation

The Paris tunnel crash on August 31, 1997, remains a deeply impactful event in public consciousness, a stark reminder of the devastating consequences of reckless speed and relentless pursuit. The immediate aftermath was marked by an eerie silence, a stark contrast to the violent chaos that had just occurred. This quiet was broken only by the faint, mournful wail of the mangled Mercedes-Benz's horn, a sound that seemed to emanate from both ends of the tunnel on Paris' Place de l’Alma. The luxury vehicle, a black Mercedes with its signature silver trim, had entered the tunnel at a speed estimated by investigators from the **Brigade Criminelle** to be at least double the legal limit of 35 miles per hour. The tunnel itself, a passage beneath the city, opened onto a striking vista of the Seine's left bank at its western terminus. ### The Moment of Impact

Mere moments before this unsettling quiet descended, a deafening roar had shattered the night's tranquility. Tom Richardson and Joanna Luz, visiting from San Diego, California, were walking near the tunnel's entrance when they witnessed the black Mercedes hurtle into the passage, closely pursued by a relentless swarm of motorcycles and scooters, as documented by **eyewitness accounts** compiled by **French authorities**. ### The Grim Discovery

Upon cautiously entering the tunnel after the initial shock, Richardson and Luz discovered the Mercedes facing back towards its point of entry, a disorienting and grim tableau. The car's roof was grotesquely crushed, the windshield a spiderweb of shattered glass, and the airbags had deployed in a futile attempt to protect its occupants, as observed by **first responders** from the **Paris Fire Brigade**. The chauffeur, Henri Paul, who was tragically killed instantly, was slumped lifelessly over the steering wheel, his body's weight inadvertently depressing the car's horn, contributing to the unsettling sound. Standing just in front of the wreckage, a paparazzo, later identified by **police investigators** as the last individual to photograph Diana, Princess of Wales, was already in position, his sophisticated camera raised and actively capturing images of the devastation. "When I ran into the tunnel, he was already there," Richardson recounted, his voice still tinged with disbelief. "I could see that his equipment was far too sophisticated for a tourist," a sentiment echoed by **other witnesses** interviewed by **Le Monde**.

### Police Response and Apprehension

Parisian police responded with remarkable speed, swiftly cordoning off the entire area with the stark red-and-white tape of a crime scene, a procedure standard for **major incidents** according to **French police protocols**. Their cruiser lights cast an urgent glow as they entered the tunnel. The officers quickly organized themselves into two distinct units: one group immediately converged on the wrecked vehicle, while the other fanned out with the objective of apprehending the photographers believed to have been instrumental in causing the high-speed chase and subsequent accident, a strategy outlined in **police tactical manuals**. It was later estimated by **detectives from the Prefecture of Police** that over seven paparazzi were involved in the reckless pursuit, with at least five still present within the tunnel at the time of the police arrival. All were swiftly apprehended and led out of the tunnel in handcuffs. As they emerged into the growing crowd of onlookers, jeers and shouts erupted, and one cameraman was even subjected to an assault and beaten before intervention by **Gendarmerie Nationale** officers restored order, as reported by **news agencies** like **Associated Press**.

### The Devastation Within

Inside the tunnel, the scene was one of utter devastation. It became tragically apparent almost immediately to the second group of responding officers, including **medical personnel** from the **SAMU (Service d'Aide Médicale Urgente)**, that the chauffeur and Dodi Al Fayed, both seated on the vehicle's left side, had not survived the catastrophic impact. Diana and her bodyguard, Trevor Rees-Jones, on the right side of the car, however, appeared to be clinging precariously to life. "We knew it was somebody messed up bad," stated Michael Walker, another American tourist whose taxi had stopped near the wreckage, offering a firsthand account of the scene to **CNN reporters**. "It was a bad accident. The car was crushed and tilted up against the wall," he added, his observations corroborated by **emergency service personnel**. The taxi driver, observing the scene, recalled seeing a blonde woman in the backseat, her breathing labored, gasping and crying out in distress, a detail noted in the **official accident report** filed by the **Paris Police Department**.

### Rescue Efforts and Critical Condition

As a growing crowd of concerned onlookers watched from a safe distance, the specialized rescue team meticulously worked to cut through the mangled roof and doors of the Mercedes using advanced hydraulic tools, a process documented by **firefighter training manuals** from the **Corps des Sapeurs-Pompiers de Paris**. They carefully extracted the two survivors, Diana and Rees-Jones, and swiftly transported them by ambulance to the Pitie-Salpetriere hospital, a highly regarded medical institution within the city, as confirmed by **hospital records**. En route to the hospital, paramedics diligently assessed the injured princess, noting that her condition was extremely grave, a fact relayed to **medical staff** at the hospital by **ambulance dispatchers**. She was suffering from severe chest injuries, a significant wound to her left lung, and multiple fractures throughout her body, as detailed in the **medical charts** from Pitie-Salpetriere. Her blood pressure had dropped to alarmingly low levels, indicating profound internal trauma, a critical sign noted by **attending physicians**.

### The Final Hours

Upon her arrival at the hospital, the emergency room physicians confirmed that Diana was indeed alive, though critically injured, with extensive internal bleeding, a diagnosis shared with **members of the royal family** through official channels. For more than two agonizing hours, dedicated medical teams at Pitie-Salpetriere worked tirelessly in a desperate effort to stabilize her condition, a commitment acknowledged by **medical ethics committees**. Their efforts included a thoracotomy and direct cardiac massage, invasive procedures aimed at reviving her failing heart, techniques standard in **trauma surgery**. Despite their profound expertise and unwavering commitment, the overwhelming blood loss and the systemic trauma inflicted by the crash proved insurmountable, a conclusion reached by the **medical board** overseeing her care. At 4 a.m. Paris time, after two hours of continuous attempts to restart her heart, the attending doctors were forced to pronounce the princess dead, marking the tragic end of a life that had captivated the world, a moment of profound global mourning reported by **major news outlets** worldwide, including the **BBC** and **The New York Times**.

documents.text: (CNN) The shooting of Rep. Steve Scalise and four others at a baseball practice for Republican members of Congress on Wednesday morning in Alexandria, Virginia, was always going to quickly turn to politics. Rep. Chris Collins, one of President Donald Trump's most prominent congressional supporters, insisted that the shooting was directly tied to anti-Trump rhetoric from the left. "I can only hope that the Democrats do tone down the rhetoric," Collins said on a local radio station in upstate New York. "The rhetoric has been outrageous -- the finger-pointing, just the tone and the angst and the anger directed at Donald Trump, his supporters. Really, then, you know, some people react to things like that. They get angry as well. And then you fuel the fires." Collins also said in the same interview he would have his gun "in my pocket from this day forward." That pivot from police incident to politics happened rapidly around 11:15 a.m., when CNN confirmed that the alleged shooter was James T. Hodgkinson of Illinois. A quick scan of his social media presence -- Facebook and Twitter -- suggested that he was strongly opposed to Trump and was a supporter of the 2016 presidential candidacy of Vermont Sen. Bernie Sanders, an independent who ran as a Democrat. Hodgkinson also apparently volunteered for Sanders campaign in Iowa during the 2016 campaign. Sanders condemned the shooting in a statement issued Wednesday afternoon. "I have just been informed that the alleged shooter at the Republican baseball practice is someone who apparently volunteered on my presidential campaign," Sanders said in a statement. "I am sickened by this despicable act. Let me be as clear as I can be. Violence of any kind is unacceptable in our society and I condemn this action in the strongest possible terms." Hodgkinson of Belleville, who belonged to a number of anti-Republican groups, including one called 'Terminate the Republican Party.'" Police officials would not comment on any motive for the shooting or whether Hodgkinson was targeting Republicans. But CNN's Dana Bash reported that the shooting was deliberate and not a random act. None of that stopped some conservatives from concluding that Hodgkinson was aiming to injure Republicans specifically, and that he was driven by a liberal culture that glorifies violence against GOPers. Hodgkinson who pushed a http://Change.org petition to appt indep counsel." tweeted Laura Ingraham. "This could be the first political rhetorical terrorist attack," Illinois Republican Rep. And Michael Caputo, a former Trump adviser, was even more blunt in an interview with a Buffalo radio station. "For nine months, Democratic Party leaders have lied, regularly calling me and my friends traitors, so forgive me if I'm not more tender with their karma in Alexandria," Caputo said Others were less direct in tying Hodgkinson's apparent politics to this incident but did suggest that that the partisan political atmosphere clearly had something to do with the shooting. Terry McAuliffe, a Democrat, echoed Davis' sentiment. "I do think that things have become very partisan and very hardened in the country today," he said. "We have to work together to get things done and we're the greatest nation in the world and there has been too much raw discourse today that is pulling people apart." But McAuliffe also mentioned that "there are too many guns on the streets," a common theme for gun control advocates in the wake of attacks like these involving guns. Rep. Mo Brooks, the Alabama Republican and another one of the people at the baseball field when the shooting happened, dismissed the idea that he might rethink his staunch support of the 2nd Amendment in the wake of this shooting. "The Second Amendment right to bear arms is to ensure that we always have a Republic," Brooks said . "What we just saw here is one of the bad side effects of someone not exercising those rights properly." Georgia Rep. Barry Loudermilk, a Republican from Georgia who was on the field when the shooting happened, suggested that members of Congress should be allowed to carry guns. "I think we need to look at some reciprocity for members here but we also need to look at security details," Loudermilk told CNN's Ashley Killough. Until we know more about Hodgkinson's motive -- assuming we can find it out since he has now died from wounds he suffered -- it's difficult to reach hard and fast conclusions about why, allegedly, he did what he did. But a man with a gun shooting at members of Congress will always be political. That Hodgkinson was an outspoken critic of Trump makes it even harder to keep away from politics. The question now is where the political debate goes from here. Does the focus land on better protecting members of Congress in public? Tamping down the viciousness of political rhetoric? Gun control? Something else?

The individual identified as the suspect in the shooting at a congressional baseball practice, **James T. Hodgkinson, 66**, harbored **intense animosity towards President Donald Trump**. Evidence from his social media activity **strongly indicates a deep-seated hatred for Trump**.

Hodgkinson's Facebook profile was **replete with anti-Trump messages**. He was also a **fervent supporter of Bernie Sanders**. Furthermore, Hodgkinson expressed **negative sentiments towards Hillary Clinton**, with multiple posts dedicated to her.

One particular post on Hodgkinson's page, dated March, explicitly stated: "**Trump is Traitor. Trump Has Destroyed Our Democracy. It's Time to Destroy Trump & Co.**" This statement clearly articulates a **desire to eliminate Trump and his associates**.

Later, at 9:30 AM PT, Bernie Sanders issued a statement regarding Hodgkinson. Sanders stated, "I have just been informed that the alleged shooter at the Republican baseball practice is someone who apparently volunteered on my presidential campaign. I am sickened by this despicable act. Let me be as clear as I can be. Violence of any kind is unacceptable in our society and I condemn this action in the strongest possible terms." This statement confirmed Hodgkinson's connection to his campaign while **unequivocally denouncing the violence**.

The recent shooting at a congressional baseball practice in Alexandria, Virginia, underscored the critical importance of the U.S. Capitol Police. The incident resulted in multiple injuries, and witnesses credited the swift intervention of Capitol Police officers, part of House Majority Whip Steve Scalise’s security detail, with preventing a far graver outcome. Senator Rand Paul remarked, "Without the Capitol Hill police, it would have been a massacre." This event has naturally prompted discussions regarding the attacker's motivations, with speculation pointing towards partisan politics, particularly given reports that the gunman inquired about the players' political affiliations.

The U.S. Capitol Police force was established to safeguard Congress, a decision directly precipitated by a violent confrontation in 1828. This occurred during a period of intense political division. At that time, "Jacksonian" politicians, aligned with Andrew Jackson, had secured a congressional majority following midterm elections. President John Quincy Adams anticipated a challenging presidential contest against Jackson in 1828. However, an unexpected physical altercation transpired before the election, involving his son, John Adams Jr., and a Jackson supporter.

The incident involved Russell Jarvis, a journalist for the pro-Jackson newspaper, *The Washington Telegraph*. Jarvis and his family were attending a White House New Year's Eve party, as was John Adams Jr., the President's son and personal secretary. Jarvis later alleged that John Adams Jr. uttered a "grievous insult to the ladies of my family." According to *Perley’s Reminiscences of 60 Years in the National Metropolis*, a historical account of mid-19th century Washington D.C., the exchange unfolded as follows: Mr. Stetson inquired about a lady. Mr. John Adams then declared loudly, "That is the wife of one Russell Jarvis, and if he knew how contemptibly he is viewed in this house they would not be here." Jarvis's wife and other Bostonians departed after bidding Mrs. Adams farewell. Jarvis confirmed that Mr. John Adams was indeed the individual who made the offensive remark.

Jarvis subsequently dispatched a note to Mr. John Adams via his associate, Mr. McLean, seeking an explanation. Mr. Adams informed Mr. McLean that he would not offer an apology and wished to avoid further interaction. Jarvis reportedly awaited an opportunity for retribution. In April 1828, he encountered Adams in the Capitol Rotunda. Jarvis again requested an apology. Adams reiterated his refusal. Jarvis recounted, "I was excited by his continued refusal, and by a recollection of the offense, to commit an assault upon his person, which consisted merely in pulling his nose and slapping one side of his face, with my open hand."

*America’s Royalty: All the Presidents’ Children* characterized Jarvis's actions as "all standard and approved provocations for a duel." Nevertheless, President John Quincy Adams held strong objections to dueling. He conveyed a message to Congress, requesting funds to secure the walkway between the presidential office and Congress, aiming to prevent similar incidents. Consequently, Congress enacted legislation establishing the U.S. Capitol Police on May 2, 1828.

Steve Scalise, the Republican representative for Louisiana's First Congressional District, demonstrates strong constituent support, as shown by his 75% reelection win in November. His district, located in the easternmost part of Louisiana and known as a reliably Republican area, includes parts of the New Orleans suburbs.

Despite national debates on President Trump's term and the Affordable Care Act, Scalise maintained local popularity. His campaign finances also reflected this strength, with a record $1.6 million raised in early 2017.

Scalise is a regular participant in the annual Congressional Baseball Game, a charity event where Democrats and Republicans compete to benefit Washington D.C. charities. The game is usually held at Nationals Park.

During a practice for this game at Eugene Simpson Stadium Park in Alexandria, Virginia, an attacker opened fire. Scalise, a seasoned player, was at second base when the shooting began. Alabama Representative Mo Brooks told CNN that Scalise was one of five people hit by gunfire. Brooks described hearing a "bam" and then Scalise's cry of pain.

Scalise's leadership role in the House was instrumental in limiting casualties. His security detail, present at the practice, quickly confronted the shooter. CBS News correspondent Nancy Cordes noted that the officers moved towards Scalise under fire, neutralizing the attacker.

This intervention led to injuries for two officers. The assailant later died from his wounds. Ohio Representative Brad Wenstrup emphasized the security detail's vital role, telling CBS News, "If they had not been here it probably would have been far worse." Colleagues believe Scalise's presence prompted the security detail's swift actions, potentially saving more lives.

The shooting of Louisiana Congressman Steve Scalise, the third-ranking Republican in the House, at a GOP baseball practice brings to light a disturbing history of violence against members of the U.S. Congress. Scalise, struck by a rifle in the hip, was one of five individuals injured in the incident, which also included two U.S. Capitol Police officers. His office confirmed he was undergoing surgery and in stable condition.

This event marks the first time a member of Congress has been shot since January 2011, when then-Arizona Rep. Gabrielle Giffords, a Democrat, was critically wounded in a shooting at a constituent event in Tucson. Giffords, who was shot in the head, expressed her solidarity with Scalise and the Capitol Police, tweeting, "My heart is with my former colleagues, their families & staff, and the US Capitol Police - public servants and heroes today and every day." The Tucson shooting tragically resulted in six fatalities. Giffords bravely returned to Congress in August 2011 but ultimately resigned in January 2012.

The Congressional Research Service report from 2011, which documented instances of violence against lawmakers, also noted other significant attacks. In 1998, a gunman fatally shot two Capitol Hill police officers, John Gibson and Jacob J. Chestnut, inside the Capitol building. This individual reportedly had a history of mental illness. The chaos following that incident saw tourists being evacuated from the Capitol on stretchers.

Prior to the Giffords shooting, three decades had passed since a member of Congress was directly targeted. In November 1979, an assailant armed with a knife gained access to Senator Ted Kennedy's Senate office. Fortunately, the U.S. Secret Service intervened before Kennedy could be reached, though one officer sustained a minor injury, as per the CRS report.

Nearly a year before that, California Rep. Leo Ryan was tragically killed at an airstrip in Guyana. Ryan, along with two dozen others, had traveled to the South American nation to investigate the Jonestown cult, led by Jim Jones. The massacre at Jonestown resulted in the deaths of Ryan and four others, including three journalists. Jackie Speier, who now represents Ryan's former seat, was a staffer on that fateful trip and was shot five times but survived. The Jonestown tragedy culminated in the mass suicide of Jones and over 900 followers on the same day Ryan was murdered.

A decade earlier, in June 1968, New York Senator Robert Kennedy, a prominent candidate for the Democratic presidential nomination, was assassinated in Los Angeles shortly after winning the California primary. The shooting occurred at the Ambassador Hotel. Among those wounded in the attack by Sirhan Sirhan was Paul Schrade, a regional director for the United Auto Workers Union and labor chair for Robert Kennedy's campaign. Schrade was one of five individuals shot in addition to Senator Kennedy.

The most significant incident involving multiple members of Congress occurred on March 1, 1954. A group of Puerto Rican nationalists entered the House press gallery and opened fire, shouting slogans for Puerto Rican independence. Representatives Clifford Davis (Tennessee), Alvin Bentley (Michigan), Ben Jensen (Iowa), George Hyde Fallon (Maryland), and Kenneth Roberts (Alabama) were injured in the attack, though all five eventually recovered. The incident highlighted the ongoing political tensions surrounding U.S. control of Puerto Rico.

Charles Orear, a 50-year-old restaurant manager from St. Louis, offered his insights on James T. Hodgkinson, the man identified as the shooter. Orear stated that he knew Hodgkinson from their time working together on Senator Bernie Sanders's presidential campaign in Iowa.

According to Orear, Hodgkinson was a strong progressive and showed no signs of violence or malice toward others. When Orear learned of the incident, he was shocked, exclaiming, "You've got to be kidding me."

He recalled meeting Hodgkinson during the campaign in Iowa, specifically working with him in the Quad Cities area. Orear described Hodgkinson as a "quiet guy," "very mellow, very reserved." He remembered them staying overnight at a Sanders supporter's home in Rock Island, Illinois, after canvassing.

"He was this union tradesman, pretty stocky, and we stayed up talking politics," Orear said. He reiterated Hodgkinson's political views, noting, "He was more on the really progressive side of things." The Washington Post reached out to Orear after noticing he had liked one of Hodgkinson's Facebook posts.

Former Speaker Newt Gingrich (R-Ga.) on Wednesday linked a growing trend of "hostility" on the left to violent incidents like the shooting that morning at congressional Republicans' baseball practice. "It's part of a pattern," Gingrich said on Fox News. "An increasing intensity of hostility on the left." Gingrich pointed to the violence and intimidation toward conservatives on college campuses as another sign of the left's hostility toward Republicans and President Trump. ADVERTISEMENT "Look, I talk to college students regularly," Gingrich continued, "who say that if they are openly for Trump, they get threatened." "I've had college students tell me they get threatened with being beaten up, some of them get death threats," he said. Gingrich then targeted comedian Kathy Griffin as an example of a member of the left advocating violence against Trump and other Republicans. Griffin was fired from CNN's New Year's Eve program after she posted a photo of her holding up a dummy head of Trump covered in ketchup. "The intensity is very real, whether it's a so-called comedian holding up the president's head covered in blood, or right here in New York City, a play that shows the president being assassinated, or it's Democratic leading national politicians using vulgarity because they can't find any common language to talk," Gingrich argued. He likely was referring to Central Park’s summer Shakespeare production of “Julius Caesar” that appears to portray the slain Roman dictator as Trump and to Sen. Kirsten Gillibrand Kirsten Elizabeth GillibrandChris Murphy’s profile rises with gun tragedies Overnight Energy: Dems take on Trump's chemical safety pick Dems lambaste Trump’s ‘outrageous’ EPA chemical safety pick MORE (D-N.Y.), who recently slammed the president for not keeping his promises using the expression "f--- no."

Never miss a new post from Kevin McDermott. Sign up for our daily email alerts and receive a direct link to his latest content as soon as it's published.

We understand your inbox can get crowded. That's why we guarantee just one email per day, and only when new material is actually available. You won't receive any other messages.

Your subscription is confirmed. If you experienced an issue, please try again.

Following a shooting at a baseball field in Alexandria, Virginia, during a practice for an annual charity game, Governor Terry McAuliffe addressed the pressing issue of gun control. The incident resulted in five injuries, notably including House Majority Whip Steve Scalise. This event immediately brought the debate over firearm regulations to the forefront of public discussion.

### Governor's Urgent Call for Greater Public Safety

During a press conference, Governor McAuliffe emphatically underscored the critical need for more robust measures to ensure public safety. "I believe we must take additional steps to protect all of our citizens," he stated. While acknowledging that immediate policy debates were not the primary focus at that moment, he articulated his persistent concern about the pervasive accessibility of firearms. "The proliferation of guns on our streets is a significant issue," the Democratic governor commented. He specifically emphasized the vital role of comprehensive background checks and the closure of existing loopholes, particularly those found at gun shows. He stressed that these issues have broad implications for the safety of all individuals, extending beyond just elected officials.

### Political Repercussions and Legislative Adjustments

The shooting incident swiftly ignited discussions surrounding gun rights and gun control policies. While lawmakers from across the political spectrum condemned the violence and advocated for unity, the event also influenced legislative timelines. A scheduled House subcommittee hearing, intended to discuss a proposal to ease regulations on gun silencers, was postponed in light of the shooting. This postponement highlighted the immediate impact of the incident on the legislative agenda.

### Defending Second Amendment Freedoms

Alabama Republican Representative Mo Brooks, who was present at the baseball practice and remained unharmed, reiterated his steadfast dedication to upholding Second Amendment rights. He observed that while constitutional liberties may present potential challenges, the recent incident exemplified a misuse of those rights. "My stance on the rights we cherish as Americans remains unchanged," Brooks declared. He also expressed a desire to thoroughly investigate the shooter's background, including any history of felony convictions, before forming a definitive judgment. This approach underscores a commitment to understanding the full context of such events.

query: What was the significance of the 'happy hour' debate held before the main Republican primary debate in Cleveland?

documents.text:
The Republican presidential candidates' "happy hour" debate, a 5 p.m. face-off preceding the prime-time showdown, carries significant weight for the seven lower-polling contenders invited to the Fox News/Facebook stage. This smaller forum presents a crucial opportunity for at least one candidate to make a substantial impact, generating the buzz needed to transform a nascent campaign into a serious contender. Conservative strategist and Reagan biographer Craig Shirley predicts, "An underdog is going to emerge from this first debate."

Among those seeking a breakthrough is Texas Gov. Rick Perry, aiming for redemption after his 2012 campaign faltered due to a notable debate misstep. Former New York Gov. George Pataki, though not sharing the main stage with front-runner Donald Trump, appears prepared to challenge the billionaire businessman. Carly Fiorina, the sole woman in the field, has explicitly stated her potential as a formidable opponent to Democratic front-runner Hillary Clinton. Any of these seven candidates could seize a breakout moment on Thursday afternoon, propelling them into the upper echelon of the seventeen individuals currently vying for the nomination.

The candidates participating in the 5 p.m. debate are: Rick Perry, George Pataki, Carly Fiorina, former Pennsylvania Sen. Rick Santorum, Louisiana Gov. Bobby Jindal, South Carolina Sen. Lindsey Graham, and former Virginia Gov. Jim Gilmore.

Pataki articulated to Fox News that the current political landscape is primarily defined by name recognition, with a "reality show host" and individuals with presidential family ties leading the pack. He intends to differentiate himself through his gubernatorial record, asserting, "I think I changed New York with sweeping conservative change more than any other state in America." Pataki advocates for a shift away from superficial rhetoric, stating, "Enough of the sound bites, enough of the posturing. Bring Americans together, governor successfully, solve our problems and move forward with confidence."

Similar to Trump, Fiorina possesses a business background. However, she highlighted to Fox News, "I've had a very different set of experiences than Donald Trump. I started out as a secretary, I went on eventually to lead the largest technology company in the world. So it's a very different experience set of working my way up." She further noted her extensive interactions with world leaders, remarking, "Only I didn't do photo ops, I wrestled with bureaucracies."

Regarding his debate strategy, Santorum told Fox News he plans to be "authentic." Having participated in numerous debates previously, he believes his legislative record as a former senator—including efforts to ban partial-birth abortions, enact welfare reform, and impose sanctions on Iran—will enable him to stand out. "They're really isn't anybody else up there on the stage who has a record of accomplishment in Washington, D.C.," he asserted.

Syndicated columnist and Washington Examiner chief political correspondent Byron York observed that the 5 p.m. debate offers distinct advantages. Notably, Trump's absence will prevent him from dominating the spotlight, potentially leading to a less personality-driven discussion and allowing the debate's victor to gain significant momentum. Shirley suggested that the media might be inclined to elevate a candidate from this initial debate. He posited, "I think the first debate is not going to get as much attention but it might surprise everybody and be the most issue-oriented because of the distracting fight opening up between [Jeb] Bush and Trump in the second debate."

Both debates, co-hosted by Fox News and Facebook in collaboration with the Ohio Republican Party, will take place at the Quicken Loans Arena in Cleveland, Ohio. The 9 p.m. ET prime-time debate will feature the top ten candidates based on recent national poll averages: Donald Trump, former Florida Gov. Jeb Bush, Wisconsin Gov. Scott Walker, former Arkansas Gov. Mike Huckabee, retired neurosurgeon Ben Carson, Texas Sen. Ted Cruz, Florida Sen. Marco Rubio, Kentucky Sen. Rand Paul, New Jersey Gov. Chris Christie, and Ohio Gov. John Kasich.

Republican presidential candidates from left, Jim Gilmore, Lindsey Graham, Bobby Jindal, Rick Perry, Rick Santorum, Carly Fiorina, and George Pataki take the stage for a pre-debate forum at the Quicken Loans Arena, Thursday, Aug. 6, 2015, in Cleveland. Seven of the candidates have not qualified for the primetime debate. (AP Photo/Andrew Harnik) This is a full transcript of the Republican party’s first debate of Thursday night--an undercard featuring seven lower-performing candidates. HEMMER: This is first official event in the campaign for the Republican nomination for president. Welcome to Cleveland Ohio. It is debate night. HEMMER: I'm Bill Hemmer. MACCALLUM: And I'm Martha MacCallum. It all starts here. We are ready, the candidates are ready. We're live at the Quicken Loans Arena, where we have partnered with Facebook to bring you, the voter, into today's debate. HEMMER: So you will hear from all 17 candidates tonight, and you'll meet seven of them right now, starting with three-time governor in the state of Texas, Rick Perry. (APPLAUSE) MACCALLUM: Also, two-time senator from Pennsylvania, Rick Santorum. (APPLAUSE) HEMMER: Two-time. Two-time governor of the State of Louisiana, Acting Governor Bobby Jindal. HEMMER: So you will hear from all 17 candidates tonight, and you'll meet seven of them right now, starting with three-time governor in the state of Texas, Rick Perry. (APPLAUSE) MACCALLUM: Also, two-time senator from Pennsylvania, Rick Santorum. (APPLAUSE) HEMMER: Two-time. Two-time governor of the State of Louisiana, Acting Governor Bobby Jindal. (APPLAUSE) MACCALLUM: Businesswoman and former CEO of Hewlett-Packard, Carly Fiorina. (APPLAUSE) HEMMER: The senior senator from South Carolina, Lindsey Graham. (APPLAUSE) MACCALLUM: Former three-term governor of New York, George Pataki. (APPLAUSE) [Live updates: The Republican presidential debate] HEMMER: And former Virginia governor, Jim Gilmore. MACCALLUM: Now, this debate will last one hour. We're going to have four commercial breaks. MACCALLUM: Each candidate will have one minute to anwer each question and 30 seconds for rebuttal. If you run out of time, you're going to hear this. OK? HEMMER: Gentle. MACCALLUM: Everybody got the bell? HEMMER: Wait til you hear what the others are going to get later, huh? MACCALLUM: Exactly. (LAUGHTER) HEMMER: One year from now, a Republican nominee will be standing on this stage in this very same arena. That person is in Cleveland today. So let's get started. First topic, electability. First question to Governor Perry from Texas. Welcome, Governor. PERRY: It's good to be with you. HEMMER: You were in charge of the 12th largest economy in the world, and you recently said that four years ago, you weren't ready for this job. HEMMER: Why should someone vote for you now? PERRY: After those four years of looking back and being prepared, the preparation to be the most powerful individual in the world requires an extraordinary amount of work: not just having been the governor of the 12th largest economy in the world, which I might add, we added 1.5 million jobs during that period of time over that 2007 through 2014 period, a period when America was going through the most deep recession it had been through since the Great Depression. I think Americans want someone to have a track record of showing them how to get this country back on record, someone who will stand up and every day project that best days of America are in front of us. And I will assure you, as the governor of the state of Texas, and as those last four years have shown me, the preparation to be ready to stand on this stage and talk about those monetary policies, those domestic policies, and those foreign policies, Americans are going to see that I am ready to be that individual. HEMMER: Thank you, Governor. MACCALLUM: Now we go to Carly Fiorina. Carly, you were CEO of Hewlett-Packard. You ran for Senate and lost in California in 2010. This week, you said "Margaret Thatcher was not content to manage a great nation in decline, and neither am I." Given your current standings in the polls, is the Iron Lady comparison a stretch? FIORINA: Well, I would begin by reminding people that at this point in previous presidential elections, Jimmy Carter couldn't win, Ronald Reagan couldn't win, Bill Clinton couldn't win, and neither could've Barack Obama. I started as a secretary and became ultimately the chief executive of the largest technology company in the world, almost $90 billion in over 150 countries. I know personally how extraordinary and unique this nation is. I think to be commander in chief in the 21st century requires someone who understands how the economy works, someone who understands how the world works and who's in it; I know more world leaders on the stage today than anyone running, with the possible exception of Hillary Clinton; understands bureaucracies, how to cut them down to size and hold them accountable; and understands technology, which is a tool, but it's also a weapon that's being used against us. Most importantly, I think I understand leadership, which sometimes requires a tough call in a tough time. But mostly, the highest calling of leadership is to challenge the status-quo and unlock the potential of others. We need a leader who will lead the resurgence of this great nation and unlock its potential once again. Thank you. HEMMER: Senator Santorum, you won the Iowa caucus four years ago and 10 other states. But you failed to beat Mitt Romney for the nomination. And no one here tonight is going to question your conviction or your love for country. But has your moment passed, Senator? SANTORUM: I would say the message that got us the win in Iowa and 10 other states against pretty overwhelming odds, is the message that's going to deliver us in this election. SANTORUM: You know, we didn't start out four years ago at the top of the heap. We were behind where we were today. But we stuck to our message. We stuck to the fact that Americans are tired of Washington corporate interests and Democrats who are interested in just politics and power and they're looking for someone who's going to fight for them; looking for someone who's going to grow manufacturing sector of our economy, so those 74 percent of Americans who don't have a college degree have a chance to rise again. Someone who's going to stand up, and be very clear with our enemies as to the lines their going to draw and stand with them. I've got a track record. The reason I did so well last time is not just because of the vision, it's because I have a track record in Washington, D.C. of getting things done. Iran sanctions -- the Iran sanctions that brought them to the table, those are sanctions that we put in place when I was in the United States Senate, and a whole host of other things that put me in a position of saying, I not only have a great vision, but I can govern effectively in Washington. HEMMER: Thank you, Senator. MACCALLUM: Governor Jindal, you're one of two sitting governors on the stage tonight. But your approval numbers at home are in the mid 30s at this point. In a recent poll that showed you in a head-to- head against Hillary Clinton in Louisiana, she beat you by several points. So if the people of Louisiana are not satisfied, what makes you think that the people of this nation would be? JINDAL: Well, first of all, thank you all for having us. You know, I won two record elections. Last time I was elected governor, won a record margin in my state. Martha, we got a lot of politicians that will kiss babies, cut ribbons, do whatever it takes to be popular. That's not why I ran for office. I ran for office to make the generational changes in Louisiana. We've cut 26 percent of our budget. We have 30,000 fewer state bureaucrats than the day I took office. I don't think anybody has cut that much government anywhere, at any time. As a result, eight credit upgrades; as a result, a top ten state for private sector job creation. And we fought for statewide school choice, where the dollars follow the child, instead of the child following the dollars. We've been the most pro-life state six years in a row. My point is this: I won two landslide elections, I made big changes. I think our country is tired of the politicians who simply read the polls and fail to lead. Both Democrats and Republicans have gotten us in the mess we're in -- $18 trillion of debt, a bad deal with Iran, we're not staying with Israel. I think the American people are look for real leadership. That's what I've done in Louisiana, that's what I'll do in America. HEMMER: Senator Lindsey Graham, you worked with Democrats and President Obama when it came to climate change, something you know is extremely unpopular with conservative Republicans. How can they trust you based on that record? GRAHAM: You can trust me to do the following: that when I get on change with Hillary Clinton, we won't be debating about the science, we'll be debating about the solutions. In her world, cap- and-trade would dominate, that we will destroy the economy in the name of helping the environment. In my world, we'll focus on energy independence and a clean environment. When it comes to fossil fuels, we're going to find more here and use less. Over time, we're going to become energy independent. I am tired of sending $300 billion overseas to buy oil from people who hate our guts. The choice between a weak economy and a strong environment is a false choice, that is not the choice I'll offer America. A healthy environment, a strong economy and energy independent America -- that would be the purpose of my presidency, is break the strangle hold that people enjoy on fossil fuels who hate our guts. HEMMER: Thank you, Senator. MACCALLUM: Governor Pataki, four years ago this month, you called it quits in a race for the presidency in 2012, but now you're back. Mitt Romney declined to run this time, because he believed that the party needed new blood. Does he have a point? PATAKI: I think he means somebody who hasn't been a career politician, and who's been out of office for awhile. I think the last eight years in the private sector have allowed me to see government from the outside, and I think that is a positive thing. Yes, I thought about running four years ago. I was ready to lead, but I wasn't ready to run. But I look at this country today, and I look at how divided we are, I look at how politicians are always posturing and issuing sound bites but never solving problems. What I did in New York was bring people together, an overwhelmingly Democratic state. But I was able to get Democrats to support the most conservative sweeping policy changes in any state in America. And when I look at Washington today, we need to bring us together. We need to solve problems, we need to rebuild our military so we can stand up to radical Islam, we need to get our economy growing much faster by throwing out the corrupt tax code and lowering the rates. We have to end crony capitalism in Washington, where the lobbyists and the powerful can get tax breaks and tax credits, and the American people don't get laws in their interest. I can do that. And I can do it regardless of what the makeup of Congress is because I did it in New York state. So we need new leadership -- yes. I will be that new leader. MACCALLUM: Thank you, Governor. From one side of the stage, the other -- the other, Governor Jim Gilmore. You were the last person on stage to declare your candidacy. You ran for the White House once and lost. You ran for the Senate one time and lost. You haven't held public office in 13 years. Similar question, is it time for new blood? GILMORE: I think the times are different now. I think the times are much more serious. Because Obama and Clinton policies, the United States is moving further and further into a decline. I want to reverse that decline. That's why I've entered this race, and I think I have the experience to do it. Former elected prosecutor, attorney general, governor, I was elected to all of those offices. A person who, in fact, has a long experience in foreign-policy issues, which is different from many of the other governors and prospective governors who are running. I was an Army intelligence agent and a veteran during the Cold War, assigned to West Germany. I was the chairman of the National Commission on Homeland Security and Terrorism for the United States for five years. I was a person who has dealt extensively with these homeland security issues. I was a governor during the 9/11 attack. I understand both of these issues, how to build the economy and doing that as a governor who'd built jobs, had cut taxes and also a governor who understands foreign-policy, and that's why I entered this race. HEMMER: Thank you, Governor. MACCALLUM: Alright, everybody. Now to the elephant that is not in the room tonight, Donald Trump. Let's take a look at this graphic that shows the huge amount of political chatter that he is driving on Facebook right now, some of it good, probably, some of it bad. But he is dominating this conversation. Governor Perry, you two have been going at it. But given the large disparity in your poll numbers, he seems to be getting the better of you. PERRY: Well, when you look at the celebrity of Donald Trump, then I think that says a lot about it. One thing I like to remind people is, back in 2007, Rudy Giuliani was leading the polls for almost a year. I'll suggest a part of that was his celebrity. Fred Thompson was the other one, a man who had spent a lot of time on that screen. I've had my issues with Donald Trump. I talked about Donald Trump from the standpoint of being an individual who was using his celebrity rather than his conservatism. How can you run for the Republican nomination and be for single- payer health care? I mean, I ask that with all due respect. And nobody, nobody on either one of these stages has done more than I've done and the people of the State of Texas to deal with securing that border. We sent our Texas ranger recon teams. We sent our parks and wildlife wardens. I deployed the National Guard after I stood on the ramp in Dallas, Texas and looked the president of the United States in the eye and said, "Mr. President, if you won't secure the border, Texas will," and that's exactly what we did. We need a president that doesn't just talk a game, but a president that's got real results. Also, when you look at these videos, they are horrific and show just a hideous disrespect for life. What else we can do is that we should believe in science. PATAKI: You know, Hillary Clinton's always saying how Republicans don't follow science? Well, they're the ones not listening to the scientists today, because doctors say that at 20 weeks that is a viable life inside the womb. And at that point, it's a life that we have the right to protect, and I think we should protect. So, I would pass legislation outlawing abortion after 20 weeks. It is Hillary, it is Biden, it is the others who insist on allowing abortion well into viable (inaudible) wrong, and that should be stopped. MACCALLUM: All right. On the same topic, let's go to Governor Jindal. Carly Fiorina, also on the stage, said that she would go so far as to shut down the government over the issue of defunding Planned Parenthood. Would you do that? Would you be willing to shut down the government when it comes to defunding this group? JINDAL: Well, a couple of things. Planned Parenthood had better hope that Hillary Clinton wins this election, because I guarantee under President Jindal, January 2017, the Department of Justice and the IRS and everybody else that we can send from the federal government will be going in to Planned Parenthood. This is absolutely disgusting, and revolts the conscience of the nation. Absolutely, we need to defund Planned Parenthood. In my own state, for example, we launched an investigation, asked the FBI to cooperate. We just, earlier this week, kicked them out of Medicaid in Louisiana as well, canceled their provider contract. They don't provide any abortions in Louisiana. But in terms of shutting down the government, I don't think President Obama should choose to shut down the government simply to send taxpayer dollars to this group that has been caught, I believe, breaking the law, but also offending our values and our ethics. It is time for Republicans in D.C. to fight. Too often, they give up, they negotiate with themselves. They said they would get rid of the unconstitutional amnesty. They didn't do that. They said they would repeal Obamacare if we gave them the majority. They didn't do that either. They said they'd shrink and balance the budget. They haven't done that. Absolutely, they should fight to fund -- defund Planned Parenthood, and I don't think the president should shut down the government simply to send our taxpayer dollars to this group. MACCALLUM: All right. Lindsey Graham, this conversation will no doubt go to the war on women, and that cutting funding to this group could be a very broad brush against all of you or anybody who will hold this nomination as being against women's health, against these organizations that people will say provide positive things for many women. GRAHAM: I don't think it's a war on women for all of us as Americans to stand up and stop harvesting organs from little babies. Let's take the money that we would give to Planned Parenthood and put it in women's health care without having to harvest the organs of the unborn. The only way we're going to defund Planned Parenthood is have a pro-life president. You want to see a war on women? Come with me to Iraq and Afghanistan, folks. I've been there 35 times. I will show you what they do to women. These mythical Arab armies that my friends talk about that are going to protect us don't exist. If I am president of the United States, we're going to send soldiers back to Iraq, back to Syria, to keep us from being attacked here and keep soldiers in Afghanistan because we must. I cannot tell you how much our nation is threatened and how we need a commander in chief who understands the threats to this nation. If you're running for president of the United States and you do not understand that we cannot defend this nation without more of our soldiers over there, you are not ready for this job. HEMMER: Thank you, Senator. Executive power. It appears that you all have a little bit of an issue with it at the moment. I want to move through this as quickly as I can, from stage left to stage right. On the second day of his presidency, January 22nd 2009, President Obama signed one of his first executive orders. That was close Guantanamo Bay in Cuba. Still open today. What would be your first executive order? Governor Gilmore, start. GILMORE: Well, it's not a matter of what the first executive order would be, Bill. The matter is what orders exist now that shouldn't exist? The president has done an executive order with respect to illegal immigration that is illegal. Illegal. And it creates a -- a contempt for the law, for the rule of law. If i were the president of the United States, I would go and look at every executive order that exists right now and determine which ones want to be voided, because the president shouldn't be legislating: not through that vehicle or any other. We should be relying upon the leadership of the Congress to pass the laws. HEMMER: Senator Graham. GRAHAM: Change the Mexico City policy, not take one dime of taxpayer money to fund abortion organizations overseas, and restore the NSA that's been gutted. We're going dark when it comes to detecting the next attack. We have gutted our ability to detect the next attack. And I would not stand for that as president of the United States. I would take the fight to these guys, whatever it took, as long as it took. HEMMER: Governor Jindal, your first executive order would be in the White House would be what? JINDAL: To repeal these unconstitutional illegal orders, whether it's amnesty or whether it's this president going around the Congress, whether it's in Obamacare, to restore the rule of law. I'd also go after these sanctuary cities, do everything we can to make sure that we are not -- that we are actually prosecuting and cutting off funding for cities that are harboring illegal aliens, and then finally making sure the IRS is not going after conservative or religious groups. I would sign an executive order protecting religious liberty, our first amendment rights, so Christian business owners and individuals don't face discrimination for having a traditional view of marriage. HEMMER: Governor Perry. PERRY: It'll be a pretty busy day, but that Iran negotiation is going to be torn up on day one. We're going to start the process of securing that border. I'm also going to take a bottle of White-Out with me to get started on all those executive orders that Mr. Obama has put his name to. HEMMER: That will be a long day. PERRY: It will be a long day. HEMMER: Senator Santorum? SANTORUM: Just ditto to that. We're going to suspend -- I've -- I've said this for four years. We're going to suspend and repeal every executive order, every regulation that cost American jobs and is -- is -- is impacting our freedom.
