Eric Paddock also said he believed his brother owned a couple guns. Police found 23 guns, including semiautomatic rifles, in Paddock's room at the Mandalay Bay Resort and Casino. They found 19 more at his home in Mesquite, Nev., 80 miles northeast of here. And in yet another home owned by Paddock in Reno, authorities found five handguns, two shotguns, and a plethora of ammunition. CLOSE Las Vegas Metro Police have released footage from the body cameras worn by officers responding to the scene of the shooting Oct. 2, 2017. Las Vegas Metropolitan Police Department A federal law enforcement official, who was briefed on the matter but not authorized to comment publicly, said police also found two tripods positioned at the hotel windows in what appeared to be a fully equipped sniper’s nest. While the investigation intensified, authorities touted the city’s resilience in the aftermath of the tragedy. Fully stocked blood banks were soon turning away would-be donors. A GoFundMe page collected $3.3 million from almost 50,000 donors in little more than 24 hours. Overall, donations for the victims have exceeded $3.7 million, Commissioner Stephen F. Sisolak said Tuesday afternoon. Pieces of normalcy slowly returned to the iconic Strip. Club promoters hawked 2-for-1 deals, tourists took selfies and police cracked down would-be criminals. “Do you know how disrespectful it is to come out here and deal drugs the day after 50 people died one block away?’’ a bike cop snapped at a man in handcuffs Monday night. “Can’t you take one day off?’’ Derek Koerner, who identified himself as a licensed club promoter, said he was encouraged by the sizable crowds — and particularly grateful for the SUV full of men interested in his 2-for-1 deals on strip clubs. A $40 cover for a two-drink minimum gets the buyer into the second strip club for free. “There’s more people out here than I thought there’d be,’’ said Koerner, 48. “People seem to be upbeat and have a good energy. People seem to be moving on with their lives.’’ But less than a mile away, the mood was noticeably different. Dozens of people, some arriving with candles and flowers, returned to the scene of the horrific crime, marked by yellow police tape. Herman Gold and his ex-wife, Sara, stood near the concert grounds and explained how they escaped the hail of bullets and why they came back. “I just want closure,’’ Herman Gold said. “Why? What’s the reason that on that particular night, one individual would sacrifice everyone else for his insanity?’’ Joe Larsen, standing at the same corner and wearing a VIP wristband from the Aldean concert, expressed gratitude. Larsen, 49, who flew in from New Jersey for the concert, said that he watched the Twin Towers fall in 2001, lost his wife to cancer in 2015 and, on Sunday night, feared he was about to leave his son and daughter without a parent. He said he saw a young woman die from a bullet to the head before he called his father during the bedlam. “I told him, ‘I don’t know if going to make it. Tell my kids to live a good life’ ” Larsen recalled. As he and others shared their stories and wiped away tears, police officers closely monitored pedestrians, especially the wayward ones. “Sir, get out of the street,’’ one officer barked. “It’s the last time I’m telling you.’’ Read more: Shooter may have used trigger crank Machine guns have long been a draw in Las Vegas Bacon reported from McLean, Va. Contributing: Kevin Johnson and Ashley May, USA TODAY; Brett Kelman, The Desert Sun; Megan Cassidy, The Arizona Republic; Mike Benner, KGW-TV, Portland, Ore. **Las Vegas Shooting: New Evidence Points to "Life Is Beautiful" Festival as Potential Initial Target**

Investigators are now scrutinizing compelling new evidence that suggests Stephen Paddock, the perpetrator of the Las Vegas mass shooting, may have initially intended to target the annual Life Is Beautiful Festival. This popular music festival, which draws thousands of attendees, took place the week prior to the tragic Route 91 Harvest Festival shooting. According to a senior law enforcement official with intimate knowledge of the ongoing investigation, Paddock had rented multiple condominium units. These units offered a clear and unobstructed vantage point of the Life Is Beautiful Festival grounds. The festival itself featured performances by prominent artists such as Lorde and Chance the Rapper, attracting a diverse crowd.

**Examining The Ogden Building's Role**

In an effort to corroborate these developing claims, The Daily Beast undertook an in-depth investigation into The Ogden. This prominent high-rise residential building is situated in a location that provides an unobstructed view of the area where the Life Is Beautiful Festival was held. A spokesperson for Fais Foley Warren, the management company for The Ogden, released a carefully worded statement. The statement declared: "We cannot confirm or deny any information concerning Mr. Paddock's alleged actions. We strongly recommend directing all inquiries to the Metropolitan Police Department, as this investigation remains active and ongoing." This statement from Fais Foley Warren underscores the sensitivity surrounding the investigation.

**A Potential Shift in Paddock's Plans**

The senior law enforcement source further indicated that Paddock's meticulously laid plans may have undergone a significant shift. This potential change in intention could have occurred even before he checked into the Mandalay Bay Hotel on September 28. It was from his suite at the Mandalay Bay, strategically positioned to overlook the Route 91 Harvest Festival, that Paddock unleashed his deadly assault. His victims were among the approximately 20,000 concertgoers who had gathered for the event. Authorities subsequently discovered an arsenal of 23 firearms within the hotel suite. Many of these were high-powered rifles that had been illegally modified to fire automatically, a detail confirmed by the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives (ATF).

Sheriff Joseph Lombardo of the Las Vegas Metropolitan Police Department officially confirmed that Paddock had meticulously transported this substantial collection of weapons. He utilized 10 separate suitcases to conceal and move the firearms. Additional extensive searches conducted at his residence in Mesquite, Nevada, by the FBI and local law enforcement uncovered an additional 19 firearms. Investigators also cataloged a significant quantity of ammunition, including thousands of rounds. Furthermore, materials indicative of bomb-making were discovered, most notably ammonium nitrate found in his vehicle, a substance commonly used in explosives. The National Bomb Squad was called in to assess the potential threat posed by these materials. The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) is continuing to analyze all recovered evidence.

Investigators trying to determine what sparked the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history have found the massacre to be the work of a sophisticated planner with the means and desire to inflict unprecedented carnage. But on the second full day after Stephen Paddock smashed out the windows of a high-floor suite on the Las Vegas Strip and opened fire on a crowd of unsuspecting concertgoers, authorities still were trying to understand what drove him to such evil. Several new details emerged Tuesday about Paddock — a 64-year-old retired accountant — and how he worked methodically to thwart law enforcement as he killed scores of people and injured hundreds more. [ Two strangers bond over country music and beer. Then the gunshots started. ] As he fired round after round during an 11-minute stretch from a suite on the 32nd floor of the Mandalay Bay Resort and Casino, Paddock used video cameras to keep an eye out for police storming his hotel room, according to Clark County Sheriff Joe Lombardo. Paddock hid one camera in the peephole of his suite and two more in the hall, at least one of them disguised on a service cart, authorities said. At one point, he shot numerous rounds through the door, wounding a security guard. Paddock eventually put a gun in his own mouth and pulled the trigger as SWAT officers closed in. They found him with blood pooling behind his head and around the empty shell casings that littered the carpet, a handgun near his body. “It was preplanned, extensively, and I’m pretty sure that he evaluated everything that he did in his actions, which is troublesome,�? Lombardo said. The sheriff said investigators were “making progress�? on determining a motive, but complete answers remained elusive. There were precious few clues in Paddock’s background. An investigator works in the room at the Mandalay Bay Resort and Casino on Tuesday, in Las Vegas. A gunman opened fire from the room, killing at least 58 people and injuring hundreds. (AP Photo/Marcio Jose Sanchez) Neighbors in several states where Paddock owned homes in retirement communities describe him as surly, unfriendly and standoffish. Paddock was the son of a bank robber who was once on the FBI’s most-wanted list and who authorities described at the time as a “psychopath,�? but Paddock’s brother said their father was not involved in their lives when they were children. Relatives say the roots of Paddock’s loner lifestyle might have been planted on July 28, 1960. On that day, when Paddock was 7, a neighbor from across the street took him swimming. The neighbor told a local newspaper at the time that she knew authorities were coming for his father, and she wanted to spare the young boy from the trauma of seeing his father taken away. From that point on, Paddock’s family was never the same. Until carrying out the massacre Sunday night, Paddock had no criminal history himself. Despite repeated claims by the Islamic State to the contrary, he also had no ties to international terror groups, authorities said. He had done some government work during his career, as a letter carrier for the Postal Service, an agent for the Internal Revenue Service and an auditor for the federal government’s Defense Contract Audit Agency in the late 1970s and 1980s. He was divorced twice and recently had been dating a woman from the Philippines who has Australian citizenship. He was known to gamble routinely and extensively. Some public officials seemed to suggest Paddock’s mind was troubled, though there were no immediate indications that he had been diagnosed with a mental illness or was anything other than fully aware of what he was doing. “A normal person would not cause this type of harm to innocent people,�? said Rep. Ruben Kihuen (D-Nev.). “Clearly, there was something wrong with this man.�? People close to the investigation also said that in the weeks before the attack, Paddock transferred a large amount of money — close to $100,000 — to someone in the Philippines, possibly his girlfriend. The significance of that development was not immediately clear, though investigators said they were interested in probing Paddock’s finances and his avid interest in high-stakes gambling. The girlfriend, Marilou Danley, returned to the United States from the Philippines Tuesday night and was met at Los Angeles International Airport by FBI agents, according to wire reports. Investigators considered her a “person of interest,�? Lombardo said. Sen. Dean Heller (R-Nev.) said that police are eager to talk to her, as Paddock “doesn’t meet any profiles�? and the “best lead is through this girlfriend.�? “They don’t know a lot about who the girlfriend is and why she left the country a week prior to the shooting,�? said Heller, who has been briefed by authorities. “She is someone they need to have this discussion with to better understand the shooting and what his thought process was.�? Douglas and Sylvia Vick from Texas pray at a makeshift memorial on the Las Vegas Strip on Tuesday. (Mark Ralston/AFP/Getty Images) At his home in Orlando, Eric Paddock, Stephen Paddock’s brother, said he doubts Danley had any prior knowledge of the incident and speculated that Stephen might have been trying to quietly ensure her financial stability. Stephen Paddock loved and doted on his girlfriend, whom he had met when she was a hostess at a casino, Eric Paddock said. The couple often gambled side by side. “He manipulated her to be as far away from here and safe when he committed this,�? Eric Paddock said. “The people he loved he took care of, and as he was descending into hell he took care of her.�? The shooting — which left 59 people dead and injured more than 500 others — reignited the debate on Capitol Hill and across the country about whether lawmakers should impose new restrictions on firearms. Clark County Coroner John Fudenberg on Tuesday evening clarified that Paddock was among the 59 slain in the incident; previously, authorities had said he wasn’t. Undersheriff Kevin C. McMahill, speaking after Fudenberg, warned that the number of dead and injured could fluctuate as the investigation progresses. “The answer that the coroner provided you is the most recent, relevant number that we have,�? McMahill said. Caution tape lines the doors of the gunman’s suite on the 32nd floor of the Mandalay Bay hotel. (Bild Exclusive/Polaris.) Several Democrats used the massacre to push for new restrictions, and House Speaker Paul D. Ryan (R-Wis.) asserted Tuesday that Republican leaders have no plans to advance a bill, which passed a House committee last month, that would make it easier to buy gun silencers. Leaving the White House to visit hurricane-ravaged Puerto Rico on Tuesday morning, President Trump said that “we’ll be talking about gun laws as time goes by.�? Paddock brought 23 guns to his Mandalay Bay suite, which overlooked the concert grounds on the southern end of the Strip. Included in that cache was an AR-15-type rifle with a high-capacity magazine and 12 “bump stocks,�? a modification that would allow a gun to fire as if it were an automatic weapon. He also brought another AR-15-type rifle with a magnification scope commonly used for hunting and a bipod stand to help steady it, according to law enforcement officials and experts who reviewed images of the weapons posted online. “For this individual to take it upon himself to create this chaos and harm is unspeakable,�? Lombardo said. [ The lives lost in the Las Vegas massacre ] The guns appeared to be but a piece of the arsenal Paddock had acquired during a period of years, at least some of it legally obtained from major retailers such as Cabela’s, national retailer of guns and outdoor gear, and Guns & Guitars, a store in Mesquite, Nev., according to people familiar with the case. ATF Special Agent in Charge Jill A. Snyder said Tuesday Paddock had purchased shotguns, handguns and rifles in Nevada, Utah, California and Texas. At Paddock’s home in Mesquite, Nev., investigators recovered 19 more guns and an explosive, and in his car, they found ammonium nitrate, a chemical that can be used in bomb making. At another property in Reno, Nev., investigators recovered five more handguns, two shotguns and a “plethora�? of ammunition, Lombardo said Tuesday. Paddock probably secreted the weapons to his Mandalay Bay suite using more than 10 suitcases he brought with him. Lombardo said hotel housekeeping had been there since Paddock checked in on Sept. 28 and spotted nothing nefarious. Lombardo said investigators had scoured Paddock’s suite, his car and the homes in Nevada, and they were examining surveillance and body camera video evidence and other electronic items they had seized in their searches. Separately, Lombardo said the department had opened an investigation into the unauthorized release of images that show the crime scene. Another sheriff’s official confirmed the authenticity of those images. Police also continued documenting the crime scene and performing the grim task of identifying those slain, some of whom died blocks away from the concert as they tried to flee or seek help. As of late Tuesday afternoon, authorities had tentatively identified all of the victims. [ How the shooting unfolded ] Since the shooting, horrific stories have emerged of lives cut short, some taking bullets as they sought to protect their loved ones. Bobby and Jenny Parks, a married couple from California who had been high school sweethearts, were swaying to a love song when a bullet struck her. He held her as she died. After Cameron Robinson, a 27-year-old from Utah, was shot in the neck, his boyfriend, Robert Eardley, carried him to a vehicle. Robinson died before they made it to a hospital. Two people who met at the festival — Kody Robertson and Michelle Vo — were watching the final act together when a bullet struck her chest. Robertson threw his body over hers to protect her. Later, they were separated; he tracked her down to a hospital and was told late Monday morning that she was dead. Paddock’s weaponry would have allowed him to fire both rapidly and precisely at the crowd that was several hundred yards away and below him. The shots — fired from two windows of Mandalay Bay’s golden facade, which Paddock had smashed out — sent thousands of people scrambling for safety under Vegas’s neon glow. Police on Tuesday showed body camera footage of officers scrambling to determine where the shots came from as they yelled for people to leave the area. “They’re shooting right at us, guys,�? an officer can be heard saying. “Stay down! Stay down!�? Hospitals across the region continued to treat patients from the scene, many of them seriously injured. Sunrise Hospital and Medical Center says as of Tuesday night, 59 patients remain in the hospital and 31 of them are still in critical condition. ### Diazepam Prescription for Las Vegas Shooter Stephen Paddock Revealed

A prescription for the anti-anxiety drug diazepam was issued to Stephen Paddock, the 64-year-old Mesquite resident responsible for the Las Vegas massacre, on June 21, 2017. This information comes from a state Prescription Monitoring Program record obtained by the Review-Journal. Paddock filled this prescription for 50 10-milligram tablets on the same day it was written, purchasing the medication, known by its brand name Valium, without insurance at a Walgreens in Reno.

#### Potential Link to Aggressive Behavior

Diazepam belongs to the benzodiazepine class of drugs, which are sedative-hypnotics. Studies have indicated a potential link between benzodiazepine use and aggressive behavior. Dr. Mel Pohl, chief medical officer of the Las Vegas Recovery Center, explained that for individuals with an underlying predisposition to aggression, sedatives like diazepam can exacerbate these tendencies, acting similarly to alcohol in disinhibiting emotional states. He noted that the drug's effects can be amplified by alcohol consumption.

#### Research on Benzodiazepines and Violence

Further research has explored the association between benzodiazepine use and violent acts. A 2015 study published in *World Psychiatry*, involving 960 Finnish adults and adolescents convicted of homicide, found a 45 percent higher likelihood of killing during periods when they were taking benzodiazepines. Additionally, a study in the *Australian and New Zealand Journal of Psychiatry* from 2014 concluded that benzodiazepine use is moderately associated with subsequent aggressive behavior.

#### Expert Opinions on Paddock's Actions

Dr. Michael First, a clinical psychiatry professor at Columbia University and an expert on benzodiazepines, acknowledged the findings of the Finnish study but suggested that the drugs are more likely to fuel impulsive aggression rather than premeditated actions. He pointed out that Paddock's attack, which involved meticulously planning and bringing an arsenal of weapons to the Mandalay Bay hotel, appeared to be highly premeditated. Dr. First emphasized the importance of understanding the reasons behind Paddock's prescription, suggesting it could offer insight into his actions.

#### Previous Prescription and Broader Concerns

The Nevada state monitoring report also indicated that Dr. Steven Winkler, the Henderson physician who prescribed the diazepam in June 2017, had previously prescribed 50 10-milligram tablets of the same drug to Paddock in 2016. That prescription was filled the same day it was written at Evergreen Drugs in Henderson, with instructions for two tablets per day. The revelation of this prescription adds to ongoing discussions and concerns about the potential link between psychiatric medications and mass shootings, although definitive connections remain elusive and researchers caution against stigmatizing individuals who rely on these medications for their well-being.

**Off-Duty Firefighters: Unsung Heroes of the Las Vegas Mass Shooting**

The October 1, 2017, Las Vegas country music festival shooting, a horrific event, underscored the extraordinary bravery and swift actions of off-duty firefighters. Present as civilians, these individuals became critical first responders amidst unimaginable terror. When a lone gunman opened fire from the Mandalay Bay hotel, thousands of concertgoers experienced a night of entertainment devolve into chaos. Among the first to react were off-duty firefighters like Benjamin Kole, Jesse Gomez, and Anthony Robone. Lacking their usual emergency equipment, such as radios and advanced medical kits, these trained professionals were compelled to act. They transformed into indispensable lifesavers in the critical moments following the initial gunfire. For eleven harrowing minutes, their extensive training, ingrained sense of duty, and inherent drive to assist propelled them into action, making them vital components of the immediate response.

Benjamin Kole, a firefighter, found himself amidst the fleeing crowd. His immediate, decisive command, "Not one (vehicle) leaves without a patient, a victim," underscored his unwavering commitment to directing every available resource towards aiding the injured. His daughter, Rachel Kole, employed by an ambulance company and present at the event, was also caught in the unfolding tragedy. After a brief, emotional reunion amidst the pandemonium, Benjamin’s focus shifted from personal safety to his professional obligation. "Time to get to work," he declared. Together, father and daughter began providing immediate medical assistance to those around them. Kole was deeply struck by the remarkable composure and effectiveness of civilian bystanders. Without hesitation, they followed instructions and contributed significantly to the rescue efforts. Their willingness to step forward and assist, even in the face of extreme danger, was a testament to the human spirit's capacity for compassion and courage.

Jesse Gomez, attending the festival with his family, initially mistook the sustained gunfire for fireworks. However, the escalating panic and the frantic exodus of performers from the stage quickly dispelled any illusions, revealing the grim reality of a mass shooting. As his family sought safety, Gomez’s attention was drawn to a woman suffering from a severe head wound. He made the difficult decision to direct his family to continue to safety while he and other bystanders attended to her. He then made what he described as "probably one of the hardest decisions" he has ever made: calling his wife to inform her that he was remaining to help. His wife, upon meeting him in the parking lot, implored him not to return to the active threat zone. Gomez recounted the terrifying proximity of perceived bullet impacts, suggesting a close-quarters firefight was underway. Despite the escalating danger and the palpable fear, he and another individual "went back to work," demonstrating an unwavering dedication to their duty. Gomez was profoundly moved by the significant number of civilians who chose to remain and offer aid. They improvised with available materials like tarps, trash cans, and even fences to transport the wounded. He also described the somber and emotionally taxing duty of relocating deceased individuals. This was a necessary action that required informing grieving relatives they had to evacuate the immediate vicinity due to ongoing risks. This task, performed under immense duress, highlighted the multifaceted and often heartbreaking responsibilities faced by those on the ground.

Anthony Robone’s experience was equally traumatic and deeply personal. He initially attempted to reassure his girlfriend that the sounds were innocuous. However, as the situation escalated and he suspected gunshots, he discovered his brother had been struck and was bleeding profusely from the mouth. While a friend escorted his girlfriend to safety, Robone and another companion worked to transport his injured brother towards what appeared to be medical triage areas. The relentless gunfire was overwhelming, with Robone noting, "The rounds seemed like they’d never stop." After administering immediate care to his brother using improvised bandages, Robone’s professional training took precedence. He witnessed a substantial number of fatalities, acknowledging the painful reality: "Unfortunately we couldn’t help at that time." However, amidst the devastation, he also witnessed an inspiring demonstration of collective resilience and selflessness. Civilians were actively applying direct pressure to wounds, utilizing belts as improvised tourniquets, and exhibiting profound altruism. Robone recounted instances where patients with survivable injuries, despite their own pain and suffering, urged first responders to prioritize those with more critical conditions. "I was shot in the arm, I can wait … hey that person is shot in the head or that person is shot in the neck, let them go (to the hospital)," they reportedly conveyed to responders. This was a testament to an extraordinary level of altruism amidst the crisis. This selflessness, even in the face of their own life-threatening injuries, was a powerful display of humanity.

These three firefighters, along with twelve other off-duty firefighters who sustained gunshot wounds themselves – two of whom were injured while performing cardiopulmonary resuscitation on victims – were among the many who responded when their community faced an unprecedented crisis. The International Association of Fire Fighters union corroborated these harrowing details. Less than 48 hours after the deadliest mass shooting in modern U.S. history, these courageous individuals convened at the Las Vegas Fire Fighters Union Hall to share their experiences and process the profound trauma they had endured. They discussed the agonizing choices they were compelled to make, including the difficult task of informing critically injured individuals that, despite their utmost efforts and the limited resources available, further intervention was not possible. This profound sense of duty, even when off-duty and without their standard operational equipment, underscores the exceptional character of these first responders and the remarkable resilience of the human spirit in the face of unimaginable tragedy. Their actions serve as a powerful testament to the dedication and bravery inherent in the firefighting profession, extending far beyond the confines of their official duties and highlighting the profound impact of their commitment to saving lives. The events of that night serve as a stark reminder of the vital role that off-duty first responders play in moments of crisis, often stepping into the breach with nothing but their training and their courage.

(Associated Press) LAS VEGAS (AP) — The Latest on the mass shooting in Las Vegas (all times local): 1:45 a.m. The Australia-based sisters of Las Vegas gunman Stephen Paddock's girlfriend say they believe Paddock sent her away so she wouldn't interfere with his plans to go on a shooting rampage. Marilou Danley's sisters were interviewed by Australia's Channel 7 TV network with their faces obscured and their names withheld. They said they were surprised to learn Danley had gone to the Philippines two weeks ago. One sister tearfully said: "I know that she don't know anything." The woman said Danley is "a good person" who would've stopped Paddock had she been there. ___ 1 a.m. A nephew of Stephen Paddock's girlfriend says he's stunned by the actions of the Las Vegas gunman and didn't even know that Paddock was interested in guns. Jordan Knights' aunt is Marilou Danley, a 62-year-old who recently returned to the United States from a weekslong trip abroad. The Australian man says he spent time in Las Vegas with Paddock and Danley just a few months ago. Knights told Australia's Channel 9 from his home near Brisbane, "It seemed like he just looked after my aunty and that was it." The 23-year-old said he didn't give Paddock another thought until he was identified as the gunman who killed 59 people and wounded more than 500 on Sunday. He said that Paddock "didn't seem like he was the type of guy to do that." ___ 12:09 a.m. The investigation of a gunman who killed 59 people at a Las Vegas concert now shifts to his girlfriend, who has returned to the United States from the Philippines. Sheriff Joseph Lombardo says investigators are hoping to get some insight from Marilou Danley on why her boyfriend Stephen Paddock opened fire on a concert crowd from a high-rise hotel room. Danley had been out of the country for weeks before the shooting. A law enforcement official says she arrived on a flight from Manila to Los Angeles where FBI agents were waiting for her late Tuesday night. The official wasn't authorized to publicly discuss the matter and spoke to The Associated Press on condition of anonymity. On Wednesday, President Donald Trump is set to arrive in Las Vegas to meet with public officials, first responders and some of the 527 people injured in the attack.

Investigators are meticulously examining the arsenal of weapons and ammunition amassed by the Las Vegas shooter, Stephen Paddock. Federal law enforcement officials revealed that two rifles were equipped with scopes and positioned on tripods, aimed through large hotel windows. Additionally, AR-15-style assault rifles were among the firearms discovered. These officials, who spoke anonymously due to their inability to publicly disclose investigative details, provided these insights.

Sheriff Lombardo detailed that Mr. Paddock transported at least ten suitcases into his hotel suite over an unspecified period. The sheriff further stated that Paddock fired through his hotel room door, striking a security guard in the leg. The guard, thankfully, survived the incident. SWAT officers entered the premises immediately after the guard was shot.

Beyond the weapons found at the hotel, authorities recovered nineteen firearms, explosives, several thousand rounds of ammunition, and various "electronic devices" from Mr. Paddock’s residence in Mesquite, Nevada.

During his acquisition of firearms, Mr. Paddock did not raise any suspicions at the stores where he made his purchases. The investigation into his extensive gun collection led authorities to New Frontier Armory, a prominent firearms dealer, distributor, and manufacturer in North Las Vegas. David M. Famiglietti, the company's president, confirmed that Mr. Paddock bought "several" rifles and shotguns from their establishment the previous spring, all in a single transaction. Mr. Famiglietti explained that purchasing multiple firearms at once is not uncommon for customers seeking to economize on background check fees.

"The firearms he purchased did not leave our store capable of what we’ve seen and heard in the video without modification," Mr. Famiglietti stated. He clarified that the weapons were not fully automatic and had not been modified in any way, legally or illegally, at the time of purchase. Mr. Famiglietti emphasized that Mr. Paddock exhibited no concerning behavior to the store's sales clerk. On the contrary, he appeared interested in competitive shooting, inquiring extensively about three-gun shooting matches, a rapidly growing sport involving a rifle, shotgun, and pistol.

"He just seemed like a normal guy," Mr. Famiglietti remarked, noting that his store has since been subjected to hate mail, threatening phone calls, and fabricated reviews. "We obviously did not sell him these firearms with the intent that he would use them to hurt anyone in any way."

On Monday, Guns & Guitars, a Mesquite, Nevada-based gun dealer, acknowledged selling two rifles and a handgun to Mr. Paddock. Similar to the transactions at Frontier Armory, these purchases successfully passed state and federal background checks. One of Mr. Paddock's brothers described him as "not an avid gun guy at all."

Mr. Paddock, aged 64, was characterized as a high-stakes gambler who resided in a tranquil retirement community and enjoyed playing golf. Officials indicated he had no significant criminal history and generally kept to himself. Investigators are currently scrutinizing his financial records for any potential clues that might illuminate his motivations.

The image shows Stephen Paddock in a photograph provided by his brother, Eric Paddock, via the Associated Press. Mr. Paddock frequently gambled substantial amounts at major casino hotels. His girlfriend, Ms. Danley, 62, who was abroad during the shooting, had previously been employed by some of these establishments. They resided in Mesquite, Nevada, approximately 80 miles northeast of Las Vegas, and he also owned a property in Reno. Ms. Danley boarded a flight from Manila to Los Angeles on Tuesday, according to Antonette Mangrobang, a spokesperson for the Philippine Immigration Bureau. Authorities, who met Ms. Danley upon her arrival in Los Angeles, have designated her a "person of interest" in the shooting investigation, a status that does not necessarily imply suspicion of criminal involvement.

One witness recounted, "I’ve never been that scared in my life." Law enforcement estimates that approximately 22,000 individuals were present at the Harvest Festival when the shooting commenced, listening to Jason Aldean, the final performer of the three-day event. Video footage captured nine seconds of continuous, rapid gunfire, followed by 37 seconds of silence from the weapon, punctuated by panicked screams from the crowd. Gunfire then resumed in extended bursts. Initially, some concertgoers mistook the sounds for fireworks. However, as the reality of the situation became apparent, attendees fled, many disoriented about the source of the shots or their safest escape route.

"Everyone was running, you could see people getting shot," stated Gail Davis, one of the witnesses. "I’ve never been that scared in my life," she added, expressing her disbelief: "To have this happen, I can’t wrap my mind around it." Video from the incident shows Mr. Aldean retreating from the stage as the gunfire erupted.

## Bump Stocks: From Niche Accessory to National Gun Control Debate

The deadliest mass shooting in U.S. history thrust "bump stocks" into the national spotlight. These controversial devices dramatically increase a semi-automatic rifle's rate of fire, effectively mimicking the capabilities of automatic weapons. Once a niche accessory popular among YouTube gun enthusiasts aiming to simulate machine gun fire, bump stocks have now become a central demand for gun control advocates seeking a ban.

### How Bump Stocks Enable Rapid Fire

Bump stocks attach to semi-automatic firearms and utilize the weapon's recoil to function. When the rifle recoils, the trigger mechanism is propelled forward, striking the shooter's finger. This action allows for a continuous firing sequence without the shooter needing to repeatedly pull the trigger. Consequently, the rate of fire is significantly accelerated. One company, Bump Fire Systems, advertised these devices on its website, promoting the ability to achieve "simulated full-auto firing" legally for $99.99.

### A "Toy" for Enthusiasts, Not a Serious Tool

Gun experts have historically described bump stocks as a "toy" or an item primarily of interest to "gun geeks." They were not considered a tool for serious shooters who prioritize accuracy. Before the Las Vegas attack, these devices were frequently showcased in online videos featuring gun stunts and enthusiast modifications.

### Calls for a Ban and Exploiting Legal Loopholes

The use of bump stocks by the Las Vegas shooter has intensified calls for their prohibition. Senator Dianne Feinstein, a long-time advocate for stricter gun laws, declared that banning bump stocks should be a top priority following the tragedy. Shannon Watts, founder of Moms Demand Action, argued that hardware capable of transforming legal firearms into automatic weapons should be prohibited. She contended that such devices circumvent federal law, which heavily regulates automatic weapons.

David Chipman, a former ATF agent and now a policy advisor for Americans for Responsible Solutions, explained that bump stocks represent a "masterful creation of a technical workaround creating a loophole to circumvent the intention of the law." He pointed out that the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives (ATF) has repeatedly confirmed that bump stocks and similar devices are not strictly regulated under existing federal firearms legislation.

### Personal Experiences and Concerns

One bump stock owner, who requested anonymity, purchased a device for approximately $100 two years before the shooting. He explained that it was the closest he could get to simulating a machine gun. This was particularly relevant in his home state of Delaware, which bans machine gun ownership. He noted that purchasing the bump stock did not require a background check, likening the process to ordering ammunition online.

While he found the device to be a fun "range toy," he also acknowledged its difficulty to master and its inaccuracy. He stated, "This thing requires practice." He suggested that many gun stores might avoid selling bump stocks to prevent attracting "irresponsible gun owners." These individuals might be influenced by online videos without fully considering the consequences. Similarly, many shooting ranges prohibit their use due to safety and liability concerns.

Ultimately, the novelty of the bump stock wore off for him. He found it unreliable for self-defense. He expressed concern that the actions of one individual might lead to a ban on the devices. He believes this would be regrettable and contrary to his principles of freedom.

query: What evidence does the article provide to refute the Daily Mail's claim of a mini-ice age due to decreased solar activity?

documents.text: The Daily Mail in Britain tends to be terrific for news of the weird — who can forget its exposé on the girl who ate nothing but chicken McNuggets for 15 years? — and terrible for science reporting. Case in point: Their latest story on how the world is about to enter a mini-ice age. The piece cites recent research from the UK Met Office on how solar activity is set to decrease in the coming years — which, in turn, will cool the planet. That’s technically true, but the decrease in solar activity won’t be enough to counteract the warming effects of all the greenhouse-gas pollution we’re putting into the air. How do we know? Just ask the UK Met Office. In a post refuting the Daily Mail, the climate research center notes that greenhouse gases are on pace to warm the planet 2.5°C over the next 90 years. At most, the decrease in solar activity is expected to cool the planet by about 0.13°C. The math here is simple: 2.5°C minus 0.13°C equals 2.37°C. That’s not an ice age. That’s the Earth getting hotter. As a bonus, the Daily Mail also repeats the canard that global warming has somehow “stopped” since 1998. The trick here is to cherry-pick numbers and start from the exceptionally hot El Niño year. Taking a longer view, the world is clearly warming. Here’s a nice animated chart from Skeptical Science demonstrating that it’s possible to pick plenty of periods in the past 100 years to imply that the world is cooling — even though the overall trend is clearly upward: Meanwhile, as the Met Office notes, the 2000s were clearly the hottest decade in the instrumental record, beating the previous record set in the 1990s, which in turn beat the previous record set in the 1980s. Despite what the Daily Mail says, the world is warming and the Thames isn’t about to freeze over. The paper does, however, raise excellent questions about the wisdom of eating nothing but Chicken McNuggets.

When considering **global warming**, public office candidates must understand that the widely promoted idea of **near-universal scientific consensus demanding drastic action is not entirely accurate**. A significant and growing group of respected scientists and engineers **hold differing views**, questioning the necessity of extreme measures.

Nobel Prize-winning physicist **Ivar Giaever** publicly resigned from the American Physical Society (APS) in September. His resignation letter stated his dissent: "I did not renew [my membership] because I cannot live with the [APS policy] statement: 'The evidence is incontrovertible: Global warming is occurring... We must reduce emissions of greenhouse gases beginning now.'" Giaever found it perplexing that the APS would allow discussions on topics like proton mass changes or the multi-universe, yet label the evidence for global warming as "**incontrovertible**."

Despite a decades-long global campaign promoting the idea that rising carbon dioxide (CO2) levels will devastate civilization, many prominent scientists echo Dr. Giaever's perspective. The number of scientific "**heretics**" is steadily increasing, supported by factual evidence. A crucial, often overlooked, fact is the **absence of significant global warming for over a decade**. Climate scientist Kevin Trenberth acknowledged this in a 2009 "Climategate" email, stating: "The fact is that we can't account for the lack of warming at the moment and it is a travesty that we can't."

This observed plateau in warming becomes evident when comparing **real-world data with computer models**. These models frequently amplify the minor warming effect of CO2 through feedback mechanisms involving water vapor and clouds. The lack of warming over the past decade, and the slower-than-predicted warming trend over the 22 years since the U.N.'s Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) began issuing projections, strongly suggest these models **significantly overestimate CO2's warming potential**. In response to this discrepancy, proponents of alarm have shifted their focus from warming itself to weather extremes, attributing any unusual climate event to CO2.

It is important to understand that **CO2 is not a pollutant**. It is a colorless, odorless gas that is **fundamental to life**, exhaled by humans and a vital component of the biosphere. Greenhouse operators routinely increase CO2 concentrations by three to four times to promote plant growth, as plants flourish with elevated CO2 levels. This is unsurprising, considering that plant and animal life evolved when CO2 concentrations were approximately ten times higher than they are today. While advancements in plant varieties, chemical fertilizers, and agricultural management have undeniably boosted crop yields, **increased atmospheric CO2 has also likely played a contributing role** in this growth.

Although the number of scientists publicly voicing dissent is growing, many younger scientists privately harbor serious doubts about the prevailing global warming narrative. However, they often **fear professional repercussions**, such as delayed promotions or more severe consequences. These anxieties are not unfounded. In 2003, Dr. Chris de Freitas, then editor of the journal Climate Research, published a peer-reviewed article concluding that recent warming was not unusual within the context of the past thousand years. The global warming establishment initiated a campaign to have him removed from his editorial position and dismissed from his university. Fortunately, Dr. de Freitas managed to retain his university employment. This suppression of scientific inquiry bears a resemblance to historical instances, such as Trofim Lysenko's manipulation of biology in the Soviet Union, where scientists who adhered to genetics, a field Lysenko dismissed as bourgeois fiction, faced persecution.

The intense fervor surrounding global warming and the reluctance of organizations like the American Physical Society to remove the word "incontrovertible" from their climate statements can be attributed to several factors, most notably **financial incentives**. Alarmism regarding climate change benefits numerous entities by facilitating government funding for academic research and expanding governmental bureaucracies. It also provides governments with a rationale for tax increases, creates avenues for taxpayer-funded subsidies for politically connected businesses, and attracts substantial donations to foundations dedicated to environmental causes.

Speaking on behalf of many scientists and engineers who have independently scrutinized climate science, we advise any candidate for public office that there is **no compelling scientific basis for drastic measures aimed at "decarbonizing" the global economy**. Even when accepting inflated climate forecasts from the IPCC, aggressive greenhouse gas control policies are **not economically justifiable**.

A recent study conducted by Yale economist William Nordhaus, examining various policy options, indicated that allowing 50 more years of economic growth without greenhouse gas controls offers the **highest benefit-to-cost ratio**. This approach would particularly benefit developing nations striving to achieve the material well-being, health, and life expectancy enjoyed by developed countries. Many other proposed policies, conversely, would yield negative returns. Furthermore, **increased CO2 and the modest warming it might induce are likely to benefit the planet overall**.

If elected officials feel compelled to take action on climate, we recommend supporting scientists who are dedicated to **enhancing our understanding of climate through well-designed instruments and rigorous data analysis**. A more profound understanding of climate will better equip us to adapt to its perpetually changing nature, which has historically presented significant challenges to human civilization. However, a substantial portion of the private and government investment in climate research warrants **critical review**. Candidates should champion sensible environmental protection measures, but investing in costly programs predicated on alarming, unsubstantiated claims of "incontrovertible" evidence is **ill-advised**.

*Claude Allegre, former director of the Institute for the Study of the Earth, University of Paris; J. Scott Armstrong, cofounder of the Journal of Forecasting and the International Journal of Forecasting; Jan Breslow, head of the Laboratory of Biochemical Genetics and Metabolism, Rockefeller University; Roger Cohen, fellow, American Physical Society; Edward David, member, National Academy of Engineering and National Academy of Sciences; William Happer, professor of physics, Princeton; Michael Kelly, professor of technology, University of Cambridge, U.K.; William Kininmonth, former head of climate research at the Australian Bureau of Meteorology; Richard Lindzen, professor of atmospheric sciences, MIT; James McGrath, professor of chemistry, Virginia Technical University; Rodney Nichols, former president and CEO of the New York Academy of Sciences; Burt Rutan, aerospace engineer, designer of Voyager and SpaceShipOne; Harrison H. Schmitt, Apollo 17 astronaut and former U.S. senator; Nir Shaviv, professor of astrophysics, Hebrew University, Jerusalem; Henk Tennekes, former director, Royal Dutch Meteorological Service; Antonio Zichichi, president of the World Federation of Scientists, Geneva.*

A recent Mail on Sunday article, authored by David Rose and titled "Forget global warming – it’s Cycle 25 we need to worry about," has been identified as misrepresenting scientific findings from the Met Office Hadley Centre. Rose's assertion that recent global temperature data indicated a 15-year period without warming is directly contradicted by the Met Office's own extensive research and data. While the Met Office provided detailed explanations concerning decadal projections and observed warming trends since 1997, these critical clarifications were not fully integrated into the published article.

A spokesperson for the Met Office elaborated on their projections, emphasizing the cutting-edge nature of their ten-year forecasts. They stated, "Our ten-year projection is still cutting-edge. The full period for the initial projection hasn't ended, and these forecasts are updated with the latest data. However, it's clear that warming continues. The 2000s were the hottest decade on record since 1850. Depending on the data used, 2010 was the warmest year for NOAA NCDC and NASA GISS, and the second warmest for HadCRUT3." This statement underscores the ongoing reality of climate change and the continuous refinement of scientific climate models.

The Mail on Sunday article also critically examined a specific Met Office study without first seeking comment on potential misunderstandings or misinterpretations of its findings. This particular research, which aligns with a broad consensus of scientific investigations, delved into the complex interplay between climate responses and variations in solar activity. The study's conclusions indicated that while solar output is projected to decrease over the next 90 years, this reduction is unlikely to significantly impede the global warming trend driven by greenhouse gases. The research estimated that a decline in solar activity would likely lead to a global temperature decrease of a mere 0.08°C. This figure represents a modest impact when contrasted with the projected warming of approximately 2.5°C over the same timeframe. This projected warming is based on greenhouse gas emissions under the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change's (IPCC) B2 scenario, which presumes a continuation of current emission levels without any mitigation efforts. Further analysis within the study posited that even if solar output were to fall below the levels observed during the Maunder Minimum (1645-1715), a historical period characterized by exceptionally low solar activity, global temperatures would only experience a reduction of 0.13°C. These findings, supported by the Met Office's own data and analysis, strongly underscore the dominant influence of anthropogenic greenhouse gas emissions on contemporary climate change, a conclusion echoed by numerous other scientific bodies including NASA's Goddard Institute for Space Studies (GISS) and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration's National Climatic Data Center (NOAA NCDC). The Met Office's own internal reviews and publications, such as those detailing the HadCRUT temperature dataset, consistently reinforce these conclusions. The article's selective use of data and its failure to fully incorporate the Met Office's detailed explanations have led to a misleading portrayal of the scientific consensus on climate change.

« Will Coral Reefs be Gone by the End of the Century? | Main | Global Warming Is Ruining The Minnesota Winter » Category: Climate • Climate Change • Global Warming • LIAR!!!! Posted on: January 29, 2012 11:21 AM, by Greg Laden Since the Daily Mail is a British thing and the latest form of entertainment in Britain is Libel Tourism, I won't say to you that the Daily Mail is a rag full of lies and deceit. Instead, I'll let you be the judge. These studies: Decline in solar output unlikely to offset global warming 23 January 2012 - New research has found that solar output is likely to reduce over the next 90 years but that will not substantially delay expected increases in global temperatures caused by greenhouse gases. Carried out by the Met Office and the University of Reading, the study establishes the most likely changes in the Sun's activity and looks at how this could affect near-surface temperatures on Earth. It found that the most likely outcome was that the Sun's output would decrease up to 2100, but this would only cause a reduction in global temperatures of 0.08 °C. This compares to an expected warming of about 2.5 °C over the same period due to greenhouse gases (according to the IPCC's B2 scenario for greenhouse gas emissions that does not involve efforts to mitigate emissions). Gareth Jones, a climate change detection scientist with the Met Office, said: "This research shows that the most likely change in the Sun's output will not have a big impact on global temperatures or do much to slow the warming we expect from greenhouse gases. Continued here and 4 January 2012 - 2012 is expected to be around 0.48 °C warmer than the long-term (1961-1990) global average of 14.0 °C, with a predicted likely range of between 0.34 °C and 0.62 °C, according to the Met Office annual global temperature forecast. The middle of this range would place 2012 within the top 10 warmest years in a series which goes back to 1850. The prediction follows provisional figures published by the Met Office and University of East Anglia last month which showed that 2011 saw temperatures 0.36 °C above the long term average and is currently ranked the 11th warmest year on record in the HadCRUT3 temperature dataset. At the same time the World Meteorological Organization published a global average temperature anomaly of 0.41 deg C based on an average of the three international global average temperature datasets1. Both the global average temperature value from HadCRUT3 and the WMO falls within the range predicted by the Met Office for 2011 of between 0.28 °C and 0.60 °C, with a most likely value of 0.44 °C above the long term average. This is consistent with the Met Office forecast which indicated that 2011 was unlikely to be a record year. source What the Daily Mail said:

A major challenge exists: it is far easier to dispense truthiness (and outright deceit)than it is to have to run after and rebut it. When it comes to anti-science syndrome suffering climate deniers and delayers, the whack-a-mole campaign of dealing with deceit, deception, and diversion is a never-ending and utterly exhausting process. And, that exhaustion is one of the powerful items in the quiver for serial deceivers — eventually the exhausted truthtellers run out of energy (and other resources) to respond. And, the deceit lives on without serious challenge. Of course, there is the not insignificant issue that serious interests are aligned with downplaying (even dismissive) climate change risks. Thus, it is easy for anyone with even a shred of credentials to get a powerful megaphone in, for example, the Murdoch disinformation empire. Today’s Wall Street Journal provides (yet) another example of this sad reality. With the publication of “No Need to Panic About Global Warming”, the editor made sure to reinforce the argument by pointing to the “authority” of “16 scientists listed at the end of the article” who signed it. (As Yes, 16 … although not all are scientists, still a heady number of some (reasonably) well known names, such as aviation pioneer Burt Rutan. These 16, however, are less well known for their passionate rejection of the scientific community’s understanding of climate risks and outright denial of many fundamental concepts. After the fold is an initial look at (yet) another recklessly misleading Wall Street Journal opinion piece. No Need to Panic About Global Warming There’s no compelling scientific argument for drastic action to ‘decarbonize’ the world’s economy. The basic point: “no need to panic” translates into delaying action. And, well, what does “compelling” mean? Acidification of the oceans, melting Arctic ice, changing planting guidance, disruptive weather patterns, and such are pretty compelling to most of the scientific community. And, of course, when speaking of “most”, we are talking of something like 97% of the relevant experts. Editor’s Note: The following has been signed by the 16 scientists listed at the end of the article: Ah, but there are 16 in disagreement … even as, when you look at the specialities and have reasons to respect some of them in their own domain (or even be in awe of them), most are not climate science experts. A candidate for public office in any contemporary democracy may have to consider what, if anything, to do about “global warming.” Candidates should understand that the oft-repeated claim that nearly all scientists demand that something dramatic be done to stop global warming is not true. In fact, a large and growing number of distinguished scientists and engineers do not agree that drastic actions on global warming are needed. Use of that “large” and “distinguished” to reinforce the appeals to authority … in the face of global scientific expertise backing climate science conclusions about a warming planet and humanity’s role in that warming. Perhaps the most inconvenient fact is the lack of global warming for well over 10 years now. Sigh … Yes, let’s peg everything on one year, one date: 1998. However, 9 of the 10 hottest years on record are in the past decade (with 1998 being the one exception). The 2000s were hotter than the 1990s globally. And, the 1990s were hotter than the 1980s. And, the 1980s … This group of 16 “scientists” is rejecting basic scientific honesty with this one sentence alone. When one speaks to climate, one speaks to trends and longer periods. One does not peg everything on a specific year. That “inconvenient fact” is nonexistent if one starts at 1997 or 1999 rather than 1998. And, well, if one uses (more appropriately) 30-year trend lines, that global warming pattern is quite clear. Their “inconvenient fact” is actually simply sleight of hand misrepresentation. This graphic is from Skeptical Science, which explains: As Figure 1 shows, over the last 37 years one can identify overlapping short windows of time when climate “skeptics” could have argued (and often did, i.e. here and here and here) that global warming had stopped. And yet over the entire period question containing these six cooling trends, the underlying trend is one of rapid global warming (0.27°C per decade, according to the new Berkeley Earth Surface Temperature [BEST] dataset). And while the global warming trend spans many decades, the longest cooling trend over this period is 10 years, which proves that each was caused by short-term noise dampening the long-term trend. This moles’ line about “no warming”, of course, shows the level of disdain that these 16 have for honest engagement. The fact is that CO2 is not a pollutant. CO2 is a colorless and odorless gas, exhaled at high concentrations by each of us, and a key component of the biosphere’s life cycle. Plants do so much better with more CO2 that greenhouse operators often increase the CO2 concentrations by factors of three or four to get better growth. This is no surprise since plants and animals evolved when CO2 concentrations were about 10 times larger than they are today. Better plant varieties, chemical fertilizers and agricultural management contributed to the great increase in agricultural yields of the past century, but part of the increase almost certainly came from additional CO2 in the atmosphere. Perhaps the 16 would like to engage with the science that shows lower mental performance with increased CO2 concentrations in classrooms … Or, how high Co2 concentrations can kill … Or the science looking how increased CO2 concentrations have mixed agricultural impacts, dependent on what type of plant and crop, and increased Co2 can actually lead to reduced quality yields in some circumstances … Or the reality of Liebig’s law which is that growth is limited to its scarcest resource. If there are temperature or nutrient or water or other limitations, that increased CO2 will not foster increased productivity. (Those Dutch roses, well, are fed lovingly massive amounts of water and nutrients in a temperature controlled environment.) Or … This argument path, ever so lovingly followed by so many climate science deceivers, is like arguing against regulations limiting arsenic or mercury pollutants since these are “natural” elements. Let us take another angle, we all need water to live and without it we will die. However, drinking too much water is also dangerous and, well, can kill you — try drinking a swimming pool … And … And … And … Whack-A-Mole … Why is there so much passion about global warming … There are several reasons, but a good place to start is the old question “cui bono?” Or the modern update, “Follow the money. Alarmism over climate is of great benefit to many, providing government funding for academic research and a reason for government bureaucracies to grow. Alarmism also offers an excuse for governments to raise taxes, taxpayer-funded subsidies for businesses that understand how to work the political system, and a lure for big donations to charitable foundations promising to save the planet. Another tool of deceivers … create an appearance of ‘pox on both houses’ by accusing the other that which seems plausible as undermining your own argument. Wow, a “lure for big donations” has led every single significant relevant scientific society in the world (with one interesting exception) to state that climate change is happening, humanity is driving it, and we should take action. Every … Single … One … For that “lure of big donations”, these thousands of scientists and these institutions have thrown science to the side. Well, what is bigger? “Big donations to charitable foundations” or the fossil fuel industry (the Exxon-Mobils, Peabody, Koch Brothers)? Lysenko and his team lived very well, and they fiercely defended their dogma and the privileges it brought them. … we have a message to any candidate for public office: There is no compelling scientific argument for drastic action to “decarbonize” the world’s economy. Declarative statements don’t necessarily make the statement truthful. Even if one accepts the inflated climate forecasts of the IPCC, aggressive greenhouse-gas control policies are not justified economically. A recent study of a wide variety of policy options by Yale economist William Nordhaus showed that nearly the highest benefit-to-cost ratio is achieved for a policy that allows 50 more years of economic growth unimpeded by greenhouse gas controls. This would be especially beneficial to the less-developed parts of the world that would like to share some of the same advantages of material well-being, health and life expectancy that the fully developed parts of the world enjoy now. How did Professor Nordhaus perceive this (mis)use of his material? “The piece completely misrepresented my work. My work has long taken the view that policies to slow global warming would have net economic benefits, in the trillion of dollars of present value. … I have advocated a carbon tax for many years …. I can only assume they either completely ignorant of the economics on the issue or are willfully misstating my findings.” Beyond sigh … Many other policy responses would have a negative return on investment. And it is likely that more CO2 and the modest warming that may come with it will be an overall benefit to the planet. If elected officials feel compelled to “do something” about climate, we recommend supporting the excellent scientists who are increasing our understanding of climate with well-designed instruments on satellites, in the oceans and on land, and in the analysis of observational data. The better we understand climate, the better we can cope with its ever-changing nature, which has complicated human life throughout history. However, much of the huge private and government investment in climate is badly in need of critical review. Classic climate delayer … let’s invest to learn more to help guide action tomorrow but put off action today because we need to know “more” before acting. Sigh, every serious look at this path comes to the same conclusion: delaying action means more expensive paths toward climate mitigation with increased risk that the actions will be inadequate. Every candidate should support rational measures to protect and improve our environment, but it makes no sense at all to back expensive programs that divert resources from real needs and are based on alarming but untenable claims of “incontrovertible” evidence. What is sad is that these “16” are arguing against taking “rational measures” with misleading representations of fact. They are not laying out a ‘no regrets strategy’, advocating aggressive action in arenas like energy efficiency and electrification of rail where, without question, the economic benefits are seriously net positive even without any consideration of the “decarbonization” implications. No, instead, they are leveraging their titles and the Murdoch disinformation machine to seek to delay action … delay that will be catastrophic if these 16 just happen to be wrong. NOTES: For one window the thinking of those signing this letter, perhaps take a few moments with just one of the signatories: “William Happer, professor of physics, Princeton”. Also on this OPED, Union of Concerned Scientists, Dismal Science at the Wall Street Journal. “While it’s entirely appropriate for scientists, like all citizens, to voice their personal opinions on public policy, the op-ed repeated a number of deeply misleading claims about climate science. To take just one example, the authors claim there has been a “lack of warming” for 10 years. Here’s what we know: 2011 was the 35 th year in a row in which global temperatures were above the historical average and 2010 and 2005 were the warmest years on record.
