Brussels Mayor Yvan Mayeur said "about 20" people had died and more than 100 had been injured, 17 of them severely. Eyewitnesses recall explosions In pictures: Brussels explosions Full coverage The station is close to EU institutions. The European Commission, the EU's executive arm, has told employees to stay indoors or at home. All meetings at EU institutions have been cancelled. Ryan McGhee, a catering worker at a college in Brussels, told the BBC: "The entire city is in lockdown. People are calm at the moment but the atmosphere is tense." Security raised Local and international travel has been suspended or disrupted and security tightened across Europe. All flights have been cancelled. The airport is due to reopen on Wednesday. Eurostar has cancelled all trains to and from Brussels. The Thalys France-Benelux train operator says the entire network is closed. In the UK, security has been stepped up at Gatwick and Heathrow airports. The UK Foreign Office has advised British nationals to avoid crowded areas in Belgium. UK Prime Minister David Cameron chaired a meeting of the Cobra response committee on Tuesday. In France the cabinet has held an emergency meeting. There is also extra security at Dutch airports. Image copyright Horst Pilger Image caption Security has been stepped up after the explosions Image copyright Ralph Usbeck via AP Image caption Passengers react in the smoke-filled aftermath at the airport 'Outrageous' attacks There has been strong international condemnation: US President Barack Obama called the blasts "outrageous attacks against innocent people". The 28 EU leaders said the bombings were an "attack on our open democratic society" in a joint statement. "The terrorists have struck Belgium but it is Europe that was targeted," said French President Francois Hollande. Russian President Vladimir Putin called the attacks "barbaric". Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption Brussels airport worker: 'My colleague hid in the luggage carousel' Belgium's Interior Minister Jan Jambon had said on Monday that the country was on the highest level of alert for possible revenge attacks after the capture on Friday of Salah Abdeslam, the main surviving suspect from the Paris attacks in November. Mr Jambon told Belgian radio: "We know that stopping one cell can... push others into action. We are aware of it in this case." Gaps in intelligence: By Chris Morris, Europe correspondent European security experts had been braced for another attack for months. But it is always a huge shock when it actually happens. If this was "revenge" for the arrest of Salah Abdeslam on Friday, it will be a source of considerable concern that a functioning terrorist network was able to respond so quickly and with such devastating effect. It is possible that a cell linked to Abdeslam brought forward the timing of a future attack because they thought he might blow their cover. Either way, it shows how advanced the planning was in terms of logistics, explosives, weapons and people willing to carry out such attacks on civilian targets. The priority now will be to ensure that anyone else who poses an imminent threat to the public is apprehended as soon as possible. But it is clear that there are still huge gaps in intelligence, and Brussels is seen as a soft target. In the words of French President Francois Hollande, the response from Europe will need to be "calm, lucid and determined" - and it will have to last for a long time.

VTM Nieuws uses cookies to enhance your online experience and safeguard your privacy. We employ cookies to understand site traffic and user interests, enabling us to offer free content, personalize your visits, and display relevant advertisements. For comprehensive details on the cookies we utilize, their specific functions, and how to manage your preferences, please refer to the "More information" link.

### How VTM Nieuws Uses Cookies

Cookies are fundamental to a user-friendly website. They can be placed by us or by third parties, such as advertising networks. Crucially, cookies **do not collect personal information** like your address or phone number, meaning they cannot be used for direct marketing purposes.

Our transparent cookie policy is designed to:

*   **Protect your privacy:** We handle your data with utmost care.
*   **Improve your experience:** Making our site easier to navigate and use.
*   **Keep content accessible:** Advertising revenue allows us to provide news at no cost to you.
*   **Maintain content quality:** Helping us deliver high-standard news consistently.

To adjust your cookie settings, click "Settings." By selecting "Continue to website," you consent to our use of cookies for an optimized browsing experience.

### Types of Cookies Used by VTM Nieuws

#### Essential Cookies

These cookies are **critical for our website's functionality**. They assist with:

*   Remembering your preferences, such as language and search history.
*   Ensuring smooth video playback.
*   Securely storing your login credentials.
*   Adapting the site to your browser's settings.
*   Detecting and preventing misuse of the site.
*   Maintaining the overall smooth operation of the website.
*   Enabling features like user comments.
*   Managing items in your shopping cart.
*   Saving your order details during the checkout process.
*   Efficiently processing data for forms and orders.

**Key Essential Cookies:**

*   **Adblock detect:** Identifies if an ad blocker is active.
*   **Adobe Typekit:** Ensures correct font display.
*   **DFP (Doubleclick):** Manages the delivery of advertisements.
*   **Drupal (post-authentication):** Supports site functions after a user logs in.
*   **Facebook connect:** Allows users to log in using their Facebook accounts.
*   **Freewheel:** Manages the delivery of video advertisements.
*   **Gigya socialize:** Handles user registration and login processes.
*   **Google IMA Ads:** Integrates Google's advertising software development kit.
*   **Google Maps GEO:** Collects geographical location data.
*   **Google Publisher tags:** Facilitates ad management through Google's platform.
*   **Google Recaptcha:** Protects against automated bot activity.
*   **Google Save Frame:** Assists in the display of advertising content.
*   **Hello Bar Smartbar:** Displays site-wide announcements.
*   **Myfonts Counter:** Tracks the usage of specific fonts.
*   **Sourcepoint:** Manages user consent and detects ad blockers.
*   **SSO Sign on:** Enables single sign-on functionality.
*   **SUMO Smartbar:** Displays site-wide notices.
*   **Usersnap:** Collects bug reports and user feedback.

#### Analytics Cookies

These cookies help us understand how you interact with different sections of our site. The data gathered allows us to refine our site's structure, navigation, and content. We receive anonymous statistics from third parties regarding visitor numbers and popular content. This data **is not linked to individual users**.

Analytics cookies help us:

*   Count unique visitors to our site.
*   Measure the duration of your page visits.
*   Analyze your navigation patterns across the site.
*   Identify areas where improvements can be made.
*   Enhance overall website performance.

**Key Analytics Cookies:**

*   **Boomerang:** Collects statistics on user behavior.
*   **Chartbeat (+ mab / video):** Provides insights into content and video engagement.
*   **CIM / Gemius:** Offers audience measurement data.
*   **CO2stats:** Tracks the website's carbon footprint.
*   **comScore Audience measurement:** Measures audience characteristics.
*   **Content Insights:** Displays data on editorial performance.
*   **Conviva:** Gathers analytics for video streaming.
*   **Crazy Egg:** Generates heatmaps and scrollmaps of user interaction.
*   **Formissimo:** Analyzes the submission of forms.
*   **Google Analytics:** Provides detailed analysis of website traffic.
*   **Google Tag Manager:** Assists in managing tracking tags.
*   **Hotjar:** Offers heatmaps and user feedback tools.
*   **Moat:** Measures ad viewability.
*   **Optimise A-B:** Supports A/B testing for site optimization.
*   **Snowplow Javascript Tracker:** Collects event data for analysis.
*   **Stats service:** Gathers general website statistics.
*   **UserZoom:** Aids in user experience research.
*   **Visual Measures:** Collects data on visual engagement.
*   **Visual Website Optimizer (VWO):** Enables A/B testing for site improvements.

For information on the data practices of third-party providers, please consult their respective privacy policies.

#### Personalization Cookies

Our goal is to deliver content that is highly relevant to your interests. These cookies help us tailor website content and advertisements to your specific preferences. Without them, you will still see ads, but they **will not be personalized**.

These cookies allow us to:

*   Suggest articles based on your reading history.
*   Recommend videos based on your viewing habits.
*   Propose topics based on your inferred interests.

**Key Personalization Cookies:**

*   **Froomle Recommendation:** Powers content suggestion algorithms.
*   **Gravity Recommandation:** Helps personalize content delivery.
*   **Trackuity iframes:** Manages advertisements within embedded content.

For details on third-party cookies, please refer to their privacy statements.

#### Advertising Cookies

A significant portion of our content is **provided free of charge, supported by advertising**. Some advertisements utilize cookies, which may be placed by us or by advertising networks. To personalize ads, we analyze your browsing activity to infer your interests, thereby displaying ads that are more likely to be relevant to you. For instance, a sports enthusiast will see different ads than someone interested in travel. Third parties may combine this data with your past interactions.

These cookies help us:

*   Record visits to infer user interests.
*   Share browsing data with other sites for advertising purposes.
*   Utilize third-party services for ad delivery.
*   Prevent the repeated display of advertisements.
*   Track ad clicks for billing purposes.
*   Monitor ad-driven sales for billing.

**Key Advertising Cookies:**

*   **Adhese:** Manages targeted advertising campaigns.
*   **Advertorials script:** Supports sponsored content.
*   **Facebook Pixel:** Enables retargeting through Facebook.
*   **Google Ad Words pixel:** Facilitates retargeting via Google Ads.
*   **Index Exchange:** Operates as an ad exchange platform.
*   **Krux:** Provides ad data management services.
*   **Ligatus:** Offers native advertising solutions.
*   **OnRecruit:** Tracks the performance of advertising campaigns.
*   **Sharethrough:** Distributes native advertisements.
*   **Shoppingminds:** Focuses on e-commerce advertising.
*   **Sticky Ads:** Delivers persistent ad formats.
*   **Teads:** Specializes in video advertising.

For information on third-party advertising cookies, please consult their privacy statements.

#### Social Media Cookies

You have the ability to share articles and videos directly through social media buttons. These buttons use social media cookies to recognize you when you share content.

These cookies enable:

*   Social networks to identify users for actions such as liking or sharing.
*   More relevant advertisements based on your social media activity.
*   Direct content sharing for users logged into their social media accounts.

For details regarding social media cookies, please review their respective privacy policies.

**Key Social Media Cookies:**

*   **Bluekai Social:** Assists in collecting social data.
*   **FB social plugins:** Enables sharing content via Facebook.
*   **Google+ Social sharing:** Supports sharing content through Google+.

Australian Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull has voiced concerns regarding European security measures, suggesting they deteriorated prior to the recent attacks in Belgium. When questioned about the possibility of a similar terrorist incident occurring in Australia, Turnbull stated, "You cannot guarantee that there will be no terrorist incident."

He further assured Australians that the nation's security infrastructure, including border protection and domestic security arrangements, is significantly more robust than those in Europe. Turnbull implied that the security lapses in Europe were connected to recent challenges faced by the continent.

In response, the federal opposition leader, Bill Shorten, commented that it was premature for the Prime Minister to critique Belgium's actions so soon after the tragedy. Shorten emphasized that the immediate focus should be on acknowledging the loss of innocent lives, with critical assessments to follow in due course. Australia's current national terrorism threat level is assessed as "probable."

Here's a revised account of Turkey's 2016 coup attempt, focusing on a direct timeline of events and employing more varied language for enhanced clarity and distinctiveness.

**Turkey's 2016 Coup Attempt: A Chronological Breakdown**

This timeline details the pivotal moments of the attempted coup in Turkey on Friday, July 15, 2016, based on Reuters reporting. All times are presented in Greenwich Mean Time (GMT).

**Early Hours: Unrest and Conflicting Declarations**

*   **19:29 GMT:** Key Istanbul arteries, the Bosphorus and Fatih Sultan Mehmet Bridges, were obstructed. Dogan News Agency footage showed traffic rerouted.
*   **19:50 GMT:** Gunfire echoed in Ankara, with military aircraft observed in the skies. Similar aerial activity was noted over Istanbul.
*   **20:02 GMT:** Prime Minister Binali Yildirim announced an ongoing coup attempt, urging public calm. He stated a military faction sought to overthrow the government, with security forces mobilized.
*   **20:25 GMT:** The Turkish military issued a statement via email and television, claiming control to preserve democratic order and international ties.
*   **20:38 GMT:** CNN Turk reported President Tayyip Erdogan was safe.
*   **20:47 GMT:** Anadolu Agency reported the Turkish chief of military staff was among hostages at Ankara military headquarters.
*   **20:49 GMT:** A presidential source stated the military's declaration lacked command authorization.
*   **20:57 GMT:** Internet monitors noted restrictions on major social media platforms like Facebook, Twitter, and YouTube in Turkey.
*   **20:58 GMT:** A TRT correspondent told Reuters soldiers had entered the Turkish state broadcaster's Ankara premises.
*   **21:02 GMT:** The head of the ruling AK Party's Istanbul branch reported soldiers entered their building, demanding evacuation.
*   **21:05 GMT:** The Turkish state broadcaster aired a military statement detailing plans for a new constitution, accusing the government of undermining democratic and secular principles, announcing a "peace council" for governance, and imposing martial law and a curfew.

**Mid-Evening: Escalation and Government Countermeasures**

*   **21:18 GMT:** A presidential source confirmed the president and government remained in power.
*   **21:22 GMT:** The Turkish Prime Minister tweeted that all necessary measures, including fatalities, would be taken to quell the coup. He mentioned sieges at undisclosed significant buildings and urged continued public composure.
*   **21:26 GMT:** President Tayyip Erdogan appealed to the public to resist the coup by a minority military faction. Via a cellphone video link to CNN Turk, he vowed a "necessary response" and announced his imminent return to Ankara.
*   **21:35 GMT:** Turkish state broadcaster TRT ceased transmissions, later resuming from London.
*   **21:51 GMT:** Eyewitnesses reported a military helicopter firing over Ankara, with an explosion heard in the capital.
*   **22:03 GMT:** The Turkish justice minister identified elements within the army linked to U.S.-based cleric Fethullah Gulen as instigators.
*   **22:08 GMT:** Tanks surrounded the Turkish parliament and opened fire. Gunfire was also audible at Istanbul airport.
*   **22:26 GMT:** Two distinct explosions struck the Turkish capital.
*   **22:37 GMT:** The commander of Turkey's First Army described the plotters as a minor faction, assuring there was "nothing to worry about."
*   **22:47 GMT:** The Prime Minister denounced the coup as terrorism by gangs and illicit formations, reaffirming government authority and urging citizens to take to the streets.
*   **22:51 GMT:** The special forces commander declared treason by a group that would ultimately fail, affirming the military's non-endorsement of the coup.
*   **22:59 GMT:** Turkish broadcaster NTV reported a fighter jet downed a coup plotters' helicopter over Ankara.
*   **23:04 GMT:** Anadolu Agency reported 17 police officers killed at Ankara special forces headquarters, a claim unconfirmed by independent sources.

**Late Evening: International Response and Apparent Resolution**

*   **23:05 GMT:** The White House stated President Obama and Secretary of State Kerry agreed all parties should support Turkey's democratically elected government, urging restraint and preventing bloodshed.
*   **23:13 GMT:** A group associated with Fethullah Gulen vehemently denied involvement, calling accusations "highly irresponsible," condemning military interference, and expressing concern for civilian safety.
*   **23:20 GMT:** U.S. Secretary of State John Kerry emphasized "absolute support for Turkey's democratically elected, civilian government and democratic institutions" in a call with the Turkish foreign minister.
*   **23:39 GMT:** Anadolu Agency reported a bomb struck the Turkish parliament in Ankara. A Reuters witness corroborated an explosion in Istanbul.
*   **23:52 GMT:** The Turkish Prime Minister declared the situation under control, attributing events to the Gulen movement and announcing a no-fly zone over Ankara. A U.S.-based organization linked to Gulen had previously disavowed participation.

On **March 22, 2016**, Brussels, Belgium, endured a series of devastating **coordinated terrorist attacks**. Explosions at **Brussels Airport** and on a **metro train** near the European Union's headquarters plunged the city into **chaos and fear**. These horrific events resulted in at least **31 deaths** and numerous injuries, marking a tragic escalation of terrorism in Europe.

The **Zaventem Airport** departure lounge became a scene of **unimaginable horror**. Shattered glass and debris filled the air after two explosions. Belgian Health Minister Maggie de Block reported **11 fatalities and 81 wounded** at the airport. Survivor Zach Mouzoun described the second blast as **catastrophic**, likening the aftermath to a **"war scene."** Airport worker Anthony Deloos noted the explosions occurred near the Swissport counters and a Starbucks. He described a disorienting sound and falling paper. Amateur video showed passengers fleeing the terminal. A European security official mentioned the discovery of at least one **Kalashnikov rifle** in the departure lounge, though its direct use remained unconfirmed. Marc Noel, a Belgian national, recounted being in a shop when the first explosion occurred about 50 yards away, calling it a **horrible experience**.

About an hour later, a third explosion struck a **rush-hour subway train** near the Maelbeek station, close to the EU's administrative center. This blast tragically killed **20 people and injured over 100**, according to Brussels Mayor Yvan Majeur. Survivor Alexandre Brans described the event as **panic everywhere**. Rescue workers set up a makeshift medical center, and police secured the area. Both the airport and the metro system were **completely shut down**.

These attacks prompted **immediate and extensive security enhancements** across Europe. Airports intensified protocols, and flights to Brussels faced significant disruption, with over **200 diversions or cancellations**. Belgian Prime Minister Charles Michel declared Belgium's **terror alert at the highest level**, leading to traffic diversions and public advisories to remain in place.

The attacks occurred amid **heightened security concerns** in Europe. Intelligence suggested the **Islamic State group** was planning strikes. The recent arrest of a key suspect in the November Paris attacks amplified fears of a broader network. While no group immediately claimed responsibility, Prime Minister Michel stated there was **no immediate evidence linking Salah Abdeslam**, the Paris suspect, to the Brussels bombings. Abdeslam had reportedly informed authorities of his involvement in planning further attacks.

query: What sparked Amazon Studios to investigate allegations against Jeffrey Tambor?

documents.text: (UPDATED with Jill Soloway statement) EXCLUSIVE: Three weeks after Amazon Studios boss Roy Price resigned over sexual harassment claims, the company is conducting an investigation into allegations against Transparent star Jeffrey Tambor. An Amazon spokesperson confirmed to Deadline that the company has initiated an investigation. In a statement, Tambor himself calls the claims “baseless.” In its early stages, the probe primarily stems from allegations by Tambor’s former assistant Van Barnes of implied inappropriate behavior on the part of the Emmy, Golden Globe and SAG Awards winner. The investigation into the claims by the transgender Barnes, which she made online in a private Facebook post, began earlier this week, we hear. In the post, she claims that her former boss, whom she does not identify by name, had repeatedly propositioned her, made lewd comments, groped her and threatened to sue her if she spoke up. “I am aware that a former disgruntled assistant of mine has made a private post implying that I had acted in an improper manner toward her,” Tambor told Deadline today. “I adamantly and vehemently reject and deny any and all implication and allegation that I have ever engaged in any improper behavior toward this person or any other person I have ever worked with. I am appalled and distressed by this baseless allegation.” Amazon’s stance on such allegations is to investigate promptly and thoroughly, a source says. The investigation is in the process of speaking to members of the Transparent production and Tambor personally. The Jill Soloway-created series, Amazon’s flagship comedy, is currently not in production. “Anything that would diminish the level of respect, safety and inclusion so fundamental to our workplace is completely antithetical to our principles,” Soloway said in a statement today. “We are cooperating with the investigation into this matter.” As well as working for Tambor on Transparent, Barnes has also appeared on E! now-shuttered I Am Cait with Caitlyn Jenner and the Amazon documentary anthology miniseries This Is Me back in 2015. This investigation into Tambor comes as the Jeff Bezos-founded studio has seen a near exodus of executives in recent weeks, led by studio head Price, who was suspended and subsequently resigned after details became public about how he had lewdly propositioned The Man in the High Castle executive producer Isa Dick Hackett during San Diego Comic-Con in 2015. Price had been the subject of an investigation by Amazon after Hackett relayed the Comic-Con incidents to company executives. The studio later said that “we looked closely at this specific concern and addressed it directly with those involved.” Price stayed in his job for over two years until the spotlight on disgraced producer Harvey Weinstein’s alleged decades long behavior of sexual harassment and sexual assault also turned toward Price and his conduct. Tambor is repped by the Gersh Agency and the Burstein Company.

Former actress Aurélie Wynn has accused Gossip Girl star Ed Westwick of raping her, days after he denied allegations of sexual assaulting actress Kristina Cohen. “I have never forced myself in any manner, on any woman. I certainly have never committed rape,” Westwick, 30, wrote on Twitter and Instagram on Tuesday. A rep for Westwick did not immediately respond to a request for comment from PEOPLE. Wynn, whose stage name was Aurelie Marie Cao, claimed Westwick sexually assaulted her in July 2014. Facebook; FeatureflashSHM/REX/Shutterstock “I said no and he pushed me face down and was powerless under his weight,” she wrote in a post shared on Facebook Wednesday. “I was wearing a one piece bathing suit that he ripped, I was in complete shock,” Wynn wrote of the alleged incident at Westwick’s rental home. Wynn also said she told her then-boyfriend Glee star Mark Salling – who recently plead guilty to child pornography possession – about the alleged incident. However, he blamed her and broke up with her, the actress said in her post. A rep for Salling did not immediately respond to a request for comment from PEOPLE. In addition, Wynn says that when she told her friends, they advised her to not tell anyone as she would be labeled “that girl” or seen as someone “trying to get my 10 seconds of fame.” However, years later, Wynn felt inspired to come forward because of Cohen. “I am so incredibly thankful all of this is finally coming to light and that there is justice in the world. I believe you Kristina Cohen and thank you for speaking up so eloquently and really encompassing what young female actresses have to go through at the hands of men like Ed Westwick. Without you I would not have had the strength to speak up publicly about my ordeal,” she concluded her post. Cohen — who has played minor roles in several movies and TV series — alleged she was raped in 2014 while she was visiting Westwick with her then-boyfriend, who is not named but referred to as a producer who was friends with the actor.

**Terry Crews Files Lawsuit Against Agent Adam Venit Over Alleged Assault**

Actor Terry Crews is pursuing legal action against Hollywood agent Adam Venit, alleging a sexual assault. Crews' engagement with the LAPD's Hollywood Division underscores his determination to achieve justice and hold Venit responsible. The actor has publicly declared his intention to file both civil lawsuits and pursue criminal charges against the agent.

Adam Venit, a senior executive and head of the motion picture group at the William Morris Endeavor (WME) agency, is named in the police report regarding these accusations. In light of the allegations, WME has placed Venit on administrative leave while an internal investigation is conducted.

The alleged incident is reported to have occurred last year at a Hollywood industry event. Reports suggest Venit may have been involved in a physical prank at the gathering, which allegedly included giving "wedgies" to attendees. Efforts to obtain a statement from Venit's representatives have not yet yielded a response.

Terry Crews has officially filed a report with the Los Angeles Police Department concerning a sexual assault allegation he made against a prominent Hollywood executive. The 49-year-old actor met with officers at the LAPD Hollywood Station approximately one month after initially disclosing the incident. Crews confirmed to TMZ that he submitted the report, stating his motivation was that "People have to be held accountable." He also indicated his intention to pursue legal action against the individual, vowing to "go all the way."

In early October, the "Brooklyn Nine-Nine" star detailed the alleged groping incident on Twitter, identifying the perpetrator as a "high level Hollywood executive." Crews cited the widespread Harvey Weinstein scandal as the catalyst for his decision to come forward, tweeting, "This whole thing with Harvey Weinstein is giving me PTSD. Why? Because this kind of thing happened to ME." He further elaborated that the alleged assault occurred at a "Hollywood function" in 2016, where the executive approached him and "groped my privates."

Accompanying the revelation was a video clip from Katie Barnes' reporting on October 10, 2017, which highlighted Crews' experience with sexual assault and harassment in the entertainment industry. Crews recounted his immediate reaction, stating, "Jumping back I said What are you doing?! My wife saw everything n we looked at him like he was crazy. He just grinned like a jerk." He admitted to contemplating a physical response but ultimately refrained, considering the potential repercussions. "‘240 lbs. Black Man stomps out Hollywood Honcho’ would be the headline the next day," Crews explained, adding that he feared being incarcerated. Consequently, he and his wife departed the event.

Following the incident, Crews engaged in discussions with numerous acquaintances within the industry who were associated with the executive. He revealed that the alleged perpetrator subsequently contacted him to apologize, though Crews noted that the apology lacked a genuine explanation for the behavior. "I decided not 2 take it further becuz I didn’t want 2b ostracized— par 4 the course when the predator has power n influence. I let it go. And I understand why many women who this happens to let it go," Crews stated. He has chosen not to identify the executive publicly and expressed empathy for those who have remained silent about similar experiences.

Charlie Sheen has vehemently denied allegations that he sexually assaulted Corey Haim when Haim was a minor. The report, which surfaced on Wednesday, claims the incident occurred when Haim was 13 years old and Sheen was 19. A representative for Sheen issued a strong statement to The Hollywood Reporter, asserting, "Charlie Sheen categorically denies these allegations."

This denial comes in the wake of a report by the National Enquirer, which cited Dominick Brascia, a longtime friend of the late actor Corey Haim. Brascia reportedly told the publication that Sheen sexually assaulted Haim during the production of the 1986 film 'Lucas.' It is worth noting that Sheen and Haim both appeared in this film, marking a professional connection between the two actors.

Corey Haim's name has recently been prominent in media discussions due to his mother's public statements. She has reportedly pushed back against claims made by Corey Feldman, who has spoken extensively about the late actor being a victim of abuse within a purported Hollywood pedophile ring. Feldman has also stated that he himself was a victim of this alleged ring. The two actors, often referred to as "the two Coreys," shared a close friendship and collaborated on several films throughout the 1980s. In related developments, the Los Angeles Police Department confirmed to THR on Wednesday that an investigation has been initiated into Corey Feldman's accusations. Ashley Cullins, a staff writer, contributed to this report.

Following a wave of sexual assault allegations against former *Entourage* star Jeremy Piven, advertising executive Tiffany Bacon Scourby has come forward with her own account, detailing an alleged assault by the actor. Scourby shared her story with PEOPLE, explaining that she felt compelled to speak out after reality TV personality Ariane Bellamar publicly accused Piven of sexual assault on October 30th. Bellamar's allegations, which Piven has "unequivocally denied," described alleged groping incidents.

Scourby stated she "100 percent believe[s] she’s being honest" after reading Bellamar's tweets. Bellamar's accusations also prompted *Longmire* actress Cassidy Freeman to share her own experience on Instagram, writing, "I know what you did and attempted to do to me when I was far too young. THAT I know. And you know it too. Unless there were so many of us, that you can’t remember." Freeman concluded her post with a plea for Piven to "keep it in your pants and you never get to do it again." In response to these developments, Piven's pre-taped appearance on *The Late Show with Stephen Colbert*, originally scheduled for November 3rd, was canceled. A statement from CBS to Entertainment Weekly explained that the interview, taped on October 30th, could not adequately address the recent allegations.

Scourby, who resides in Washington, D.C., recounted her interactions with Piven over several phone conversations. She claims to have first met him in October 2003 during a work event in New York City. Scourby, now 39, described Piven, 52, as "very nice" when they met at a club. He mentioned an upcoming appearance on *Late Night with Conan O’Brien* and invited her to attend as his guest, providing his phone number.

Scourby stated that nothing untoward occurred that evening. The following morning, after her work meeting, she met Piven around 11 a.m. at the Trump International Hotel & Tower, where he was staying. She recalled wearing business attire, specifically a "white ribbed turtle neck and brown slacks," and sitting beside him on a couch. After a brief conversation, during which Piven mentioned waiting for his publicist to join them, Scourby alleges he "jumped on top of me." She attempted to resist, but he allegedly "forced me to the ground." Scourby claims Piven exposed himself and rubbed against her while she remained clothed. She states that during the approximately 15 minutes she was in his suite, he "held down my hands" and ejaculated onto her turtleneck.

Following the alleged assault, Scourby recounted, "I pushed him off of me and ran down the short hallway to get to the door." She then "rubbed my chest against the wall to get his [semen] off of my sweater." She immediately hailed a cab and "burst into tears," crying for the entire journey back to her hotel. From the cab, she called a close friend, who has since corroborated Scourby's account to PEOPLE. Upon returning to her hotel, she showered before catching a train back to Washington, D.C., continuing to cry throughout the journey.

In the 14 years since the alleged incident, Scourby married and became a mother. She stated she "never thought about saying anything" until she witnessed Piven's strong denial of Bellamar's accusations. This prompted her to believe, "It couldn’t have just happened to me and couldn’t have just happened to her."

In a statement to PEOPLE, Piven categorically denied Scourby's account, stating, "Let me be absolutely clear, this simply did not happen. I would never force myself on a woman, ever. I cannot speak as to why a person would create a story like this." A representative for Piven added that a polygraph test would "clearly proven this allegation to be entirely fabricated, a complete lie," and that the actor was "looking at legal options."

On Thursday, Piven issued a statement via his Twitter account, asserting that all claims against him are "completely fabricated." He began, "Let me begin by saying that the accusations against me are absolutely false and completely fabricated. I would never force myself on a woman. Period. I have offered to take a polygraph to support my innocence. I keep asking myself, ‘How does one prove something didn’t happen?’" Piven continued, expressing his inability to "speculate as to the motivations of these women." He conveyed compassion for victims of such acts but expressed perplexity regarding the "misdirection of anger with false accusations against me," hoping they do not detract from legitimate stories. The actor concluded by writing, "We seem to be entering dark times — allegations are being printed as facts and lives are being put in jeopardy without a hearing, due process or evidence. I hope we can give people the benefit of a doubt before we rush to judgment." He further stated, "Continuing to tear each other down and destroy careers based on mere allegations is not productive on any level. I hope we can use this moment to begin a constructive dialogue on these issues, which are real and need to be addressed."

### Portia de Rossi Recounts Alleged Sexual Misconduct by Steven Seagal

Portia de Rossi has detailed a disturbing alleged sexual misconduct incident involving actor Steven Seagal, adding her voice to a growing number of women accusing the action star. The "Arrested Development" actress, 44, shared her experience of a troubling audition with the 65-year-old Seagal on Twitter, bringing renewed attention to his alleged behavior.

De Rossi described her final audition for a Seagal film, which she stated took place in his office. During the audition, Seagal allegedly stressed the importance of off-screen chemistry. He then reportedly sat her down and, according to de Rossi's account, proceeded to unzip his leather pants. De Rossi immediately left the office and contacted her agent, whose dismissive response was, "Well, I didn’t know if he was your type."

This account resonates with similar allegations from Julianna Margulies, who recently shared her own story. Margulies, who co-starred with Seagal in "Out for Justice," recalled an incident when she was 23. A casting director informed her that Seagal wanted to rehearse a scene in his hotel room late at night. Margulies, living in Brooklyn, expressed hesitation due to taxi costs and concerns about subways after dark. The casting director allegedly promised reimbursement and assured her a female presence would be there.

Upon arriving at the hotel around 10:40 PM, Margulies found the casting director absent and Seagal alone in the room. She recalled him deliberately displaying a firearm, an object she had never seen before. Margulies managed to leave the situation unharmed.

These testimonies emerge as Steven Seagal faces a growing number of sexual misconduct accusations, a trend amplified by the Harvey Weinstein scandal. Other accusers include Lisa Guerrero, a correspondent for "Inside Edition," and Jenny McCarthy, who recounted an alleged incident in 1998 where Seagal purportedly asked her to strip for "Under Siege 2." A spokesperson for Seagal previously denied McCarthy's claims, and representatives for the actor have not yet responded to inquiries regarding these latest allegations. The recurring nature of these accusations points to a concerning pattern of behavior.

query: What steps are Congressional Republicans taking to dismantle the Affordable Care Act?

documents.text: (Sarah Parnass/The Washington Post) Congressional Republicans on Wednesday launched their long-promised effort to dismantle the Affordable Care Act, even as they acknowledged that they may need several months to develop a replacement along conservative lines. Signifying how enormous a priority the issue is for the incoming administration, Vice President-elect Mike Pence met privately to discuss it with House and Senate Republicans. He offered no details afterward about what a new health-care law might look like but vowed to unwind the existing one through a mixture of executive actions and legislation. Meanwhile, President Obama made a rare Capitol Hill appearance, meeting behind closed doors with Democrats from both chambers. He urged members of his party not to help the GOP devise a new health-care law. The dueling high-level visits, on the same day that the Senate opened debate on a budget resolution that would begin rolling back the law, highlighted the sharp political fault lines that surround the future of the government’s health policies. The president, who was accompanied by Senate Minority Leader Charles E. Schumer (D-N.Y.), House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi (D-Calif.) and Rep. Frederica S. Wilson (D-Fla.) as he entered the Capitol, took no questions from reporters before or after the nearly two-hour meeting. But participants said he told members of his party that they did not have to “rescue” Republicans and that they should “stay strong” as the GOP strives to replace the law. (Reuters) Pence told reporters that he and Trump would pursue a “two-track approach” to chip away at the ACA through executive powers and legislation. Trump is “working on a series of executive orders that will enable that orderly transition to take place,” Pence said, and is eyeing other policies that can be reversed. [With Obamacare, GOP faces the ‘Pottery Barn rule’: You break it, you own it ] While Pence did not identify other policy targets, Republicans from Western states urged him Wednesday to undo some of the public land protections Obama has created through the 1906 Antiquities Act. Other executive actions, including those providing new safeguards for LGBT Americans and curbing greenhouse gas emissions linked to climate change, also could come under fire. According to a lobbyist in touch with congressional aides on the ACA issue, the Trump transition team has been considering ways to strip down the health benefits that insurers must provide in plans that they sell to individuals and small businesses. This list of “essential health benefits” was envisioned by the law but was defined in a regulation written by the Department of Health and Human Services. As a result, the incoming administration could alter it without help from Congress. [Why Obamacare is unlikely to die a swift death] Trump took to Twitter on Wednesday morning to urge Republicans to “be careful in that the Dems own the failed Obamacare disaster.” In a dig at Schumer and his allies, Trump added: “Don’t let the Schumer clowns out of this web.” Less than half an hour after Trump’s social media messages, Schumer tweeted: “Republicans should stop clowning around with America’s health care. Don’t #MakeAmericaSickAgain.” Schumer and other Democrats echoed Obama, saying they did not feel any responsibility to craft a substitute health-care bill. “If you are repealing, show us what you’ll replace it with. Then we’ll look at what you have and see what you can do,” said Schumer, who met briefly with Pence on Wednesday. (Reuters) White House press secretary Josh Earnest said that Obama told Democrats that they are well positioned to defend the law, which has extended insurance to more than 20 million Americans. However, Obama also told lawmakers that he recognized that he had not succeeded in selling the law to the public. “There was an acknowledgment that so many features were so popular, but there was a failure to communicate that,” Sen. Jeff Merkley (D-Ore.) said. [Donald Trump, meet your toughest hurdle: The U.S. Senate] Pence, who was greeted by applause as he entered the House GOP meeting with Reince Priebus, Kelly Anne Conway and several other future White House aides, made it clear that Trump, starting on the first day of his presidency, intended to embark on an aggressive campaign to reverse executive actions made by Obama. “They didn’t enumerate a list, just that which came by the pen can die by the pen,” said Rep. Mark Sanford (R-S.C.). The first bill Republicans introduced in the new Senate that convened Tuesday was budget legislation with instructions for House and Senate committees to begin repealing the Affordable Care Act. The bare-bones spending outline requires members of four committees — Ways and Means and Energy and Commerce in the House; and Finance, as well as Health, Education, Labor and Pensions, in the Senate — to produce bills just seven days after Trump’s inauguration that each would save $1 billion over a decade by slashing elements of the heath-care law. [Democrats: Trump will make America sick again] House Republicans tasked with writing the repeal legislation said that no final decisions have been made on what it will include. Rep. Patrick J. Tiberi (R-Ohio) who chairs a House subcommittee that oversees health care, called the incoming administration’s Jan. 27 deadline “a challenging goal.” Among the unanswered questions is whether Republicans will immediately end health-care taxes, such as an additional 0.9 percent Medicare payroll tax and a 3.8 percent investment income tax, that were created to help pay for the Affordable Care Act. Some Republicans have insisted that the taxes should be eliminated immediately, even if lawmakers decide to delay the repeal of other parts of the law. Others worry that repealing the taxes will make it impossible to pay to keep Obamacare afloat while a replacement is finished. “We’re not sure yet,” Tiberi said. “There’s just not consensus on this.” Rep. Chris Collins (R-N.Y.), a member the Trump transition team, said in an interview that “we have six months to work out the replacement plan” for the sweeping health-care law. Senate rules allow budget resolutions to pass by a simple majority — a maneuver that guarantees that the chamber’s Democratic minority will not have enough votes for a filibuster to block repeal. Only changes related to taxes, spending or the long-term federal budget are eligible for the simple-majority treatment, however. Other parts of the law, such as the structure of the insurance marketplaces, probably would require a filibuster-proof margin of 60 votes in the Senate, a trickier task since the new Senate has 52 Republicans. (The Washington Post) Since its passage by Congress in the spring of 2010 — entirely with Democratic votes — the ACA has spurred the most significant changes to U.S. health policy since the creation of Medicare and Medicaid during the Great Society legislation of the 1960s. It also has faced sustained opposition from Republicans, leading to two Supreme Court cases and a lawsuit over cost-sharing subsidies that is now before the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit. The ACA is best known for having expanded insurance coverage, starting in 2014, in two ways: new marketplaces selling private health plans to Americans who do not have access to affordable health benefits through a job, and an expansion of Medicaid in about three-fifths of the states. The marketplaces got off to a shaky start because of computer dysfunction with HealthCare.gov, the federal enrollment website. [Trump just met with super-influential health-care executives you’ve probably never heard of] Three years later, the U.S. Census reported that the nation’s uninsured rate had declined to 9.1 percent, with most of a recent decrease coming from people who bought insurance on their own and more who had joined Medicaid. The president’s visit to the Hill was part of a broader, last-ditch effort by his administration to preserve a central element of his domestic legacy. On Wednesday morning, New York Gov. Andrew M. Cuomo (D) drew attention to the stakes for his state by issuing a county-by-county breakdown of the estimated 2.7 million New Yorkers who would lose coverage if the law were repealed. He pointed out that New York would lose $595 million this year in federal money that has helped the state expand its Medicaid program. HHS officials have been trying to use to their advantage the fact that the change in administrations will take place while the fourth year’s enrollment period for ACA health plans is underway. On Wednesday, HHS released HealthCare.gov enrollment figures showing that 8.8 million Americans chose or were automatically re-enrolled in ACA health plans between Nov. 1 and Dec. 31 in the 39 states that rely on the federal exchange website. That is about 200,000 more than at the same time a year ago, although the proportion of new customers was lower. The new drive to unwind the health-care law will take time. Senate leaders must allow Democrats to offer a nearly unlimited number of amendments before a final budget vote. Democrats plan to use the process, known as a “vote-a-rama,” to offer a long string of potentially toxic amendments that could make it difficult for Republicans to vote for the final legislation, Democratic leadership aides said. Mike DeBonis, Ed O’Keefe, Sean Sullivan and David Weigel contributed to this report. Read more at PowerPost

"Make America Sick Again." That's the new slogan being touted by Democrats on Capitol Hill this week as Senate minority leader Chuck Schumer and his progressive colleagues fight to save Obamacare from what they fear would be a national health care catastrophe in its absence. Republicans despise the law so much they're already wrangling to put a repeal bill on the fast track as a first order of business in the 115th Congress. Devising a replacement policy that won't leave millions of Americans uninsured will be daunting, leading some to question whether it will be a case of RINO — or repeal in name only. Here's what to know about the controversial law. What is Obamacare? The Affordable Care Act, also known colloquially but not pejoratively as Obamacare, is a federally run insurance exchange. Marketplaces were set up so Americans lacking health insurance could shop for government-regulated health care plans that would cover standard and essential health benefits. Uninsured citizens would also be able to check if they're eligible for a government subsidy. An elderly patient receives treatment at an Emergency Room in Illinois. (Jim Young/Reuters) Americans not only can buy insurance through this enrolment process, they must buy it or face a fine until they do, unless an exemption can be obtained. (The annual penalty in 2016 for uninsured Americans was $695 per adult.) The ACA was passed by Congress and signed by Obama in 2010 as a way to expand health insurance coverage to more Americans. That included broadening Medicaid eligibility and boosting funding for the program, which covers low-income Americans. Americans already happy with their Obamacare-compliant private health insurance could stick with their current plans, renew them or ditch them for the marketplace. How many people are on it? As of February 2016, about 20 million people gained insurance either through signing up in the marketplace, benefiting from the Medicaid expansion, being allowed to stay on their parents' plan or through other provisions. The Kaiser Family Foundation estimated at the end of 2015 that 28.5 million non-elderly Americans remain uninsured. Why do Republicans oppose it? Costs are part of it. Conservatives opposed to tax hikes object to the idea of Medicaid expansion and of exchanges subsidizing insurance coverage for lower-income people at the expense of higher-income taxpayers. A Tea Party member at a conservative rally in Michigan carries a sign voicing his concern over Obamacare. (Rebecca Cook/Reuters) Some young adults complained they felt forced to buy health care even if they couldn't afford it. Republicans embracing free-market principles also argue that the kind of uniformity and regulation baked into the health exchanges would suppress medical innovation. Conservatives have also blamed Obamacare for killing job growth by imposing costs on larger businesses. The complexity of introducing major health insurance reform also made Obamacare an easy target, with the bill itself totalling 2,700 pages. Some users found the system confusing, and the October 2013 rollout of the Healthcare.gov state-run websites were beset with glitches. In late October, days before the 2016 presidential election, Obamacare was hit with another PR snafu when the White House announced that premiums were set to rise an average of 25 per cent across the 39 states served by the federally run online market. Can the Republicans repeal Obamacare? Georgia congressman Tom Price, 62, has a plan to replace the Affordable Care Act, also known as Obamacare. (J. Scott Applewhite/Associated Press) Yes. Especially with Republicans now in control of the levers of power in Congress and the White House. A bid to have Obamacare declared unconstitutional failed in 2015, when the Supreme Court backed it in a 6-3 decision. Republicans have also tried over 60 times since 2010 to repeal the law, to no avail. Last year, an effort to dismantle major parts of the law went all the way to Obama's office, though he unsurprisingly vetoed it. This time is different, now that Republican president-elect Donald Trump is at the desk. How to replace the ACA could be complicated, however. The president-elect, who campaigned vigorously on repealing and replacing Obamacare, somewhat softened his tone after a meeting with Obama shortly after winning the 2016 election. Trump said he favoured preserving two key pieces of the law, including a provision to allow adults up to 26 years old to remain on their parents' insurance policies, and a ban on allowing insurers to deny coverage for pre-existing conditions. "I like those very much," Trump said of the two pillars. What are the Republicans offering instead? So far, there is no firm alternative, but Trump has nominated someone who has brought a concrete and detailed vision in the past. Georgia congressman Tom Price, tapped to become the next Secretary of Health and Human Services, has proposed an Empowering Patients First Act that would have the federal government offer those without insurance a tax credit toward the cost of private insurance. During his campaign, president-elect Donald Trump promised to protect Social Security, Medicare and Medicaid. (Mary Altaffer/Associated Press) The credits would be granted based on age rather than income, which Democrats say could harm lower-income Americans. Price's program would also eliminate the expansion of Medicaid, which under Obamacare helped around 14 million poor Americans gain coverage. When is this expected to happen? The time line can't be so sudden as to leave millions of Americans — an estimated 22 million, according to the nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office — suddenly uninsured and on their own if they wind up getting sick. Doing so would be politically ruinous. Any repeal-and-replace transition would have to be gradual, over the course of two to four years. Or Republicans may take a repeal-and-delay approach until they decide they have a more satisfying health care law option.

Donald Trump's approach to the Affordable Care Act (ACA), often called Obamacare, shows how campaign promises can differ from governing realities. During his 2016 presidential run, Trump vowed to "repeal and replace Obamacare." However, as his inauguration neared, he adopted a more measured tone, advising Republican leaders to proceed cautiously. This shift is consistent with his history of changing policy views; he reportedly took 15 new stances on nine issues after the election, following 141 distinct positions on 23 major topics during his campaign. His evolving thoughts on the ACA, noted eight times since he announced his candidacy, highlight the challenges of turning campaign pledges into law.

Early in his campaign, Trump acknowledged the effectiveness of single-payer healthcare systems, referencing Canada and Scotland. He suggested a private U.S. system but stressed removing state-based barriers to encourage competition and ensure care for those who couldn't afford it otherwise. He also committed to universal coverage, stating, "I am going to take care of everybody."

A notable shift occurred in February 2016 when Trump expressed support for the ACA's individual mandate, explaining, "I don't want people dying on the streets." This suggested an understanding of its role in protecting individuals with pre-existing conditions. However, during a debate, his replacement strategy was vague, offering only general ideas about "many different plans" and "competition."

Later, his published healthcare plan leaned towards traditional Republican ideas. The Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget (CRFB) estimated this plan could lead to millions losing insurance and cost billions. It proposed unspecified block grants to states for Medicaid, leaving questions about coverage for vulnerable populations.

By November 2016, Trump indicated openness to amending the ACA rather than a full repeal, specifically mentioning keeping protections for pre-existing conditions and allowing young adults on parents' plans. He stated, "Either Obamacare will be amended, or repealed and replaced."

Leading up to his inauguration, Vice President-elect Mike Pence signaled that ACA repeal would be an early priority. Yet, the details of a replacement plan remained unclear. Trump himself then advised Republican lawmakers to "be careful" when dismantling the ACA, suggesting it might collapse on its own and urging them to avoid blame.

On January 3, 2017, a mock swearing-in ceremony took place on Capitol Hill in Washington. House Speaker Paul Ryan of Wisconsin administered the oath of office to Rep. Marsha Blackburn, a Republican from Tennessee. This event, captured by AP Photo/Zach Gibson, occurred shortly before a notable Twitter interaction involving Representative Blackburn.

Rep. Marsha Blackburn, a vocal opponent of the Affordable Care Act (ACA), also known as Obamacare, initiated a Twitter poll. The poll asked her followers for their views on repealing the health-care law. She specifically requested that supporters of repeal retweet her poll. The question posed was: "Do you support the repeal of Obamacare? RT if you do, and share what you want to see as the replacement." Blackburn also used the tweet to solicit ideas for a replacement for the ACA.

However, this poll experienced a significant backlash. Far more people responded with "No" to repealing Obamacare than with "Yes." This outcome was a considerable setback for Blackburn's efforts to garner support for repeal. The Hill.com reported that the poll's reception may have been further complicated by actions from the White House.

White House Deputy Press Secretary Eric Schultz retweeted Blackburn's poll to his substantial following of over 23,000 Twitter users. This action occurred on the same day the poll was posted. President Barack Obama, Schultz's superior, considers the ACA his most significant legislative accomplishment. President Obama was actively engaged in efforts to protect the ACA. On Wednesday, he visited Capitol Hill. His objective was to rally Democratic support for defending the law against Republican attempts to repeal and replace it.

Meanwhile, President-elect Donald Trump, who had consistently advocated for the ACA's repeal, also weighed in on the issue. On Wednesday, Trump sent out several tweets. He cautioned his fellow Republicans to proceed with caution regarding the ACA repeal and replace efforts. Trump's tweets suggested that Democrats were responsible for the perceived problems with Obamacare. He implied that if Republican efforts to repeal and replace the ACA resulted in increased rates of uninsured individuals or higher insurance premiums, Republicans could face negative political consequences.

Here's a revised version of the document, aiming to meet your quality targets:

**Fact Check: Deconstructing Claims About the Affordable Care Act (ACA)**

As the Affordable Care Act (ACA), commonly known as Obamacare, remains a focal point of political discourse, contrasting perspectives from lawmakers highlight its complex impact on American healthcare. Proponents, primarily Democrats, champion its preservation, emphasizing its role in expanding coverage and affordability. Conversely, Republicans advocate for its repeal, arguing it has led to detrimental consequences. This fact check delves into key assertions made by both sides regarding the ACA's effects, drawing on data and expert analyses.

**Examining ACA Premium Increases**

A frequently cited claim is that Obamacare has precipitated "massive premium increases." While it's true that some individuals purchasing insurance through ACA marketplaces have experienced substantial price hikes, the overall picture is nuanced. According to the Kaiser Family Foundation (KFF), the average benchmark plan on these marketplaces saw a nationwide increase of approximately 25%. However, this national average masks significant state-level variations. For instance, Arizona witnessed an average premium rise of 116%, a stark contrast to Indiana and Massachusetts, where premiums actually decreased. Crucially, a substantial portion of marketplace enrollees benefit from government subsidies, which significantly mitigate their out-of-pocket expenses. Research from the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) suggests that, even without these subsidies, marketplace plans were, on average, about 10% less expensive than typical employer-sponsored plans. The perception of higher costs can also be influenced by the way employer plans are subsidized by businesses, making them appear less costly to employees than they might otherwise be.

**Assessing Choice of Insurance Options Under the ACA**

Another common assertion is that under Obamacare, individuals are "stuck with one option" for health insurance. While there has been a documented decrease in competition within ACA marketplaces, this does not represent the reality for the majority of consumers. The number of insurance companies participating in ACA marketplaces has declined, leading to reduced choices for some. Data from the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) indicates that this year, one in five marketplace customers had access to only a single insurer, a notable increase from just 2% in 2016. Nevertheless, nearly 60% of customers still have a choice of three or more insurers. This decline in competition, which can contribute to price increases, is more prevalent in rural areas and certain Southern states. Insurers have cited various challenges, including lower-than-anticipated enrollment of younger, healthier individuals, as contributing factors. Proposed solutions to bolster competition have included more generous subsidies or stronger enrollment penalties. Former President Obama also explored the concept of a public insurance option to supplement existing private plans.

**Impact on the Healthcare System and Coverage Loss**

Claims that "the health care system has been ruined, dismantled under Obamacare" are not substantiated by available data. Healthcare costs were already on an upward trajectory prior to the ACA's implementation. The majority of Americans under 65 continue to obtain health insurance through their employers, although this percentage has seen a gradual decline. While the costs of employer-sponsored coverage have risen since the ACA's passage, the annual rate of increase was considerably higher in the decade preceding the law. Some of the savings from slower premium growth have been offset by increases in deductibles, as noted by the American Medical Association (AMA).

Democrats contend that repealing the ACA would have far more detrimental consequences. Senate Democratic leader Chuck Schumer has stated, "Instead of working to further ensure affordable care for all Americans, [Republicans] seek to rip health care away from millions of Americans, creating chaos in our entire economy." He has also voiced concerns that repealing Obamacare would severely impact the financial stability of rural hospitals.

The assertion that ACA repeal would "rip health care away from millions" is accurate if protections or replacements are not effectively implemented. The ACA has demonstrably extended health coverage to approximately 20 million Americans through subsidized individual policies, the expansion of Medicaid, and by allowing young adults to remain on their parents' insurance plans until age 26. The uninsured rate has consequently fallen to a historic low of around 10%, according to the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS). This figure would be even lower if all 19 states that have not expanded Medicaid had done so. Repealing the ACA without a viable substitute could jeopardize coverage for a significant portion of the population. Millions more purchasing insurance on marketplaces could lose coverage if the market becomes unstable. Estimates from organizations like the Urban Institute suggest that up to 30 million people could lose health coverage, effectively doubling the uninsured rate. Republicans have pledged an "orderly transition" with a replacement plan, and the effective date of repeal could be delayed. However, insurers may exhibit hesitancy to participate if the individual market is perceived to be in a state of phase-out, as reported by industry analysts.

**Addressing Concerns for Rural Hospitals**

The concern that rural hospitals could face significant challenges if the ACA is repealed outright is a valid one, and it is intrinsically linked to the ACA's original legislative design. The potential impact on these facilities stems from a deliberate trade-off made during the ACA's enactment. The federal government reduced payments to hospitals for treating Medicare patients and the uninsured, operating under the assumption that increased insurance coverage would offset these reductions. Hospitals express concern that if repeal leads to a decrease in insurance coverage without a restoration of these payment structures, they could experience a substantial rise in uncompensated care. Both the American Hospital Association (AHA) and the Federation of American Hospitals have formally urged lawmakers to either preserve existing insurance coverage levels or reinstate the prior hospital payment structures, as documented in their public statements.

WASHINGTON — Republicans hope to repeal major parts of the Affordable Care Act using an expedited procedure known as budget reconciliation. The process is sometimes called arcane, but it has been used often in the past 35 years to write some of the nation’s most important laws. “Reconciliation is probably the most potent budget enforcement tool available to Congress for a large portion of the budget,” the Congressional Research Service, a nonpartisan arm of Congress, has said. Here is a primer. Q. What is the budget reconciliation process? A. It is a way for Congress to speed action on legislation that changes taxes or spending, especially spending for entitlement programs like Medicare and Medicaid. Although conceived primarily as a way to reduce federal budget deficits, it has also been used to cut taxes and to create programs that increase spending — changes that can raise deficits. In the Senate, a reconciliation bill can ordinarily be passed with a simple majority. For other bills, a 60-vote majority is often needed to limit debate and move to a final vote.

## Obamacare Marketplace Sees Strong Enrollment for 2017 Coverage

As of last Saturday, nearly 8.8 million individuals have enrolled in health insurance plans offered through the federal Obamacare marketplace for the 2017 coverage year. This figure represents an increase compared to the approximately 8.6 million plan selections recorded on HealthCare.gov during the same period for the previous open-enrollment season.

### HealthCare.gov Reach and State-Run Marketplaces

HealthCare.gov facilitates enrollment for consumers in 39 states. The remaining states operate their own state-run marketplaces to serve their residents.

### Secretary Burwell Highlights Demand for Coverage

U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Secretary Sylvia Burwell emphasized the significance of these numbers, stating, "With 8.8 million Americans signed up for coverage through HealthCare.gov, more than last year at this time, it is clear that Americans want and need this vital coverage."

### Deadline for Uninsured Individuals

Secretary Burwell also reminded those who remain uninsured that the deadline to sign up for coverage starting February 1st is January 15th.

### Breakdown of Enrollment

Of the total plan selections, approximately 6.55 million were made by returning customers. The remaining 2.2 million enrollees were new to the HealthCare.gov marketplace.

query: What is the significance of Florida in the context of the U.S. presidential election?

documents.text: Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption Donald Trump follows a time-honoured electoral tradition in Tampa Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump have been campaigning in Florida, a key battleground state that could tip Tuesday's US presidential election. Florida is the largest swing state and is seen as a must-win for Mr Trump. The Republican candidate later flew to North Carolina and will then head west to Nevada. Opinion polls in recent days have suggested Mr Trump is gaining support but he still remains behind Mrs Clinton in most surveys. In Florida, the contest appears to be tight. Real Clear Politics' poll average puts the Democratic candidate ahead, but poll analysis website FiveThirtyEight says Mr Trump has a 52.6% chance of winning the state. US President Barack Obama won Florida in 2012 by a margin of just 0.9% over Republican Mitt Romney. Candidates need 270 electoral college votes to win the presidency. Florida is worth 29. Some 37 million early voters have already cast their ballots. Reports suggest many more Latino voters are turning out early in key states including Florida, Arizona and Nevada compared to past elections. Media playback is unsupported on your device Media caption Beyonce and Jay Z turn out for Hillary Clinton Florida has a significant Latino population, including many Cubans. Mr Trump told a rally in Tampa: "I say to the Hispanic community living in the inner city, to the African-American community, I say: what the hell do you have to lose?" He said Cubans supported him. He said he would provide jobs and solve crime for inner-city communities. Mrs Clinton courted voters in a Haitian-American district of Miami before holding a rally in Pembroke Pines. Her short address there ended in a downpour of rain. She will later make an appearance in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, alongside pop star Katy Perry. Florida's Latino vote Image copyright Reuters Image caption Some Latinos support Trump, despite his harsh rhetoric targeting immigrants Hispanics make up 16% of registered voters in Florida this election, the Associated Press reports, up from 13.5% in 2012. American Hispanics mostly vote Democrat but Florida's large Cuban population - which make up 31% of the state's Hispanic vote - have traditionally voted Republican. Donald Trump recently began to speak out against the US lifting its economic embargo on Cuba in a play for older voters, analysts say. But they also point out that as more younger Cubans have settled in Miami, support for warming US-Cuba relations - which Mrs Clinton backs - is growing among the community. In Cleveland, Mrs Clinton ended the day's campaigning at a concert, where she was joined by the singer Beyonce and her husband, rapper Jay Z. The Clinton campaign is putting on several events with high-profile figures from the entertainment world as it tries to energise young and minority voters. No guitar, no piano, no nothing," he said. **Pro-Trump Websites Operated from a Macedonian Town: A Digital Gold Rush Fueled by Misinformation**

In a surprising revelation, BuzzFeed News uncovered over 100 pro-Trump websites being managed from a single town in the former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia. These sites, often featuring sensationalist headlines and fabricated stories, have become a significant source of misinformation, particularly for American conservatives and Trump supporters.

One such site, WorldPoliticus.com, published a baseless story, citing unnamed FBI sources, claiming Hillary Clinton would be indicted in 2017 for her email scandal. This fabricated news generated over 140,000 shares, reactions, and comments on Facebook, demonstrating the power of sensationalism to drive engagement.

Meanwhile, thousands of miles away in Veles, a town of 45,000 in Macedonia, a digital gold rush was underway. Locals launched at least 140 US politics websites with American-sounding domain names like TrumpVision365.com and USADailyPolitics.com. These sites primarily published aggressively pro-Trump content.

The young Macedonians behind these websites admitted their motivation was purely financial. They recognized that a US Facebook user is worth significantly more to advertisers than a user outside the US. The fraction-of-a-penny-per-click revenue from US display advertising, a declining market for American publishers, proved highly lucrative in Veles.

These individuals learned that the most effective way to generate traffic on Facebook was to publish sensationalist and often false content that appealed to Trump supporters. As a result, this Macedonian hub of pro-Trump sites has played a substantial role in propagating the kind of false and misleading content identified in recent analyses of hyperpartisan Facebook pages.

These operations highlight the economic incentives behind producing misinformation specifically for lucrative advertising markets, particularly on platforms like Facebook and through online advertising networks such as Google AdSense. A university student from Veles, who wished to remain anonymous, explained the rationale: "**Yes, the info in the blogs is bad, false, and misleading but the rationale is that 'if it gets the people to click on it and engage, then use it.'**"

BuzzFeed News identified over 100 active US politics websites run from Veles, with some boasting Facebook pages with hundreds of thousands of followers. Another 40 domains registered in Veles are no longer active.

The Macedonians interviewed stated their primary reason for launching these sites was financial. "**I started the site for an easy way to make money,**" shared a 17-year-old who runs a site with four others. "**In Macedonia the economy is very weak and teenagers are not allowed to work, so we need to find creative ways to make some money.**" He added that the revenue from a small site is enough to afford many things, unlike in his home country.

Most of the content on these sites is aggregated or plagiarized from fringe and right-wing US websites. The Macedonians would find a story, create a sensationalized headline, post it, and then share it on Facebook to drive traffic and earn ad revenue. While some experimented with left-leaning content, Trump-related stories consistently performed better on Facebook. "**People in America prefer to read news about Trump,**" stated a 16-year-old operating BVANews.com.

BuzzFeed News' research also found that the most successful posts from these Macedonian sites were overwhelmingly false or misleading. For instance, a highly successful post from ConservativeState.com, claiming Hillary Clinton praised Donald Trump in 2013, garnered an astounding 480,000 Facebook interactions. This story was sourced from TheRightists.com, a site that admits to publishing both real and fake content. While Clinton did express a desire for more successful business people in politics in a private speech, she did not mention Donald Trump, making the headline quote false.

Four out of the five most successful posts identified by BuzzFeed News from these Macedonian sites were false. These included claims that the Pope endorsed Trump and that Mike Pence called Michelle Obama the "most vulgar first lady we've ever had." These four posts alone generated over 1 million shares, reactions, and comments on Facebook, leading to significant traffic and ad revenue for the site owners, while misinforming countless readers.

The Macedonians interviewed noted that the market has become crowded, making it harder to earn money. Those who started their sites in early 2016 are seeing the most significant financial returns. One university student mentioned a friend earning "$5,000 per month, or even $3,000 per day" during viral hits. The 16-year-old running BVANews.com, along with a partner, also operates health websites and stated their politics site averages 1 million page views per month.

The 17-year-old and his partners were still awaiting Google AdSense approval for their site, earning only about 800 views daily with no revenue. The university student, who launched his site in August, stopped updating it to focus on a more successful health and well-being site, estimating "thousands" of such sites operate out of Veles. He explained, "**I stopped because I didn't really enjoy doing it and we didn't actually make any money from it since there are so many people posting already. The people who started early are the ones reaping the rewards.**"

A **Washington Post-ABC News tracking poll** reveals a **tight national race** between Donald Trump and Hillary Clinton as the campaign enters its final weekend. Clinton currently holds a **slim four-point lead**, with **47% support** compared to Trump's 43%. This margin falls within the poll's margin of sampling error. Notably, **30% of likely voters have already cast their ballots**, underscoring a significant early voting trend.

While this lead is numerically the largest in the past week, it mirrors Clinton's 47-44 advantage from the preceding four-day period. The poll also indicates a slight uptick in support for Libertarian Gary Johnson, now at 4%, while Jill Stein remains steady at 2%.

Despite her overall lead, Clinton faces **challenges in the campaign's closing days**. On the critical issue of **honesty and trustworthiness, she trails Trump by five points**. Furthermore, her advantage among early voters has diminished. Trump supporters are also **more likely to report following the contest "very closely,"** suggesting a potential for heightened turnout among his base.

Clinton's current 4-point edge is **identical to a mid-October Post-ABC poll** and matches President Obama's 4-point winning margin in 2012. She benefits from **substantial advantages with demographic groups that previously supported Obama**.

Specifically, African American likely voters back Clinton by a commanding **86% to 6% over Trump**, though this is slightly lower than Obama's 93-6 margin in the 2012 exit poll. Latinos favor Clinton by **71% to 23%**, a figure similar to recent polling and mirroring Obama's 71-27 margin in 2012.

The gender gap in support for Clinton and Trump is comparable to past elections, but the divide is **particularly pronounced among younger voters**. Among likely female voters under 40, Clinton leads Trump 59% to 31%. In contrast, among younger men, the split is 41% for Clinton and 38% for Trump. This demographic is an area where Clinton may see stronger turnout, as younger women have historically voted at higher rates than their male counterparts.

Party loyalists continue to support their respective nominees at high levels, with **88% of Democrats backing Clinton and 85% of Republicans supporting Trump**. These figures are slightly lower than in previous years, partly due to the significant support for third-party candidates. Typically, party nominees receive over 90% support from their own party members.

Independents currently favor Trump by **45% to 39%**. Both Clinton and Trump receive support from over 80% of independents who lean toward a specific party. However, among those who lean toward neither party, Trump holds a stronger lead, **43% to 34%**.

**Early Voting Trends Highlight Engagement**

Early voting is rapidly increasing, with **30% of likely voters reporting they have already voted**. This is a notable increase from the 21% who had voted by the end of the previous weekend and is **higher than at the same point in the Post-ABC Tracking Poll four years ago**, when 22% reported voting early five days before the 2012 election.

Among these early voters, Clinton holds only a narrow lead over Trump, **49% to 44%**. While this is similar to her overall lead among likely voters, it is smaller than her 54-41 advantage earlier in the week. Although the preferences of early voters cannot change, the growing number of early voters can influence overall election outcomes, though polls can only estimate these shifts.

With turnout being a critical factor in the final days, Clinton supporters who have not yet voted are slightly less likely to report following the race closely. The poll indicates that **73% of Trump supporters planning to vote in the coming days say they are following the race "very closely,"** compared to 64% of Clinton supporters.

Voters also perceive Trump as more honest and trustworthy than Clinton by **44% to 39%**. This represents a slight narrowing from Trump's eight-point lead over the past weekend.
