However, this reticence, coupled with the cast's collective avoidance of discussing the show's political and feminist dimensions, appears less like an effort to achieve universality and more like an anxious attempt at sanitization. Hulu's promotional efforts, including a package sent to female reporters containing a signed novel, a Handmaid's sweatshirt, and an invitation to a Facebook group called #Maidez, further complicate this narrative. Moss's accompanying letter, referencing the eradication of rights for "women, LGBTQ people and those of diverse faiths" by a "newly formed theocratic dictatorship," offers a remarkably pointed, albeit uncharacteristic, description of the show's relevance. She describes it as "a story as relevant now as the day it was first written," yet invites discussion within a framework of "positive discussion among solution-oriented people who believe in the power of sharing ideas and personal connections," a call to action conspicuously devoid of political specificity.

The motivation behind such a carefully curated apolitical stance remains unclear. The impulse to distance the narrative from its potent feminist implications is baffling, particularly for those who deeply appreciate the novel's message. Ultimately, the cast's apparent fear of engaging with the show's inherent political and feminist dimensions raises questions about their motivations and perceived audience. Pretending that *The Handmaid's Tale* is merely a fictional narrative, detached from the pressing realities it reflects, offers no genuine protection in our current socio-political landscape.

We are all necessarily prisoners of our own place and time, and thus, I was in my youth necessarily a fan of "The Handmaid’s Tale." I read it; I discussed it very earnestly with like-minded friends; I copied author Margaret Atwood’s muted style and dystopian preoccupations in my own, less competent fiction. But that youth has fled, alas; it has been two decades since I last waxed indignant about the drinking age, or picked up my copy of the book. Even that copy -- paperback, dogeared and waterstained and threatening to come apart at the spine -- has been left behind somewhere, presumably the same place I lost my velvet chokers and my Suzanne Vega CDs. However, a new television show has been made out of the book, and is attracting rave reviews, less for its acting, script, or stunning visuals than for its “unexpectedly timely” message. Nonsense. Whatever future we should fear Donald Trump will usher in, it will bear precious little resemblance to Atwood’s Gilead. But people keep saying the TV adaptation of "The Handmaid's Tale" is "unexpectedly timely" in this age. Perhaps I had forgotten some Trumpian intimations from the text. So I reread the book again. To try to get as close to the original experience as possible, I listened to Suzanne Vega on Spotify. Alas, my household does not contain anything that may safely substitute for a velvet choker. After completing a rereading, I am interested to see the show. But I am, if anything, even more stunned that anyone thinks it is particularly timely in the era of Trump. It is as if someone had read “Brave New World,, gone to modern Pyongyang, and shouted: “You see! This is just what Huxley was warning about!” Um. Not really…. My quarrel is not with the politics of "The Handmaid's Tale," nor with its realism. Expecting plausibility from dystopian fiction is like expecting haute cuisine from a highway service area. Of the dystopian fiction I’ve read, only "1984" comes even remotely close to feeling real, and that’s because Orwell was working from two vivid contemporaneous examples, from which he lifted freely. Fictional dystopia is sort of the photonegative of the movies produced by actual totalitarian regimes. Masses of people wearing identical creepy clothes, forming into precise lines, chanting the same things. Yes, in regimes like North Korea and Hitler’s Germany, those mass rallies occurred. The men marched and the girls danced in eerily infinite lines. But afterward, most of them went home to the banal, the ordinary, and the familiar -- altered by political fear and economic shortages, but not wholly transformed into something unrecognizably inhuman. In interviews since its publication, Atwood has emphasized that all the details in the book were based on things that really happened in the world (or at least, are recounted in tales; the “handmaids” of the book, concubines given to elite men of Gilead’s theocracy in order to bear them the children their barren wives could not, is based on the story of Rachel and Jacob in the Book of Genesis). One sees the historical referents when reading, and yet the entire effect is completely unrealistic, because she’s drawn details from too many oppressive regimes and collaged them all together. Thus a regime that is clearly supposed to be some sort of fundamentalist Protestant theocracy is enthusiastically adopting extramarital sex, infanticide and Tibetan prayer wheels. This makes for some dramatic imagery, but not for what Mary McCarthy, in her review of the book, called “the essential element of a cautionary tale”: “…recognition. Surprised recognition, even, enough to administer a shock.” Dystopian regimes in real life have common features, yes, but they are not actually interchangeable; indeed, they are surprisingly specific. Reading accounts of those actual regimes, I’m always surprised at how culturally embedded they remained, even as they proclaimed that they were enacting a new world order in which everything would be different. People still got married and settled into family domesticity under communist regimes that were supposed to be sweeping away all the vestiges of private lives in favor of creating “new Soviet man” or his many cousins; people in theocratic states still had considerable variance in the level of religious observance; theoretically internationalist ideologies fell back on nationalist sentiment to motivate the masses. All of which is to say: The Taliban certainly existed, but it could not exist in America, because it would have no popular base from which to launch its attacks, no historic practice of burqa-wearing to ratify bringing them back. Nor could such a movement gain power here along the lines that Atwood outlines. I’ve seen her praised for actually thinking through the mechanism by which her fictional state might emerge, and kudos for the effort, but we must also acknowledge that, as written, it doesn’t really make all that much sense. The inciting event is a lightly fictionalized version of the Reichstag fire, but a careful student of history would note that a decade after the Reichstag fire, most of German society still looked pretty much like it had in 1925. No, I’m not excusing Nazi atrocities in any way shape or form, nor discounting the sweeping changes that Hitler did make. But they didn’t gut-renovate the economy, wipe out all religions that competed with the state, and completely reorganize society in the space of a few years; they left much of the economy and the culture alone. For structural reasons -- she needs her handmaid to remember the world before as an adult, and yet still be young enough to be fertile -- Atwood needs changes that are both unrealistically sweeping and ludicrously fast. She is not illustrating how Trump will transform America. America hasn’t had a unified theocratic tradition since the early days of the Massachusetts Bay Colony, and the descendants of those Puritans are now pouring their fervent moralism into buying Priuses and complaining about Trump. The closest modern equivalent, the statewide hegemony of the Latter-day Saints in Utah, doesn’t look very much like The Handmaid’s Tale, and hasn’t the faintest prayer of co-opting the rest of the nation’s fractured religious traditionalists, many of whom do not even consider the Mormons to be Christian. And even if some movement did, somehow, gather a Mormon-like critical mass, Trump is hardly likely to be its avatar; our most religious red state was also the one where Trump had the greatest trouble. But these are, as I say, not necessarily flaws with the novel; dystopia’s job is to illustrate the conflict between the individual and society by heightening it into its starkest contrast, not to paint it in photorealistic fashion. However, if one can forgive a speculative novelist for taking great liberties with reality, one cannot offer the same to gushing critics who are now arguing that Trump’s election has somehow made the story more relevant. First, these critics seem to have failed to notice that Trump has been unable to usher in even the far more limited changes he promised, such as dramatically restricting immigration, reshaping NATO, and bringing foreign governments to heel. He hasn’t got control of Congress, or the courts, and has nothing like the mass movements behind him that brought other dystopian governments to power, whether fascist, communist or theocratic. Moreover, even such a mass movement is not necessarily enough. Think of the most famous examples of these sorts of regimes: Stalin’s Russia, Hitler’s Germany, Mao’s China, the Khmer Rouge, the Taliban. Those revolutions were born out of catastrophe, either colonialism or war. They moved into power vacuums as weak institutions were failing. Bad as our current political moment is, “congressional gridlock” and “declining wages for unskilled men” are not in the same class as the disasters that launched the careers of history’s worst tyrants. Meanwhile, the culture is moving the other way. Women are gaining more economic power relative to men; the nation is becoming less religious. "The Handmaid’s Tale" is becoming less plausible a future with each passing year, no matter how hard feminists insist that there is only a brief and slippery slope between overturning Roe v. Wade and forcing women into state-sanctioned breeding programs. Does it matter that this misogynistic theocracy is not on the rise? Does it matter that whatever dark future Trump may presage will be quite different from the one on the television? Yes, I think it does. There is nothing wrong with enjoying implausibilities on a screen or page. But there is something very wrong with hysterically declaring that those things are reality. That risks confusion so we will not notice the real dystopia rising -- or the rest of the world will be too tired of our cries to hear any warnings we shout. This column does not necessarily reflect the opinion of the editorial board or Bloomberg LP and its owners. To contact the author of this story: Megan McArdle at mmcardle3@bloomberg.net To contact the editor responsible for this story: Philip Gray at philipgray@bloomberg.net

Hulu's striking adaptation of Margaret Atwood's novel, *The Handmaid's Tale*, starring Elizabeth Moss, Samira Wiley, and Alexis Bledel, has inadvertently ignited a national conversation about the meaning of "feminist" in our current socio-political climate. While cast members like Wiley and Ann Dowd have readily identified the series as a feminist text, others involved have shied away from the label, preferring to frame it as a universal human story. This defensive posture, perhaps, stems from a fear that embracing the term "feminist" might alienate audiences or even doom the show's commercial prospects.

The apprehension surrounding the word "feminist" is understandable, particularly if it's perceived as synonymous with political ideologies that have recently faced public backlash. In marketing, aligning too closely with perceived "losers" can be a significant risk, especially when courting a broad viewership. While the widespread support for reproductive rights and the visibility of movements like the Women's March are significant, there's a pragmatic inclination to align with perceived winners. This can lead to a dilution of core messages, as seen when a focus on racial diversity, while important, might overshadow the original text's engagement with eugenics and white supremacy, and the specific plight of women's rights.

A further layer to this hesitation emerges from the perception that the term "feminist" can diminish the nuance of a work. Elizabeth Moss, for instance, has expressed that while *The Handmaid's Tale* is recognized as a seminal feminist novel, she views it as a broader exploration of human rights. The implication is that labeling it solely as "feminist" might reduce its scope, making it seem less universally relatable. This dichotomy between "feminist" and "human" is particularly striking given that the dystopian society depicted in the series was facilitated by a societal reluctance to defend women's rights specifically, not just general human rights.

Even Margaret Atwood herself has articulated a nuanced perspective on what constitutes a "feminist" book. She clarifies that if "feminist" implies an ideological tract where women are purely angelic or utterly victimized, then her novel does not fit. However, if it signifies a narrative where women are depicted as complex human beings, with all their varied behaviors and motivations, and where their experiences are central to the story's themes, then yes, it aligns with that definition. This distinction feels somewhat anachronistic in an era where television is increasingly showcasing complex, unlikable, and deeply human female protagonists, moving beyond simplistic archetypes.

The anxiety surrounding the "feminist" label for *The Handmaid's Tale* can be seen as a product of living within a society steeped in ambient misogyny. Narratives perceived as overtly female-centric can be unfairly dismissed as niche, mean-spirited, or frivolous. This cultural tendency devalues women's stories, much like "chick flicks" are often relegated to a lower tier due to their expected audience. For the entertainment industry, "feminist" can unfortunately become a shorthand for something preachy and lacking in entertainment value, a perception that *The Handmaid's Tale* series, with its horrifying and beautiful execution, actively defies.

Ultimately, the series risks being overwhelmed by the discourse surrounding its political implications, a common fate for shows with predominantly female casts. Accusations of virtue-signaling or self-indulgence, which rarely plague shows about men, can overshadow artistic merit. The sentiment that characters might be performing rather than acting authentically, driven by a desire to "look good," mirrors a broader cultural suspicion of female expression, especially when it carries a message.

It is perhaps unsurprising, then, that figures like Moss and Atwood, who have become unintentional feminist icons, might express caution. This phenomenon, described by some as the "All Lives Matter-ing" of a feminist text, highlights the challenging cultural landscape in which such works are produced. The pervasive societal filters and biases make it difficult to engage directly with the core of a story about women, leading to a mediation that obscures the "Thing Itself." This piece, in its focus on the political reception, inadvertently contributes to the fear that the show's politics will eclipse its artistic achievements.

The dilemma is a Catch-22: labeling the show as feminist risks erasing its other qualities, while avoiding the label can lead to the same outcome. While many critics have successfully delved into the show's substance, the pervasive "analytical angst" surrounding its reception is a hurdle that must be overcome. As Offred observes, "Given our wings, our blinkers, it's hard to look up, hard to get the full view... But we can do it, a little at a time." The power of *The Handmaid's Tale* lies in its ability to be dissected and understood incrementally, allowing for a broader appreciation of its artistic and thematic complexities beyond the immediate political storm.

## "The Handmaid's Tale": A Chilling Prophecy's Enduring Power

Margaret Atwood's 1985 novel, **"The Handmaid's Tale,"** emerged from a world already **wrestling with threats to reproductive rights and environmental degradation.** Atwood's chilling vision presented a **speculative future** where these anxieties culminated in a **theocratic society that subjugated fertile women**, forcing them into a system of state-controlled reproduction. Today, in 2017 and beyond, the novel's **prescient themes resonate with renewed urgency.** As women's **bodily autonomy faces renewed challenges** and environmental anxieties persist, "The Handmaid's Tale" has experienced a **remarkable resurgence in popularity.** This enduring appeal, mirroring the impact of George Orwell's "1984," has prompted Atwood herself to reflect on why her work continues to captivate audiences, especially with the recent launch of a **10-episode Hulu series.**

### Fiction Becomes Frighteningly Real

The timing of the Hulu series' production, beginning before the 2016 election and continuing afterward, has undeniably contributed to the novel's current prominence. Atwood notes that the cast's realization that they were no longer merely portraying fiction but a **potential reality, significantly amplified public interest.** While the iconic red robes of the handmaids may remain fictional, the **erosion of rights women once took for granted has instilled a palpable sense of unease**, making the novel's depiction of societal regression all the more frightening. This sentiment was echoed by critics like **Sarah Lyall** in *The New York Times*, who noted the series' "unsettling timeliness."

### Examining "Traditional Values": A Dangerous Illusion

Atwood challenges the notion of **"traditional values"** often invoked in contemporary discourse, particularly when juxtaposed with the themes in her book and other best-selling works. She posits that a focus on **state-mandated childbearing, without adequate societal support structures** like orphanages or financial assistance, leads to tragic outcomes. Citing the example of Nicolae Ceaușescu's Romania, where enforced procreation resulted in overcrowded orphanages, as documented by **historians like Mary Ellen Fischer**, Atwood warns against policies that would compel impoverished women to bear children they cannot support, creating an **inherently unjust and tragic situation.**

### Generational Interpretations and Urgent Warnings

The way "The Handmaid's Tale" is received has evolved across generations. While some early readers in the United States viewed it as a fantastical "jolly good yarn" or a distant possibility, others questioned how much time remained before such a dystopia could materialize. In Canada, the novel often prompted a more immediate "could it happen here?" reaction. Today, **young women who have grown up with established rights are engaging with the book with a heightened sense of alarm, recognizing the fragility of those freedoms.** This is vividly illustrated by the women who wore the handmaid's attire to protest reproductive rights legislation in Texas, a protest that visually echoed scenes from the novel and the new series, as reported by **local news outlets like the Austin Chronicle.**

### The Power of Series Adaptation

The advent of well-produced, streamed series has opened new avenues for adapting literary works, particularly longer novels that struggled to be fully realized in a 90-minute film format. The Hulu series, with its **extended runtime, allows for a more comprehensive exploration of the novel's narrative.** Crucially, it can delve into the fates of characters who faded from view in Atwood's original text, offering a more expansive and potentially more terrifying portrayal of the Gilead regime. Atwood suggests this adaptation **surpasses both the book and the earlier feature film in its ability to evoke fear**, a sentiment shared by many viewers and critics, including those at **Vanity Fair**.

### Historical Parallels and Societal Control

Atwood draws a stark parallel between the control exerted over reproduction in Gilead and **historical instances of totalitarian regimes.** She points to the brutal practices of Argentina's generals, who would wait for pregnant women to give birth before disposing of them, a period extensively documented by organizations like the **Mothers of the Plaza de Mayo**, and Hitler's appropriation of "Aryan" children, a practice detailed by **Holocaust historians like Raul Hilberg**. While the United States has historically upheld a distinction between private and public life, Atwood argues that an **exception has been made concerning women's bodies, allowing a purportedly liberal democracy to assert agency over them.** This mirrors the state's power to conscript men into the military, drawing a parallel between bodily autonomy and civic duty. The common argument that unwanted babies can simply be adopted is also challenged, as Atwood notes that **adoption systems have finite capacities**, a fact often highlighted by **child welfare organizations**.

### Canada: A Haven and a Mirror

In "The Handmaid's Tale," Canada serves as a symbol of escape and freedom, much like it did for enslaved people during the Civil War, a historical parallel often drawn by **historians of the Underground Railroad**. Atwood, as a Canadian, offers a unique perspective on the exercise of power. She acknowledges the tendency for nations to observe others more closely than they are observed in return, suggesting that while Canadians may believe they possess a distinct understanding, this self-perception might be a form of self-flattery. Nevertheless, **Canada's history as a refuge for various groups**, including Vietnamese refugees in the 1970s as documented by **Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada**, does provide a distinct vantage point, even if its accuracy is not always guaranteed.

### Censorship and Evolving Dialogue

The book's controversial status, banned in some educational settings while being required reading in others, highlights ongoing debates about age-appropriateness and the confrontation of difficult themes. Atwood reflects on the historical tendency to shield young people from explicit content, a practice often debated by **educational organizations like the American Library Association**, but asserts that **students are capable of engaging with complex issues.** She recounts instances where students have successfully advocated for their right to study the novel, demonstrating their capacity for reasoned argument, as seen in **school board meeting minutes** from various districts.

### The Trump Era and Amplified Misogyny

Atwood views the current political climate not as the origin of misogyny, but as a **powerful amplifier.** She argues that when a world leader's actions and rhetoric signal approval of such sentiments, it **emboldens others to express them more openly.** The galvanizing effect of the current administration on women's movements is undeniable, as it fuels the energy required to sustain activism, a phenomenon observed by **political scientists like Kathleen Hall Jamieson**. The potential enactment of certain policies could lead to consequences that profoundly alter societal landscapes, a concern frequently raised by **policy think tanks like the Brookings Institution**.

### Environmental Degradation and Fertility: A Looming Crisis

Beyond the immediate concerns of reproductive rights, Atwood emphasizes the **interconnectedness of environmental protection and women's well-being.** The degradation of environmental laws directly impacts families and health, and the novel's premise includes the devastating effects of environmental damage on human fertility. Atwood points to scientific studies linking declining male fertility to plastic and chemical contaminants in water, as published in journals like **Environmental Health Perspectives**, warning that **denial of these findings will inevitably lead to severe consequences**, a sentiment echoed by **environmental advocacy groups like the Natural Resources Defense Council**.

### Echoes of Past Struggles

Recalling her interviews with doctors who treated women attempting self-induced abortions before Roe v. Wade, Atwood underscores the grim realities that could resurface: **increased orphanages, deaths from unsafe procedures, and infertility resulting from botched illegal abortions.** These historical events, she argues, are not merely relics of the past but potential outcomes if legal and safe reproductive options are curtailed, a concern frequently voiced by **reproductive health organizations like Planned Parenthood**. The fundamental questions of who bears children, how many, and whose responsibility they are, remain central. Atwood notes that historical figures like Napoleon and Hitler implemented "have-more-babies" programs for strategic reasons, highlighting the **long-standing use of reproductive control for political ends**, a practice analyzed by **military historians**.

### Navigating Hate Mail and Consequences

Atwood acknowledges receiving significant hate mail over the years, anticipating an increase with the television series' release. However, she maintains that her work is not an advocacy for specific policies but a **cautionary exploration of the results that arise from particular laws.** She urges careful consideration of the consequences of banning birth control, restricting reproductive information, or defunding related services, questioning whether society is prepared to accept and pay for such outcomes, a question often posed by **public health ethicists**.

### The Abandonment of Science

In response to a question about what she might have been asked, Atwood touches upon the **abandonment of science.** She observes a societal preference for scientific advancements that yield profit, rather than those that warn of potential dangers, a trend discussed by **sociologists of science like Robert K. Merton**. This selective embrace of science, she suggests, goes hand-in-hand with the broader societal trends she explores in her work, a perspective often shared by **science communicators**.

"The Handmaid's Tale" offers a chillingly prescient look at societal decline, a theme that feels alarmingly relevant decades after Margaret Atwood's groundbreaking novel first appeared. In the third episode of Hulu's acclaimed series, protagonist Offred, powerfully portrayed by Elisabeth Moss, articulates this central message with stark clarity: "Now I'm awake to the world. I was asleep before. That's how we let it happen." This profound realization dawns as she walks in a somber procession with another handmaid, their iconic crimson robes a stark contrast against the oppressive watch of armed authorities.

### The Subtle Erosion of Freedoms

Offred's inner thoughts reveal the terrifying, incremental nature of her society's downfall. "When they slaughtered Congress, we didn't wake up. When they blamed terrorists and suspended the Constitution, we didn't wake up then either," she recalls. "They said it would be temporary. Nothing changes instantaneously. In a gradually heating bathtub, you'd be boiled to death before you knew it." This potent metaphor vividly illustrates how significant societal shifts can occur so gradually that people fail to recognize the looming danger until it's too late to fight back.

### Modern Parallels and Collective Indifference

In the context of current sociopolitical discussions, especially following major political changes, the series' timeliness is undeniable. Samira Wiley, who plays Moira, emphasized this point in a recent CNN interview: "I think it's scarily relevant right now. I think one of the things we can see -- especially through the flashbacks [in the show] -- is how it doesn't happen all at once. And that's the thing that's so scary."

### The Gradual Path to Authoritarianism

The show's flashbacks meticulously detail a society's descent into totalitarian rule. Before Gilead's rise, the former United States was a place where Offred and Moira shared everyday moments, finding comfort in simple pleasures like running and coffee. Their lives were irrevocably altered as women were systematically stripped of their basic rights, including their careers and financial autonomy. These freedoms weren't taken away suddenly but were dismantled piece by piece through legislative actions and insidious societal changes.

Wiley further elaborated on this dangerous progression: "I think right now we think that, 'Well, this can't happen.' And then it does. And then we think, 'Well, that next thing can't happen.' And it does," she noted. "People in the show -- and I think it's dangerous now -- become complacent and [aren't] vigilant." This widespread complacency, she warns, is a significant and perilous weakness.

### The Actors' Deep Connection to Their Roles

Wiley was drawn to Moira's resilient spirit, a departure from her earlier role as Poussey in "Orange is the New Black." "She's just a badass," Wiley stated. Elisabeth Moss, on the other hand, found a deep connection with Offred's enduring strength. The role became even more personally significant for Moss after the events of November 2016. "When you do roles and you do characters, you always try to personalize it and make it relevant for yourself, obviously. With something like this, it became more personal than anything I've ever done in a way that was unavoidable," she explained.

Moss, having recently finished her impactful work on "Mad Men," was the first actor to join "The Handmaid's Tale" project, having read the script in April 2016. She also serves as a producer on the series. Bruce Miller ("ER") is credited as the creator, with veteran television executive Warren Littlefield also holding an executive producer role. Moss views her producing position as crucial for creative input. "I think if you are going to commit that time, you do want to make sure you're going to have some creative say," she said. "And you know, as a woman in this business, I so admire other women who have taken that step themselves and taken some ownership of their career and gotten either behind the camera or gotten involved as a producer. Those are the women I look up to."

As an actor, Moss prefers characters who display genuine humanity over idealized heroic figures. She admired Peggy Olson from "Mad Men" for her "belief in herself." "For me as an actor, I don't look for heroes. I don't look for someone who is inspiring or some sort of iconic person, I look for someone who is human and who is real," Moss elaborated. "[Offred] is a hero in the end, but because she has to be."

Hulu released the first three episodes of "The Handmaid's Tale" on Wednesday, with new episodes following weekly.

Elisabeth Moss gives what might be the TV performance of the year in Hulu’s The Handmaid’s Tale. The actress is probably best known for her work as Peggy Olson on AMC’s long-running drama Mad Men. In her new role, she plays the character of Offred — a woman forced to live as a “handmaid” and valued only for her fertility — in the alternate reality of the series, which is based on Margaret Atwood’s classic 1985 novel. Much of the adaptation is filmed in extremely intimate close-ups on Moss’s face, leaving her no room for anything false. Every eye twitch, every facial muscle shifting — they all register. That Moss navigates this challenge with aplomb is evidence of her talent. She’s immensely thoughtful about the technical aspects of playing Offred, like how the character’s elaborate costume informs her performance — but she’s also engaged with the story’s more political elements, like how the world of the show has warped sex into something brutal and terrifying instead of enjoyable. And as a producer on the series, she was deeply involved in helping shape and build the world of the show. I met up with Moss shortly before the series’ debut to talk about the links between Peggy and Offred, the role of accidental feminists, and what it’s like to have a camera in your face all of the time. The following conversation has been lightly edited for length and clarity. Todd VanDerWerff Actors often talk about how their costumes inform their performances, and that seems like it might be particularly true here. How did putting on the Handmaid’s outfit affect your work? Elisabeth Moss So much, honestly. The dress itself took the longest to figure out, because that was a blueprint for the rest of the Handmaids. Whatever we decided I was going to do, they were going to make 100 of them. So we needed it to be almost universal in a way. There were specifications, like it couldn't be too revealing. At the same time, we didn't want it to be a sack that was unflattering that I had to put on every day. There were two things I wanted, which was it needed to be comfortable, because I knew I was going to have to wear it to death. Then I needed it to be something that I wanted to put on every day. From Ane Crabtree, our costume designer, I got both in spades. It sounds odd to say you loved something that represents something negative, but at the same time, I did love that dress. I really did. It makes you move in a different way. It makes you stand in a different way. It makes you feel different than you feel wearing jeans. Then when you add the other elements — the cloak, which is constricting in the sense that you can't really move your arms up that high. Then the bonnet, which we call the wings, is the most restrictive thing. It dulls your hearing. It's like putting both hands over your ears. It also cuts off all peripheral vision, so you have to turn your head to see. The bonnet enabled some interesting things for us to play with as Handmaids with the camera, because tip it low enough and you can't see my face. You could reveal just one eye. You could do both eyes. It made every movement of the head deliberate and specific. When all movement, all conversation, is either forbidden or has to be very specifically chosen, it made playing with that much easier. Todd VanDerWerff There are a lot of flashbacks where you’re essentially dressed as a ... normal person, so to speak. Elisabeth Moss A lot of that was my own clothes, so I'm glad you didn't say dressed like a freak. Todd VanDerWerff What did you realize about moving through the world just dressed in your own clothes, as opposed to wearing the much more restrictive Handmaid costume? Elisabeth Moss The choice, the individuality that we have now, the Handmaids don't have. Two of the things that are stripped away from the Handmaids are the right to privacy and the right to be an individual. The privacy is taken away, because as soon as anyone sees your outfit or your dress, they know which class you belong to and they know you're a fertile woman. As soon as you’re all wearing the same thing, you have no individual expression. It was interesting playing with the flashback clothes, with the flexibility that we have in the modern world with how we dress, gender-wise. You'll notice that the Handmaid's dress is much more typically "female," and it's female in accordance with this very specific little narrow path of what a woman should look like and what a woman should wear. So we played in the flashbacks with June [Offred’s given name], maybe looking a little bit more like a tomboy, being able to dress in a pretty little summer spring dress one day and wearing jeans and a T-shirt or jeans and a baseball tee another day. We have that flexibility at the moment in our country. Obviously, it doesn't exist in other countries, but we have that. We can be who we want to be. Todd VanDerWerff Many of the reviews of the show have mentioned its timeliness, but in regard to women’s rights throughout the world, this book has always been timely. When you’re actively playing that character, though, can you think about the politics of the story? Or is it more of an emotional, intuitive thing, and then the more political aspects creep in later when you’re reflecting on it or watching it? Elisabeth Moss Politics does intrude. You can't help it. I wish it wasn't so relevant, but you can't be in the world today and awake as a woman and not be aware of your rights being infringed upon. It's something that I have always taken personally. It's something I have always tried to be active and vocal about, but you can't help but take it perhaps a little bit more personally than you would other characters or other projects. You can't help but feel a sense of responsibility to other women as well, which is something I'm not afraid of and something I feel privileged to be able to do. Todd VanDerWerff Obviously, you played another famous TV character for quite a while. Do you see a line between Peggy and Offred, or is their main similarity that they look like you? Elisabeth Moss I see so many similarities. The main thing that I see is this sense of, they're normal people. They're normal women. Peggy, I always say, was sort of an accidental feminist. She didn't know about the phrase “the glass ceiling.” She reads in the paper that women are not getting paid as much as men, and she's confused and then goes and asks for a raise because she doesn't understand. That doesn't seem fair. It's that fantastic stubbornness, that fantastic naiveté almost, that is exemplary of so many women of that time. Those are the sorts of women that made those strides in the '60s. In Offred, I see the same thing. She's a woman. She's a wife. She's a mother. She has her own set of problems. She has a job. She is not meant to be a heroine. I think the great lesson she learns is there's a heroine in all of us, that we all actually do have the power to change things. That's something she has to learn. That’s perhaps one of the many parallels. They're not perfect people, which I think is great because nobody's perfect. They're flawed, both, hugely, but there's a strength to them as well. Todd VanDerWerff You called Peggy as an accidental feminist, and one thing that’s fascinating about Offred in the book is that she probably wouldn’t have defined herself as a feminist either, but she ends up in this situation that makes her realize what she took for granted. Do you think that character’s been radicalized in a way maybe Peggy wasn’t? Elisabeth Moss I do. I think that is true of many women. I know it's true of a lot of women in my generation and younger than I am. We've grown up thinking that we were going to have these inalienable rights, that you couldn't overturn Roe v. Wade, for instance. Why wouldn't we have the right to choose? Somebody was going to say something. Somebody was going to fight for you. We weren’t taking it for granted but expecting that we would always have certain things, I think. I do think in the last few years, there's been this sense of waking up amongst my generation and the younger generation and teenagers as well, of going, "Oh, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, you can't take that away." We've perhaps made June more of a feminist in the flashbacks than she is in the book, but I do think there is definitely a sense of when she is put into these circumstances where all of her rights are taken away, of course that's going to radicalize you. Of course that's going to make you have to fight back in a way that maybe you didn't think you had to. What I think is important though for us, as women now in 2017, is to fight now and not wait for that to happen. Todd VanDerWerff The Handmaid’s Tale is a story where sex is inherently political, where Offred is raped, and it’s sanctioned by the state, and her flirtation with another man is also a kind of political rebellion. But I always hear that actually filming a sex scene is really boring and technical for actors. Did that technical stuff drown out the larger political messages around sex in the story? Elisabeth Moss It was very important to us that the ceremony is very clearly a sexual assault and to not shy away from that idea. It is the center of the story in so many ways. Fertility and making a baby are what everyone is after. Whoever you are, whatever class you're a part of, that's what everyone wants. It was very important for us to make sure that was something that was accurately portrayed with brutality, but also have it be clinical, have it be rote, and then also bring the three different people and the three different ways they would feel about that into it as well. As opposed to most sex scenes where you're like, "Ugh, this is awkward and terrible," or it's boring, or whatever it is. This was a scene. It was acting. It was not just taking your clothes off. It was something that was a true performance scene because of the circumstances of it. The other thing that's interesting as well is the desexualization of sex that happens in Gilead [the name of the dystopian society in the series]. Men and women aren't allowed to enjoy sex. You're not supposed to enjoy having an orgasm, and we see in coming episodes that come into play for June. The right to be a human who enjoys sex is taken away, and how would that make one feel? Taking back that ownership of enjoying sex is something that is important and just another right that's been taken away. Todd VanDerWerff The camera is right in your face for much of this show, so much so that it catches if your eyebrow moves even a millimeter. It really doesn’t leave you any space to have a false moment. Is that exciting for you? Elisabeth Moss It's awesome. It makes my job so much better and so much easier. If you're playing a character who can't speak [freely], I can think things and the camera sees them. If I swallow, you see it. I've seen all the episodes, and I've seen them many, many times. When I first watch scenes, it's always really interesting for me because things happen in my face or something will twitch in my neck, and I wasn't doing that on purpose. That's just me internalizing emotion, and that's just me feeling things but not being able to say them or trying not to show them. For me, that sort of relationship happens between you and the camera regardless of what scene you're doing, even if the camera's all the way at the other end of the street. To have that relationship be [so close], it's like you're one body, the [director of photography] or [director Reed Morano] or whoever was operating the camera. The way that the matte box [around the lens] is, it sort of shuts everything else out so you can't even see anything. Then they're moving with you as well. You end up feeling like you're one entity. I love it. We'd do other [wider] shots, but then we'd always redo it with what we called our Offred lens. Any time it came in, I would always be like, "Oh, good, good, good, perfect. Now I can actually show what I'm feeling." The Handmaid’s Tale is now streaming on Hulu.

## "The Handmaid's Tale": A Chilling Mirror to Today's World

The recent Hulu adaptation of Margaret Atwood's novel, *The Handmaid's Tale*, sparked a surprising reaction. Some conservative viewers, seeing the trailer, mistakenly believed it was a new commentary on the Trump presidency, unaware it was based on a book written decades ago. This confusion, however, is **understandable**. The series taps into **deep-seated current anxieties**: the **heart-wrenching images of children separated from their parents**, the **persecution of individuals deemed "unnatural,"** and the **forced pregnancies of victims** all feel disturbingly familiar.

### More Than Just Timely: The **Enduring Power** of Dystopia

What truly makes *The Handmaid's Tale* resonate beyond its immediate relevance is its **timeless quality**. After watching seven episodes, it's not just the overt atrocities that stick with you. It's how the series **masterfully exposes the pervasive fear women experience due to everyday sexism**. Long before the oppressive Handmaid era begins, we see the **casual cruelty** of a coffee shop cashier hurling insults at Offred (Elisabeth Moss) and her friend Moira (Samira Wiley) for no clear reason. This moment feels like a glimpse into a world where societal collapse liberates misogynists to express their true feelings. It's a chilling parallel to **online trolls emboldened by anonymity** and a stark reminder of real-world incidents where individuals have voiced hateful views.

### The **Subtle Horrors** of Everyday Misogyny

While the series doesn't shy away from violence and sexual assault, it's the **more insidious forms of misogyny** that strike a deeper chord. We witness the boss who offers a **perfunctory apology** while laying off female employees. We see the soldier who holds the door for departing women, knowing they won't return to their jobs. We observe the "commander" who allows his Handmaid to read a forbidden magazine, not out of kindness, but to **feel a fleeting sense of benevolence**. This is the **creeping sexism** many women have encountered – the condescending pat on the head rather than outright harassment.

### Patriarchy's Grip: From Personal Fear to **Political Control**

Atwood's novel, and now the series, powerfully illustrates how **personal anxieties surrounding masculinity fuel this dystopian society's political structure**. The very idea of male infertility is forbidden. Women are barred from reading, lest intellectual pursuits distract them from their sole purpose: childbearing. Even well-meaning men become **unwitting participants in patriarchal systems**. When Offred learns women's bank accounts have been seized and transferred to male relatives, her husband assures her he will "take care of" her. This well-intentioned but dismissive promise highlights his inability to grasp the systemic crisis, framing it as a personal matter he can control, thereby **reinforcing his own perceived power**.

### The **Unsettling Question** of Trust

Ultimately, *The Handmaid's Tale* is a terrifying exploration of sexism, not just in its most extreme forms, but in its **everyday, insidious manifestations**. It's not solely about a society where politicians dehumanize pregnant women or where public figures refuse to dine alone with women. It's about the **erosion of trust**, not just in government, but in the men closest to us. The series reveals the danger that lies not only in overt male disdain for women but also in the **apathy and self-serving attachment to privilege** exhibited by "good" men. These are the **dual horrors** that women have long understood.

Margaret Atwood's 1985 novel, *The Handmaid's Tale*, has seen a significant resurgence in public discussion, with many drawing parallels between its fictional world and contemporary American society. This renewed interest, fueled by a popular Hulu miniseries, has led commentators in publications like *The Washington Post*, *The San Francisco Chronicle*, and *The New York Times* to describe the novel as "timely" and "chillingly real." They often link its themes to the political climate during the Trump administration. Rebecca Nicholson, writing for *The Guardian*, observed that readers are finding new connections between the fictional Republic of Gilead and current events, a trend evident as the book climbed Amazon's bestseller list, coinciding with Women's Marches where signs urged, "Make Margaret Atwood fiction again."

Atwood herself commented on this phenomenon in an interview with the *Los Angeles Times* on April 19th, suggesting that the lines between fiction and reality had blurred following the election. "The election happened, and the cast woke up in the morning and thought, we’re no longer making fiction — we’re making a documentary," she stated. This sentiment is echoed in various analyses, with *The New Republic* suggesting that states like Texas and Indiana, and potentially the entire nation with Mike Pence as Vice President, were increasingly resembling Gilead.

However, a closer look at this discourse reveals a tendency to overlook specific factual details. For instance, the idea that Republicans universally oppose reproductive rights is challenged by their historical support for over-the-counter birth control. The financial situation of organizations like Planned Parenthood, which reportedly receives substantial taxpayer funding annually, is also often presented in an oversimplified manner. Even Supreme Court Justice Neil Gorsuch, during his confirmation hearings, indicated he would have opposed any directive to overturn Roe v. Wade. These verifiable points are frequently sidelined in the broader narrative.

The prevailing feminist discourse, as seen in the *Handmaid's Tale* discussions, sometimes implies a lack of personal agency for women, framing them as passive recipients of reproductive outcomes. This narrative often overlooks the existence and accessibility of modern contraception and downplays the capacity for individual decision-making. Despite advocating for bodily autonomy, some feminist arguments seem to suggest women cannot manage their reproductive health without extensive government intervention, a paternalistic framing that ironically mirrors the control mechanisms depicted in Atwood's dystopia.

The focus on figures like Vice President Mike Pence, whose personal habits have been scrutinized as indicative of a Puritanical mindset, often distracts from more complex societal dynamics. The insinuation that women lack personal agency is a recurring theme, present even when discussing a country where minors can obtain IUDs without parental consent in 21 states. This contrasts with the political debate surrounding taxpayer funding for organizations like Planned Parenthood, which paid its CEO a significant salary in 2014. The portrayal of America on the brink of a totalitarian regime, where women are "forced" to reproduce, seems detached from the realities of available reproductive healthcare and individual choice. The notion that babies simply "happen" to women fails to acknowledge the role of contraception and personal agency in family planning. The assertion that birth control is about to be banned and will remain heavily subsidized is a factual claim often unaddressed in the widespread panic. Ultimately, the *Handmaid's Tale* discourse, while tapping into anxieties, often oversimplifies the intricate interplay of policy, individual autonomy, and reproductive rights in contemporary America.

query: What new features are expected in the upcoming iPhone models, particularly the high-end model?

documents.text:
**iPhone 8 Leaks: Infrared Facial Recognition and Bezel-less Design Revealed**

New details about Apple's upcoming iPhone models have surfaced, thanks to a leaked HomePod firmware update. This accidental release offers developers a preview of the next generation of iPhones, including a premium model alongside the expected standard "S" versions for the iPhone 7 and 7 Plus.

**Infrared Facial Recognition Confirmed**

A key revelation from the firmware code points to the integration of an **infrared facial recognition system**. Developer Steve Troughton-Smith has corroborated reports of this biometric feature within Apple's BiometricKit framework, referencing code like "BKFaceDetect." The "BK" prefix likely stands for Biometric Kit, a common naming convention in Apple's developer tools. This indicates the new iPhone will be able to **detect faces using infrared technology**, even in low-light conditions. Previous reports from Bloomberg had suggested Apple might use an infrared camera for 3D facial scanning, but its inclusion across all new iPhone models was uncertain.

**A Glimpse of the Bezel-less iPhone Pro**

The leaked firmware also appears to contain an embedded image showcasing a **novel, bezel-less design for the high-end iPhone**. This model, speculated to be named "iPhone Pro," "iPhone 8," or "iPhone X," is expected to feature a display that extends nearly to the device's edges. Notably, this screen seems to curve around the speaker and camera sensors at the top. Unlike previous potentially fabricated leaks, this visual evidence is directly integrated into the HomePod's code, making it more credible.

**Touch ID Status Unclear, Facial Scanning Takes Priority**

The leaked information also addresses ongoing speculation about Touch ID, the iPhone's fingerprint sensor. For months, rumors have debated its placement: under the display, on the back, in the power button, or omitted entirely. While the definitive status of Touch ID remains unconfirmed, the firmware leak strongly suggests the new iPhone will support unlocking through **facial scanning**. The fundamental visual profile of the premium model, characterized by its edge-to-edge display, has also been disclosed.

The prospect of a $1,400 iPhone, as explored by Christopher Mims in The Wall Street Journal, prompts a closer examination of Apple's pricing strategies and its carefully cultivated brand positioning. While concrete details regarding the next flagship iPhone remain elusive, industry analysts and supply chain intelligence collectively suggest a starting price point hovering around $1,000, with the potential to reach as high as $1,400. This projected price marks a substantial escalation from the current iPhone 7 lineup, which begins at $649 and extends to $969. The central question facing Apple is whether this represents a deliberate strategy to further elevate the brand's prestige or a potentially perilous gamble that could alienate its devoted customer base.

### The Enduring Strength of Apple's Brand

While some commentators propose that an ultra-premium iPhone could serve to invigorate the brand, Apple's global brand valuation, estimated at a staggering $170 billion by Forbes, already dwarfs that of competitors like Google. This impressive valuation constitutes more than half of Apple's substantial $250 billion cash reserve and rivals the combined market capitalization of prominent companies such as Netflix, Tesla, and eBay. Although a limited-edition "halo" product might possess some strategic appeal, Apple's historical experiences, exemplified by the 20th Anniversary Macintosh, suggest a need for caution. Industry observer Jon Gruber considers a $1,200-$1,300 "Pro" model to be plausible but views it as a niche offering rather than a mainstream product. Apple's enduring success is largely rooted in the concept of "obtainable aspiration"—the inherent desire for high-quality, desirable products that are within reach, as opposed to the unattainable exclusivity associated with ultra-luxury items.

### Navigating Technology, Pricing, and Market Receptiveness

Marketing professor Janell Townsend highlights that halo products can indeed function as testing grounds for novel technologies. However, Apple has a well-established pattern of integrating innovative features into its primary product lines, a strategy evident with the introduction of the stylus on the iPad Pro and the dual-lens camera systems on larger iPhone models. Apple has consistently delivered meaningful upgrades to its standard iPhones, often without necessitating price increases, thereby incentivizing existing users to upgrade. A hypothetical iPhone 7S priced at $649, lacking significant new features, would likely experience considerably lower adoption rates. Similarly, reserving the most advanced innovations exclusively for a substantially more expensive model could deter a significant portion of loyal customers from upgrading. Apple's experience with the iPad line demonstrates that while price increases can be justified by enhanced features, maintaining robust sales necessitates a careful assessment of market receptiveness. The iPhone's remarkable success is attributable to a steady stream of incremental improvements that render each iteration desirable, mirroring the personal computing model of acquiring new devices for enhanced capabilities at a consistent expenditure.

### Supply Chain Dynamics and Pricing Consistency

Both Jon Gruber and Christopher Mims point to the inherent complexities of the supply chain as a potential catalyst for price increases. However, this concern surfaces annually, and Apple has consistently demonstrated its ability to ramp up production post-launch, effectively overcoming initial supply gaps. This resilience is a testament to the intricate engineering of each iPhone and the dedicated period required for production scaling. Challenges such as sourcing novel components or manufacturing sophisticated parts are undeniably significant. Yet, CEO Tim Cook's profound expertise in component procurement and manufacturing processes is widely recognized. The anticipated integration of an OLED screen in the upcoming iPhone, for instance, underscores Apple's success in securing substantial supply from Samsung. Historically, Apple has adhered to a consistent pricing strategy, deliberately avoiding premium charges for early access, a crucial point emphasized by analyst Rakesh Agrawal. This deliberate approach is intrinsically woven into their brand identity and overarching business strategy.

### Average Selling Price (ASP) and Market Realities

While Apple may harbor ambitions to elevate the Average Selling Price (ASP) of its iPhones, a drastic price hike is unlikely to represent the most optimal or sustainable solution. The iPhone's ASP has maintained a relatively stable trajectory, generally fluctuating between $550 and $700, with predictable seasonal peaks. Apple adeptly manages this metric through the strategic deployment of a diverse product portfolio, which includes more accessible options like the iPhone SE alongside premium variants. Nevertheless, a significant upward adjustment in pricing is neither essential nor strategically prudent. Fundamental economic principles unequivocally suggest that higher prices typically correlate with diminished demand.

The assumption that a substantial increase in iPhone pricing would not significantly impact sales carries considerable inherent risk. While some proponents suggest that an anniversary iPhone could evolve into a Veblen good (a rare category where demand increases with price), a consumer product selling over 200 million units annually with a starting price of $649 is highly unlikely to conform to this exceptional economic classification. Veblen goods, such as ultra-luxury automobiles, derive their value from exclusivity and elevated prices that function as potent status symbols. A reduction in price would, in fact, diminish their inherent appeal. Giffen goods, where demand paradoxically rises as prices increase due to a reduction in purchasing power for more affordable alternatives, are entirely irrelevant to Apple's established premium market position. A significantly higher iPhone price would almost certainly precipitate a reduction in sales volume, a scenario that Wall Street typically views with considerable disfavor. Furthermore, Apple is contending with escalating competition, particularly in emerging markets like China, where affordable domestic smartphones are rapidly gaining market traction. A scenario where Chinese consumers reject substantially more expensive iPhones could easily outweigh any short-term financial gains. A hypothetical 10% price increase met with a corresponding 10% drop in sales could invite significant analyst disapproval and potentially undermine the app and service ecosystem, which represents a critical growth driver for Apple.

While the introduction of a $1,400 iPhone variant or a higher entry-level price point is not entirely beyond the realm of possibility, it represents an improbable and strategically questionable trajectory for Apple. The company already commands substantial financial resources and should logically prioritize the expansion of its user base over the sole objective of augmenting its cash reserves. A super-premium iPhone might offer a temporary surge in profitability but fundamentally deviates from the core principles that have cemented Apple's status as a preeminent innovator in the technology sector.

This September, Apple is poised to launch not just its typical two new iPhone models, but also a **special 10th-anniversary edition**. This **ultra-deluxe model** is expected to feature **cutting-edge technology**, including an **OLED screen**, **3-D imaging capabilities**, and a **retina scanner**. Analysts predict this premium device will come with a **significant price tag**, potentially ranging from **$1,000 to $1,400**. This anticipation has fueled considerable discussion...

There are just days until the launch of the iPhone 8 - in fact, the evidence now strongly suggests it'll be called the iPhone X - and hype is reaching almost unbearable levels. Rumours suggest the iPhone 8 will feature a larger display, made possible by removing the Home Button and moving the fingerprint scanner - if Apple can overcome the technical challenges. It is also said to feature wireless charging and Face ID, a new form of facial recognition that will support Apple Pay. In this article, we round up all the rumours about the new iPhone 8: its UK release announcement (12 Sept) and onsale date (probably 22 Sept) - UK price, specs and new features, and what it's going to look like. We also cover leaked photos and videos. For advice on the current iPhone range, read our iPhone buying guide and best cheap iPhone deals UK. Or, if you'd like to look even further into the future, read iPhone 9 and beyond. Release date The iPhone 8 will be announced on 12 September, as confirmed by Apple's sending of press invites on 31 August. We'll save you from the mad interpretations people make from the invite designs, but there is a suspiciously curved edge in that light colour... no, we'll stop. But it's going to be great, isn't it? Preorders are likely to begin on 15 Sept with an onsale release date of 22 Sept. Read more about the Apple September 2017 event here. Delays & shortages Some sources have suggested there may be longer-than-usual delays between the iPhone 8 announcement and products hitting shops, or that there may be initial supply shortages. Deutsche Bank analysts predict that "key component shortages and technical challenges could delay the release of a high-end iPhone 8", while IHS Markit analyst Brian Huh claims Samsung is having trouble meeting demand for the OLED screens. Ming-Chi Kuo is among the analysts to state that problems have now been resolved and iPhone 8 mass production will begin in mid September as planned - but while the flagship device will now go on sale at the same time as the two cheaper models, he warns that there may be a shortage at first. DigiTimes too predicts that "the supply of the OLED version [iPhone 8] could fall short of demand." What will the new iPhone be called? We expect Apple to announce three new handsets: the first two will be simple updates to the 7 and 7 Plus (they may even be thicker), but we'll all be focused on the flagship model and its wireless charging, 3D scanning, AR and edge-to-edge OLED screen. One developer reckons he's found the names of these handsets. Steve Troughton-Smith, combing through the latest leaked firmware, has found references to iPhone 8, iPhone 8 Plus and iPhone X. (The latter makes sense because it's the Roman numeral for 10, and 2017 is the iPhone's 10th anniversary.) iPhone 8 iPhone 8 Plus iPhone X pic.twitter.com/OVLfhLxTdT — Steve T-S (@stroughtonsmith) September 9, 2017 This fits with an earlier report from iCulture (in Dutch), citing "a reliable source", which predicted the same thing. There had been a claim by 9to5Mac that the phones would be called the 8, 8 Plus and iPhone Edition, but X is now looking the strong favourite. Macworld poll: What do you think Apple will call its next iPhone? iPhone 8 design & leaked images Noted tech blogger Robert Scoble has posted an exhaustive list of predictions for this year's iPhone, and he expects big things. "It's the 10th anniversary of the iPhone," he writes. "It's the first product introduction in Apple's new amazing headquarters. It's a big f**king deal and will change this industry deeply." Here's what we expect from the new design, and the leaked images we've seen to back it all up. Edge-to-edge screen The iPhone 8 will have a screen running almost edge to edge, with a slimmed-down bezel of just 4mm on each side. An image of this design, also showing the loss of the Home button and the rumoured 'notch' along the top edge, has been spotted in the HomePod's firmware. (It also offers clues about face-unlocking features, the lack of a Home button, a new Apple Watch-esque 'tap to wake' feature and much more.) This is mirrored by the leak of what is believed to be the user guide from the iPhone 8 packaging: And an alleged leak of the iPhone 8's front glass, posted on Weibo: Similarly, Macworld has been sent an iPhone 8 dummy from the case maker Olixar, which has already started making cases for the device. (It's very detailed and the buttons press in, but contrary to reports you may have seen elsewhere, this is absolutely not a "working prototype".) "It's the dummy that Olixar has been using to design their cases for the iPhone 8," said our contact. "Apart from some minor details that might still change in the final design stages we believe this is what the iPhone 8 will look like!" We're told that the company obtained these details from "a regular group of contacts that help them to source these items for the production of their accessories"; while we'd rate the authenticity as unproven, it does seem to fit in with the currently popular rumours. Note the cut-out in the display at the top, to allow for the speaker and sensors: we think this section will be used for the status bar, which is split into two groups of icons anyway. This is a theme that's repeated in other concept illustrations and leaks we've seen - such as this one from Forbes. Here's a render with measurements for that edge-to-edge screen: And this tweet shows the measurements of the top status bar area, as revealed in the HomePod firmware leak: These are the metrics used by the status bar on the edge-to-edge iPhone, including notch height and ear width. Designers, have fun pic.twitter.com/lPD2RbEJFA — Steve T-S (@stroughtonsmith) August 10, 2017 Finally, one bright spark appears to have got semi-confirmation from Apple itself, by designing iMessage stickers depicting this design and having them rejected from the App Store for being "too similar to iPhones". So, I tried to submit an iMessage sticker pack. pic.twitter.com/HNziE4SSRx — Saif Aslam (@msaifaslam) August 17, 2017 No Home button It's also believe that there will be no Home button on the front of the iPhone 8 (we'll discuss the implications of this for Touch ID in a moment). The HomePod firmware leak mentioned previously contains code referring to a new variable called "deviceHasHomeButton" (which could be tagged with a yes or no), which seems like a strong hint that this is going ahead. @stroughtonsmith And the death of the home button is confirmed. RIP. pic.twitter.com/w638s4Tgzn — Guilherme Rambo (@_inside) August 1, 2017 A June 2017 leak backs up the 'no Home button' theory. As you can see, that's a massive screen, with no physical home button in sight. Aside from Touch ID, this also removes one of the traditional ways of accessing Siri - but source code suggests this may now be done by holding down the power button instead (presumably for less time than it would take to turn the iPhone off). And you'll still be able to use Hey Siri, of course. Home button function area If the physical Home button is ditched, Apple is likely to replace it with a software Home button. In February 2017 Ming Chi Kuo predicted that the iPhone 8 would have a function area, somewhat like the Touch Bar on the 2016 MacBook Pro, which will take the place of the lower bezel and Home button and contain "virtual buttons". Near the end of August 2017 Bloomberg revealed further details of how the iPhone 8's interface will work, citing "images of the new device viewed by Bloomberg News and people familiar with the gadget". The site claims that the Home button of previous handsets will be replaced by new gesture controls. "Across the bottom of the screen there's a thin, software bar in lieu of the home button," Bloomberg writes. "A user can drag it up to the middle of the screen to open the phone." Further gestures, many of them context-specific, will enable users to close apps, multitask and return to the home screen. These stunning mockups by Gabor Balogh give an idea of how the function area could be used, and how it could interact with the also-rumoured new augmented reality features. Looks great, right? But not so fast. The developer Steve Troughton-Smith, "supported by API evidence", has posted a summary of what the Home button area will and won't be able to do - and it seems that there will be no ability for developers to change the colour of the area to fit in with their apps. We know some facts re iPhone 8 home button area: • it resizes • indicator can be hidden • no API to change color • tab bars extend under it — Steve T-S (@stroughtonsmith) August 10, 2017 "Fullscreen video does hide it," he adds, but "there is no evidence to suggest any app UI moves to the home button area at all. No toolbars, no other junk." Initially at least, the area will remain a comparatively standardised area that either shows the Home button in its conventional design or gets out of the way entirely for full-screen video, gaming and so on. What happens to the Touch ID fingerprint sensor? Developer betas of iOS 11 suggest Touch ID will remain in some form this autumn: the lock screen still displays its icon when trying to access locked notifications. But if Apple gives the iPhone 8 with a larger screen and ditches the Home Button, what happens to the fingerprint sensor? There's four main options: a scanner built into the screen; a scanner on the back of the phone; a scanner built into the power button; and no scanner at all, with Touch ID replaced by Face ID. No scanner Right now it seems most likely, based on multiple leaks, that the iPhone 8 won't have a fingerprint scanner - and that the Touch ID code in iOS 11 is there for existing handsets and possibly the iPhone 7s and 7s Plus. In this scenario Touch ID's security functions will be covered by Face ID. Scanner under the screen iDrop News, citing an unnamed source, reported that Apple has been testing fingerprint technology beneath the glass display, but that this isn't ready for prime time this year. Technology is moving forward quickly; Qualcomm, for example, has come up with new sensors that can recognise fingerprints through OLED display stacks of up to 1200um. But it seems these breakthroughs may have come too late for the iPhone 8. KeyBanc Capital Markets analyst Andy Hargreaves has written that it's "entirely unclear if Apple will be able to fix the problem" within time for the launch, and Ming-Chi Kuo says Apple has "cancelled" plans to embed Touch ID in the iPhone 8's screen. Scanner on the back Because of the technological obstacles preventing in-screen Touch ID, iDrop News predicts that the scanner will go on the back of the device. This was backed up by a video that leaked in late August 2017, and purportedly shows a factory worker in China testing the fingerprint scanner on an iPhone 8. We're not totally convinced, to say the least, and it could easily show testing on an iPhone clone by a different company, or an early prototype that did have rear Touch ID. Further 'evidence' of this came in the form of leaked images in a tweet by Sonny Dickson, which appears to feature a space at the back of the iPhone to accommodate a scanner. iPhone 8 looks to be taking a new direction pic.twitter.com/mG19bcDYiC — Sonny Dickson (@SonnyDickson) April 19, 2017 There's also this (suspiciously blurry) photo, posted on a Chinese forum and supposedly from a Foxconn assembly line. You can see the circular cutout for the fingerprint scanner just below the Apple logo: It's also apparent in an August 2017 leak which appears to show off the rear-facing fingerprint scanner. The video shows an Apple leather case for the iPhone 8, although it's widely believed to be a fake - the details on the case are shoddy, and the acting is a little cheesy too. The designs have not gone down well with people critical of Samsung's similar solution to the same problem on the S8+. Scanner in the power button Our last suggestion is that Touch ID could be integrated into the power button. Forbes has posted a mock-up based on CAD files leaked by case designer Nodus which shows what appears to be a bigger lock button on the side of the iPhone, which could be in order to house Touch ID. Vertically aligned twin-lens camera The third big expected change to the design affects the rear-facing camera, which looks to have twin lenses arranged vertically (as opposed to the side-by-side layout on the iPhone 7 Plus). Forbes' Gordon Kelly is worried that this will stick out at the rear of the phone and make it wobble when placed on a flat surface. The vertical camera design was illustrated in an early July 2017 leak showcasing an alleged iPhone 8 back panel. Two short videos, which have been posted on Chinese social media in August 2017 and uploaded to YouTube by the Dutch site TechTastic, purportedly show the iPhone 8 production line - and while you can't see much, it's quite clear that the devices have vertically aligned camera lenses. And if you're yet to be convinced that all this is the finalised design of the iPhone 8, you need look no further than infamous (and remarkably reliable) leaker OnLeaks' Twitter account. More specifically, a tweet posted on 23 June shows what appears to be the next-generation iPhone 8, which can be seen below. Glass front and back 9to5Mac has got hold of a report from the ever-quotable and usually reliable Ming-Chi Kuo of KGI Securities - a report that predicts, based on the popularity of the scratch-prone Jet Black iPhone 7, that the iPhone 8 will have a (more scratch-resistant) glass front and back. Glass would also make it easier to implement wireless charging. "If Apple does follow through with what KGI suggests, an all-glass design could extend the glossy finish to all colours of the iPhone lineup depending on how Apple handles the design," 9to5Mac says. The rumour was also backed up by Benjamin Geskin on Twitter, a source that has provided many iPhone 8 leaks over the past few months. He claims that one of the colour options for the iPhone 8 will be a mirror-like finish. Slashleaks has posted a video allegedly showing metal iPhone 8 rear shells being assembled in a Foxconn factory. But it's possible this actually shows iPhone 7 or 7s Plus handsets, which may not get the all-glass design of the top-end model. (Or the cases may, as OnLeaks observes, be intended for clone handsets that are nothing to do with Apple!) Colour options The iPhone 7 handsets are available in six colours: silver, gold, Rose Gold, black, Jet Black and (Product) Red. (The red option was not added until several months after launch, however.) Things look to be different for the iPhone 8. Benjamin Geskin's source says the iPhone 8 will feature a mirror-like finish plus only three other colour options (most likely silver, gold and black); while analyst Ming-Chi Kuo predicts that the mirror option has been canned so we'll get just silver, gold and black. But hold up! Geskin has tweeted again since then to offer an entirely new colour option, which he refers to as Blush Gold and which looks like a sort of coppery reddish-pink. This appears to be the same finish MyDrivers referred to nearer the start of August, admittedly in translation, as "champagne gold". Geskin acknowledges, however, that this name is based on a working translation from the Chinese and may not be accurate. He says the information comes from a leaked Foxconn report, and was posted on the Weibo Chinese social media site. New features That covers design. But what new features should we expect in the iPhone 8? Face ID The loss of the Home button means the Touch ID fingerprint scanner is in design limbo - with separate theories claiming it will move to the back of the device, or be embedded under the glass, or be removed entirely. But many of its functions may be replaced by a new feature tentatively known as Face ID. The HomePod firmware mentioned earlier contains references to facial recognition and expression detection that could be used to unlock the device. There's also a lot of new references to facial expression detection pic.twitter.com/8PsPVj1QqU — Guilherme Rambo (@_inside) August 1, 2017 Face detection/unlock has been expected for a while. Back in December 2014, USPTO awarded Apple a patent relating to a "personal computing device control using face detection and recognition", and US Patent 20170076077, published in March 2017, describes a method for "Locking and unlocking a mobile device using facial recognition". DigiTimes has predicted that Apple is likely to launch iPhones equipped with iris-recognition technology in 2018; but at the end of August 2016 the site went further, reporting that Taiwan-based Xintec is expected to provide iris scanners to Apple for the iPhone launching in 2017. JPMorgan analyst Rod Hall backed up this theory with his own prediction that the iPhone 8 will have a 3D laser scanner for face recognition, replacing Touch ID. (If you'd like to know how powerful a mere rumour can be, check this out: the share price of the UK company expected to provide laser wafers for this feature, IQE, has tripled this year.) A photo of this scanner appears to have leaked, too: And unlocking the phone might not be the only thing facial recognition is used for. Code in the HomePod firmware leak indicates that it will also be able to authenticate Apple Pay and be accessible to third-party apps; and that it will also serve to mute notifications if the user is perceived to be 'paying attention' already. About Pearl ID: 1 - The software definitely supports it for payments 2 - 3rd party apps can use it 3 - You can add multiple faces pic.twitter.com/aUotHwD64f — Guilherme Rambo (@_inside) August 9, 2017 Augmented reality and VR Apple debuted ARKit at WWDC 2017, showcasing how developers can implement AR abilities into iPads and iPhones. Surely the iPhone 8 will arrive with cool new features that take advantage of that? We previously spotted an Apple patent application that appears to back up all this speculation. Patent 9,488,488, for 'Augmented reality maps', describes the use of a mobile device to view live video of whatever is in front of the user, and to then superimpose images related to nearby places of interest on top of the video. Loup Ventures analysts speculate that a company named Finisar may be providing lasers for AR depth mapping in the iPhone 8. You can read more in our Apple VR and augmented reality rumours article. Wireless charging Apple has been offering wireless (inductive) charging for a while now on the Apple Watch, and we're hearing increasingly confident predictions that the feature will come to the iPhone in 2017. One strong hint came from Robert Hwang, the CEO of Apple manufacturer Wistron, who (in an apparent slip-up) said "new features like waterproof and wireless charging" will appear in the next generation of Apple handsets; and SlashLeaks has posted what it says is the charging coil that will be used to deliver this feature. Benjamin Geskin, a well-known leaker, has posted what he says are images of components in the iPhone 8, and these include a similar wireless charging module: #iPhone8 Foxconn Production Photos.
