**Representative Peter King** is calling on **President Obama to cease apologizing** for the National Security Agency's (NSA) surveillance activities in Europe. Appearing on "Meet the Press," King advocated for a **firmer approach**. He highlighted the NSA's **crucial function in averting many fatalities** both domestically and throughout Europe, mentioning nations such as France and Germany.

This stance emerges after revelations that the NSA had been tracking millions of phone calls in Europe. Reports specifically noted the agency's access to the private phone of German Chancellor Angela Merkel.

King also voiced **significant opposition** to fellow Republicans, **Senator Rand Paul** and **Representative Justin Amash**. He found it highly concerning that they would **"try to commend" NSA whistleblower Edward Snowden**. King maintained that the NSA has made **substantial strides in safeguarding national security**. He also stated that the agency has **bolstered the President's power**. Consequently, King feels the President should **unreservedly back** the NSA.

Former Sen. Rick Santorum said on Sunday that Sen. Ted Cruz had harmed the Republican Party in his crusade to defund Obamacare that helped bring about the government shutdown. "I would say in the end, he did more harm. I think it was not his objective," the former Republican senator from Pennsylvania and GOP presidential candidate said on NBC's "Meet the Press." "I think his objective was a laudable one. I think he didn't do a very good job of planning it out. I think it's one thing to have a goal and another thing to have a plan to get you to that goal." Santorum said Texas senator is not the face of the Republican Party. (PHOTOS: Key quotes from Ted Cruz) "Unlike the Democratic party who has a leader, has the president, there isn't a leader in the Republican Party right now. That's part of the problem. That's part of the mess and the confusion," Santorum said. "But that's always the way it is with a party out of power. You have lots of different faces and those faces, as we've seen, they come and they go." Read more about: Rick Santorum, Ted Cruz, Government Shutdown, Obamacare

The Internet Archive is dedicated to **preserving the web's history** by actively archiving web pages as they are created, along with the content they reference. This proactive strategy guarantees that even if linked pages are subsequently modified or deleted, a **permanent record** of the original material remains available.

The **"No More 404" initiative** is a key effort to address the pervasive issue of broken links online. By capturing and storing linked content, the Internet Archive aims to offer a **reliable alternative**. When a hyperlink directs users to content that has vanished, a link to its archived counterpart can be substituted. This archived version can also function as a **supplementary resource**, providing readers with context about the initial purpose of a page's creators. The overarching **objective is to mend the web's fractured connections** through systematic crawling and archiving of participating websites.

**Calls for Secretary Sebelius's Resignation Grow Amidst Obamacare Rollout Woes**

The implementation of President Obama's landmark healthcare law has encountered significant hurdles, leading to increased demands for the resignation of Health and Human Services Secretary Kathleen Sebelius. A prominent critic, Republican Congressman Darrell Issa, has publicly stated that Sebelius should step down if she cannot effectively lead her department to achieve the administration's objectives. During an appearance on CBS's "Face the Nation," Issa expressed his view that "The president has been poorly served in the implementation of his own signature legislation." He also raised questions about Sebelius's awareness of the ongoing issues, asking, "Why didn’t she know that the president’s signature legislation was in trouble?"

**Lawmakers Seek Accountability for Website Malfunctions**

Issa's concerns are echoed by other members of Congress. House Budget Committee Chairman Paul Ryan, a Republican from Wisconsin, has specifically urged Sebelius to resign. His call stems from her management of the technical difficulties plaguing the Obamacare website. In a letter addressed to Sebelius, Ryan pointed to her lack of response to congressional inquiries. He wrote, "Your continued silence on these important inquiries after refusing to testify raises serious questions about the administration’s commitment to transparency and accountability."

**Democrats Focus on System Functionality Over Blame**

In contrast, Democratic Senator Jeanne Shaheen of New Hampshire is prioritizing the repair of the system over assigning blame. When questioned about Sebelius, Shaheen emphasized that the **main objective is to ensure the healthcare marketplaces and their associated websites are fully operational**. "Our No. 1 goal is to get the market places working, get the websites working," she stated. Shaheen indicated that the process of determining responsibility can be deferred until the technical problems have been successfully addressed.

*Correction: An earlier version of this article incorrectly stated that Rep. Darrell Issa appeared on a different Sunday morning program.*

query: What was Rep. Nancy Pelosi's response to the calls from some Democrats for her to step down as House Minority Leader?

documents.text: Rep. Nancy Pelosi is trying to tamp down any intra - caucus criticism of her leadership. | Getty Congress Pelosi defiant as restless Democrats consider options ‘I am a master legislator,’ Pelosi told reporters. Later that day, some of her Democratic colleagues met to discuss replacing her. A defiant House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi on Thursday said she has no plans to step down anytime soon, despite calls from some rank-and-file Democrats for new leadership and at least one meeting to plot to replace her. Pelosi spent a good chunk of her weekly news conference defending her longtime perch atop the Democratic Caucus, at times even mocking colleagues within her party who have in recent days said it’s time for her to step aside. Story Continued Below “I respect any opinion that my members have, but my decision about how long I stay is not up to them,” Pelosi told reporters. “My caucus is overwhelmingly supportive of me.” But as Pelosi was defending her status in the caucus, of group of Democrats were already planning a secret afternoon meeting to talk about potentially trying to replace her. A dozen Democrats attended the meeting out of the nearly 20 members invited. Afterward, leaders of the group said no decisions have been made about what to do next, but that support is growing within the caucus to topple Pelosi. “There are many more people today, even from November, who are much more vocal, who have approached us and said, ‘I’m done. We need to move forward and we need to get a new leadership team in place,’” Rep. Kathleen Rice (D-N.Y.) said after the hour-long meeting. Rice and another group leader, Rep. Seth Moulton (D-Mass.), said the ultimate goal would be to have a new leadership team in place before the 2018 campaign heats up. “The takeaway from the meeting is that we want to win again in 2018,” Moulton said, noting he’s seen a shift in the caucus since Pelosi won her leadership race last year. “I think people see the stakes more clearly.” Among the Democrats who attended the confab were Congressional Black Caucus Chairman Cedric Richmond (La.), Tony Cardenas (Calif.), a member of House Democratic leadership, and Reps. Ami Bera (Calif.), Filemon Vela (Texas), Ruben Gallego (Ariz.) and Del. Stacey Plaskett (V.I.). But despite the renewed noise surrounding Pelosi’s leadership, no one within the caucus has stepped forward to challenge her and an actual coup against the long-time leader is still a long shot. During the news conference earlier in the day, Pelosi boasted about her effectiveness as a leader, even though Democrats lost their majority in 2010 under her watch and have never come close to winning it back. Sign up here for POLITICO Huddle A daily play-by-play of congressional news in your inbox. Email Sign Up By signing up you agree to receive email newsletters or alerts from POLITICO. You can unsubscribe at any time. “I am a master legislator,” Pelosi declared. “I am a strategic, politically astute leader. My leadership is recognized by many around the country. That is why I am able to attract the [financial] support that I do, which is essential to our election, I am sad to say.” Pelosi also pointed out that she led Democrats to the majority in the first place back in 2006 after a dozen years out of power. One other reason Pelosi has held on to her leadership mantle: It’s unclear who could replace her and keep the conference’s disparate factions united. Questions about Pelosi’s longevity as Democratic leader have dogged her for a long time, but demands for new blood in leadership were given fresh life after Democrats’ disappointing loss in the Georgia special election Tuesday. Republicans constantly painted Democratic candidate Jon Ossoff as a Pelosi puppet representative of her “liberal San Francisco values” in a barrage of campaign ads and mailers and during appearances by Republican winner Karen Handel. Pelosi tells critics she's 'proud' of her leadership poster="http://v.politico.com/images/1155968404/201706/418/1155968404_5480204835001_5480140275001-vs.jpg?pubId=1155968404" true Pelosi is trying to tamp down any intracaucus criticism of her leadership and said the fact that Democrats came so close in a district that’s been a Republican stronghold for nearly four decades should count as a win. But critics of Pelosi, including some members of her own caucus, say that kind of mentality is exactly the reason why she should step down and let someone else lead the party before they get too deep into the midterm campaign. Pelosi shrugged off her colleagues’ criticism, at one point even daring them to keep up the drumbeat demanding her ouster. “When it comes to personal ambition and having fun on TV, have your fun. I love the arena, I thrive on competition, and I welcome the discussion,” Pelosi said. Rep. Tim Ryan, the Ohio Democrat who ran against Pelosi in November, said on MSNBC on Thursday morning that “it’ll be very hard” to win back the House with Pelosi at the helm. Rice, also on MSNBC Thursday morning, said Pelosi's time has "come and gone." Republicans — including President Donald Trump — have also engaged in the chatter over whether Pelosi should step down, taunting her leadership struggles and saying that as long as she’s leader, they’ll keep winning. “I certainly hope the Democrats do not force Nancy P out. That would be very bad for the Republican party – and please let Cryin’ Chuck stay!” Trump tweeted Thursday about Pelosi and Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.). In maybe the most concise response describing her reaction to the GOP attacks on her, Pelosi asserted, “I think that I am worth it.”

Democrats are grappling with how to effectively counter President Trump's perceived economic strength with working-class voters, following a closely contested special election in Georgia. Representative Debbie Dingell (D-Mich.) urged Democrats to "go on offense" and directly address the anxieties and fears of working men and women, arguing that Trump's approach treats them as mere political pawns.

The Democratic candidate in Georgia, Jon Ossoff, ran a campaign that raised approximately $25 million, largely through small donations. He actively sought right-of-center voters with promises of economic development and fiscal restraint. However, Democrats acknowledged that this message was insufficient to overcome a barrage of Republican advertising that portrayed Ossoff as a proxy for national liberal Democrats, particularly House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi. Pelosi, a frequent target of Republican criticism, is viewed as an unpopular figure on the right.

While Ossoff made some headway by capitalizing on Trump's unpopularity and opposition to the Affordable Care Act, Democrats recognize the need for a more robust strategy to secure victories. Representative Eric Swalwell (D-Calif.) emphasized the importance of crystallizing the party's message around jobs and healthcare. He suggested that the results from Georgia and other special elections should encourage Democrats to compete in a wider range of districts, stating, "We need to compete everywhere."

At a meeting of lawmakers discussing economic messaging, Representative Ben Ray Luján (D-N.M.), chairman of the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee, indicated that the committee would analyze the Georgia results for insights. He encouraged lawmakers to frame the race positively in public comments, highlighting Democrats' consistent overperformance in special elections within solidly Republican territory.

During this meeting, Representative Tony Cárdenas (D-Calif.), a member of the Democratic leadership, raised Pelosi's role in the Georgia race, referring to it as "the elephant in the room." A spokesperson for Cárdenas clarified that he invoked Pelosi in the context of developing strategies to counter future attacks. Pelosi herself has consistently resisted calls to step down, and her leadership position appears secure. In a "Dear Colleague" letter to Democratic lawmakers, she acknowledged the party's improved performance in conservative areas and affirmed that "every effort was made to win" in Georgia. She also called for Democrats to "put forth our message," specifically an economic one that "we can all embrace and utilize in our districts."

However, some Democrats who have previously opposed Pelosi believe her continued leadership undermines the party's candidates. Representative Seth Moulton (D-Mass.), an outspoken critic, described the Georgia result as "frustrating" and advocated for a change in party leadership. Representative Kathleen Rice (D-N.Y.) suggested that the entire Democratic leadership team should resign. Representative Tim Ryan (D-Ohio), who challenged Pelosi for minority leader, argued that she remains a political liability for Democrats. Ryan also contended that the Democratic brand has become "toxic" in many parts of the country, as voters perceive the party as disconnected from their concerns, stating, "Our brand is worse than Trump."

A senior aide to Pelosi dismissed the notion that her high-profile status harmed the Democratic effort in Georgia, noting that Republican Speaker Paul D. Ryan sometimes polls even less favorably. Drew Hammill, Pelosi's deputy chief of staff, asserted that any Democratic leader would become a target for Republicans. He argued that Republicans spent millions to retain a traditionally red seat and resorted to "demonizing the party's strongest fund-raiser and consensus builder" in their efforts.

In a potential sign of shifting sentiment, Joe Cunningham, a Democratic candidate challenging Representative Mark Sanford in South Carolina, immediately pledged not to support Pelosi for leader if elected. Cunningham stated that Democrats need "new leadership now." Even Democrats not openly critical of Pelosi acknowledged the cumulative impact of a decade of Republican attacks. Representative Bill Pascrell Jr. (D-N.J.) commented on the difficulty of overcoming such sustained negative campaigning.

The internal debate over the Democratic campaign message echoes a broader division within the party, stemming from the 2016 presidential primary. Senator Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) and his supporters advocate for a more populist message focused on assailing wealthy special interests and expanding social welfare programs. Conversely, more moderate Democrats in leadership favor an approach similar to Ossoff's. However, in four special elections held in Republican districts, including contests in Kansas and Montana featuring Sanders-aligned candidates, neither strategy yielded a breakthrough victory. Without a decisive win to resolve this ideological debate, Democrats may face a prolonged period of internal deliberation to forge a broadly acceptable approach.

Democrats face the challenge of competing in districts with low unemployment and growing economies, similar to the Georgia seat, where voters have seen their portfolios increase since Trump took office. Despite the recent defeat, Democrats remain committed to competing across a wide geographic area in 2018. Luján circulated a memo expressing confidence that Democrats can regain control of the House in the midterm elections, suggesting that numerous Republican-held seats are more attainable than Georgia's Sixth.

Citing private polling data, Luján identified Republican seats in Arizona, Florida, New Jersey, and the Kansas City suburbs as areas where Democratic challengers currently hold leads. Democrats need to win 24 Republican-held seats to secure a House majority.

On the Republican side, the Georgia victory was tempered by concerns about the broader political climate. While Karen Handel defeated Ossoff by approximately 10,000 votes, Republican outside groups invested $18 million to defend a district that had historically been a safe seat. Simultaneously, a special election in South Carolina saw an unexpected challenge from Democrat Archie Parnell, who narrowly lost a solidly Republican congressional district.

Republican strategist Nick Everhart cautioned against complacency, emphasizing that Republicans have primarily won in staunchly red districts. He warned that ignoring "serious enthusiasm-gap issues with the G.O.P. base and, more crucially, independents fleeing" would be a mistake.

The aftermath of the Georgia election proved more challenging for Democrats, marking their fourth special election with a strong showing by a defeated candidate. While this pattern suggests potential gains in 2018, the extended timeline presents a significant wait for a party eager to win.

The Democratic Party is grappling with internal divisions following a significant special election loss in Georgia's 6th Congressional District. This setback has amplified existing tensions within the party, which have been simmering since President Trump's election, sparking urgent calls for strategic reevaluation and leadership changes. Liberal activists and lawmakers are dissecting the reasons behind this electoral defeat, seeking to chart a more effective path forward.

"It is too early to pinpoint the exact reasons for our lack of success and to suggest necessary changes," stated Rep. Linda Sánchez (Calif.), vice chairwoman of the House Democratic Caucus, emphasizing that a comprehensive analysis will require time.

Some Democrats attribute the loss to a failure to learn from the 2016 election, arguing that campaigning against Trump without a robust positive message proved insufficient. This, they contend, contributed to Jon Ossoff's defeat by Republican Karen Handel. Rep. Tim Ryan (Ohio) believes the fundamental issue lies within the party's own identity, describing Ossoff as a strong candidate undermined by the "national baggage of the Democratic Party."

Rep. Seth Moulton (D-Mass.) echoed this sentiment, stressing the critical need for a clear and compelling vision. "We need a strategic plan, a compelling vision, and a message that explains why Democrats offer a better choice, not just why Trump is bad," Moulton asserted. "Our efforts are lacking, and this Georgia race highlights that."

Republicans effectively capitalized on linking Ossoff to House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi (D-Calif.), whose image is unpopular in many conservative districts. Ryan, Moulton, and other younger Democrats, who had previously challenged Pelosi for leadership for this very reason, revived this tactic. "Those ads continue to be effective and harmful to our candidates," Ryan commented, referencing his past challenge to Pelosi. "My view on this hasn't changed."

Moulton expressed skepticism about the party's ability to rebound under its current leadership. "It's not impossible, but it's much harder," he remarked. Rep. Kathleen Rice (D-N.Y.) went further, calling for a complete leadership overhaul. "We need a change in leadership," Rice told CNN. "It's time for Nancy Pelosi and the entire leadership team to step down."

This criticism extended beyond elected officials. Filmmaker Michael Moore declared the party lacks "a message, a plan, and leaders." David Wasserman, an analyst at the Cook Political Report, questioned whether Pelosi's fundraising prowess justifies the negative impact of her association in Republican advertisements. "It's very hard for Democrats to argue that Nancy Pelosi's fundraising benefits still outweigh the cost of her appearing in GOP ads," Wasserman tweeted.

Pelosi's office defended her record as a fundraiser, strategist, and whip. "Republican voters don't decide the leadership of the Democratic Party," said spokesman Drew Hammill, noting that Pelosi's approval ratings are comparable to Speaker Paul Ryan's. Hammill added, "Since Newt Gingrich, the GOP has used personal destruction. They will target any Democratic leader because their majority relies on desperation."

Conversely, many Democrats, including senior leaders, pointed to the historically Republican nature of Georgia's 6th District. Despite the loss, they remain optimistic about the message the close race sends for the 2018 midterms. "In a normal year, we wouldn't have had a chance. We've significantly closed the gap," stated Rep. Keith Ellison (D-Minn.), deputy chairman of the DNC. "They should be worried."

However, frustration among rank-and-file Democrats is palpable. Many lawmakers are exasperated by the party's four consecutive losses in competitive special elections during the Trump administration. "Close only counts in horseshoes," declared Rep. Bill Pascrell (D-N.J.). "A loss is a loss, and there are no excuses."

Other Democrats highlighted that Handel secured victory in a district held by Republicans for decades, noting that former Rep. Tom Price had won by a substantial margin the previous year. While Trump had only narrowly carried the district, Democrats believed an upset was within reach given the president's low approval ratings. Outside donors contributed nearly $24 million to Ossoff's campaign, making it the most expensive House race in history.

While many Democrats cautioned against definitive conclusions, some questioned how the party could invest so heavily and still fall short. "I believe it shows momentum is on our side, but there are no moral victories here. It's 218 seats or nothing," remarked Rep. Tim Walz (D-Minn.), referring to the number of seats required for a House majority. "If you invest that much, you must win."

## Democratic Party's Post-Georgia Election Reckoning: Leadership and Direction Under Scrutiny

The Democratic Party is grappling with significant internal debate following a costly loss in a Georgia congressional election, a result that has amplified concerns about the party's ability to connect with voters and its future direction. The outcome has intensified discussions surrounding leadership and the party's overall strategy.

### Internal Divisions on Party Strategy

Within the Democratic Party, differing perspectives have emerged regarding the reasons for electoral challenges. Some supporters of progressive ideals, like those aligned with Bernie Sanders, suggest that the nomination of moderate candidates, exemplified by Jon Ossoff's four-point loss to Republican Karen Handel in Georgia, is a primary issue. Conversely, others identify House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi as a significant liability, arguing that her public image alienates crucial voter segments. Republican advertising effectively leveraged this sentiment, linking Ossoff to Pelosi, whose approval ratings are notably low outside of California. Despite substantial financial backing from California Democrats, this support was strategically used against Ossoff.

### Scrutiny of Nancy Pelosi's Leadership

The Georgia election outcome has placed Nancy Pelosi's leadership under renewed scrutiny. Democratic strategist Sean Clegg remarked, "It was a very rough night for Nancy Pelosi." He observed that Republican attacks targeting Pelosi appeared to resonate more strongly with voters than Democratic efforts to criticize President Trump, presenting a considerable hurdle for future House races. Following the defeat, several Democratic members of Congress publicly voiced their concerns. Representative Filemon Vela of Texas stated to Politico, "I think you’d have to be an idiot to think we could win the House with Pelosi at the top." While acknowledging Pelosi was not the sole factor in Ossoff's loss, Vela conceded she was a contributing element. This sentiment marks a shift for Vela, who had previously supported Pelosi's leadership. Her most recent bid for leadership saw her secure the support of only about two-thirds of the Democratic caucus, her weakest performance in years.

During a private meeting, Representative Tony Cárdenas of California reportedly urged House Democrats to directly address the impact of Pelosi's standing on their candidates. A spokesperson for Pelosi declined to comment. However, Pelosi herself advised her caucus against overreacting to the loss in a traditionally Republican district, characterizing it as a setback rather than a definitive victory for Republicans.

Pelosi faces potential opposition from various factions within the party. Moderate Democrats view her as a detractor from the party's centrist appeal, while progressive Democrats perceive her as a symbol of the party establishment. Joey Aszterbaum, a former Sanders delegate, described Pelosi as a "liability" in competitive districts due to her perceived association with the party's perceived shortcomings.

Despite these criticisms, Pelosi remains a formidable fundraiser. Since assuming leadership in 2003, she has raised approximately $560 million for House Democrats, a figure unmatched by any other non-presidential candidate in history.

### The Georgia Race as a Referendum and a Sign of Hope

Nationally, the Georgia race was widely interpreted as a referendum on President Trump. Clegg suggested that the intense focus on the election may have galvanized Republican turnout. However, he also highlighted Ossoff's relatively strong performance in a district held by Republicans since 1978 as a positive indicator for Democrats heading into the 2018 elections. He pointed to Democrats outperforming expectations in several special elections throughout the year, stating, "While it would have been nice to have that trophy of Newt Gingrich’s former district, we have an energized, mobilized Democratic base that’s turned up and turned out again in four very red districts and very red states."

Both Ossoff and Handel largely avoided direct engagement with the Trump issue. Ossoff positioned himself as nonpartisan, opposing tax increases for the wealthy and a single-payer healthcare system. Liberals, such as Aszterbaum, viewed this approach as a misstep, expressing limited optimism for 2018 unless Democrats nominate more liberal candidates.

The Georgia race represented a significant financial commitment from California donors, becoming the most expensive House race in U.S. history. In April and May alone, the Ossoff campaign garnered more funding from the San Francisco Bay Area than from the entirety of Georgia. This record-breaking fundraising is unlikely to be replicated in the following year, as Democratic donors are expected to distribute their contributions across a wider array of competitive races nationwide. Clegg characterized the race as a "chew toy" for Democrats to channel their frustrations.

Donors who contributed to Ossoff with the explicit aim of opposing Trump expressed disappointment. Rosaria Haugland, a Palo Alto philanthropist who donated $2,167, lamented, "It feels like we don’t live in a democracy anymore. The whole thing is crazy." She voiced concerns that the outcome would embolden Republicans to repeal the Affordable Care Act.

Ironically, the substantial California support may have inadvertently backfired. Television advertisements highlighted Ossoff's Bay Area donations, often featuring stereotypical San Francisco caricatures. However, Aram Fischer of Indivisible San Francisco, an anti-Trump organization, stated that his members were not discouraged by the loss. He emphasized the party's growing capacity to compete in Republican-held districts and expressed confidence in securing victories in these areas in 2018.

### Looking Ahead to 2018: California's Crucial Role

As the Democratic Party shifts its focus to the 2018 elections, California is expected to be a key battleground. Seven Republican House members represent districts that voted for Hillary Clinton, making these seats critical for Democrats aiming to regain a House majority. Clegg's firm is already experiencing a surge in inquiries from potential candidates interested in running in these targeted races, even though official filing deadlines are not until March. He anticipates a competitive and potentially contentious landscape of Democratic primaries in these districts.

President Trump offered his perspective on the situation via Twitter, advising, "Democrats would do much better as a party if they got together with Republicans on Healthcare,Tax Cuts, Security. Obstruction doesn’t work!"

### Pelosi's Leadership Under Scrutiny as Democrats Eye House Majority

As Democrats **strategize to reclaim the House of Representatives**, the upcoming election looms large. House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi **projects confidence**, calling the coming year a **critical juncture** for the party's resurgence. However, a **palpable sense of urgency** is growing among some Democrats, underscored by the sentiment, "**It better be.**" This feeling has **intensified following a recent setback** in a closely watched special election in Georgia. The outcome of that race has **intensified scrutiny on Pelosi's leadership**, particularly in light of the **extensive and pointed negative advertising** that targeted her. These ads **portrayed her as an out-of-touch liberal from San Francisco**, aiming to **directly link her image** with the Democratic candidate.

The **undeniable effectiveness of this messaging** has prompted some within the party to question whether the 77-year-old Californian might be **hindering Democratic efforts** to win back the House. Adding to these concerns is Pelosi's **previous miscalculation** regarding Democrats' prospects of taking back the House in the last election. This past inaccuracy has **increased the pressure on her to deliver tangible results** this time. In a communication to her fellow House Democrats, Pelosi acknowledged the ongoing competitive nature of the House, stating, "**The House was in play before the Georgia race. The House remains in play now.**"

#### Electoral Success and the Future of Leadership

The **trajectory of Pelosi's leadership is intrinsically linked to the party's electoral performance**. "**If we take back the House in 2018 then I think she'd stay leader**," stated Rep. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.). "**If we don't, then I think it's incumbent upon her and all of us to reassess who our leadership should be.**" Pelosi has **guided House Democrats for over a decade**, navigating periods of both majority and minority status and demonstrating a **consistent capacity to manage political challenges and overcome internal dissent**. This includes a notable challenge last fall from Democratic Rep. Tim Ryan of Ohio. While Ryan's bid was unsuccessful, it garnered a **significant number of votes**, signaling some dissatisfaction with Pelosi and the **perceived inertia of her leadership team**. This situation has reportedly **impeded the advancement of promising young Democrats** within the party.

#### Renewed Divisions and the Call for New Leadership

Following Donald Trump's inauguration and the Republican push to repeal the Affordable Care Act, Democrats found common ground in opposition. This shared objective temporarily masked the **deep internal divisions and generational rifts** that have long characterized the party. However, the recent loss in Georgia, coupled with three other special House elections where Democrats failed to achieve unexpected victories, has **reignited these divisions**. For many Democrats, these electoral defeats are prompting **critical questions about the efficacy of their current leadership**, particularly Pelosi's ability to craft and articulate an economic message that can **effectively counter the Trump administration's agenda and secure electoral wins**.

"**We as Democrats have to come to terms with the fact that we lost again**," remarked Rep. Seth Moulton (D-Mass.), echoing a widespread sentiment. "**We are the party that stands up for working families and the middle class, yet many of them are not voting for us. So it's time for change. Personally I think it's time for a new generation of leadership in the party.**" Ryan concurred, noting that Pelosi's continued presence as a Republican target makes it "**much harder**" for Democrats to win in the crucial Republican-leaning districts they need to capture to regain power. To secure a House majority, Democrats must gain a net of 24 seats. "**One of the disappointing things from the last couple days is that that approach has a little bit of punch to it, it still moves voters**," Ryan added, referring to the effectiveness of the anti-Pelosi ads.

#### Pelosi's Enduring Strengths and Strategic Value

Despite these mounting concerns and the clear calls for change, the current political landscape does not indicate an **imminent leadership challenge for Pelosi**. Her **significant strengths as a highly effective fundraiser** for the Democratic party are widely recognized. Furthermore, she is regarded as a **legislative strategist with few peers on Capitol Hill** when it comes to the intricate negotiations required to maintain government operations. Even in the minority, her support is often **crucial for passing key spending bills and other vital legislation** that may face opposition from House conservatives, frequently leading Republican Speakers to seek her assistance. Pelosi continues to command **strong loyalty from a substantial bloc of House Democrats**, and her supporters have consistently dismissed any suggestions that her leadership is precarious.

"**I'm in my 10th term and I certainly have never seen someone who is smarter in the kind of backroom maneuvering that Nancy Pelosi does**," said Rep. Jan Schakowsky (D-Ill.), a staunch supporter. "**We've seen her run circles around (former House Speaker) John Boehner and get things done that nobody thought Democrats could do in the minority. That's Nancy Pelosi.**" Others have sought to downplay Pelosi's direct influence on the Georgia outcome, where Democrat Jon Ossoff ultimately lost to Republican Karen Handel by approximately five percent. The race, held in Atlanta's affluent suburbs, was notably the **most expensive House race ever**, with over $50 million spent by both campaigns. "**She's easy to demonize. Do you think people went to the polls and said, 'Oh my God, we've got to stop Nancy Pelosi?' I don't think so**," commented Rep. John Larson (D-Conn.). Instead, Larson emphasized the **critical need for Democrats to focus on developing a strong message that genuinely resonates with voters** in Republican-leaning districts. "**What was the Democratic message?**" he asked, highlighting a perceived weakness. "**It's not enough to dislike Trump.**" The path forward for Democrats, therefore, requires not only astute election strategies but also a **fundamental reevaluation of their core messages and leadership** to effectively connect with a broader spectrum of voters.

Associated Press writers Matthew Daly, Kevin Freking, Stephen Ohlemacher, and Andrew Taylor contributed to this report.

The Georgia special election, a closely watched contest, has amplified internal Democratic Party discussions regarding House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi's leadership. While an immediate challenge to her position is not anticipated, a palpable frustration has emerged among rank-and-file members, fueled by a series of significant electoral setbacks over the past half-decade. The efficacy of Republican campaign advertisements, which consistently painted Democratic candidate Jon Ossoff as aligned with Pelosi and progressive agendas, highlighted her enduring role as a liability for Democratic contenders in swing districts.

A growing contingent within the Democratic Party believes that regaining the House majority is an insurmountable task with Pelosi at the helm. This sentiment has even swayed some of her former allies. Representative Filemon Vela (D-Texas) candidly expressed this viewpoint, stating, "I think you’d have to be an idiot to think we could win the House with Pelosi at the top." He further elaborated, "Nancy Pelosi is not the only reason that Ossoff lost. But she certainly is one of the reasons."

Representative Kathleen Rice (D-N.Y.), who has previously signaled openness to a leadership challenge, views the Ossoff race results as a clear indicator for the need for new leadership. "There comes a time when every leader has to say, ‘For the good of the order and for the betterment of the party, it’s time for me to step aside.’ And I wish that that would happen right now," Rice remarked. She emphasized that her position is not personal but rooted in the urgent necessity to restore the party to majority status.

Representative Tim Ryan (D-Ohio), who mounted a previous challenge for minority leader, opted not to comment directly on Pelosi's potential resignation, maintaining his established stance. He voiced significant concern over the continued effectiveness of campaign tactics that link Democratic candidates to Pelosi. "I think it’s very concerning that that tactic still has some punch," Ryan commented. "Again, it’s part of the broader national brand that average people don’t feel connected to the Democratic Party. Walk up the street and ask 10 people what the Democrats stand for, you’ll get 10 different answers. That’s no way to build a national party."

During a private Democratic caucus meeting, Pelosi characterized Ossoff as a "young and enthusiastic candidate who attracted national support." She highlighted that Ossoff's narrow 3.8 percent loss margin was a marked improvement compared to the previous Democratic candidate's defeat in November. While acknowledging the loss as a setback, Pelosi cautioned against hasty conclusions, noting historical patterns of special election victories followed by substantial House seat losses, and vice versa. She also suggested that the recent shooting of Representative Steve Scalise (R-La.) might have influenced the outcome, as Republican groups sought to connect the incident to anti-Trump rhetoric.

Drew Hammill, a spokesperson for Pelosi, asserted, "Republican voters don’t get to select the leaders of the Democratic Party." He characterized "politics of personal destruction" as a Republican strategy dating back to Newt Gingrich, arguing that Republicans target any Democratic leader out of desperation to maintain their majority. Some Democrats expressed surprise and disappointment that Pelosi's response to attack ads was not directly addressed during the caucus meeting. Rice described the meeting as "one of the more disturbing caucus meetings that I’ve ever been in," noting the palpable silence and shock among members regarding the party's interpretation of the loss.

With former President Barack Obama no longer in the White House, Pelosi has become the primary Democratic figure targeted by Republican opposition. Her extensive tenure on the national stage has seen "San Francisco values" frequently employed as a pejorative against Democratic challengers. Despite robust enthusiasm among the Democratic base, the party confronts the reality that an anti-Trump message alone may prove insufficient to secure a majority in the upcoming 2018 elections.

Pelosi's formidable fundraising capabilities remain a significant asset. She is recognized as the most successful non-presidential political fundraiser in U.S. history, having amassed over $560 million for House Democrats since assuming leadership in 2003. However, critics contend that this fundraising success is overshadowed by persistent electoral defeats. Nevertheless, Pelosi retains considerable support among many House Democrats. Representative Keith Ellison (D-Minnesota) lauded her, stating, "I think she’s one of the best speakers ever, and I am glad to support her."

Democratic Caucus Chairman Joe Crowley (D-N.Y.) dismissed the GOP's attacks on Pelosi as "nothing new," suggesting that a comprehensive review of all campaign advertisements and issues would be necessary. He cautioned against drawing premature conclusions immediately following an election.

Several Democrats running for statewide office advocate for a strategic shift away from nationalizing every race, emphasizing the importance of focusing on local issues to foster stronger connections with constituents. They argue that while the Trump-Russia probe garners significant attention, it is not a daily concern for the average voter. Representative Tim Walz (D-Minn.), currently campaigning for governor, stated, "I think what people are looking for is that they want to believe someone knows where they are coming from. They want to believe that you’re a decent person." He also noted that his district, while potentially winnable for Democrats, would still present a closely contested race, with the outcome largely attributable to President Trump himself rather than the party's national platform.

**Georgia Special Election Intensifies Debate Over Democratic Leadership, Focusing on Pelosi**

The recent Georgia special election, a closely watched contest where Jon Ossoff narrowly lost, has intensified internal Democratic discussions about party leadership. Specifically, the spotlight has turned to House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi. This race, the most expensive in House history, saw Pelosi heavily featured in Republican advertising. This tactic has amplified ongoing conversations within the Democratic party regarding its long-standing leadership team.

President Donald Trump weighed in, suggesting that Pelosi's potential departure would benefit the Republican party. Joe Cunningham, a Democratic congressional candidate in South Carolina, publicly stated he would not support Pelosi for Speaker if elected. He advocated for a new direction to achieve electoral success.

Echoing these sentiments, Rep. Kathleen Rice (D-NY) told MSNBC that Pelosi's era of significant influence has concluded. She argued that the party's consistent electoral setbacks since 2010 demand new leadership. Rice, along with others who have voiced similar concerns, previously backed Rep. Tim Ryan's (D-OH) challenge to Pelosi. Ryan indicated to MSNBC that Pelosi's continued leadership would hinder efforts to regain control of the House. He cited the "toxic Democratic brand" that candidates are forced to confront and the persistent effectiveness of attack ads linking them to her.

Ryan's challenge last year represented the most substantial opposition to Pelosi's leadership. However, she ultimately secured the support of most of her caucus. Pelosi is recognized as a highly effective Democratic fundraiser and a skilled legislative strategist. She also maintains a strong base of support. Her office reports substantial fundraising achievements. These include raising $568 million for Democrats since she joined leadership in 2002. In the most recent election cycle alone, $142 million was raised. Despite these recent calls for change, a private meeting indicated no immediate challenges to the current leadership structure.

Pelosi's association has proven to be a significant liability for Democrats in swing districts. Republican Karen Handel, who defeated Ossoff, even used a Spanish-language advertisement to criticize Pelosi. An independent group also ran an ad featuring San Franciscans thanking Ossoff. Unlike Speaker Paul Ryan, who actively campaigned for Republican candidates, Pelosi's connection is seen as detrimental in Republican-leaning areas. The Congressional Leadership Fund, a GOP super PAC, identified Pelosi as an effective attack message in the Georgia race. This strategy aimed to undermine Ossoff's moderate image. Former GOP House Speaker Newt Gingrich remarked that Republicans "know exactly how to run against a Nancy Pelosi led party." He expressed a desire for her to remain leader for another decade.

query: What key promises did President Trump make during his address to Congress?

documents.text: President Trump promised to lower taxes, combat terrorism and replace the Affordable Care Act in a speech to a joint session of Congress, Feb. 28. Here are key moments from that speech. (Sarah Parnass/The Washington Post) President Trump delivered a strong speech to a joint session of Congress on Tuesday night, an address sure to embolden both the chief executive and congressional Republicans who support him. I watched, tweeted and took some notes about the best and the worst of the night. My picks are below. Winners * Donald Trump: This was the best “big” speech he has given as president. It may well have been the best speech Trump has given since he entered politics way back in June 2015. Trump didn't walk away from his decidedly dark vision of the current state of the country, but his overall tone was more conciliatory and optimistic than I've ever heard him. Trump hit a few very nice notes: His condemnation of threats against Jewish community centers at the start of the speech was a very nice grace note, and his honoring of the widow of the Navy SEAL killed in the recent Yemen raid was a remarkably powerful moment. Carryn Owens, the widow of William "Ryan" Owens, a Navy SEAL killed in the Trump administration's first counterterrorism operation, was honored by President Trump during his address to a joint session of Congress. Trump called Owens a "warrior and a hero." (The Washington Post) Critics will rightly point out that several of Trump's claims — about the rising violence in America, for example — missed the factual mark by a wide margin. And, at times, Trump seemed to be on the verge of returning to his confrontational self — particularly when discussing immigration and the border wall. But, top to bottom, Trump delivered both a forceful defense of his nationalist worldview — “My job is not to represent the world. My job is to represent the United States of America,” he said at one point — and a proof point that he can be, dare I say it, presidential when the moment demands it. 1 of 45 Full Screen Autoplay Close Skip Ad × The scene as President Trump delivers his first address to a joint session of Congress View Photos The president returned to familiar themes from his campaign in his speech to Congress, promising to reduce regulations and taxes, combat terrorism, crack down on illegal immigration and replace the Affordable Care Act. Caption The president returned to familiar themes from his campaign in his speech to Congress, promising to reduce regulations and taxes, combat terrorism, crack down on illegal immigration and replace the Affordable Care Act. Feb. 28, 2017 Vice President Pence and Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell (R-Ky.), behind Pence, shake hands with attendees as they walk into the House Chamber for the president’s speech to a joint session of Congress in Washington. Melina Mara/The Washington Post Buy Photo Wait 1 second to continue. * Congressional Republicans: Anyone who tells you House Speaker Paul D. Ryan (R-Wis.) and Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell (R-Ky.) weren't a little bit worried about how Trump would do on Tuesday night — and what that would mean for his ability to rally Republicans going forward — is lying to you. Trump's speech will go a long way to quieting the nerves of congressional Republicans and convincing them that Trump might just be capable of being the president they desperately hope he can be. His address will serve as a validation for the likes of Ryan and McConnell, who have steadfastly supported Trump throughout the rough seas of his first month in office. *Polarization: If you needed to understand just how polarized our politics are, the repeated shots of Republicans rising to cheer Trump while Democrats sat on their hands would do it. The theater of the State of the Union is always a bit overdone but what was clear from the start on Tuesday night was that Republicans were bound and determined to cheer for virtually everything Trump said and that Democrats were bound and determined to do the exact opposite. Just watch this clip. * Stephen K. Bannon and Ivanka Trump: If you are looking for the two biggest influencers in terms of what Trump said and how he said it, look no further than Bannon, the president's chief strategist, and Ivanka Trump, the president's elder daughter. The unapologetic nationalism and indictment of the political system was pure Bannon. The push for paid family leave and focus on education was all Ivanka Trump. If you polished and repackaged the "deconstruction of the admin. state" for a nat'l TV audience, you'd get this speech. Bannonism. — Robert Costa (@costareports) March 1, 2017 Another clear influence on this speech: Ivanka. I've seen her talk through these issues with Trump. She pushes him. He listens. — Robert Costa (@costareports) March 1, 2017 * Joe Scarborough and Elijah E. Cummings: Symbolism can be empty. But it can be important too. Kudos to Scarborough, a conservative Republican House member turned cable news host, and Cummings, a prominent Democratic Maryland congressman, for sitting together to show that just because we disagree doesn't mean we have to be disagreeable. * Joe Biden: Sometimes you don't know what you've got until it's gone. And the State of the Union speech — which this was in all but name — was always a moment for Biden (and his face of a thousand expressions) to shine. Without Biden to watch as the president delivered his speech, the night felt slightly less momentous. Here's one for the road, Diamond Joe: * C-SPAN's Howard Mortman: I loved Howard before this. And my love for C-SPAN is also well known. But, this image, captured by Howard, of Trump en route to the speech, made me love him even more. Losers * The devilish details of replacing the Affordable Care Act: In the run-up to the Trump speech, Republican members of Congress voiced hopes that Trump might give them guidance about how he'd like to replace the Affordable Care Act. What Trump did talk about — lowering the price of prescription drugs, keeping the preexisting conditions provision — are widely popular idea. The issue, of course, is how to pay for all of this if you strip out the rest of the elements of the health law. * People rooting for Trump's imminent demise: He's not going anywhere, folks. And that speech suggests he might have more upside than almost anyone thought. This speech is clearest example Trump is beginning to own and understand the powers of the office — Zeke Miller (@ZekeJMiller) March 1, 2017 * “Aisle hogs”: I H-A-T-E the annual stories of the people — Eliot L. Engel (D) of New York and Sheila Jackson Lee (D) of Texas are two of the most prominent — who stake out spots on the aisle to ensure they shake hands with the various luminaries, including the president. There was a noticeable lack of crowding along the aisles this time around. Possible we can drop this as a story in the future? Okay, it's agreed! * Georgetown basketball: I needed a win — just one — to keep me going through a long night ahead. My alma mater couldn't get one — again. Three turnovers in the last two minutes of the game against Seton Hall and a terrible final attempt to tie the game. I have but one question to ask: Come on, man. What are we even doing out here, man?

President Trump's address to Congress, intended to foster bipartisan unity, largely avoided the specific policy details needed to enact his ambitious campaign promises. The hour-long speech marked a departure from his typical online posts and rally speeches, adopting a more measured and hopeful tone. While he mostly read from a teleprompter, Trump aimed to "deliver a message of unity and strength." However, these conciliatory remarks lacked concrete, bipartisan policy proposals.

### Policy Ambiguities and Contrasts

Trump reiterated his commitment to repealing the Affordable Care Act and significantly reducing regulations at agencies like the Food and Drug Administration. Despite optimistically stating that "dying industries will come roaring back to life," he did not outline specific strategies to aid the blue-collar workers who had supported him. Furthermore, Trump pledged to "promote clean air and clear water," a statement that sharply contrasted with his administration's proposal to cut the Environmental Protection Agency's budget by 25%. He also declared, "the time for trivial fights is behind us," a sentiment that clashed with his presidency's early tendency for frequent online disputes.

### The Commencement of Governance

Despite the general absence of specific policy plans, this address effectively signaled the true start of the Trump presidency. It marked the beginning of a crucial period requiring direct engagement with Congress, moving beyond reliance on executive orders that bypass legislative input and oversight. The president now faces the necessity to build consensus on complex societal issues, such as creating a fair, affordable, and equitable healthcare system for over 320 million Americans, rather than continuing to rely on reheated campaign slogans and the belittling of rivals to enthusiastic supporters.

Sarah Anderson, a political science professor at UC Santa Barbara, noted, "What I heard was more of a recognition that talking to Congress was really different from talking at a rally. A recognition that he’s bargaining with Congress and the courts. That’s what he’s finding out right now — that this isn’t an authoritarian regime. There are checks and balances." This observation highlights the shift from campaign rhetoric to the realities of governing.

### The Demands of Governing

The time has come for President Trump to show his ability to achieve real results. By this point in his term, his predecessor, Barack Obama, had already passed the substantial $831 billion stimulus package and signed the landmark Lily Ledbetter Fair Pay Act into law. These were significant legislative achievements that directly impacted the nation's economy and workers' rights.

President Trump is now showing early signs of realizing that governing is considerably more demanding and complex than campaigning. This week, he told a gathering of the nation's governors that healthcare is "an unbelievably complex subject. Nobody knew that healthcare could be so complicated." In reality, the complexity of healthcare has been widely understood by most informed people for a long time. Since the Great Depression, only two presidents, Lyndon B. Johnson and Barack Obama, have managed to broadly expand healthcare coverage nationwide. Similar to them, President Trump benefits from a supportive Congress. However, despite seven years of consistent calls to repeal the Affordable Care Act, the Republican party remains notably divided on a workable replacement strategy, underscoring the inherent difficulties in reaching agreement on such a critical issue.

Chris Hoene, executive director of the nonpartisan California Budget and Policy Center in Sacramento, commented, "The speech was mellow and optimistic, without a lot of specifics. The rhetoric about helping low- and middle-income people is optimistic, but the policy proposals we expect to come down are not aimed at helping them." Hoene pointed to the potential negative impact on "the 5 million Californians who would lose their health care (should the Affordable Care Act be repealed) or all the safety net programs that would be cut" to fund Trump's proposed $54 billion increase in defense spending. This perspective highlights a significant concern about the gap between optimistic talk and potentially harmful policy outcomes for vulnerable groups.

### Navigating a New Arena

Dealing with the intricate dynamics of a co-equal branch of government is unfamiliar territory for President Trump. He spent most of his adult life working in a family business he inherited, a professional setting where he was used to operating without the direct scrutiny of adversarial boards or the constant threat of termination. His established business negotiation style, as famously described in his book "The Art of the Deal," often involved aggressive opening offers to gain strategic advantage. This approach may not work well or produce the desired results in the complex and nuanced political arena. "Once he got into office he’s facing things that a CEO of a company like his wouldn’t face," Anderson said, emphasizing the fundamental differences in accountability and operational limits between the corporate world and public service.

### Avenues for Collaboration: Infrastructure

Certain parts of his address, however, did offer potential paths for bipartisan cooperation and progress. President Trump announced his administration's plans to formally ask Congress for a substantial $1 trillion infrastructure bill, aiming to revitalize the nation's aging roads and bridges. Democrats have generally shown openness to such infrastructure initiatives, though they might object to his proposed funding methods, especially those relying on "both public and private capital." Trump stated that this ambitious effort "will be guided by two core principles: Buy American, and hire American"—a distinctly populist message likely to appeal to a wide range of Democrats, particularly those representing constituencies in the historically industrial Rust Belt states.

Carah Ong Whaley, co-author of "American Government: Roots and Reform" and a political science lecturer at the University of Virginia, stated, "The clearest outreach was on infrastructure. For the Democrats who do want to address infrastructure, will this be an area will they work on with him or will they prevent them from working across lines?" This question highlights the critical point at which bipartisan cooperation on infrastructure could either succeed or fail, depending on the political will and strategic choices made by both parties.

### Persistent Divides: Immigration

While President Trump declared on Tuesday that "every problem can be solved. And every hurting family can find healing, and hope," his administration's rhetoric and actions continued to target immigrants. He dedicated a significant portion of his speech, notably without providing specific statistical proof, to suggesting a tendency toward crime among immigrant populations. To advance this agenda, he announced the creation of a new office within the Department of Homeland Security, called Voice (Victims of Immigration Crime Engagement), with the stated goal of helping "American victims." On a night when President Trump ostensibly tried to adopt a more conciliatory and unifying tone toward certain groups of Americans who felt left out, immigrants did not receive the same consideration or a similar gesture of goodwill. This selective approach highlights a persistent division in his administration's messaging and policy priorities.

President Donald Trump's recent address to Congress presented a notable departure from his characteristic speaking style, omitting several familiar themes and pronouncements. This address, delivered to a joint session of Congress, was a more subdued rendition of his typically boisterous and unpredictable persona. Several hallmarks of a Trump speech were conspicuously absent, including discussions of crowd sizes, unsubstantiated claims of voter fraud, and his frequent assertions of unfair treatment by the media. Instead, the president offered adjustments to some of his more contentious stances. For instance, he declared, "we strongly support NATO," a significant shift from his recent characterization of the alliance as "obsolete."

Here are several key topics and sentiments that President Trump did not address in his speech:

**A Positive Stance Towards Russia:** Amidst ongoing investigations into potential ties between his campaign and Russian officials, Trump avoided his usual emphasis on fostering a strong relationship with Russia, which he has often presented as beneficial for America. The country was not mentioned by name throughout the entire address. While he alluded to a desire for new international relationships by stating America is willing to "find new friends, and to forge new partnerships, where shared interests align," the overall tone suggested a distancing from Russia, a nation that has significantly shaped his presidency thus far.

**Criticism of the Press:** Trump, who has frequently labeled the media as "dishonest," particularly targeting CNN as the "Clinton News Network" and The New York Times as "failing," and even calling the press the "enemy of the people," was notably silent on this front. The intense animosity he typically displays towards journalists, a sentiment amplified by his chief strategist Steve Bannon's portrayal of the media as the "opposition party," was absent. Instead of expressing his usual grievances about being treated "very, very unfairly," Trump appeared to direct his frustrations inward earlier in the day, attributing communication failures to his own team. This strategic shift, possibly recognizing that such attacks might not resonate even with his base in a formal congressional setting, was evident.

**Wars in Iraq or Afghanistan:** For the first time since George W. Bush's initial address in 2001, neither Iraq nor Afghanistan, countries where the United States has been involved in conflicts this century, were mentioned. Even President Obama included these conflicts in his 2009 address concerning healthcare reform. Syria, a nation whose civil war and the fight against ISIS were central to Trump's campaign, was also omitted. While he briefly stated his intention to "demolish and destroy ISIS," this mention was fleeting. The most poignant moment of the evening, honoring Carryn Owens, the widow of Navy SEAL William "Ryan" Owens who died in a raid in Yemen, served as the sole reference to American troops engaged in combat.

**Expanding Healthcare Access:** Candidate Trump had pledged to "broaden health care access" following the repeal and replacement of Obamacare. Although he detailed Speaker Paul Ryan's five-point plan, much to the satisfaction of House Republicans seeking specifics, he omitted any mention of concrete steps to increase healthcare accessibility for Americans.

**Deporting Undocumented Immigrants:** In a pre-speech meeting with television anchors, Trump hinted at a potential softening of his strict immigration policies, reportedly suggesting that the time was opportune for an immigration bill contingent on mutual compromise. He indicated a willingness to allow non-violent undocumented immigrants to remain in the United States without fear of deportation, a departure from his campaign promise to deport the nation's 11 million undocumented immigrants. While his speech did not explicitly mention compromise, he did express belief in the possibility of "real and positive immigration reform," envisioning an effort to "improve jobs and wages for Americans, to strengthen our nation’s security, and to restore respect for our laws."

**Overturning Roe v. Wade and State Abortion Laws:** Abortion was not a topic of discussion during Trump's address. He also avoided mentioning same-sex marriage. Even when commending conservative Justice Antonin Scalia and his Supreme Court nominee Neil Gorsuch, the president refrained from engaging in cultural debates. He did not reference the executive order rolling back protections for transgender students or any plans to defund Planned Parenthood, a common Republican platform. By largely sidestepping these contentious issues, which have historically divided Congress and the public, Trump appeared to adhere to his opening promise of delivering "a message of unity."

President Trump's address to a joint session of Congress on Tuesday evening, his most significant public appearance since his inauguration, offered several key takeaways for observers, particularly concerning his approach to governance and national identity.

**A Conventional Approach for the GOP**

While President Trump won his election by defying political norms, his Tuesday address was notably restrained. He avoided attacks on the media, flamboyant claims, and personal insults, largely sticking to his prepared remarks. This conventional approach was a welcome relief for Republican lawmakers, including House Speaker Paul Ryan, who have urged Trump to temper his self-defeating impulses and focus on a clear message. Although the speech offered few specific policy details, it outlined clear objectives that could energize the Republican base, such as the nomination of Neil Gorsuch to the Supreme Court, an instance where he adhered to traditional political conduct.

Trump's tribute to Carryn Owens, the widow of a Navy SEAL killed in Yemen, was particularly impactful. This emotional high point, often sought by presidents in such addresses, drew praise across the political spectrum. Commentator Van Jones suggested that if Trump can replicate such moments, he could remain in office for eight years, a sentiment echoed by some political strategists.

**From "Carnage" to a "New Chapter"**

In contrast to his inaugural address, which described "American carnage" and a landscape of "rusted out factories," Trump's Tuesday speech presented a more optimistic outlook. He declared that "a new chapter of American greatness is now beginning" and encouraged the audience to "think of the marvels we can achieve if we simply set free the dreams of our people." This shift in tone was noted by analysts at the Brookings Institution.

While Trump did not solely rely on unalloyed optimism, addressing concerns about crime rates and immigration enforcement, his overarching message was one of hope and resolution: "Everything that is broken in our country can be fixed. Every problem can be solved. And every hurting family can find healing, and hope." This more harmonious tone, even if temporary, could make him more appealing to centrist and independent voters, according to polling data analyzed by Gallup.

**An American Nationalist Perspective**

Unlike recent presidents who have positioned themselves as global leaders, Trump emphasized his role as the representative of the United States. "My job is not to represent the world," he stated. "My job is to represent the United States of America." This sentiment, reflecting the influence of his chief strategist Stephen Bannon, highlights a belief in the nation-state as a defense against globalism and corporatism, a perspective often discussed by foreign policy experts at the Council on Foreign Relations. Trump affirmed respect for international institutions like NATO and the UN, but also for "the sovereign rights of nations."

Regarding the fight against ISIS, Trump pledged to work with allies in the Muslim world, a commitment that received cautious approval from some Middle Eastern diplomats. He also reiterated his commitment to national security through "improved vetting procedures" to "keep our nation safe — and to keep out those who would do us harm," a stance consistent with his executive order on immigration and refugees, as detailed by the Department of Homeland Security.

Despite criticisms that he has exacerbated racial and religious divisions, Trump made a clear effort to counter these perceptions. He condemned anti-Semitic threats and a hate crime in Kansas City that resulted in the death of one Indian man and injuries to another, actions that were acknowledged by civil rights organizations like the Southern Poverty Law Center.

**A Vision for Healthcare**

Trump reiterated his goal to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act (ACA), drawing some negative reactions from Democratic lawmakers, including Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer. He expressed a desire for new laws that would "expand choice, increase access, lower costs and, at the same time, provide better healthcare." He criticized the ACA's "mandate" but assured that individuals with pre-existing conditions would retain coverage and that no one would be excluded from Medicaid, a promise that drew scrutiny from healthcare policy experts at the Kaiser Family Foundation.

However, most experts, including those at the Congressional Budget Office, believe achieving such an ideal outcome is improbable, especially in a divided Congress.

**Improved Fact-Checking**

Critics have often pointed to President Trump's tendency to misrepresent facts. For instance, he recently claimed the largest Electoral College victory since Ronald Reagan, a statement later challenged by fact-checkers at PolitiFact. On Tuesday, however, there was a noticeable improvement in his factual accuracy. His comments on the 2015 murder rate and violent crime in Chicago were accurate, unlike previous statements, a point highlighted by the FBI's Uniform Crime Reporting program. He also cited a 116 percent premium increase in Arizona for ACA plans, a figure supported by a Department of Health and Human Services report.

While some interpretations of his remarks on immigration's economic impact or his credit for job announcements may be debated, Trump largely avoided factual missteps that could be used against him. As Glenn Thrush of The New York Times noted, the speech appeared to have undergone nominal fact-checking, a process that may have been influenced by advice from communications director Sean Spicer.

Washington (CNN) President Donald Trump reached for poetry and conjured a vision of common national purpose Tuesday during his first address to Congress, shifting his tone from the dark, searing approach of his previous big speeches to the nation. Trump adopted a statesmanlike cadence, hitting notes of inspiration. For once, this most unorthodox of politicians struck a conventional presidential posture as he sought to stabilize his administration after a tumultuous five weeks in office. Though his language was more lofty and unifying than normal, Trump gave little quarter on the substance of his policies on issues ranging from trade, defense, immigration and counterterrorism. The result was a populist, nationalistic prescription that he said would yield "a new chapter of American greatness." "From now on, America will be empowered by our aspirations, not burdened by our fears, inspired by the future, not bound by failures of the past, and guided by a vision, not blinded by our doubts," Trump said, from the Speaker's rostrum in the House of Representatives. During a vitriolic campaign and a raucous start to his term, Trump has done little to reach beyond his base of deeply committed voters who revile the kind of political elites that the President was staring down as he spoke on Tuesday. But, beset by the lowest approval ratings of any new commander-in-chief of modern times, Trump made a palpable effort to court voters who didn't support him with an offer to lay down the battles of the past. In fact, his address ticked almost all the boxes of a traditional State of the Union style appearance. 'Embrace this renewal' "I am asking all citizens to embrace this renewal of the American spirit. I am asking all members of Congress to join me in dreaming big, and bold, and daring things for our country," Trump said. "I am asking everyone watching tonight to seize this moment. Believe in yourselves. Believe in your future. And believe, once more, in America." Hide Caption 22 of 22 It was an uplifting and unifying message that many Americans have rarely heard from Trump, who argued "the time for trivial fights is behind us." While Trump is not solely responsible for the coarsening of political life, his brash, Twitter-fueled approach has rocked the nation's politics. The question now is whether the President was previewing a new, more sober political persona or whether he will return to his old habits. The change in his tone was evident from the first moments of his speech when he condemned the recent spate of threats against Jewish community centers, vandalism at Jewish cemeteries and the shooting of two Indian men in Kansas. He said the violence was a reminder that "while we may be a nation divided on policies, we are a country that stands united in condemning hate and evil in all its very ugly forms." The comment followed heavy criticism of Trump for not addressing such violence. The new President entered the House chamber to thunderous applause and spoke of the "renewal of the American spirit." A senior White House official said Trump wrote the speech himself with input from almost every member of his presidential team of advisers. In the emotional high point of the speech, Trump turned to the first lady's box and acknowledged Carryn Owens, the widow of a US Navy Special operator, William "Ryan" Owens, who was killed in an anti-terror raid in Yemen in the first major military engagement of the new administration. "Ryan died as he lived: a warrior, and a hero -- battling against terrorism and securing our nation," Trump said as the House floor erupted in a prolonged standing ovation. Owens, with tears streaming down her face, looked to the Heavens and joined in the applause. Less explosive style welcomed His less explosive presentation style was welcomed by many lawmakers. JUST WATCHED McConnell: American's expect Obamacare repeal Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH McConnell: American's expect Obamacare repeal 01:10 "Donald Trump did indeed become presidential tonight, and I think we'll see that reflected in a higher approval rating," Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell, R-Kentucky, said on CNN after the speech. "The Donald Trump I heard tonight was a lot more focused and disciplined and subdued, and it was a lot more uneventful in a good way," moderate House Republican Charlie Dent told CNN's Tom LoBianco. "There were not a lot of distractions tonight, this speech was much better than the inaugural speech." North Dakota Democratic Sen. Heidi Heitkamp, who is up for re-election next year in a state that Trump carried, said the President delivered a "very good speech." "It was delivered with a sense of 'this is who I am, this is what I want to accomplish' and I think the goals are great," she said. "How we get there is the $10,000 question." Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer said on CNN that Trump's "speeches and the realities are very, very far apart." "Until his reality catches up with his speeches, he's got big trouble," Schumer said. Though his rhetoric was soaring, Trump still struck hardline notes. He overruled national security adviser H.R. McMaster, according to a senior administration official, to warn of "radical Islamic terrorism." Hitting themes familiar from his campaign, Trump vowed to restore "integrity and the rule of law to our borders." "We will soon begin the construction of a great, great wall along our southern border," Trump said, drawing Republican cheers even as he didn't mention his earlier promise that Mexico would pay for construction. "As we speak tonight, we are removing gang members, drug dealers, and criminals that threaten our communities and prey on our very innocent citizens. Bad ones are going out as I speak, and as I promised throughout the campaign" Sending shockwaves through Washington While such language could please conservatives, Trump sent shockwaves through Washington earlier Tuesday by telling reporters he wants to pass an immigration reform bill that could grant legal status to millions of undocumented immigrants living in the US. "The time is right for an immigration bill as long as there is compromise on both sides," Trump said at the White House. But he did not provide further clarity on that position during his address. So far there is little sign that the new President's legislative agenda, which includes repealing and replacing Obamacare, a big tax overhaul, and a $1 trillion infrastructure program , is anywhere near coming to fruition. That explains why he devoted a considerable portion of the address to touting his achievements so far. He argued that his election alone had convinced big firms like Ford, Sprint, SoftBank and Intel to invest billions of job-creating dollars in the US. He noted that stocks have put on $3 trillion in value since his election and claimed to have saved hundreds of millions of taxpayer dollars, including on the new F-35 jet fighter. Trump said he also kept his word by cutting government regulations, clearing the way for the Keystone and Dakota Access pipelines and pulling out of the Trans Pacific Partnership trade deal. The President also laid down clear principles for the repeal and replacement of Obamacare, a key policy goal that is threatening to become overwhelmed by the complications of writing health policy. A new system, he said, must retain coverage for Americans with pre-existing conditions, should offer plans backed by tax credits and expanded health savings accounts and should preserve Medicaid expansion in the states. Trump also vowed to bring down the high price of drugs "immediately." 'Obamacare is collapsing' "Obamacare is collapsing -- and we must act decisively to protect all Americans," he said, "Action is not a choice -- it is a necessity." House Democratic Leader Nancy Pelosi, who was instrumental in passing the law, shook her head as Trump condemned it. The President also signaled action on another key piece of his agenda -- tax reform, promising "massive" relief for the middle classes and cuts in corporate tax. Yet Trump also pushed for his $1 trillion infrastructure plan and spoke of his effort to boost military spending. Given that he has also said he will protect entitlements, questions will be asked about how the administration can finance its ambitious plans. Trump's speech was closely watched around the world, given that his pronouncements on foreign policy have caused alarm and confusion. There were, however, few details on national security policy to clear up misconceptions. Trump recommitted himself to a strong "America First" foreign policy, but also backed NATO -- as long as its members pay their dues -- while bemoaning trillions of dollars the US has spent in foreign wars abroad. "My job is not to represent the world. My job is to represent the United States of America," said Trump. "But we know that America is better off when there is less conflict, not more. We must learn from the mistakes of the past. We have seen the war and the destruction that have ravaged and raged throughout the world." Despite some positive reviews for Trump in the room on Tuesday, the official Democratic response by former Kentucky Gov. Steven Beshear accused the President of deserting the working people who voted for him by picking a cabinet of millionaires and billionaires. "That's not being our champion. That's being Wall Street's champion," Beshear said. "Real leaders don't spread derision and division.
