Real leaders strengthen, they unify, they partner, and they offer real solutions instead of ultimatums and blame," said Beshear, accusing Trump of waging war on refugees and immigrants and endangering US security by reaching out to Russia.

During President Donald Trump's February 28, 2017, address to Congress, Democratic lawmakers subtly signaled their disagreement with his policies and rhetoric through a series of non-verbal cues. While consciously avoiding overt protests like the "pussyhats" seen at the Women's March, their dissent was evident in quiet actions.

Democrats conveyed their disapproval through gestures such as turning thumbs down and maintaining neutral expressions. They also sat with their hands folded. A shared moment of reaction occurred when Democrats collectively laughed after Trump vowed to "drain the swamp." Conversely, audible groans came from their side of the chamber following his announcement of a new office to aid victims of crimes committed by immigrants.

As President Trump entered the House chamber, several Democrats deliberately chose not to shake his hand. Empty seats on the Democratic side also visually represented their opposition. For instance, Rep. Eliot Engel, a New York Democrat, opted not to take his usual center aisle seat, a spot he had held for nearly thirty years during presidential addresses.

Democratic women in the House collectively wore white, a color historically associated with the suffragist movement, to highlight their commitment to women's issues. This symbolic clothing choice differed from the more direct "pussyhats." House Democratic leader Nancy Pelosi tweeted that those wearing white supported women's rights, "in spite of a @POTUS who doesn't!"

Many Democratic senators and House members also strategically used their guests in the galleries for a political statement. These guests often included individuals directly affected by Trump's immigration policies or those dependent on the Affordable Care Act, a healthcare program President Trump opposed. President Trump, while appearing restrained, subtly criticized Democrats by gesturing toward them when he stated, "the time for trivial fights is behind us."

Donald Trump's recent speech has been widely hailed as his most effective to date, marking a notable shift towards a more unifying and conventional tone. For some time, it remained a puzzle why the President didn't adopt simpler strategies to project a more inclusive and normal image. However, in this address, he successfully implemented such measures.

### A Shift in Tone and Message

The speech featured a clear denouncement of bigotry, an optimistic outlook, and an invocation of the American narrative. Trump also strategically highlighted sympathetic individuals in the audience and concluded with the powerful statement that "God created us equal." These elements are not inherently difficult to incorporate and carry no cost for Trump. Essentially, he managed to deliver his core message in a manner more befitting the presidency.

A crucial question now arises: if Trump can moderate his approach and allow the constant controversy to subside, will his policies genuinely resonate across partisan lines as one might expect? It was indeed striking to witness Republicans enthusiastically supporting passages on protectionism and infrastructure spending, a testament to the transformative effect Trump has had on the party.

### Policy Ambiguities and Key Observations

Despite the speech's positive reception, uncertainties remain regarding Trump's precise policy stances. He appeared to cautiously approach endorsing a border adjustment tax but ultimately stopped short of a full commitment. Similarly, while he mentioned a $1 trillion figure for infrastructure, he did not explicitly confirm it would entail increased spending. His remarks on replacing the Affordable Care Act were also vague. An endorsement of a tax credit, while noted, is reportedly unlikely to significantly influence the debate in Congress.

Further observations from the speech include:

*   **Over-promising:** The tendency to over-promise was particularly pronounced, even within a context where presidential addresses commonly feature such rhetoric.
*   **Limited Social Conservatism:** Beyond the nomination of Gorsuch, there was a distinct absence of social conservative themes in the speech.
*   **Focus on Immigration:** While Trump and his speechwriters emphasized immigration, its centrality as a primary issue was questioned, even by those who identify as immigration hawks.
*   **Economic Messaging:** The political landscape was altered by a Republican president discussing the restoration of American jobs, prompting Democrats to remain largely silent.
*   **Deregulation:** While not a staunch fiscal conservative, Trump appears to be a comprehensive deregulator, and his remarks on FDA reform were particularly well-received.

Ultimately, Trump likely benefited from this speech. However, his sustained success will depend on his ability to maintain this more measured tone and demonstrate tangible progress on his stated objectives.

query: What significant milestone could Rashida Tlaib achieve if she wins the election in November?

documents.text: Rashida Tlaib Rashida Tlaib could become one of the nation’s first Muslim women in Congress if she wins in November. Former Michigan state legislator Rashida Tlaib won the Democratic primary on Tuesday for a U.S. House seat in the state’s 13th Congressional District. She beat out five other Democratic candidates seeking to fill the seat of Rep. John Conyers, who resigned in December amid allegations of sexual harassment. As the Democratic nominee in a district that has historically voted Democratic ― and with no Republican challengers ― Tlaib is all but guaranteed a seat in Congress in November’s general election. If she wins the seat, she could be one of the nation’s first Muslim women in Congress ― she may be joined in the historic feat by one or more other candidates also running in 2018. Tlaib will, however, be the only one to claim the status of the first Palestinian-American U.S. congresswoman. Breaking barriers will be nothing new to the Detroit-born mother of two, who already made history in 2008 as the first Muslim woman to serve in the Michigan Legislature. “People think it’s corny ― I do want to change the world, and I want people like me to have a seat at the table,” the daughter of Palestinian immigrants told HuffPost last month. Tlaib is part of a record wave of Muslim candidates running for office in 2018, alongside unprecedented ﻿numbers of women running since Donald Trump’s election ― and part of a movement of progressive candidates seeking to push the Democratic Party establishment further left. Rashida Tlaib Rashida Tlaib beat out five other﻿ Democratic candidates seeking to fill the seat of Rep. John Conyers, who resigned amid allegations of sexual harassment. Tlaib, who grew up the eldest of 14 children, is running on a progressive platform, including support of Medicare for All and a $15 per hour minimum wage. She was endorsed by the Justice Democrats ― the progressive political group that backed Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez in New York in her now-famous upset win. Like Ocasio-Cortez, Tlaib doesn’t accept money from corporate PACs, and she told HuffPost she’s “going to abolish ICE” ― a leftist stance that has gained traction among some leading Democrats since Ocasio-Cortez ran on that idea. Running in Michigan ― a state with a significant Muslim and Arab-American community ― Tlaib’s candidacy comes at a particularly trying time for Muslim Americans. Over the past couple years, the Muslim community in the United States has faced a spike in hate crimes amid Trump’s campaign, Islamophobic rhetoric and policies from the White House, and a Trump administration ﻿travel ban upheld by the Supreme Court that targets mostly Muslim-majority countries. “People are ready for someone like me,” Tlaib said.

The Ohio 12th District special election results have revealed a surprisingly competitive contest, with Republicans clinging to a slim lead over Democrats. This race, initially anticipated as a straightforward Republican victory, has instead underscored significant Democratic energy as the party looks toward the November midterm elections.

As of late Tuesday, Republican state Sen. Troy Balderson held a narrow advantage over Democrat Danny O’Connor, with the margin less than one percent. With over 3,300 provisional ballots still to be tallied, the final outcome remains undecided and could take several days to confirm. While Republicans may have narrowly averted a notable electoral setback, the closeness of this election serves as a clear warning for the party as it seeks to maintain its 23-seat majority in the House.

Concurrently, Tuesday's elections also featured a robust performance by female candidates across various contests, reinforcing the ongoing narrative of 2018 potentially being the "Year of the Woman." The influence of President Trump's endorsements, alongside those from progressive figures such as Sen. Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, proved to be varied and uncertain in their impact.

Here are the key insights from Tuesday's elections:

**GOP Holds a Lead, But Democrats Show Encouraging Momentum**

Despite trailing, Democrat Danny O’Connor's strong showing demonstrates the formidable nature of the Democratic challenge. They successfully transformed a district that has been Republican-held for decades into a closely contested race, signaling potential difficulties for President Trump with suburban voters in Ohio's 12th District. O’Connor's campaign benefited from his youth, energetic outreach, and a strategy focused on appealing to moderate voters without directly confronting Trump. This approach bears resemblance to the successful tactics employed by Rep. Conor Lamb in a Pennsylvania special election earlier this year.

Republicans attempted to leverage House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi as a central campaign theme, associating O’Connor with her. However, O’Connor largely managed to sidestep these attacks and keep the race tight, even after acknowledging in an MSNBC interview his eventual support for his party's leadership.

**Suburban Districts Present a Growing Challenge for the GOP**

The Ohio race highlights a mounting concern for Republicans: their standing in suburban districts. Democrats are actively targeting well-educated suburban voters, particularly women, who have historically leaned Republican but are now expressing dissatisfaction with the current administration and seeking congressional representation that will provide a check on presidential power.

There are 68 other Republican-held seats considered more favorable to Democrats than Ohio's 12th District. Notably, President Trump secured 45 of these seats with a smaller margin than he did in Ohio. An additional 23 seats are held by Republicans but were won by Hillary Clinton in the 2016 presidential election. Democrats are concentrating their efforts on many of these districts, often situated in suburbs surrounding major cities, particularly in the Midwest and Rust Belt, as they strategize to regain control of the House.

**Trump's Electoral Impact Remains a Mixed Factor**

President Trump quickly claimed credit for Balderson's narrow lead following a last-minute campaign rally. While his appearances can galvanize the Republican base, their effectiveness in attracting moderate and suburban voters remains a subject of debate. Furthermore, Trump's inclination to endorse more ideologically extreme candidates, sometimes even over the preferences of fellow Republicans, raises questions about their general election viability.

In Michigan, Trump's endorsement of military veteran John James in a Senate primary proved successful, with James winning his race. James offers a compelling narrative as a Black Republican candidate in a Senate currently lacking significant Black representation from the GOP.

However, Trump's endorsement of conservative figure Kris Kobach in Kansas's gubernatorial primary over incumbent Gov. Jeff Colyer yielded a different outcome. While that race was too close to call, Kobach could face a significant challenge in the general election, even in a traditionally Republican state.

**The Influence of Sanders and Ocasio-Cortez is Still Developing**

The primary campaigns of Sen. Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez garnered considerable attention. However, their impact on general election results is not yet definitively established. In Michigan, their preferred gubernatorial candidate, Abdul El-Sayed, lost to Gretchen Whitmer. Similarly, in Missouri's 1st District, an Ocasio-Cortez-backed candidate, Cori Bush, failed to unseat incumbent Rep. Lacy Clay.

Sanders and Ocasio-Cortez have emerged as prominent voices for the progressive movement, and their influence on Democratic primaries has been closely observed. Nevertheless, questions persist regarding whether their message will resonate with voters in more rural and suburban areas, particularly in the Midwest.

In a Democratic House primary in Kansas's 3rd District, another candidate supported by the duo, Brent Welder, held a slight lead early Wednesday, though the race had not yet been called. Both Welder and his challenger, Sharice Davids, campaigned on progressive platforms, presenting a key test for policies like "Medicare for All" in a competitive race against incumbent Rep. Kevin Yoder.

**Another Strong Performance by Female Candidates**

The "Year of the Woman" narrative continued to unfold on Tuesday, with numerous female candidates securing primary victories. This trend was particularly evident in Michigan, where women achieved success across the ballot. Gretchen Whitmer secured a decisive win in the state's gubernatorial primary, defeating two male opponents.

In two competitive House races in Michigan, Democrats Gretchen Driskell and Elissa Slotkin won their primaries, positioning them as strong contenders against Republican incumbents Tim Walberg and Mike Bishop, respectively. In races to replace retiring Rep. David Trott, both the Democratic and Republican nominees are likely to be women. Lena Epstein, a staffer on Trump's Michigan campaign, held a lead in the Republican primary, while Haley Stevens, endorsed by Hillary Clinton, had a narrow lead in the Democratic primary.

Furthermore, in the race to succeed former Rep. John Conyers Jr., former state Rep. Rashida Tlaib secured the Democratic nomination in a heavily Democratic district, making her likely to become the first Muslim woman elected to Congress. In Washington state, women also appeared poised for victories in two key House races.

## Missouri Senate Race: A Crucial Battleground in a Shifting Political Landscape

The Missouri Senate race has emerged as a pivotal contest, capturing national attention as a key battleground in the upcoming midterm elections. This election pits incumbent Democratic Senator Claire McCaskill against Republican challenger Josh Hawley, the current Missouri Attorney General. Having both successfully navigated their respective primaries, the stage is set for a fiercely competitive general election. The significance of this race is amplified by Missouri's recent political trajectory, which has demonstrably shifted towards the Republican Party in presidential elections, as evidenced by the 2016 results.

### Senator McCaskill's Vulnerability and the Republican Strategy

Senator McCaskill faces a formidable re-election challenge in a state that decisively voted for Donald Trump by a substantial 20-point margin in the 2016 presidential election. This electoral landscape positions her as one of the most vulnerable Democratic incumbents in the 2018 cycle. Consistent polling data indicates a closely contested race, with both McCaskill and Hawley frequently running neck and neck. For the Republican Party, unseating McCaskill has been a paramount strategic objective. She initially secured her Senate seat during the Democratic wave of 2006 and was re-elected in 2012. Her previous victory was against Republican Todd Akin, whose campaign was significantly derailed by controversial remarks concerning abortion. This historical context has profoundly shaped the Republican Party's approach this year, leading them to coalesce around Josh Hawley as their chosen candidate to challenge the incumbent.

### Josh Hawley: The Conservative Contender's Profile

Josh Hawley, aged 38, currently serves as Missouri Attorney General and actively promotes himself as a staunch conservative. While he has generally maintained a relatively low political profile, certain public statements have attracted considerable attention. Notably, during an address at a religious conference, Hawley recently drew a connection between the sexual liberation movements of the 1960s and 1970s and the issue of sex trafficking. This particular statement has become a subject of widespread discussion and has contributed to shaping his public image.

### Republican Confidence and Hawley's Perceived Strengths

In an effort to avoid a recurrence of the 2012 scenario that saw Todd Akin's campaign falter, Republicans identified Hawley as a strong and potentially unblemished candidate. His academic background, including his distinguished achievements from Stanford and Yale, was viewed as a significant asset. The Missouri Republican Party has demonstrated a robust commitment to Hawley's campaign, even permitting the Republican National Committee to allocate campaign funds to support him prior to his official primary victory. This deviation from typical party procedures underscores the high stakes involved and the party's focused strategy in this particular race.

### Navigating the Lingering Greitens Legacy

However, Hawley's campaign has encountered notable challenges, particularly concerning the lingering impact of former Missouri Republican Governor Eric Greitens. Greitens' tenure was significantly marred by scandal, culminating in his resignation in late May. This resignation occurred more than a month after a state report detailed serious allegations against him. While Hawley has actively strived to distance himself from Greitens, the former governor's controversial departure has undeniably created a complex political environment. A potential relief for Hawley's campaign is that the burden of potential allegations against the former governor is no longer a direct threat to his own electoral prospects.

### Conservative Base Concerns and Fundraising Hurdles

Despite these developments, Hawley's relatively low public profile and a primary election path that was largely uncontested have reportedly led to some friction within the state's conservative base. Donors and activists in Missouri have expressed dissatisfaction with Hawley's fundraising efforts, with some describing them as "sluggish." At a time when Republican candidates nationwide are striving to energize their supporters, Hawley's perceived lack of robust engagement has reportedly frustrated conservative activists and organizers whose backing is considered crucial for his success. This situation has also raised concerns among prominent Republicans in Washington, who worry that Hawley's campaign may inadvertently be creating an opening for Senator McCaskill.

### Financial Disparity and Escalating Campaign Attacks

By mid-July, Hawley's campaign reported having $2.3 million in cash reserves. This figure represents only one-third of the campaign funds available to Senator McCaskill, highlighting a significant financial disparity. Even before the general election campaign has fully intensified, Hawley has begun to launch targeted attacks against McCaskill. He has specifically criticized her for aligning with her party's opposition to Brett Kavanaugh's nomination to the Supreme Court, attempting to portray her as too liberal for the state of Missouri. With the primary contests now concluded, the general election race is fully underway, and the strategies and attacks from both sides are expected to escalate as November approaches. The outcome of this race will undoubtedly have significant implications for the balance of power in the U.S. Senate, as noted by political analysts from organizations like the Cook Political Report and FiveThirtyEight.

Secretary of State Kris Kobach (R) and incumbent Gov. Jeff Colyer Secretary of State Kris Kobach (R) and incumbent Gov. Jeff Colyer In the Republican primary in the Kansas governor's race, Trump-backed Secretary of State Kris Kobach and incumbent Gov. Jeff Colyer were waiting on results from the highly populated Johnson County, in the Kansas City area. Kobach and Colyer were locked in a tight race as ballots were counted. The two each had about 40% of the vote, but most of Johnson County was left to be counted. Why this matters: President Trump on Monday endorsed Kobach over incumbent Colyer. In doing so, he ignored the pleas of the Republican Governors Association to stay out of the race. Kobach is widely seen as uniquely vulnerable in a general election due to his controversial national profile. Kansas is a deep-red state -- Trump won there by 20 percentage points in 2016 -- and any Republican would be favored in the gubernatorial election in November. But Democrats believe momentum from this spring's protests over education funding against Republicans who control the state government could make the state surprisingly competitive this fall. And Kobach -- who publicly backed Trump's false claim that millions of people voted illegally in 2016 and who advocates a hard-line approach to immigration -- could alienate Republican voters in areas like the Kansas City and Wichita suburbs.

Voters cast their ballots among an array of electronic voting machines in a polling station at the Noor Islamic Cultural Center, Tuesday, Aug. 7, 2018, in Dublin, Ohio. Two-term state Sen. Troy Balderson,... (Associated Press) Voters cast their ballots among an array of electronic voting machines in a polling station at the Noor Islamic Cultural Center, Tuesday, Aug. 7, 2018, in Dublin, Ohio. Two-term state Sen. Troy Balderson, is fighting off a strong challenge from Democrat Danny O'Connor, a 31-year-old county official,... (Associated Press) WESTERVILLE, Ohio (AP) — The Latest on primaries in Missouri, Kansas, Michigan and Washington state and a congressional special election in Ohio (all times local): 12:30 a.m. A Washington state representative who resigned his chairmanship following allegations of inappropriate conduct is in third place in early primary returns. Democratic Rep. David Sawyer on Tuesday trailed Democrat Melanie Morgan and Republican Terry Harder. Under Washington's primary system, the top two vote-getters advance to November, regardless of party. Sawyer represents a district that includes parts of Tacoma. In June, Sawyer resigned as the chairman of the House Commerce and Gaming Committee a day after an outside investigation found he violated the chamber's policies on harassment, decorum and ethics. Sawyer apologized and said in an email at the time that it was "clear that I messed up and that it's time for me to acknowledge some personal mistakes." ___ 12:25 a.m. A Kansas official says long lines at polling places delayed election results in the state's most populous county as Gov. Jeff Colyer and Secretary of State Kris Kobach were locked in a tight race for the Republican nomination for governor. State elections director Bryan Caskey said Tuesday night that some polling places in Johnson County in the suburbs of Kansas City remained open until about 8 p.m. to accommodate people who were in line to vote when polls officially closed at 7 p.m. He says that led local officials to delay reporting their first results, from votes cast in advance. Johnson County has nearly 408,000 registered voters, or almost 23 percent of the state's total of 1.8 million. ___ 11:45 p.m. A black Ferguson city councilman leads longtime St. Louis County prosecutor Bob McCulloch in a contest some see as a referendum on McCulloch's handling of the investigation into Michael Brown's death. With the vote counted from nearly 90 percent of precincts, the county reported Wesley Bell leading the 67-year-old McCulloch by a 55 percent to 45 percent margin in Tuesday's Democratic primary. No Republicans are running for prosecutor. McCulloch, who is white, is seeking an eighth term. Bell is a 43-year-old attorney and former municipal judge and prosecutor. He was elected councilman in 2015 as protests raged over Brown's death. Brown, an unarmed black 18-year-old, died Aug. 9, 2014, in a street confrontation with white officer Darren Wilson. A St. Louis County grand jury declined to indict Wilson, who later resigned. Some critics accused McCulloch of skewing the investigation in favor of the officer. ___ 11:40 p.m. Democrat Danny O'Connor is trying to rally his supporters as he's locked in a close race with Republican Troy Balderson in Ohio's congressional special election. O'Connor says, "We're not stopping now." The candidates were locked in a razor-thin contest Tuesday night. There were at least 3,367 provisional ballots left to be reviewed. That's enough for O'Connor to potentially pick up enough votes to force a recount. The Associated Press does not declare winners in races subject to an automatic recount. Balderson, meanwhile, was celebrating, saying he's ready to get to work in Congress. He says, "America is on the right path and we're going to keep it going that way." The winner takes the seat previously held by Republican Pat Tiberi, who resigned in January to take another job. ___ 11:35 p.m. President Donald Trump is already taking credit for helping elect his favored candidate in a special House election in Ohio, though the race is too close to call. In a tweet Tuesday night, Trump declared "a great victory" for Republican Troy Balderson over Democrat Danny O'Connor. While Balderson has a slight lead, the race is close enough that there could be a mandatory recount. There are also at least 3,367 provisional ballots left to be reviewed. Trump says Balderson had been far behind in early voting before he hosted a rally for the candidate Saturday night in the suburban Columbus district. The president says that after his speech "there was a big turn for the better." Trump offered another prediction — that Balderson will "win BIG" in November's general election. ___ 11:30 p.m. Republican Susan Hutchison has gained a top-two finish in Washington state's primary to advance to the November general election. The former television newscaster and GOP state party chairwoman was finishing second in the voting in Tuesday's contest. Under Washington's primary system, the top two vote-getters go on to November, regardless of party. Hutchison is the former state Republican Party chairwoman. She is making a longshot bid to oust the Democratic incumbent, Sen. Maria Cantwell, who is seeking a fourth term. ___ 11:25 p.m. Sen. Maria Cantwell has advanced to the November ballot in Washington's primary election. The Democrat seeking her fourth term easily outpaced all other candidates. Under Washington's primary system, the top two vote-getters go on to November, regardless of party. Cantwell is Washington's junior senator and the ranking member of the Energy and Natural Resources Committee. Cantwell will likely face Republican Susan Hutchison in November. Hutchison is the former state Republican Party chairwoman. ___ 11:20 p.m. The last special election of the year for the U.S. House is too close to call. With election officials in Ohio done counting Tuesday night, Republican Troy Balderson has a slight lead over Democrat Danny O'Connor in the special election in the state's 12th Congressional District. But there are at least 3,367 provisional ballots left to be reviewed. That's enough for O'Connor to potentially pick up enough votes to force a mandatory recount. The Associated Press does not declare winners in races that go to an automatic recount. The race in suburban Columbus was one of the most-watched contests in Tuesday's primaries as O'Connor tried to pick up a seat long held by Republicans. ___ 11 p.m. The deadline has passed for turning in primary ballots in Washington, a vote-by-mail state. The contest getting the most attention is a U.S. House seat Democrats hope to capture in November for the first time since the district east of Seattle was created in 1980. Voters began receiving their state primary ballots in the mail weeks ago, and Tuesday was the last day to get them in or postmarked for mail delivery. In some of the more competitive races, results may not be known for days as most counties will update vote counts only once a day. In Washington's 8th District, Republican U.S. Rep. Dave Reichert is retiring after more than a decade. Republican Dino Rossi, a former state senator, is expected to advance along with one of three Democrats. ___ 10:40 p.m. Missouri voters have rejected a right-to-work law banning mandatory union fees in workplace contracts. The vote Tuesday marked a major victory for unions, which poured millions of dollars into a campaign to defeat Proposition A. The right-to-work law originally was enacted in 2017 by Missouri's Republican-led Legislature and governor. But it never took effect, because unions gathered enough petition signatures to force a public referendum on it. Unions argued the measure would have led to lower wages, while business groups claimed it could have led to more jobs. Economic studies showed mixed and sometimes conflicting results. Twenty-seven other states have similar laws against compulsory union fees, including five Republican-led states that have acted since 2012 — Indiana, Michigan, Wisconsin, West Virginia and Kentucky. ___ 10:20 p.m. Kansas Secretary of State Kris Kobach (KOH'-bahk) and Gov. Jeff Colyer are locked in a close Republican primary race for governor. Colyer and Kobach topped a seven-candidate GOP field in Tuesday's election. President Donald Trump tweeted a full endorsement of Kobach on Monday. Kobach is nationally known for advocating tough policies on illegal immigration and strict voter identification laws. He served as vice chairman of Trump's now-disbanded commission on election fraud after advising Trump's 2016 campaign and the White House. Colyer had endorsements from Kansas political icon Bob Dole and the National Rifle Association in his quest to remain in office after becoming governor in January. In the Democratic primary, state Sen. Laura Kelly of Topeka held a lead over former Wichita Mayor Carl Brewer and ex-Kansas Agriculture Secretary Joshua Svaty. ___ 10:15 p.m. A veteran Kansas legislator has won the Democratic primary for governor after stressing her Statehouse experience and fending off questions about her voting record. State Sen. Laura Kelly of Topeka defeated former Wichita Mayor Carl Brewer, former Kansas Agriculture Secretary Joshua Svaty and two other candidates Tuesday. The 68-year-old Kelly has served 14 years in the Senate and is the top Democrat on the budget committee. She stressed those credentials in running and suggested that she was best able to fix problems created by Republican policies. She faced criticism from Svaty and Brewer for votes she made in representing a GOP-leaning district for looser gun laws and for some of the nation's toughest voter identification requirements. But she also had the backing of former two-term Democratic Gov. Kathleen Sebelius. ___ 10:05 p.m. Detroit-area businessman and Iraq War veteran John James has won the Republican nomination to run against Democratic Sen. Debbie Stabenow this fall. James is a political newcomer endorsed by President Donald Trump. He defeated Sandy Pensler in Tuesday's primary. Stabenow is seeking a fourth Senate term and has easily fended off past challengers, but Trump's narrow 2016 victory in Michigan has the GOP hopeful it can flip the seat. The 37-year-old James is an executive at his family's automotive logistics companies and a West Point graduate who flew helicopters during the Iraq War. He is Michigan's first black Republican nominee for a major statewide office in more than three decades, but he has said he only wants to be assessed on his character. ___ 9:55 p.m. Gretchen Whitmer has won the Democratic nomination for Michigan governor, besting two competitors to advance to the November race to succeed term-limited Republican Gov. Rick Snyder. Whitmer, a former legislative leader, defeated chemical-testing businessman Shri Thanedar (shree TAN'-eh-dahr) and ex-Detroit health director Abdul El-Sayed in Tuesday's primary. She will face Attorney General Bill Schuette (SHOOT'-ee), who defeated Lt. Gov. Brian Calley, state Sen. Patrick Colbeck and Dr. Jim Hines in the GOP primary. Whitmer was considered the favorite because of her political experience and broad support from organized labor and other key groups. El-Sayed especially courted the party's more liberal, pro-Bernie Sanders wing, as did Thanedar, who spent millions of dollars of his own money on his campaign. The Michigan governorship is a top target for the Democratic Party. ___ 9:50 p.m. U.S. Rep. Roger Marshall has won the Republican primary in the sprawling rural 1st District of western and central Kansas. He advances to a November matchup against Democrat Alan LaPolice of Clyde in the heavily Republican agricultural district. Marshall, a Great Bend physician, first gained national attention in 2016 for knocking off then Rep. Tim Huelskamp in the Republican primary for the seat. Democrats did not have a candidate in 2016, but LaPolice launched a long-shot bid as an independent. LaPolice, an educator, is taking another shot at it this year running as a Democrat. While the district strongly supported President Donald Trump in 2016, some worry tougher immigration policies make it harder to fill agricultural jobs. Marshall wants to couple border-security measures with changes in visas for guest farm workers. ___ 9:30 p.m. Missouri Attorney General Josh Hawley has won the Republican nomination in one of the nation's most hotly contested Senate races. Voters on Tuesday picked Hawley as expected over 10 other GOP challengers in the race for Democrat Claire McCaskill's seat. He was the only candidate to have previously won a statewide election and had considerably more money than the other Republicans in the field. He's backed by President Donald Trump. Republicans are eyeing the now-Democratic seat as a prime pickup opportunity in a state Trump won by nearly 19 points. A McCaskill-Hawley matchup is expected to be one of the nation's top showdowns. McCaskill is running as a moderate in the red state. Hawley is campaigning largely on support for Trump. He is attempting to paint his rival as a liberal obstructionist. ___ 9:25 p.m. Sen. Claire McCaskill of Missouri has won the Democratic primary in her campaign for a third term. Voters picked McCaskill on Tuesday as expected over six other Democratic challengers. Republicans are eyeing the Democratic seat in a state that President Donald Trump won by nearly 19 points in 2016. Missouri Attorney General Josh Hawley was favored in an 11-candidate Republican primary for the right to face McCaskill in November. A McCaskill-Hawley matchup is expected to be one of the most hotly contested races in the nation. McCaskill is running as a moderate in the red state. Hawley is campaigning largely on support for Trump and is attempting to paint his rival as a liberal obstructionist. ___ 9:20 p.m. State Attorney General Bill Schuette (SHOOT'-ee) has won the Republican nomination for Michigan governor, defeating three other candidates vying to succeed term-limited GOP Gov. Rick Snyder. Schuette beat Lt. Gov. Brian Calley, state Sen. Patrick Colbeck and Dr. Jim Hines on Tuesday. Democrats running were former legislative leader Gretchen Whitmer, ex-Detroit health director Abdul El-Sayed and entrepreneur Shri Thanedar. Schuette, a former congressman, state lawmaker and appellate judge, was endorsed by President Donald Trump. He says he would cut the state income tax, like Trump reduced federal taxes. The Michigan governorship is a top target for the Democratic Party, which is eager to make gains in the Midwest, where Republicans have dominated state governments and which helped President Donald Trump take the White House in 2016. ___ 9 p.m. Polls have closed in Kansas and Michigan primary elections that are testing anew President Donald Trump's political clout. Tuesday's races, like dozens before them, are pitting the strength of Trump's fiery supporters against the Democratic Party's anti-Trump resistance. The results will help determine the political landscape before the GOP defends its congressional majorities across the nation. One of the top races is in Kansas, where Secretary of State Kris Kobach (KOH'-bahk) is trying to unseat Gov. Jeff Colyer. Should Kobach win the primary, some Republican operatives fear he could lose the governor's seat to Democrats this fall. In Michigan, three mainstream Democrats are among those vying for a chance at retiring Republican Rep. Dave Trott's seat. The field includes Fayrouz Saad (fay-ROOZ' sahd), who would be the first Muslim woman in Congress. ___ 8:10 p.m. Democrat Danny O'Connor has jumped out to a significant lead over Republican Troy Balderson in early returns in Ohio's congressional special election. Voters on Tuesday were choosing between Balderson, a state senator, and O'Connor, the Franklin County recorder, to complete the term of a Republican who retired in January. The race is a test of voter sentiment before the general election in November, when Balderson and O'Connor will battle again for the full two-year term. President Donald Trump campaigned for Balderson, arguing Republicans need to control Congress and casting the midterms as a referendum on himself. A Balderson victory would buoy Republicans concerned about how Trump might be playing in political battleground states. An O'Connor win would elate Democrats hoping for a Trump backlash. ___ 8 p.m. Polls have closed in Missouri and are beginning to close in parts of Kansas and Michigan on a night of primary elections. Tuesday's races, like dozens before them, pitted the strength of President Donald Trump's fiery supporters against the Democratic Party's anti-Trump resistance. The results will help determine the political landscape — and Trump's standing within his own party — just three months before the GOP defends its House and Senate majorities across the nation. One of the top races of the night is in Kansas, where Secretary of State Kris Kobach (KOH'-bahk) was trying to unseat Gov. Jeff Colyer. Kobach has been backed by the president. Should the polarizing Kobach win the primary, some Republican operatives fear he could lose the governor's seat to Democrats this fall. ___ 7:30 p.m. The polls have closed in Ohio, where a Republican state senator backed by President Donald Trump is competing with a Democratic county official in a special election for an open U.S. House seat long held by the GOP. Voters Tuesday were choosing between Sen. Troy Balderson and Franklin County Recorder Danny O'Connor to complete the term of a Republican who retired in January. The race is a test of voter sentiment before the general election in November, when Balderson and O'Connor will battle again for the full two-year term. Trump campaigned for Balderson, arguing Republicans need to control Congress and casting the midterms as a referendum on himself. A Balderson victory would buoy Republicans concerned about how Trump might be playing in political battleground states. An O'Connor win would elate Democrats hoping for a Trump backlash. ___ 7:15 p.m. A St. Louis County election official blames a "comedy of errors" for some voters being turned away in one Missouri town. The St. Louis Post-Dispatch reports that turnout in the St. Louis region for Tuesday's primary election was brisk, with about 35 percent of registered voters going to the polls. But there were sporadic problems, most notably at Holman Elementary School in Berkeley. Democratic elections director Eric Fey says a Republican poll worker had to leave because of a family emergency. The other two went to lunch, apparently got lost and didn't return. It was unclear how many people were turned away during the 45 minutes it took to get additional Republican poll workers to the polling place. Elsewhere in St. Louis County, some poll workers mistakenly gave voters nonpartisan ballots that included only issues, not party primary races. ___ 6:20 p.m. Battleground Ohio is deciding the year's final special election, a congressional faceoff that tests anew President Donald Trump's political clout and the appeal of his signature tax cuts. Seventy-one-year-old retiree Gregg Schirra voted for Hillary Clinton in 2016 and Democrat Danny O'Connor on Tuesday in Westerville. He says, "We gave Trump a fair chance and now it's time to get the legislature working and get back on track." At Delaware Township Hall north of Columbus, 71-year-old retiree Katheryn Terry voted for Republican Troy Balderson, saying she felt the 31-year-old O'Connor was just too young and she wasn't happy with anything about his candidacy. She says: "I'm trying to give Trump the benefit of the doubt, and so I'm trying to be as supportive as I can, because things have turned around."

Michigan's 2018 primary election results are in, setting the stage for a gubernatorial showdown between Republican Bill Schuette and Democrat Gretchen Whitmer. Both candidates secured their party's nominations by 10 p.m. on Tuesday, paving the way for a closely watched general election on November 6th.

Following hard-fought primary campaigns, both Schuette and Whitmer extended olive branches to their former opponents, emphasizing unity within their respective parties. This sentiment was echoed by second-place finishers Brian Calley, the Republican lieutenant governor, and Dr. Abdul El-Sayed, former head of the Detroit health department. Turnout appeared to exceed expectations in several areas, with reports of ballot shortages in Oakland County and other locations, prompting the Michigan Secretary of State's Office to advise voters to remain at polling places.

In the Democratic primary, Gretchen Whitmer secured 52% of the vote, with Dr. Abdul El-Sayed receiving 30% and Shri Thanedar taking 18%. On the Republican side, Bill Schuette garnered 51% of the vote, while Brian Calley trailed with 25%.

Schuette, the current Michigan Attorney General, declared victory in Midland, stating, "I am proud to be your Republican nominee for governor. Michigan, it's time to win again." He immediately positioned Whitmer as a "tax-and-spend liberal."

Whitmer, addressing supporters in Detroit, spoke of the opportunity to "reject the policies of division" and return a Democrat to the governor's office. She highlighted critical issues such as public education, the Flint water crisis, and infrastructure, stating, "As a child, I never once worried about literacy or drinking water and infrastructure, but our leaders have failed us."

Political consultant Ed Sarpolus of Target-Insyght noted that both Whitmer and Schuette face the challenge of differentiating themselves from past candidates. Sarpolus suggested Whitmer needs to "run as herself" to avoid being painted as a successor to former Governor Jennifer Granholm or Hillary Clinton. He also advised Schuette to distance himself from President Donald Trump and current Governor Rick Snyder.

The primary campaigns offered a preview of the general election battle. Schuette characterized Whitmer as a "Granholm clone" who would revert Michigan to the "lost decade" of the 2000s. Whitmer, in turn, portrayed Schuette as beholden to special interests, unwilling to champion the needs of middle-class voters.

Michigan Republican Party Chairman Ronald Weiser expressed confidence that Schuette "will lead our state into another decade of prosperity." The Democratic Governors Association Chairman, Governor Jay Inslee, congratulated Whitmer, calling Michigan a "top target for pickup this cycle" and praising Whitmer as a "terrific candidate."

The Republican race was marked by sharp exchanges. Brian Calley, who finished second, criticized Schuette for alleged ethical lapses, including hiring Republican activists for civil service positions and using state resources for political activities. Calley largely ran on the record of Governor Rick Snyder, highlighting efforts to reduce business taxes and regulations. In his concession speech, Calley acknowledged Schuette's victory, stating, "we took our shot" and "we left everything out there on the field."

Schuette's campaign, with support from figures like political consultant Stu Sandler, also targeted Calley, criticizing his past involvement with a failed ballot initiative for a part-time legislature and his role in creating the Michigan Business Tax. Schuette emphasized his experience as a former congressman, state department director, state senator, and appellate court judge, advocating for continued economic growth and a personal income tax cut. Other GOP candidates, Senator Patrick Colbeck and Dr. Jim Hines, garnered 13% and 11% of the vote, respectively.

Both parties are planning "unity rallies" to foster cohesion. Vice President Mike Pence is scheduled to attend a Republican event in Grand Rapids, while Democrats will hold a similar meeting in Detroit.

In the Democratic primary, Whitmer, an East Lansing attorney, secured endorsements from unions and elected officials, including the Detroit Regional Chamber, positioning her as the establishment choice. She notably did not support a move to a single-payer healthcare system but emphasized her track record, including her role in expanding Medicaid under the Affordable Care Act.

Dr. Abdul El-Sayed was widely seen as the progressive frontrunner, earning endorsements from figures like Senator Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez. El-Sayed garnered significant national media attention and drew large crowds. In his concession speech, he endorsed Whitmer and called for unity to defeat Schuette.

Shri Thanedar, a scientist and entrepreneur, utilized television advertising and invested heavily in his campaign. He advocated for policies similar to El-Sayed's, such as single-payer healthcare and universal pre-school. However, Thanedar faced scrutiny due to past financial support for Republican candidates and reports of considering a GOP run. He pledged party unity, stating, "Absolutely, a Democrat must win the governor’s office in 2018."

Whitmer welcomed El-Sayed and Thanedar, stating, "I extend a hand out to them, there is a place for you as we move forward. I appreciate your energy and ideas — let’s work together to get this done." Detroit Mayor Mike Duggan, who campaigned with Whitmer, cited her "leadership in 2013" in supporting her bid, referencing her role in passing the Healthy Michigan plan.

In the Libertarian race for governor, Bill Gelineau won with 58% of the vote, defeating John Tatar, who received 42%.

Indications suggest that voter turnout may have surpassed the 2002 midterm primary record of 23.3%. Absentee ballot numbers showed a significant increase, with 694,129 ballots sent out and 527,050 returned, marking a 53% and 50% rise respectively compared to previous cycles.

In the closely watched special elections of August 2018, the political landscape was intensely scrutinized as voters cast their ballots, particularly in battleground Ohio and deep-red Kansas. These contests, which commanded millions in campaign spending, served as crucial tests of President Donald Trump's influence and the burgeoning momentum of the Democratic Party's anti-Trump movement.

In Ohio's 12th Congressional District, the race between Republican Troy Balderson and Democrat Danny O'Connor was too close to call, with the Associated Press unable to declare a winner due to the potential for a recount. Despite the tight margin, President Trump claimed victory, attributing Balderson's performance to his endorsement. O'Connor, a 31-year-old county official, vowed to continue his challenge through the November general election, signaling a spirited rematch. This district, which had been held by Republicans for decades, demonstrated a significant shift in voter sentiment, with Democratic candidates performing notably better than in previous elections. Data compiled by the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee indicated that 79 House races in the upcoming fall elections were considered more competitive than this Ohio district, based on Trump's 2016 performance. O'Connor's vote total significantly surpassed that of his Republican predecessor's Democratic opponent in 2014, while Balderson's vote count was notably lower than the previous Republican incumbent's. The two candidates are set to face each other again in November for the seat in the 119th Congress.

The Ohio contest highlighted the evolving demographics of the district, often described as a "Whole Foods" district, characterized by a more affluent and educated electorate than typical Trump strongholds. The race also became a referendum on Trump's tax cuts, with O'Connor and his allies criticizing the plan as a boon for the wealthy that would exacerbate deficits and endanger Medicare and Social Security. Balderson and his Republican supporters, conversely, shifted their focus to criticizing House Democrat Nancy Pelosi. Campaign finance reports revealed a significant disparity in local advertising spending, with O'Connor's campaign investing $2.25 million compared to Balderson's $507,000. However, Republican campaign committees and allied super PACs collectively spent over $4 million to support Balderson.

Meanwhile, in Kansas, the gubernatorial primary saw a fierce intra-party battle. Secretary of State Kris Kobach, who had received Trump's endorsement, was in a tight race with incumbent Republican Governor Jeff Colyer. Concerns were raised by some Republican operatives that a Kobach victory could jeopardize the governorship for the GOP in the general election, especially with the potential for independent candidate Greg Orman to enter the race. Trump had publicly endorsed Kobach, tweeting, "He is a fantastic guy who loves his State and our Country - he will be a GREAT Governor and has my full & total Endorsement! Strong on Crime, Border & Military."

In other races, the Democratic primary in Kansas's 3rd Congressional District featured a competitive field vying to challenge Republican Rep. Kevin Yoder. Labor lawyer Brent Welder campaigned with prominent figures like Vermont Sen. Bernie Sanders and New York congressional candidate Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez. Also competing were Native American attorney Sharice Davids and former teacher Tom Niermann.

Beyond Ohio and Kansas, the elections set the stage for several other key contests. In Michigan, three mainstream Democrats competed for the seat of retiring Republican Rep. Dave Trott, including Fayrouz Saad, who aimed to become the first Muslim woman in Congress. In suburban Seattle, Democrats vied for the seat of another retiring Republican, Rep. Dave Reichert. Senate races also took shape, with Democratic Sen. Claire McCaskill securing her party's nomination in Missouri against Republican Josh Hawley, and in Michigan, Democratic Sen. Debbie Stabenow preparing to face military veteran and business executive John James, who won the Republican nomination. James's potential victory could make him one of the few Black Republican senators, alongside Tim Scott of South Carolina. President Trump had also weighed in on James's candidacy, calling him "a potential Republican star."

These special elections, occurring in a period leading up to the midterm elections, provided early indicators of voter sentiment and the effectiveness of campaign strategies, particularly concerning President Trump's influence and the Democratic Party's mobilization efforts.

query: What was Sen. Ted Cruz's response when asked if he would support Sen. Mitch McConnell as Senate leader?

documents.text:
Following the Republican takeover of the Senate and John Boehner's expanded House majority, the GOP is poised to demonstrate its governing capabilities. Prior to the election, Republican strategists and lawmakers outlined a plan to secure early legislative wins before the 2015 presidential election cycle intensifies. This strategy, championed by Boehner and other leaders, aimed to pivot from crisis management to a proactive agenda.

A primary objective is to pass a comprehensive funding bill that would sustain government operations through most of the following year. This would clear legislative bandwidth for other priorities, such as a Republican-authored budget and a long-term highway infrastructure bill. Congressional activity is slated to resume in earnest after Thanksgiving, with both parties focusing on organizing for the new partisan landscape in Washington. Capitol Hill is expected to become fully operational by December 1st, marking the potential for significant legislative action.

Republicans are keen to avoid fiscal confrontations in early 2015. Consequently, they intend to utilize the initial weeks of December to enact a long-term spending measure, ensuring government continuity through the majority of the next year. Government funding is set to expire on December 11th, coinciding with the expiration of the authority for President Barack Obama's administration to train and equip moderate Syrian rebels in their fight against the Islamic State. This specific provision could introduce complexities to the passage of a smooth spending bill.

Beyond appropriations, Congress will also aim to address a backlog of expired targeted business tax provisions and renew a federal program designed to protect insurance companies against terrorist attacks. Furthermore, depending on the Senate's final composition, Democrats may seek to confirm a new Attorney General in the closing months of 2014, following Eric Holder's anticipated retirement. The overarching hope is that this concentrated legislative push will free up the calendar for the first half of 2015, enabling Congress to tackle a long-term highway and infrastructure bill, raise the debt limit, and pass a budget.

With Republican control of both chambers, the budget process presents a significant opportunity for the party to influence the Affordable Care Act. This is particularly true if Senate Republicans employ budget reconciliation, a procedure requiring only 51 votes. Congress is also expected to address the Sustainable Growth Rate, a contentious formula dictating Medicare reimbursements to physicians. However, the legislative landscape could be complicated by GOP Senators Ted Cruz of Texas and Mike Lee of Utah, who have publicly committed to obstructing any legislation deemed non-essential during the lame-duck session. While senior aides acknowledge the potential for shifting priorities, it is clear that major initiatives such as immigration reform, tax reform, or large-scale fiscal agreements will not be addressed during this period.

Equally crucial to year-end legislating are the internal party meetings scheduled to occur. A Republican Senate majority will need to establish committee structures and determine the ratio of Democrats to Republicans on these panels. A notable shift will be the appointment of new GOP committee chairmen, many of whom are expected to challenge the Obama administration's agenda. Senator Jim Inhofe of Oklahoma, a vocal critic of the administration's energy policies, is anticipated to chair the Environment and Public Works Committee. Senator John McCain of Arizona is likely to lead the Senate Armed Services Committee, a prominent platform for his critiques of Obama's foreign policy. Senator Jeff Sessions of Alabama, known for his conservative stance, is in line to chair the Senate Budget Committee.

The expanded Republican majority in the House will necessitate considerable organizational adjustments on Capitol Hill. New members will convene for orientation and leadership elections on November 12th, including the selection of new Democratic and Republican chairmen for Senate campaign committees. The House will also elect new chairmen for its key committees. Representative Paul Ryan of Wisconsin is set to lead the Ways and Means Committee, which is preparing to revise the tax code. Representative Mac Thornberry of Texas, a seasoned legislator, will take the helm of the Armed Services Committee. The Oversight and Government Reform Committee, Congress's primary investigative body, will see new leadership as Representative Darrell Issa (R-Calif.) reaches his term limit. Representatives Jason Chaffetz of Utah and Mike Turner of Ohio are considered leading candidates for this position. One chairmanship that is not expected to face a competitive challenge is that of the National Republican Congressional Committee. Despite some House Republicans expressing dissatisfaction with Representative Greg Walden's leadership during the fall, the election results are anticipated to quell these concerns.

Following the Republican Senate victory on Tuesday night, Texas Senator Ted Cruz declared the "era of Obama lawlessness is over." Speaking to supporters in Austin, Cruz emphasized the responsibility Republicans now hold to govern effectively, especially if they achieve a national majority. He stated, "If and when we retake the majority nationally, it will be incumbent on Republicans to lead." Cruz outlined a Republican vision centered on offering a "bold positive, optimistic agenda to turn this country around, to bring back jobs and growth and opportunity." He added, "The American people have risen up and frankly, at the national level, they have given Republicans another chance."

Cruz, who is often discussed as a potential 2016 presidential candidate, elaborated on a wide-ranging conservative agenda for the upcoming Congress. His proposals included significant shifts in tax policy and regulatory measures. He also advocated for an end to what he termed "amnesty" for undocumented immigrants. Furthermore, Cruz stressed the importance of protecting religious liberties and upholding the right to bear arms. A primary objective for the senator was the resolute repeal of the Affordable Care Act, commonly known as ObamaCare. Cruz urged Republicans to "do everything humanly possible to repeal ObamaCare."

The 2014 U.S. midterm elections marked a significant shift in American politics, primarily with the Republican Party securing a majority in the Senate. This outcome meant Republicans achieved their goal of controlling the Senate, needing a net gain of six seats. While final counts in some states, like Alaska, were still being finalized, the overall Republican objective was met. Early wins, such as Cory Gardner's victory in Colorado, hinted at a competitive election, but the results ultimately demonstrated a broader Republican success across the nation.

Several key races solidified the GOP's Senate control. In Kansas, Senator Pat Roberts successfully defended his seat against independent challenger Greg Orman. North Carolina's House Speaker, Thom Tillis, unseated the incumbent Democrat Kay Hagan. The Virginia Senate race proved particularly surprising; had a third-party candidate not been a factor, Ed Gillespie might have defeated incumbent Democrat Mark Warner.

Democrats faced a challenging electoral landscape in 2014, with many Senate races concentrated in former Confederate states. The historical tendency for the party holding the presidency to experience losses in midterm elections also negatively impacted Democrats. They lost seats even in states previously considered strongholds. The election also saw Republicans win governorships in key states, with Maryland electing a Republican governor for the first time since Spiro Agnew in 1969, and Rick Scott retaining the Florida governorship for the GOP.

With Republicans leading Congress for the first time in a decade, the focus turned to how they would wield their legislative power. Mitch McConnell, poised to become Senate Majority Leader, expressed a desire for cooperation, stating, "Just because we have a two-party system doesn't mean we have to be in perpetual conflict." However, the political realities of Washington suggested that conflict might persist among certain factions.

Kentucky's other Senator, Rand Paul, offered a different outlook, indicating that a Republican Senate would aim to counter the President's agenda, including efforts to repeal the Affordable Care Act. Despite this sentiment, McConnell's influence would be constrained. The projected Republican majority of 53 or 54 seats fell short of the 60 votes needed to overcome a filibuster, meaning Democrats could still obstruct or influence the Republican legislative agenda.

Internal dynamics within the new Republican Senate could also present challenges for McConnell. Senators like Tom Cotton, who defeated Mark Pryor in Arkansas, had previously supported government shutdowns, a tactic McConnell had worked to avoid. New Senators Joni Ernst from Iowa and Thom Tillis from North Carolina might also prove to be independent thinkers, not always adhering strictly to party directives.

The Senate's internal dynamics were expected to become clearer before the new members were sworn in. A significant portion of the government was operating under a continuing resolution set to expire on December 11th, making it likely that McConnell would prioritize avoiding another shutdown. Potential areas for bipartisan agreement with the President might include trade policy, corporate taxes, and increased visas for skilled workers. However, those anticipating immediate solutions to major issues due to the Senate's partisan shift should recognize the complexities of divided government, which often leads to protracted negotiations and gradual progress. The success of the Republican majority would ultimately depend on internal party unity, the procedural leverage of the Democrats, and the President's willingness to engage. The ensuing months would reveal the extent of Republican influence and their capacity to navigate Washington's intricate political processes.

**Harry Reid Acknowledges Mitch McConnell's Senate Leadership Amidst Shifting Political Landscape**

Following a significant Republican victory in the Senate, Senator Harry Reid, a Democrat from Nevada, has extended congratulations to Senator Mitch McConnell, a Republican from Kentucky, on his upcoming role as the new Senate Majority Leader. This leadership change occurs as the Republican party has successfully secured a majority of key Senate races, indicating a notable shift in the chamber's political direction.

In a statement issued via email, Senator Reid acknowledged the clear mandate from the voters. "The message from voters is clear: they want us to work together," he stated. He expressed his willingness to collaborate with Senator McConnell, saying, "I look forward to working with Senator McConnell to get things done for the middle class."

Senator Reid's concise remarks highlighted a moment of introspection for the Democratic party. Many within the party are expected to analyze the election results. Some have already privately linked the outcomes to the President and the White House. Senator Reid's strategic approach, described as a "prevent defense," did not yield the desired outcome.

The statement also confirmed Senator Reid's decision to remain in his leadership position rather than step down. Despite some internal dissatisfaction, no official challenge to his leadership has materialized.

Attention now turns to the approaching lame-duck session. This period precedes Senator McConnell's formal assumption of Senate leadership. Important issues scheduled for discussion and action during this time include nominations and the approval of a comprehensive spending bill. These items represent the immediate priorities for the Senate as it navigates this leadership transition.

The recent Republican electoral success has significantly reshaped the nation's political landscape, ushering in an era of legislative gridlock and governmental stagnation for President Obama's final two years. This outcome, which granted Republicans full control of Congress for the first time since 2006, prompts an examination of how the party plans to wield its newfound power and what this signifies for American governance. The GOP successfully tapped into widespread public frustration with Washington D.C.'s perceived ineffectiveness, achieving a complete congressional takeover. This victory not only bolstered their House majority but also secured crucial wins in several key governor's races.

Senator Mitch McConnell of Kentucky, a seasoned politician, is set to become the Senate Majority Leader following his successful re-election. After his win, McConnell expressed a desire for bipartisan cooperation, stating, "We have a duty to work together on issues where we can agree. I think we have a duty to do that." However, this sentiment contrasts with the Republican Party's legislative history since regaining the House in 2010, a period marked by a general reluctance to advance significant legislation. Therefore, even with McConnell's stated goals, substantial legislative achievements in the coming years appear limited.

This political climate suggests that critical national issues, such as comprehensive immigration reform, decisive action on climate change, addressing income inequality, and modernizing infrastructure, will likely remain stalled. In this context, President Obama may increasingly rely on his veto power to block legislation from the Republican-controlled Congress. The future of the Affordable Care Act remains uncertain, as it was not a primary campaign focus for many Republican candidates.

The Republican Party's rise, particularly their Senate takeover, could empower more conservative factions, potentially shifting the party's platform further right. For instance, Senator Ted Cruz initially expressed reservations about endorsing McConnell for Majority Leader. House Speaker John Boehner did not explicitly prioritize immigration reform for the incoming Congress but acknowledged the significant responsibility entrusted to them by voters, remarking, "We are humbled by the responsibility the American people have placed with us, but this is not a time for celebration. It’s time for government to start getting results and implementing solutions to the challenges facing our country, starting with our still-struggling economy."

The loss of the Senate also significantly curtails a key presidential power Obama had largely maintained after Republicans took the House in 2010: his ability to confirm judicial nominees. This development signals prolonged political disputes over any potential Supreme Court vacancies. The implications for the 2016 presidential election are complex. While Republicans' increased ability to obstruct Obama's agenda could further fuel voter dissatisfaction, potentially boosting their desire for a White House change, this strategy might also backfire. If Republicans are seen as overreaching or excessively catering to their base, they could alienate a broader electorate, appearing to be part of the problem they campaigned against.

This election night outcome mirrored the significant Republican gains of 2010, a result President Obama himself called a decisive "thumping" of the Democrats. Exit poll data indicated that the results were largely driven by the President's declining approval ratings and widespread dissatisfaction with an economic recovery that, despite statistical improvements, many Americans felt did not translate into tangible benefits. On a positive note for Republicans, they achieved notable successes by electing two women to the Senate, along with one Black senator and one Black female representative. Key Democratic Senate incumbents lost in Arkansas, Colorado, and North Carolina, while Republicans also secured Senate seats in Montana, West Virginia, Iowa, and South Dakota. Many of these Senate races were expected to be highly competitive. Even on election night, there was a possibility that Senate control would remain undecided for weeks, with potential runoffs and an independent senator yet to declare his party affiliation for caucus purposes. A panel discussion highlighted the Republicans' historic House majority and potential challenges Speaker John Boehner might face in managing more ideologically driven colleagues.

However, states like Kansas, Arkansas, Georgia, Kentucky, Iowa, and Colorado appeared to shift decisively towards the GOP in the campaign's final days. Republican candidates effectively targeted the President in their messaging, a tactic that proved highly successful. The NBC News national exit poll revealed the President's approval rating at 45%, a significant nine-point drop from his re-election two years prior. Concurrently, 70% of voters expressed dissatisfaction with economic conditions, with only 29% believing the economy was performing well.

In closely watched gubernatorial contests, Wisconsin Governor Scott Walker defeated Democrat Mary Burke, while Ohio's John Kasich secured re-election. These victories position both Walker and Kasich as potential 2016 presidential contenders. In Florida, Republican Governor Rick Scott narrowly won against Democrat Charlie Crist. Texas saw Republican Attorney General Greg Abbott achieve a decisive victory against Democratic State Senator Wendy Davis. Illinois elected Republican Bruce Rauner, unseating incumbent Democrat Pat Quinn. In a notable exception for Democrats, businessman Tom Wolf ousted the unpopular Republican Governor of Pennsylvania, Tom Corbett.

The Fix’s Chris Cillizza breaks down how close the battle for the Senate really is. (The Washington Post) The Fix’s Chris Cillizza breaks down how close the battle for the Senate really is. (The Washington Post) Sen. Ted Cruz spent the final weekend of the midterms on the far edge of the country trying to help fellow Republican Dan Sullivan win a race the GOP is counting on in its effort to retake the Senate. It’s a team-player role the tea party firebrand from Texas has filled a handful of times this fall — but one he plans to abandon if Republicans win control of both congressional chambers. In an interview at the Hotel Captain Cook here between campaign stops for Sullivan, Cruz made it clear he would push hard for a Republican-led Senate to be as conservative and confron­tational as the Republican-led House. Piggybacking on what House leaders have done, Cruz said the first order of business should be a series of hearings on President Obama, “looking at the abuse of power, the executive abuse, the regulatory abuse, the lawlessness that sadly has pervaded this administration.” (Live coverage: On the campaign trail) 1 of 13 Full Screen Autoplay Close Skip Ad × New faces to Washington? Or bigger roles? View Photos Hopes among these politicians are high that they’ll be headed to the nation’s capital – or upgrading from House digs to the Senate side of the Capitol. Caption An eclectic group is coming to Congress — or moving across the Capitol from the House to the Senate. Here are a few. Mia Love — Utah Love, a former small-town Utah mayor and fitness instructor from Brooklyn, became the nation’s first black, female, Republican member of Congress. She lost a House race in 2012 to incumbent Democrat Jim Matheson, who is retiring. Rick Bowmer/AP Buy Photo Wait 1 second to continue. Cruz also would like the Senate to be as aggressive in trying to repeal the Affordable Care Act as the House, which has voted more than 50 times to get rid of the law. Republicans should “pursue every means possible to repeal Obamacare,” Cruz said, including forcing a vote through parliamentary procedures that would get around a possible filibuster by Democrats. If that leads to a veto by Obama, Cruz said, Republicans should then vote on provisions of the health law “one at a time.” And when asked whether he would back Sen. Mitch McConnell of Kentucky for Republican leader, Cruz would not pledge his support — an indication that there are limits to how much of a partner he’s willing to be. At the heart of Cruz’s shift from the insular approach that defined his first year in office is a belief that he can use his popularity with conservatives to expand his influence in the Senate and improve his standing as he considers a 2016 presidential campaign. Cruz’s desire to turn his party further right in the coming months is one of the challenges already facing McConnell should Republicans regain the Senate, with tea party leaders inside and outside the Capitol spoiling for a number of hard-line moves. “Senator Cruz has been rather quiet over the past few months,” said Ron Bonjean, a spokesman for Trent Lott when the Mississippian was the Senate Republican leader. “That time seems to be coming to an end. I understand why he’s eager to go after Obamacare. But the reality is that it’ll take 60 votes to repeal it and Republicans will have nowhere near that amount. If Obamacare remains the focus, he will certainly get the base jazzed up about what he’s doing, but he won’t get rid of the law.” Cruz has gained some traction in terms of shaping the contours of what a Republican Senate would do, in part because McConnell and House Speaker John A. Boehner (R-Ohio) have not offered their own definitive vision of what a Republican-led Congress would look like. View Graphic Election Lab: See our current forecast for every congressional race in 2014 Two weeks ago, Cruz wrote an opinion piece in USA Today laying out 10 conservative priorities he thinks Republicans should pursue, including moving toward a flat tax and drawing a hard line on illegal immigrants. In the interview here, Cruz reiterated some of those points, such as approving the Keystone XL pipeline. McConnell has mostly been coy about what he would like to accomplish other than adding amendments to curb federal regulations to spending bills as a means of putting pressure on the president. “It’s never a good idea to tell the other side what the first play is going to be,” McConnell said at an event last month. Cruz is not interested in adding amendments that may put indirect pressure on Obama. He favors direct political combat. That way, either the president gives in, or, Cruz said, “you have clear accountability. It becomes transparent to everyone that it is the Democrats blocking meaningful progress.” Rep. Peter King (R-N.Y.), a Long Island moderate, said Republicans should be wary of Cruz’s guidance. “He is the last one we should listen to,” King said in an interview Sunday. “Don’t forget — a year ago he brought Republicans over the cliff.” Still, Cruz’s help on the trail has improved his image among some Republicans, while easing a measure of the anger aimed at him from some of the donors and colleagues he infuriated in October 2013 when he led a shutdown of the government. Cruz should be able to count on a handful of new friends, if not allies, when the Senate convenes next year. In recent weeks, he has campaigned for Senate contenders who beat Cruz-admiring insurgents in Republican primaries, from businessman David Perdue in Georgia and state Sen. Joni Ernst in Iowa to Sullivan and Sen. Pat Roberts, Kansas’s embattled incumbent. If she wins, Ernst is poised to be a powerful player in the run-up to the Iowa caucuses, the first nominating contest in the 2016 race for the GOP presidential nomination. Perdue, who has weak ties to his red state’s GOP base, could hew close to Cruz on some votes to keep conservatives in Georgia at bay. Sullivan, for similar reasons, could do the same. Cruz is not the only conservative Republican with national ambitions who is working to show skeptical Republicans that they can do more than squabble. Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.) has cut ads for the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, one of the groups that opposed tea party candidates in Senate primaries, and stumped for moderate former Massachusetts senator Scott Brown, who is running for Senate in New Hampshire. Cruz declined to say whether he’s going to run for president, but was dismissive of moderates in his party, particularly those who may challenge him for the 2016 Republican nomination. Of Jeb Bush, for instance, Cruz said he likes and respects him, “but I think we have seen election after election that when Republicans fail to draw a clear distinction with the Democrats, when we run to the mushy middle, we lose.” “At some point,” Cruz continued, “after Gerald Ford and Bob Dole and John McCain and Mitt Romney . . . we shouldn’t keep making the same mistakes over and over again. “One of the reasons Republicans have lost elections recently is that we have failed to engage in a meaningful way on the great issues of the day,” Cruz said. “We’ve played a prevent defense. You don’t win elections that way.”
