A commentary from a Chinese state-run news agency has ignited significant controversy. The piece, published by Xinhua's International Herald Leader, targeted Taiwan's President Tsai Ing-wen. It characterized her leadership as "extreme" and "personalized." The article attributed these traits to her being unmarried and childless.

President Tsai Ing-wen, a distinguished alumna of the London School of Economics and Cornell University, assumed the presidency of Taiwan. In her inaugural address, she urged citizens to look forward and cast aside prejudice.

Conversely, the Xinhua opinion piece offered a contrasting viewpoint. It posited that as a single female politician, Tsai is free from the "emotional burden of love, of 'family' or children." The author, identified as Wang Weixing, an official within a Beijing-controlled body overseeing Taiwan relations, suggested that interactions with Tsai should account for her "experience, personality and mindset."

In the wake of the widespread criticism, the article was removed from the newspaper's website. It was subsequently replaced by a "page not found" notification.

This incident stands in stark contrast to President Xi Jinping's publicly stated dedication to gender equality. Xi has previously underscored the importance of empowering women across society and the economy, and of championing their leadership roles. However, reports suggest that feminist activism in China has encountered escalating limitations since Xi assumed power in 2012. For example, commemorations for the 20th anniversary of the 1995 UN Fourth Conference on Women, held in Beijing, were reportedly prohibited. Furthermore, the Communist party's highest seven-member ruling council currently includes no women, and women hold very few senior positions within Chinese politics.

Hillary Clinton's public image often highlights her **extensive professional achievements**, leading to scrutiny of her personal life. While friends see her as **warm and compassionate**, her public persona emphasizes her **résumé and policy stances**. A biographical video, "Fighter," showcased her advocacy. However, a contemporary interview, with its **carefully controlled setting and attire**, presented her as a polished brand, not an accessible person.

This focus on her professional identity can suggest **workaholism**, a form of **emotional self-alienation**. When deeply immersed in work, personal feelings may not guide decisions. The professional role can overshadow personality, intruding on human intimacy. As Martyn Lloyd-Jones noted, some lives become so defined by their job that their epitaph might read: "Born a person, died a [profession]."

Clinton projects an **exclusively professional image**: industrious, strategic, and guarded. This makes it **hard for others to connect with her personally**; she appears as a role, not an individual. This career-centric persona clashes with social media's need for **intimacy, disclosure, and trust**. It also differs from most Americans' lives, who find **vitality outside work**. Consequently, some see her as **Machiavellian, cunning, and untrustworthy**.

This offers insight for those with fulfilling careers. Even a good calling can become all-consuming, leading to disconnection from one's true self. It's crucial for those with demanding jobs to cultivate **personal sanctuaries separate from work**. These can be found in **play, solitude, family, spiritual practice, hobbies, and leisure**.

Abraham Joshua Heschel called the Sabbath "**a palace in time we build**." He saw it as more than rest, but a temporal elevation. Josef Pieper argued leisure is a **disposition of mind**, not an activity. It means stepping away from effort to foster stillness for contemplation. Even successful people need these sanctuaries to remain fully realized persons, not just productive units. Voters may not fully trust candidates who **don't show these personal sides**.

query: What does the R+L=J theory claim about Jon Snow's parentage in Game of Thrones?

documents.text: Warning: This post contains spoilers that prove fan theories can sometimes turn out true. It's a weird world. Where were you when a fan theory actually came true? R+L=J is something most Game of Thrones fans have heard about for years, but on Season 6's very bloody and eventful finale, the show finally confirmed the truth about Jon Snow's lineage. Well, basically. If your GoT math is a little dusty, the R+L=J theory, lovingly explained here, basically posits that Jon Snow was not a bastard Ned Stark brought home from Robert Baratheon's rebellion. In fact, the theory claims, Jon is the son of Ned Stark's sister Lyanna and her captor Rhaegar Targaryen, older brother to Daenerys. Clues in the books, hints on the show and wild conversations have led many fans to this conclusion. In the finale, Bran Stark, through his three-eyed raven powers, observed Ned finding his sister in the terribly-named Tower of Joy and taking her new baby as she slowly expires. So, that's it right? We can all go home and pat ourselves on the back for being so smart right? Eh, not exactly. While now we do know that Jon is not a bastard and indeed does have Stark blood flowing through his know-nothing veins, Lyanna never explicitly tells who the father is. The show creators have her whisper it into Ned's ear because they love more rampant speculation. But that didn't stop a self-congratulatory internet from loving itself. Tbh I'm so happy about R L = J because we got there just by the small hints scattered here and there and it's so satisfying #GameOfThrones — drama queen (@CiaoHalsey) June 27, 2016 R L = J. My favorite theory finally come to life. #GameofThrones pic.twitter.com/pNvbIKvPCa — Haley Beth (@lil_holmie94) June 27, 2016 R+L=J. It's done. We did it. It is law. It is canon. We've done our job guys. Cheers to the old gods and the new. #GoTFinale — MsStark (@notWendySon) June 27, 2016 IT IS CONFIRMED GUYS, AFTER YEARS THE R L = J IS CONFIRMED #GameofThrones pic.twitter.com/paM3Cqg0wI — clary • (@skywalkerules) June 27, 2016 Well, done, internet. We're proud of you. Have something to add to this story? Share it in the comments.

Warning: Contains spoilers for the Game of Thrones Season 6 finale. LONDON — Back in Season 1 of Game of Thrones, you probably never thought you'd be crying over the death of a Lannister — but here we are. Take a bow, Tommen Baratheon. You were too damn good for the world of Westeros. Although a lot of us predicted that Tommen would be killed during the Season 6 finale — some thought he'd be killed by the Mountain, while others thought he'd burn in the fire at the Great Sept — his actual death was much more poignant than that. Tommen's face when he sees the Great Sept burning says it all. The kid is completely distraught. Image: hbo In that moment, Tommen knows that Margaery — who, it turns out, he really did love a hell of a lot — has likely died. He also knows, at least on some level, that his mother is the one responsible. The fact that he takes his crown off before jumping is significant. This could obviously mean a few things, but to us it seems to signify that Tommen doesn't want any part of the game — or any part of the brutal world of Westeros — any more. Essentially, it's his suicide note. The way the whole scene is done — the lingering shot of the open window and the complete lack of music — makes the whole thing even more upsetting. Damn Tommen really loved Margarey. Now Cersei's prophecy has finally come true in full. 💔 #GameofThrones — Zakiya Jamal (@ZakiyaJamal) June 27, 2016 At the end of the day, Tommen was the antithesis of Joffrey and Cersei — he was just too nice for the backstabbing harshness of the Game of Thrones world. RIP, Tommen. Image: hbo Have something to add to this story? Share it in the comments.

Warning: Spoilers ahead.

The "three-headed dragon" prophecy, a pivotal element, posits three riders for three dragons. Daenerys Targaryen is confirmed to ride Drogon. The central enigma remains the identity of the individuals who will command the remaining two formidable wyrms. A persistent and compelling hypothesis suggests the prophesied trio will consist of Daenerys, Jon Snow, and Tyrion Lannister.

This theory's foundation rests on the premise that both Jon Snow and Tyrion harbor secret Targaryen lineage. Jon's parentage has been definitively established as Rhaegar Targaryen and Lyanna Stark. Tyrion's lineage is speculatively attributed to Aerys II Targaryen and Joanna Lannister. The probability of all three dragon riders sharing Targaryen blood significantly bolsters the relevance of these parentage theories. If both hypotheses are substantiated, Jon and Tyrion emerge as exceptionally potent candidates.

The Season 6 finale provided substantial narrative progression. The R+L=J theory was unequivocally confirmed, solidifying Jon Snow's status as half-Targaryen. The episode depicted Lyanna Stark's poignant demise during childbirth. As astutely observed by a Reddit contributor post-episode, this revelation establishes a singular commonality among Daenerys, Jon, and Tyrion. While seemingly an improbable nexus, its potency derives from the unique shared trajectories of these three characters.

Each has surmounted formidable obstacles to attain positions of significant authority. They have all experienced arduous formative years. Their respective odysseys commenced from ostracized circumstances, compelling them to perpetually contend for their legitimate standing. Tyrion currently occupies a crucial position as Hand of the Queen, operating in close proximity to Daenerys. Earlier in the season, his discernible affinity for dragons was evident.

Should the theory of his Targaryen parentage be corroborated – and it is plausible that figures such as Varys possess this critical intelligence, awaiting an opportune juncture to impart it to Tyrion – the "three-headed dragon" prophecy would be unequivocally validated. Even in the absence of confirmation regarding Tyrion's Targaryen heritage, he still presents a more probable candidate than most to mount Rhaegal or Viserion. Apart from Daenerys, he is the sole other character to have cultivated a discernible rapport with the dragons.

The sixth season finale of *Game of Thrones*, titled "The Winds of Winter," masterfully wove together grand spectacle with moments of profound quiet, leaving an indelible mark on audiences through its striking visuals and pivotal plot turns. Key developments included the catastrophic wildfire explosion that decimated King's Landing and its populace, Jon Snow's proclamation as King in the North, and Cersei Lannister's ascension to the Iron Throne as Queen of the Seven Kingdoms. Daenerys Targaryen also finally set sail for Westeros. However, it is the episode's hushed, introspective opening sequences that truly linger in the mind.

Composer Ramin Djawadi's score begins with a delicate, somber piano melody, accompanying the silent procession of central figures confronting their destinies during Cersei's trial. This includes the Queen Mother, Tommen Baratheon, Margaery Tyrell, the High Sparrow, and Loras Tyrell. The profound silence that follows the trial's brutal conclusion is equally arresting. This quietude descends after Loras's public humiliation and subsequent injury, and after Tommen is isolated by his mother's guards. It also envelops the scene as Lancel Lannister's desperate efforts to avert the wildfire's detonation beneath the Sept prove tragically futile. This silence leads to the young king's solitary chamber. Witnessing his city consumed by flames and comprehending his immense personal losses, Tommen removes his crown. He gazes at the empty sky from his window, then makes the heart-wrenching decision to end his own life. This scene, arguably the episode's most poignant, stands in stark contrast to Samwell Tarly's exhilarating discovery within the Citadel's vast library. Both of these impactful moments would have resonated deeply even without a single word of dialogue.

Tommen's suicide serves as a stark illustration of revenge's destructive potential. Sansa Stark's chilling smile as Ramsay Bolton meets his end, torn apart by his own hounds, hints at the episode's underlying message: that such grim satisfaction is a perilous and ultimately hollow pursuit. The clearest parallels to Lady Stark's dark amusement are Cersei Lannister's gleeful annihilation of thousands to eliminate her adversaries, and Arya Stark's chilling presentation of Walder Frey's severed head, his stomach still containing the remains of the children he murdered. Cersei's subsequent actions, employing the reanimated Gregor "The Mountain" Clegane against a former captor, further underscore this theme of brutal retribution. The episode demonstrates that a simple categorization of characters as "good" or "bad" offers little solace or justification for the immense suffering inflicted throughout the narrative.

The narrative strongly suggests that the terrible vengeance witnessed in recent episodes sows the seeds for future conflict. Sansa's harrowing experiences with the Lannisters and Boltons likely render her susceptible to Petyr Baelish's machinations. He may exploit her claim to the Northern throne, particularly in opposition to her half-brother, Jon Snow. Jon Snow's earnest plea for unity, "We have to trust each other," is undermined by Sansa's own reluctance to fully depend on the Knights of the Vale. The relentless pursuit of power or revenge directly impedes the cultivation of such trust, a dynamic Petyr Baelish skillfully exploits. Notably, Jon himself refrains from executing Melisandre, even after Ser Davos presents compelling evidence of her complicity in Shireen Baratheon's tragic death, indicating a developing capacity for restraint and a more nuanced approach to justice.

In the south, Ellaria Sand and Olenna Tyrell have forged an alliance with Daenerys Targaryen and Yara Greyjoy. These formidable women, representing the last surviving powers from Dorne and the Reach respectively, command an impressive coalition. Their combined forces include the Ironborn fleet, the Dothraki warriors, the Unsullied soldiers, and Daenerys's three dragons. This formidable alliance is poised to embody the Targaryen motto, "Fire and Blood," against their enemies. However, Daenerys must still navigate the delicate balance between justice and revenge, and power and mercy. This task is significantly complicated by her allies' more aggressive inclinations; for instance, the Sand Snakes and the Queen of Thorns openly desire the complete obliteration of King's Landing.

The story is clearly progressing towards its ultimate resolution. While unpredictable elements such as the Hound and his Brotherhood, and the erratic Euron Greyjoy, remain active players, the three primary factions are now clearly defined. These are the Lannisters, governed by the ruthless Cersei in King's Landing; the North and the Vale, loosely united under Jon Snow; and Daenerys's burgeoning alliance, en route to Westeros. The advent of winter and the inevitable breach of the Wall will undoubtedly test the Seven Kingdoms, demanding a level of unity against the existential threat of the dead. Failure to unite will almost certainly result in their destruction by internal strife. The ultimate question remains: who will be left to celebrate when the true winter finally descends upon Westeros?

**Game of Thrones Season 6 Finale: A New Era of Female Power and Vengeance**

The *Game of Thrones* Season 6 finale, "The Winds of Winter," dramatically reshaped Westeros, ushering in a new era of female power and vengeance. This episode served as a powerful culmination of a season that consistently challenged traditional gender roles, signaling a deliberate departure from the patriarchal norms that have long defined the Seven Kingdoms. Showrunner David Benioff has firmly stated that this creative direction was independent of external criticism, expressing frustration with the notion that the strengthening of female roles was a direct response to backlash. Regardless of the initial impetus, the elevation of female characters throughout Season 6, culminating in this finale, is an undeniable and impactful narrative development.

### Women Seizing Power

"The Winds of Winter" showcased women not just surviving, but actively seizing and wielding power. Cersei Lannister's chilling coronation as Queen of the Seven Kingdoms was a watershed moment, solidifying the presence of women in the highest echelons of governance. We now witness female rulers in King's Landing, the formidable Ellaria Sand ruling in Dorne, and the late Olenna Tyrell, despite the tragic decimation of Highgarden, remaining a potent force. Beyond these established positions, Daenerys Targaryen, with her formidable army and dragons, and Yara Greyjoy, who has audaciously seized control of the Iron Islands, are poised to exert significant influence over the continent. Even in the North, where Jon Snow holds the title of King in the North, it was the unwavering conviction of the young Lyanna Mormont that shamed a council of seasoned men into action, ultimately placing him in that position. This demonstrates that even in traditionally male-dominated spheres, female leadership and influence are proving decisive.

### Thematic Exploration of Vengeance

Beyond the realm of governance, this season has been marked by women enacting brutal and calculated acts of revenge. Yara's audacious seizure of her uncle's fleet, while a significant power play, pales in comparison to the visceral retribution witnessed elsewhere. Daenerys' fiery annihilation of the Dothraki rapists, Sansa's meticulously planned revenge on Ramsay Bolton, and Cersei's devastating inferno that consumed her enemies at the Sept of Baelor all underscore a thematic exploration of female rage and its consequences.

Arguably the most striking and thematically resonant act of vengeance belongs to Arya Stark, fulfilling a long-anticipated plotline from George R.R. Martin's novels. The decision to bestow the gruesome act of serving Walder Frey's sons to him in a pie upon Arya offers a more potent, character-driven resolution, showcasing her transformation into a formidable and ruthless assassin. This choice, however, does raise questions about the fate of the serving girl whose identity Arya assumed, adding a layer of moral complexity to Arya's actions.

### Cautionary Paths Ahead

Despite the compelling narrative of female empowerment and the subversion of traditional power structures, it is crucial to remember that *Game of Thrones* is only six seasons into an anticipated eight-season saga. As Daenerys herself has observed, maintaining control within the intricate "wheel of power" is an arduous feat, fraught with peril and requiring constant vigilance. David Benioff, in a post-episode interview, characterized Arya's descent into darkness as a "worrisome narrative," stating, "If you love Arya you have to be worried about where she goes from there." This sentiment suggests that the path of vengeance, while empowering, can also lead to a dangerous and morally ambiguous future.

This same cautious perspective should likely be applied to Sansa Stark, particularly in light of her own unsettling smile as Ramsay Bolton met his gruesome end. While Sansa demonstrated profound strength throughout the season, including her apology to Jon and her decisive rejection of Littlefinger's manipulative advances, the lingering impact of her trauma is undeniably evident. These harrowing experiences have instilled deep-seated trust issues and a burgeoning hunger for power, impulses that may very well lead to a significant rift between her and Jon in future seasons as their differing approaches to leadership and justice come into conflict.

### Familial Discord and Prophecy

Shifting focus to familial discord, Tommen Baratheon's tragic demise appears poised to create a definitive schism between Cersei and Jaime Lannister. The novels contain a prophecy, largely omitted by the series, suggesting that Cersei will be murdered by her younger brother. Cersei has long believed this prophecy referred to Tyrion; however, Jaime is technically younger than Cersei, raising the chilling possibility that he is destined to end her reign. His unreadable expressions throughout the finale certainly did not convey affection, hinting at a complex and potentially dark future for the incestuous siblings.

### The Formidable Female Coalition

Currently, the most formidable alliance of female power within the Seven Kingdoms appears to be the coalition comprising Daenerys Targaryen, Yara Greyjoy, Olenna Tyrell, and the Sand Snakes. This formidable group, with Tyrion Lannister and Varys in advisory capacities, is poised to bring about significant and potentially revolutionary change. Driven by a shared desire for vengeance against those who have wronged them and a commitment to forging a more just world, these women are actively dismantling existing power structures. Daenerys, in particular, has no intention of ascending to power through conventional means; her dismissal of Daario Naharis signals her evolving understanding of power consolidation, suggesting that marriage may not be a necessary component of her strategy for ruling Westeros.

While the women of Westeros remain susceptible to the whims of fate and the unpredictable nature of military conflicts, their status has fundamentally changed. They are no longer mere chattel or pawns to be manipulated by men. Although this dramatic shift may not be a direct reaction to the controversy surrounding Sansa's storyline, as Benioff suggests, it undeniably represents a profoundly welcome and impactful evolution in the narrative of *Game of Thrones*, ushering in a new era where female agency and power are at the forefront.

The enduring mystery surrounding the parentage of Jon Snow has captivated fans of "Game of Thrones" for years, sparking countless theories and fervent discussions. While speculation has ranged from the fate of Sansa Stark to the true identity of Tyrion Lannister's parents and the enigmatic Benjen Stark, one question has consistently dominated the discourse: Who are Jon Snow's real parents? The season six finale offered a significant revelation, bringing the series tantalizingly close to answering this pivotal question.

A pivotal flashback sequence revisited the events at the Tower of Joy in Dorne, a location central to many fan conjectures. Viewers were reminded of Ned Stark's confrontation with Ser Arthur Dayne outside this formidable structure, where Lyanna Stark was being held by Rhaegar Targaryen, the brother of Daenerys. Prior to Bran Stark, utilizing his extraordinary abilities to witness past events, gaining a clear view of the tower's interior, he was abruptly pulled away by the ever-present Three-Eyed Raven.

The narrative returned to this crucial moment in the finale, allowing Bran to witness Lyanna Stark giving birth to a baby boy. This infant, it was revealed, was the very child Ned Stark brought back to Winterfell, presenting him to the world as his own illegitimate son. This child, of course, is Jon Snow. The lingering question, however, pertains to Lyanna's final words to Ned just before her passing.

If the widely accepted "R+L=J" theory holds true, Lyanna's whispered words would have identified Rhaegar Targaryen as the father, thereby confirming Jon Snow's lineage as a potent blend of Stark and Targaryen blood. Her desperate plea, "They'll kill him," underscores the perilous implications of this revelation. Had King Robert Baratheon discovered the existence of a child with Targaryen heritage, Jon Snow's life would have been in immediate jeopardy, as his bloodline would have presented a formidable claim to the Iron Throne.

In an act of profound protection, Ned Stark declared the child his own, a deliberate deception designed to shield Jon from Robert's wrath. While the television series has yet to offer an official, explicit confirmation, the events depicted in this scene lend substantial credence to the prevailing theory. Indeed, the revelation, much like Jon Snow's own resurrection, was a development anticipated by many, including the showrunners D.B. Weiss and David Benioff. Their prescient understanding of Jon's parentage was instrumental in convincing George R.R. Martin to entrust them with the monumental task of adapting his epic saga for the screen.

The climactic episode, "The Winds of Winter," definitively resolved the enduring fan theory concerning Jon Snow's parentage, meticulously detailing the events at the Tower of Joy. The narrative confirmed that a young Ned Stark discovered his sister, Lyanna, mortally wounded following a procedure akin to a medieval Cesarean section. He was sworn to secrecy regarding the child's paternity, specifically that Rhaegar Targaryen, the eldest son of the Mad King, was the father. Given the episode's narrative trajectory, the acknowledgment of the "R+L=J" equation by showrunners David Benioff and D.B. Weiss felt like a deliberate validation for the audience. The scene's artistic execution, from Lyanna's repeated plea, "Promise me, Ned," to the visual transition from the infant's dark eyes to Jon's troubled gaze, was particularly effective. While the sixth season of Game of Thrones occasionally grappled with justifying its more tangential plotlines as it approached its conclusion, with some narrative threads seemingly destined for permanent incompletion (such as Margaery Tyrell's arc), others, like The Hound's continued presence, suggest future developments. However, the satisfaction derived from witnessing these pivotal narrative elements coalesce into a coherent whole was substantial.

Lenika Cruz noted that the long-anticipated scene of Daenerys Targaryen's voyage to Westeros, a moment fans had clamored for, was finally delivered. "The Winds of Winter" presented several significant narrative resolutions within its 70-minute runtime, enhanced by Miguel Sapochnik's masterful direction. The episode underscored the advent of a new era through events such as Qyburn's network of informants assassinating Grand Maester Pycelle, Littlefinger's declaration to Sansa that "The past is gone for good," and Jon Snow's coronation as the "White Wolf," succeeding his brother, the "Young Wolf." Olenna Tyrell's confrontation with Cersei, accusing her of stealing the future, further emphasized this shift.

Despite the introduction of new developments, a sense of cyclical recurrence permeated certain plot points. The Red Wedding's architect met a fate reminiscent of the original massacre, and a council of Northern lords proclaimed Jon Snow the "King in the North." Littlefinger once again proposed a partnership with Sansa, and Daenerys embarked on her journey with a formidable army. Her pattern of dismissing men who professed love continued, mirroring earlier instances. Jon's merciful dismissal of Melisandre echoed Daenerys's treatment of Jorah Mormont and Ned Stark's past interactions with Cersei. Cersei's continued familial losses and the ascension of another ruler on the Iron Throne who favored wildfire underscored recurring themes.

Although "The Winds of Winter" revisited familiar narrative structures, it avoided feeling redundant. Much like the predictable yet distinct nature of seasonal changes, each subtle re-exploration of established patterns within the finale felt purposeful and essential. The parallels drawn between characters, such as Arya and Cersei's shared promise to their victims regarding their final vision, and Cersei's methods evoking Ramsay Bolton's brutality, added layers of poignancy. Tyrion and Qyburn's appointments as Hand of the Queen represented significant career advancements. While the audience and characters alike are eager to progress, the profound, almost prescient, influence of history imbues every unfolding moment with significant weight.

Warning: Like winter, Game of Thrones spoilers are coming. House Stark officially slayed the Bolton House flay. The opening credits to the Game of Thrones Season 6 finale unleashed one last, symbolic dog on Ramsay Bolton by replacing his family sigil with that of the Starks. As you may fondly remember in episode nine, Jon Snow defeated Ramsay Bolton (née Snow) in the Battle of the Bastards to reclaim his rightful place as Lord of Winterfell. query: What pivotal deadline are Florida counties facing regarding the election recount, and what impact could it have on the Senate and gubernatorial races?

documents.text: Florida’s election recount reaches a pivotal point on Thursday as most counties across the state complete reviews that could determine the next senator and governor in one of America’s top political battlegrounds. Many of the swing state’s 67 counties have finished running their ballots through tallying machines for a second time to decide US Senate and governor’s races but, more than a week after midterms election day, major Democratic strongholds are still struggling amid partisan recriminations, a barrage of lawsuits and untested allegations of fraud. Barring a dramatic last-minute court-mandated extension, Florida counties face a 3pm ET deadline to wrap up their machine recounts. Some counties have warned that they may not be able to make the deadline. On Wednesday, the US president once again stirred the pot by claiming – without evidence – that people wear disguises to vote illegally in Florida. “The Republicans don’t win and that’s because of potentially illegal votes,” Trump told the conservative Daily Caller website. “When people get in line that have absolutely no right to vote and they go around in circles. Sometimes they go to their car, put on a different hat, put on a different shirt, come in and vote again. Nobody takes anything. It’s really a disgrace what’s going on.” He also called for new national ID laws with a bizarre assertion: “If you buy a box of cereal – you have a voter ID.” 'Stop lying' about Florida recounts, Democrats warn Trump Read more The state’s Republican governor, Rick Scott, running closely against the incumbent Democrat Bill Nelson for the Senate seat, has also asserted that the election is being stolen. The state elections department and the Florida department of law enforcement, both run by Republican appointees, deny seeing any evidence of fraud. With the eyes of the political world on Florida, still haunted by Bush v Gore in the 2000 presidential election, the malaise deepened on Thursday when Susan Bucher, elections supervisor of Palm Beach county, admitted that it only has “a slim chance” of meeting the 3pm deadline because of dated technology. Bucher and other officials were still feeding ballots into a machine that kept stopping and starting, the Washington Post reported. “We were very close to the end, and our machines went down,” she was quoted as saying. There was some better news from Broward county, which said it had finished its recount except for fewer than 400 damaged ballots. The county’s elections supervisor Brenda Snipes has been the focus of fierce criticism. But in a further complication, a federal judge ruled that at least 4,000 voters whose ballots were rejected because their signatures did not match will get two days to correct the problem. Judge Mark Walker of the US District Court in Tallahassee ruled that the signature match rule was applied unlawfully because voters were given no chance to correct the discrepancy and prove their identity. Marc Elias, lead recount lawyer for the Nelson campaign, welcomed the decision and said: “The court’s ruling impacts thousands of ballots, and that number will likely increase as larger counties like Broward add their ballots to the total pool which can be cured.” Scott’s campaign said it was appealing the decision. The Senate race between him and Nelson is so close that it is expected to move to a manual recount starting at 7am on Friday. The deadline is 12 noon on Sunday. Eight million votes were cast in Florida and the counties’ deadline is Thursday at 3pm, pending court action by Democrats seeking to extend it. Indeed, the disputed election is proving a bonanza for lawyers on both sides. Six federal lawsuits have been filed so far in the state capital, Tallahassee. State law requires a machine recount in races where the margin is less than 0.5 percentage points. The machine recount may essentially bring a conclusion to the governor’s race. Republican Ron DeSantis leads Democrat Andrew Gillum by 0.41 percentage points in unofficial results, but the election won’t be certified until Tuesday. Scott has agreed to step down from the state panel responsible for certifying the final results. State law requires a machine recount in races where the margin is less than 0.5 percentage points. In the Senate race, Scott’s lead over Nelson was 0.14 points. Yet on Wednesday, Scott was at the US Capitol in Washington, standing at Mitch McConnell’s left shoulder as the majority leader welcomed Republican senators who will take their seats in January when the new Congress is sworn in. He ignored a shouted question about whether he still contends there was fraud in the election. Lawyers for Democrats asked a federal judge on Wednesday to set aside the state law mandating that mailed-in votes be thrown out if the signature on the envelope does not match the signature on file. Nelson’s campaign has also filed a lawsuit seeking public records from a north Florida elections supervisor who allowed voters in the Republican-heavy Bay county to email their ballots in apparent violation of state law. The US district judge Mark Walker, referring to an episode of the science fiction series Star Trek, said during a hearing: “I feel a little bit like Captain Kirk in the episode with the Tribbles where they start to multiply.”

Zina Rodriguez, a registered Democrat and behavioral health care consultant from Lake Worth, Florida, experienced significant frustration with mail-in ballot signature verification when her ballot for the Nov. 6 election was rejected. The rejection notice arrived at 7 p.m. the night before the election, crucially, two hours after the appeal deadline had passed. Ms. Rodriguez, 47, who was accustomed to strict handwriting enforcement from her Catholic school days in the Bronx, was surprised her ballot was disqualified due to a signature mismatch.

Upon protesting at the Palm Beach County Board of Elections the following morning, she discovered the discrepancy was with a signature from her driver's license, a signature hastily scrawled on an electronic pad two years prior. "There were 13 amendments on that ballot. The only reason I chose to write in was because I wanted time to research all the questions. I was fulfilling my responsibility," Ms. Rodriguez stated. "All of that got thrown away because I wanted to get out of the D.M.V. office as fast as I can. It is incredibly upsetting."

This experience underscores a major hurdle in election administration, especially with mail-in ballots. Signature discrepancies have become a key issue in the ongoing recounts and subsequent legal challenges across Florida following the 2018 election cycle.

A federal judge sharply criticized Florida on Thursday for its persistent failure to prepare for election issues, suggesting the state's recount law may conflict with the U.S. Supreme Court's 2000 presidential decision. U.S. District Judge Mark Walker stated, "We have been the laughing-stock of the world, election after election, and we chose not to fix this." He expressed frustration not only with state lawmakers but also with Palm Beach County officials, who he felt should have ensured adequate equipment for the recount. Walker also voiced dissatisfaction with the prospect of unlimited extensions for recount deadlines.

The core issue, according to Walker, stems from a Florida Legislature-passed recount law that appears to contravene the 2000 Bush v. Gore ruling by mandating procedures that do not account for potential problems. Six election-related lawsuits are currently pending in Tallahassee. Earlier on Thursday, Walker had ordered that voters be given until Saturday at 5 p.m. to present valid identification and correct any ballots with mismatched signatures that had not yet been counted.

Florida's 67 counties faced a Thursday 3 p.m. deadline to complete recounts that could decide the next senator and governor in a key political state. Republicans announced their intention to appeal immediately. State officials testified that nearly 4,000 mail-in ballots were set aside because local officials determined the signature on the envelope did not match the signature on file. If these voters can now prove their identity, their votes will be counted and included in the final official returns due from each county by Sunday noon.

The ongoing recount process risks extending into the weekend. The election supervisor in Palm Beach County, a Democratic stronghold, indicated they might not meet the initial Thursday deadline. U.S. Sen. Bill Nelson and Democrats are seeking to have this looming deadline waived, and other lawsuits could introduce further delays. A swift resolution seems unlikely more than a week after Election Day.

Once the machine recount is finished, state law mandates a hand review for races with margins under 0.25 percentage points. This will almost certainly trigger another recount in the Senate race, where unofficial results show Republican Gov. Rick Scott leading Nelson by 0.14 percentage points. Furthermore, the election will not be certified until Tuesday, even though the machine recount may effectively conclude the governor's race, with Republican Ron DeSantis leading Democrat Andrew Gillum by 0.41 percentage points in unofficial tallies.

Nelson, a three-term incumbent, defended his legal strategy that led to Walker's ruling, stating Wednesday that "it remains the most important goal of my campaign to make sure that every lawful vote be counted correctly in this Senate race, and that Floridians' right to participate in this process is protected." Republicans, however, contend in their own lawsuits and motions that Democrats are attempting to alter the rules after unfavorable election results. "We will continue to fight to defend Florida law and uphold the will of the voters," said Chris Hartline, a spokesman for Scott.

Nelson and Democrats had urged Walker to order the counting of all mail-in ballots rejected due to mismatched signatures, arguing that local election officials are not handwriting experts. Walker declined this request. "Let this court be clear: It is not ordering county canvassing boards to count every mismatched vote, sight unseen," Walker wrote in his 34-page ruling. "Rather, the county supervisors of elections are directed to allow those voters who should have had an opportunity to cure their ballots in the first place to cure their vote-by-mail and provisional ballots now, before the second official results are fully counted. This should give sufficient time, within the state's and counties current administrative constraints, for Florida's voters to ensure their votes will be counted."

Lauren Schenone, a spokeswoman for Scott, described Walker's ruling as "baseless" and expressed confidence it would be overturned by the Atlanta-based appellate court. These developments are fueling frustration among both Democrats and Republicans. Democrats are pushing for state officials to take all necessary steps to ensure every eligible vote is counted. Republicans, including President Donald Trump, have alleged without evidence that voter fraud is threatening to sway races away from the GOP.

The timeline for state officials to receive recount results from all counties remains uncertain. Tallying machines in Palm Beach County overheated earlier this week, leading to discrepancies in the recount of 174,000 early voting ballots and requiring staff to redo their work. The county's Supervisor of Elections, Susan Bucher, noted that while the machines had undergone maintenance before the election, "I don't think they were designed to work 24/7."

President Donald Trump has voiced strong criticism of Broward County Elections Supervisor Brenda Snipes, labeling her a "disgrace" and calling for her immediate removal. In an exclusive interview with The Daily Caller, the President detailed his significant concerns regarding Snipes and the controversial Florida recount that followed the midterm elections, which heavily impacted the state's closely contested Senate and gubernatorial races.

Snipes has faced considerable criticism from both the media and Republican officials due to documented issues within her department's vote reporting processes. Following the initial declaration of the Florida Senate race winner, Republican Governor Rick Scott, supervisors in Broward and Palm Beach counties continued to update vote totals, a practice that contravened Florida election law regarding reporting deadlines. Snipes also reportedly disregarded a court order mandating the public review of "over-voted" and "under-voted" absentee ballots. Furthermore, she admitted to "accidentally" mixing illegal ballots with legitimate ones, rendering the illegal ballots unidentifiable and unusable.

President Trump described Snipes as a "disaster," highlighting her history of alleged election improprieties, including claims of ballot destruction and the unlawful opening of mail-in ballots. Recalling his own experience in Florida during the 2016 election, where Broward County's results were delayed, Trump suggested Snipes possesses the ability to manipulate the electoral system. He dismissed notions of incompetence, stating, "When they call this woman incompetent, they’re wrong. She's very competent but in a bad way." He firmly believes she should have been removed much earlier, suggesting her dismissal should have occurred "in the middle of this mix-up." Governor Scott had indeed been considering Snipes' removal.

The President reiterated his belief that Florida's election results should have been finalized on election night, expressing concern over the vote tabulation process. "Well, many votes were added to that, and you know what’s going on. And now they have mixed them up. They mixed the votes up and now you can’t find the ones that were put in, they just put ’em in to a batch," he explained.

In light of these concerns, President Trump renewed his call for national voter identification laws, condemning what he described as widespread illegal voting. "The Republicans don’t win and that’s because of potentially illegal votes," Trump asserted, believing fraudulent ballots significantly influence election outcomes. He illustrated potential voter fraud by describing individuals voting multiple times by altering their appearance, stating, "If you buy a box of cereal — you have a voter ID." Trump criticized opponents of voter ID laws, noting, "They try to shame everybody by calling them racist, or calling them something, anything they can think of, when you say you want voter ID. But voter ID is a very important thing."

Looking ahead to the 2020 presidential election, Trump emphasized the necessity of removing Brenda Snipes and her associates to prevent a recurrence of electoral irregularities. "First thing you do is fire her and her cronies. You get ’em out," he advised. He recalled the prolonged delays in receiving Florida's results in 2016, noting, "Remember how long it took to get Florida? But I had, fortunately, enough votes, and they were sitting there waiting. They said, ‘Broward County is not reporting.’ This went on for hours." He also commented on how vote reporting from different regions, like the Panhandle and Broward County, impacted the final results.

At the time of this report, election workers across Florida were diligently working to meet a critical Thursday 3:00 p.m. deadline for finalizing statewide recounts for the Senate and gubernatorial races. Amber Athey of TheDC also contributed to this report.

President Donald J. Trump's recent statements about the Florida election recounts have sparked significant debate. He claimed that voters wore disguises to cast multiple ballots. This idea suggests people would go to their cars, change clothes, and vote again. However, this scenario has been widely disproven. It is not practical. No election monitors or law enforcement agencies have found any proof. This includes the Florida Department of Law Enforcement and state election monitors in Broward County. These groups report to Governor Rick Scott.

Even without evidence, President Trump has frequently alleged widespread voter fraud. He specifically focused on Broward County. He said, "The Republicans don’t win and that’s because of potentially illegal votes. When people get in line that have absolutely no right to vote and they go around in circles. Sometimes they go to their car, put on a different hat, put on a different shirt, come in and vote again. Nobody takes anything. It’s really a disgrace what’s going on." This story does not match the facts about election security.

The idea of voter fraud using elaborate disguises seems like a made-up story. It sounds more like old movies than real elections. Experts and election officials state that very few votes are ever cast illegally. If someone wanted to cheat in an election, they would use much smarter methods. Trump's described method is very unlikely to work.

This specific claim was shared by news sources like the Daily Caller. It was also spread on social media by people like Governor Rick Scott, Senator Marco Rubio, and Sean Hannity from Fox News. This has added to the spread of false information about the Florida election recounts. While Brenda Snipes, the Broward Supervisor of Elections, has faced questions, no official group has found any fraud like Trump described. The president's words, filled with unproven claims and a poor understanding of how elections work, have been called inflammatory and misleading. Reports suggest Twitter might take action against Trump's account. However, any steps are not expected until 2019. By then, public discussion may have already been harmed.

President Donald Trump has amplified unsubstantiated claims of fraud in Florida's high-stakes Senate and gubernatorial election recounts, a pattern consistent with his past use of conspiracy theories. These allegations, often initiated via social media, have targeted election officials and processes in the Sunshine State.

Trump's accusations began shortly after the midterm election polls closed, with a tweet warning of a "big corruption scandal" in Florida as vote tabulation proved challenging, particularly in Broward County. He later referenced "massively infected" ballots and accused Democrats of attempting to "falsify" his 2016 victory in the state, all without providing concrete evidence.

Further escalating his claims, Trump suggested in an interview that voters were using disguises to cast multiple ballots. He described a scenario where individuals would change their attire to vote more than once, calling the situation a "disgrace." He also drew a parallel between voter identification requirements and purchasing everyday items, implying a lack of stringent verification.

These assertions represent the latest in a series of conspiracy theories embraced by Trump concerning the Florida elections. The machine recount of approximately 8 million votes concluded Thursday afternoon after a federal judge denied a request to extend the deadline. Incumbent Democratic Senator Bill Nelson trails Republican Rick Scott by roughly 13,000 votes.

Experts in political conspiracy theories note that the Florida recounts possess all the elements conducive to fueling Trump's long-standing inclination for embracing claims of subterfuge. The uncertain outcome, high drama, and complexity of the vote-counting process make it difficult for the public to discern fact from fiction.

Joseph Uscinski, a political scientist at the University of Miami, stated that Trump's actions align with his established patterns. He explained that the underlying message of Trump's conspiracy theories often involves a critique of "elites" betraying the interests of the American people.

Trump's strategy of employing conspiracy theories to energize voters and divert media attention is a recognized aspect of his communication style. His history includes questioning President Barack Obama's citizenship and making unsubstantiated claims about political opponents during his 2016 campaign.

The current situation in Florida echoes past controversies, including the contested 2000 presidential election. Election officials are once again navigating a statewide recount amidst numerous lawsuits. An email reviewed by the USA TODAY NETWORK indicated a Florida Democratic party leader had encouraged volunteers to submit altered election forms to address ballot signature issues, raising further questions about the vote-counting process.

While initial results showed Republican candidates Rick Scott and Ron DeSantis leading in the Senate and gubernatorial races respectively, these margins have narrowed as outstanding ballots are counted. Trump and other Republicans have focused on Broward County, which has faced challenges in meeting reporting deadlines. They also cited a past ruling against Broward County Supervisor of Elections Brenda Snipes for prematurely destroying ballots.

Despite these concerns, there is no evidence of tampering with the election outcome by Snipes or other officials. Democrats have attributed the issues to outdated voting machines and underfunded election offices.

Political scientists like Joanne Miller of the University of Delaware emphasize that the Florida recount is fertile ground for conspiracy theories due to high uncertainty and anxiety. She noted that the competitive nature of elections and the high stakes involved can lead individuals to embrace unsubstantiated claims as a means of protecting their self-esteem, suggesting it is more comforting to believe an outcome was due to fraud than to acknowledge the opposing party's more resonant policy positions.

While other prominent figures, such as Hillary Clinton and Richard Nixon, have engaged in conspiracy theorizing, Trump's frequent and public embrace of such claims without evidence sets him apart. Experts suggest this approach, even predating his political career, serves to keep adversaries and allies off balance. Trump biographer Gwenda Blair described him as a "salesman" whose success relies on maintaining constant engagement.

WASHINGTON — President Donald Trump sat for an exclusive Oval Office interview Wednesday with The Daily Caller’s Saagar Enjeti and Benny Johnson for approximately 31 minutes. WATCH: The transcript is as follows: THE DAILY CALLER: Let’s get the news of the day out at the top. There’s all of these things that are being discussed about the chief of staff [John Kelly], about Kirstjen Nielsen. Why are you disappointed in the DHS secretary and is John Kelly going to be staying in his job right now? POTUS: So, you know, always in an administration after the midterms you make changes, so, I’m looking at things and I’ve got a lot of options. A lot of people want to come in. A lot of politicians that have had very successful careers that are very good want to come in. So I’m looking at things — haven’t made a decision yet. I will be making a decision on Homeland shortly. But I have not made decisions yet. But I will be making changes on various things. Overall, I have a very good cabinet. I think our cabinet’s great. You know, you’re talking about a few names, you’re not talking about many. Uh, we’ve been doing incredibly on trade deals and trade with my whole staff of traders, because that’s what they are. What we’ve done on trade deals is incredible — with Mexico, with Canada, with South Korea — taking deals that were horrible and making great deals. And now we’re in the midst of China and others. But we have a great cabinet, we have — we’ll see, uh, there will be some changes made before the end of the year. THE DAILY CALLER: Sure. Could you tell us where your thinking is currently on the attorney general position? I know you’re happy with Matthew Whitaker, do you have any names? Chris Christie — POTUS: Matthew Whitaker is a very respected man. He’s — and he’s, very importantly, he’s respected within DOJ. I heard he got a very good decision, I haven’t seen it. Kellyanne, did I hear that? WHITE HOUSE ADVISER KELLYANNE CONWAY: 20 pages. POTUS: A 20-page? THE DAILY CALLER: It just came out right before this, sir. POTUS: Well, I heard it was a very strong opinion. Uh, which is good. But [Whitaker] is just somebody that’s very respected. I knew him only as he pertained, you know, as he was with Jeff Sessions. And, you know, look, as far as I’m concerned this is an investigation that should have never been brought. It should have never been had. It’s something that should have never been brought. It’s an illegal investigation. And you know, it’s very interesting because when you talk about not Senate confirmed, well, Mueller’s not Senate confirmed. THE DAILY CALLER: Right. POTUS: He’s heading this whole big thing, he’s not Senate confirmed. So anyway, I have a lot of respect for Matt Whitaker, based primarily on reputation. And I think he’s really — I think a lot of people are starting to come out very much in favor of him during this period of time. THE DAILY CALLER: What about who will eventually replace him, sir? POTUS: Well, I’m looking at a lot of people. I have been called by so many people wanting that job. We have some great people. In the meantime, I think Matt’s going to do a fantastic job. THE DAILY CALLER: Banning Jim Acosta. Taking his White House credentials. His hard pass. Is this going to be something that happens with repeated nature inside of the White House? Who you feel are disrespecting the White House, sir. POTUS: Your world has changed quite a bit and you have grandstanders much more than you did before. He’s just a grandstander. He’s just an average guy who’s a grandstander. And he’s got the guts to stand up and, you know, and shout. It’s primarily led by CNN because they pay him to do that. CNN is low on the ratings now, relative to Fox and others, and they’re doing very poorly actually. But because of me — the age of Trump — because of me, they’re doing better than they’ve done but they’re doing very poorly in compared to others. Very very poorly. And, um, and getting worse. They’ve lost a tremendous amount of credibility and, you know, Jim Acosta’s just somebody that gets up and grandstands. He doesn’t even know what he’s asking half of the time. So, we’ll see how the court case goes. It’s today. THE DAILY CALLER: Are you confident that you’re going to win the court case? POTUS: I don’t know, I think that we should. Certainly, you shouldn’t be able to go up into a White House and, on principle, it’s very disrespectful to the rest of the press. You were actually getting up to do a question. THE DAILY CALLER: That’s right, sir. POTUS: — And this guy’s screaming. And I kept saying to you, ‘come on,’ you didn’t give me much help. I kept saying, ‘give me the question already.’ But, you know, he just screamed and, you know, you knew it because you’re standing, you didn’t know what to do, and I understand that. He was very rude to the young lady. I won’t, I won’t even — who knows. Who knows. Certainly, he didn’t act very respectful to the press. He’s actually more disrespectful, I think, to the rest of the media. Because he gets up and grandstands and he wants to ask three or four questions and everyone else is, you know, you’re trying to get one question in and the room was packed with a couple of hundred people that want to ask questions. So, I think he’s very disrespectful to the media, I think he’s very disrespectful to the office, and I think he’s bad for the public. You know, when I say that the fake news is the enemy of the people, it really is. A lot of the animosity that we have in our country is because of fake news. They’re so angry at the news. They get it. You guys are at my rallies all the time, you see the anger when I mention the words ‘fake news’ and they turn around. And they use CNN because they — it just sort of works for them I guess. But it’s ABC, it’s — NBC is maybe worse than anybody. I mean, NBC’s a total fraud as far as I’m concerned. Their news is disgusting. But I think NBC is as bad as anybody. You look at — and CBS — you look at what’s going on with the fake news and the people get it. Now they get it — you know they had a very high approval rating before I became president and I think it’s actually a great achievement of mine. Their approval rating now is down as low as just about anybody. And much lower than your president. I actually have good approval ratings, which nobody ever writes. I was at 51, I guess, with Rasmussen the other day. So I will say that I really think that when you have guys like Acosta, I think they’re bad for the country. Because they show how fake it all is. And it’s a grandstander, and we’ll see how the court rules. You know, then they talk, ‘Oh, freedom of the press.’ But can you really have — is it freedom of the press? It’s actually the opposite. Is it freedom of the press when somebody comes in and starts screaming questions and won’t sit down after having answered a couple of them? And then won’t sit down and then I can’t ask you guys because he’s standing — I don’t think that’s freedom of the press, I actually think that’s the opposite. THE DAILY CALLER: Which is an important follow-up question, why this administration? This doesn’t seem to be the norm. It certainly wasn’t the norm in previous administrations, that reporters behave in this capacity. POTUS: Well, I think they behaved badly. I remember Sam Donaldson was terrible at two presidents, and, you know, we tend to forget. I think that now it’s become, with cable television, playing such a role, although, you know, cable television was supposed to be a dying medium. And because of me it’s now hotter than it’s ever been. But someday I won’t be here and it will die like you’ve never seen. And so will The New York Times — will die — and every one of them will just be dead. I mean, look, I remember picking up before I announced for president, I picked up The New York Times and I said to somebody, ‘Boy, this paper is dead, look at it.’ The paper was dead. It was like a leaflet that you hand out at the supermarket, and now it’s a vibrant paper. But you look at the stories, many of the stories on the front page are about me. You know, all my life I told this story, had stories on the front page. A few, not a big deal. Which wasn’t bad, you know, maybe seven, but, you know, a few. And, you know, now if I have a few each day it’s surprisingly low. But it has made a big difference. THE DAILY CALLER: Sir, I do want to turn to policy. POTUS: That’s why I always joke when I say they’ll all be endorsing me. Cause I don’t know what happens to their business after I’m gone. THE DAILY CALLER: Sir, right now, in 2010 we saw several pieces of major legislation passed in a lame-duck Congress. What can we expect your and the Republican agenda to be in this Congress? Is it going to be an immigration fix? What about criminal justice reform? What are the two to three things you’re looking at? POTUS: We’re working on many things. Criminal justice reform we’re working on very hard. We have a meeting today, do you know about that? We have a meeting today. THE DAILY CALLER: We heard about that. POTUS: Get these two in, alright? I think we have a chance at that. We should be able to fix health care. We should be able — THE DAILY CALLER: Just one second, sir, on that criminal justice bill. Is that the Jared Kushner-backed bill that you want to focus on? POTUS: The answer is I’m looking at it very closely, okay? I am. It’s a good thing. You know, Texas is backing it, if you look at Mississippi and Georgia and a lot of other places, they believe in it, those governors, and they’re conservative people. Rick Perry’s a big fan. You know, a lot of people are backing it. Look at the people that are backing it. Even, you know, like Mike Lee, he votes against a lot of things and we respect Mike and Mike is backing it. We have a lot of people that are backing this. THE DAILY CALLER: What about immigration, sir? Are you willing to shut down the government if you don’t get a certain set of policies? POTUS: I may be. I may be. I’ll have to see how it plays out. But I may very well be willing to shut down the government. I think it’s horrible what’s happening and, you know, building the wall, it’s in smaller stages, we can build it very quickly. I’m building the wall in smaller stages and we moved the military there, we put up barbed wire, we did all sorts of things. You have to have a barrier. You have to have a barrier. Look, we have a chance of, they can do presidential harassment, put very simply, and I’ll be very good at handling that and I think I’ll be better than anybody in the history of this office. And in a certain way I look forward to it because I actually think it’s good for me politically, because everyone knows it’s pure harassment. Just like the witch hunt, the Mueller witch hunt. It’s pure harassment. It’s horrible. It’s horrible that they’re allowed to get away with it. Again, not Senate confirmed but, you know. You have 17 people — half, many of them worked for Hillary Clinton, some on the Foundation. The Hillary Clinton Foundation. I mean, you think of it. So, I think we’ll do very well if they want to play the presidential harassment game. If they play the presidential harassment game I don’t think anything’s going to be done ’cause why would I do that, okay? If they want to get things done I think it will be fantastic, I think we can get a lot done. I think we can almost get more done because they’re gonna want to prove something, too. So if they wanna do health care, we can do health care. There a very good fixes to health care that could solve a lot of problems. We’ve really, you know, terminated a lot of the Obamacare, as it was referred to. But we could do a new health care bill that would be fantastic that could take everybody’s — that could incorporate good pieces of everybody’s good ideas. So there’s a chance at that, there’s a great chance at comprehensive immigration reform. If they wanna do it, they wanna do it, they wanna do it right. And there’s a good chance at doing things that maybe you couldn’t do. Don’t forget, I didn’t really have a majority. I had one senator. And I had a few Republicans in the House. You know, a very small number. Um, and now the pressure’s on them because they’ve gotta come to me with things. Hey, the beauty is, and I’ve said this, you heard me at the news conference, I don’t know if you agree with me — let’s say I won with two Republicans or three. I’d have to make deals with Republicans. I’d have guys coming to me, you know, out of that group there’ll be people. Let’s not say they’re good or bad but they’re people: ‘well I don’t want to do this.’ Well, I’m gonna have three or more. I mean, literally, unless I had like a 25 majority, you know, something substantial, they would come to me and they would say, ‘well, I don’t like it.’ You wouldn’t even be able to get something through there. It would be too close. Where I am now, when they have a small majority, I can sit back and say, ‘hey.’ And the beauty is when it gets passed, when we pass things, we’ll get it passed in the Senate, which now we can’t because we need 10 Democrat votes. Because we’ll have Democrats and I’ll be able to get enough Republicans to pass. But the beauty is, the beauty is that we actually have something that can work much better than it worked before, because before it was politically very difficult for a lot of people. THE DAILY CALLER: While we’re on the subject of Congress, I do want to get — does Kevin McCarthy have your full endorsement in the speaker’s race? POTUS: Well, I didn’t endorse because I stay out of it, but I like Kevin a lot and I like Jim [Jordan] a lot. I like both of ’em. THE DAILY CALLER: How would you like them to work it out? POTUS: Who else is running, nobody, right? THE DAILY CALLER: That’s all there is right now. POTUS: Well, I like both of ’em a lot. I think Jim is a fantastic guy and I think Kevin’s a fantastic guy. They’re very different but Kevin is a very political person, which is good. He’s a very honorable guy, and I think he’s gonna do great, and it looks like, you know, he’ll probably get it. THE DAILY CALLER: Would you like to see Jim on the ranking member on Judiciary? POTUS: I would like to see Jim in a high position ’cause he deserves it. He’s fantastic, but I haven’t gotten into the endorsement or not. I just like both of ’em too much, too much to get very much involved. It’s something I shouldn’t be involved in. But Jim is a fantastic guy and Kevin is a fantastic guy and they’re very different and they complement each other. THE DAILY CALLER: Broward County election commissioner Brenda Snipes is in violation of Florida law. She’s in violation of Florida reporting laws, she’s in violation of a judge’s order. She’s also reportedly mixed in illegal ballots with legal ballots and asked for them to be counted. Is she behaving criminally and should she be removed? POTUS: Look, you know, the bottom line — it’s, she’s a disaster. You look at her past, she’s a disaster. Even with me. I won Florida and, you remember? That area, Broward, didn’t come in. It didn’t come in. I think Palm Beach was like — they wouldn’t call Florida, I won. Then it got to a point where I won by enough that all the votes of the people that lived there would, you know, in other words — you can only put in so many votes, although she may change that system. You can only put in a number of people that are registered voters or live there. Now they’re, I guess they were trying to take illegal voters but these are — I’ve been saying, this is a problem all over the country, by the way. This is what I’ve been saying. This is a problem in California that’s so bad of illegals voting. This is a California problem and if you notice, almost every race — I was watching today — out of like 11 races that are in question they’re gonna win all of them. The Republicans don’t win and that’s because of potentially illegal votes, which is what I’ve been saying for a long time. I have no doubt about it. And I’ve seen it, I’ve had friends talk about it when people get in line that have absolutely no right to vote and they go around in circles. Sometimes they go to their car, put on a different hat, put on a different shirt, come in and vote again. Nobody takes anything. It’s really a disgrace what’s going on. The disgrace is that, voter ID. If you buy, you know, a box of cereal, if you do anything, you have a voter ID. Well, over here, the only thing you don’t is if you’re a voter of the United States. A voter in the United States of America. I think it’s a disgrace what’s going on. Really a disgrace. And they try to shame everybody by calling them racist or calling them something, anything they can think of, when you say you want voter ID. But voter ID is a very important thing. If you look at what happened in New Hampshire, where thousands of people came up and voted from a very liberal part of Massachusetts and they came up in buses and they voted. I said, ‘what’s going on over here,’ my people said, ‘you won New Hampshire easily except they have tremendous numbers of buses coming up.’ They’re pouring up by the hundreds, buses of people getting out, voting. Then they’re supposed to go back within 90 days. And of the people that are supposed to go back, almost none of them do. In other words, they go back after the vote is over. They go back — and I think it’s like three percent — I mean, almost nobody goes back to show that, you know, that they were allowed to vote. And so what do you do? Recall the election. Recall the election. I mean, there, you should be able to recall the election. THE DAILY CALLER: You think they should call Florida right now, sir? POTUS: Uh, I think they should’ve called Florida election evening. Well, many votes were added to that, and, you know what’s going on. And now they have mixed them up. You said it better than I did. They mixed the votes up and now you can’t find the ones that were put in, they just put ’em into a batch. When they call this woman incompetent, they’re wrong. She’s very competent but in a bad way. THE DAILY CALLER: Should she be removed from office? POTUS: Oh, she should have been removed — I think she should have been removed in the middle of this mix-up. THE DAILY CALLER: How do you prevent this in 2020? From happening when you run for re-election? POTUS: First thing you do is fire her and her cronies. You get ’em out of — you get ’em out. And it should have been done, I mean, if there’s anything really that I would be — because, again, in the 2016 election, you guys remember, remember how long it took to get Florida? THE DAILY CALLER: It took a long time. POTUS: Had I not been winning Florida by more than they could — I mean, you can’t produce — if you have a million people, you can’t give 1,200,000 votes, okay? So actually, what happened is they went with fairly accurate numbers because whether I won by 10 votes or by half a million votes it didn’t matter. But I had, fortunately, enough votes, and they were sitting there waiting. They said, ‘Broward County is not reporting.’ This went on for hours. WHITE HOUSE ADVISER KELLYANNE CONWAY: The Panhandle came in an hour later. POTUS: Well, the Panhandle was so devastating to Crooked Hillary, that, frankly, it didn’t make any difference, okay? Because the Panhandle was so — it was like 98 percent. That thing came in, then all of a sudden Broward came in. And I won by, you know, I won by a lot of votes. I call it four Yankee Stadiums. THE DAILY CALLER: Sir, as a resident of both D.C. and New York City, what do you think about Amazon’s relocation and the decision of both Virginia and New York to give billions of subsidies to Amazon. Do you support that? POTUS: I think they’re paying a very big price. I think that it was a competition. I know all about those competitions, I’ve been in those competitions, you know, and it’s a lot of people, a lot of cities who are competing for it. They took the best deals. THE DAILY CALLER: So you support the cities giving them tax breaks? POTUS: Well, I think they’re giving up a lot. They’re very expensive deals. Only time will tell. Maybe Amazon will have massive competition and they won’t be the same company in five years, in which case it will be a big mistake. You know, Amazon could have — I see Walmart is doing very well and others are building sites. I think that’s going to be a very competitive business someday, Amazon. And I’m not sure that size necessarily helps when you get — THE DAILY CALLER: Do you still think they’re cheating the U.S. postal system? POTUS: Oh, I think they’re getting the bargain of the century. I think that Amazon’s getting — and that’s why I’ve asked for a review of that. And others too, you know, not just Amazon. There are classes of companies that are getting the bargain and I think that — I may be wrong about this, you’ll check — but I think the contract they signed with Amazon was a closed contract. You can’t even see it. It was a sealed contract. No, I think Amazon has the bargain of the century with the U.S. Post Office, which is losing a fortune. THE DAILY CALLER: Tucker Carlson had his house attacked, he had a mob outside of his house while his wife was home. They cracked the door — what is this violence? Where does it come from? Do you have a message for Tucker and his family? POTUS: I do, I spoke to Tucker and I think Tucker’s a great guy and I think it’s terrible, they were actually trying to break down the door. THE DAILY CALLER: How do you think the police should handle Antifa, generally? POTUS: These people, like the Antifa you’re talking about, the Antifa — they better hope that the opposition to Antifa decides not to mobilize. Because if they do, they’re much tougher. Much stronger. Potentially much more violent. And Antifa’s going to be in big trouble. But so far they haven’t done that, and that’s a good thing. But they better hope that the other side doesn’t mobilize, you understand what I’m saying. Because if you look, the other side is the military, it’s the police, it’s a lot of very strong, a lot of very tough people. Tougher than them. And smarter than them. And they’re sitting back and watching and they’re getting angrier and angrier. THE DAILY CALLER: What’s your takeaway from the 2018 election and what do you think that means for 2020 for you? POTUS: I think I did very well. Because if you look at — Obama was 60-something odd House seats and lost seven Senate seats. So we picked up three or four Senate seats depending on how it all goes — it’s a big pickup. In fact, they say in 80 years I think the presidential party’s only picked up two Senate seats, I picked up three. I mean, assuming that they don’t do any further shenanigans in Florida. Almost picked up Tester. Almost picked up, you know, if you look — and that was somebody that wasn’t even in play. And that was another one at the very last moment, all of a sudden, that was over. Almost picked up Arizona and that was another one that I question, I have to question that more strongly than our candidate, a wonderful person. But she didn’t question strongly, so I’m not going to, but I think that was very odd the way that all happened in Arizona. But we picked up — I mean, it looked like we picked up five. But probably three. It’s a lot. I think this — if I didn’t go around, and you were there, you saw the crowd — in the history of politics, and I say this proudly, I wasn’t even running. In the history of politics nobody’s ever gotten crowds like that or close because you were in those stadiums and those arenas, but outside you had many more times — you know, in Houston we had 109,000 people sign up for 22,000 seats. We actually took ads saying, ‘please don’t come’ and that helped Ted Cruz a lot. So if you look at Ted Cruz, and you look at some of the people that won, they wouldn’t have won without my helping them. And then you look at the new senators, you look at Indiana, Donnelly was not going to happen. Nobody said Donnelly was going to lose. Nobody said — even look at a case of a congressman. I couldn’t help too many congressmen because I don’t have that much time. The only congressman I went for was Andy Barr and that was in Kentucky, Mitch was there but he wasn’t running, and Rand Paul was there. Good guy, Rand Paul, by the way. And he was there, and the only congressman — I specifically went, there was no senator running in Kentucky, it was Andy Barr, and he won nicely. And he was down 10 and he won his race. Every place I went, look at Georgia, I’m assuming he wins. That’s another one now they’re trying to play around with the votes. Honestly, it’s a disgrace what’s going on. Oprah went, Obama went, and Michelle Obama went. They went, and they spent a lot of time, and I went and did a rally, and the real number was probably 55,000 people, cause, you know, were you there in Georgia? THE DAILY CALLER: Not Georgia, sir. POTUS: Because we had a hangar, another hangar holding 18,000 at the top of the hangar. These are massive, like 747 hangars. It went way back, and he won. And everybody said he wasn’t going to win. But they had Obama, Mrs. Obama and Oprah. Oprah spent three days there and every place I went, we won or came real close with areas, like, as an example, Tester. Tester, nobody wanted even to contest it. Well, look at what happened in North Dakota with Heidi. Heidi, they said don’t contest. One year ago when we were looking, they all said don’t contest Heidi, she can’t be beat. She lost by a lot. I went there three or four times. I mean, the truth is, every place I went, we either won or did well or did really well. If I didn’t go, if I didn’t do those stops — I did 31 stops in 30 days or something like that. If I didn’t do those stops, I think we would’ve lost 10 Senate seats, seven to 10 Senate seats, and we would’ve lost 60 to 70 House seats or more. By doing the stops, then again a lot of times I’d have the congressmen in the room, so they wouldn’t be the prime focus but I’d get up and I’d be able to talk about them for a couple of minutes apiece, right? If I didn’t do the stops — and I’ve been thanked by a lot of Republicans — if I didn’t do those stops we would definitely not have control of the Senate. It would be a question, so what are we up, three? Two or three. We would be down five or six or seven. And they know that. Nobody has ever had a greater impact. Well, I’ll give you another. You take Georgia. He was 10 points down when I endorsed him, he ended up winning by 40 points in the primary. He’s now in, but he was 10 points down. It was 70 to 30, something like that, 70-30 or 70-40, maybe 70-40. But it was an easy win. Take DeSantis. Ron DeSantis was a three, had no money. He was running against in the Republican primary, who was at 31 and he had $21 million cash in the bank. The Department of Agriculture, right? Nice guy, too. But I didn’t know him so I don’t feel guilty. I endorsed DeSantis. I endorsed DeSantis and he won by 20 points. Okay, it wasn’t even a race. And his opponent, who I spoke to afterward because he is a nice guy, he said, ‘I’ve never seen anything like it.’ He said, ‘you endorsed him, the race was over.’ There’s never been an impact — I don’t say it braggingly. I mean, it’s hard for me to say it because I’d rather have them say it but they don’t say it very well. No, there’s never been a story, nobody ever writes it. But you take a look at the races that I was involved in, I had a massive impact. Then they said, ‘well, DeSantis is not gonna make it in Florida, he’s never gonna get elected.’ So I went down and made a speech, he got elected, he won. Now they’re trying to take votes away from him, it’s a disgrace what’s going on, but it looks like he’s gonna be good and hopefully, Rick [Scott] is gonna be good. Maybe the machines are bad, now the machines are all smokey, they’re all — it’s crazy. Honestly, it’s a disgrace. Georgia, what they’re doing is a disgrace, and Florida, what they’re doing to the two people — no, to the three. Look at what they’re doing, how about the agriculture guy. He was leading all night long and then he ends up losing by 5,000 votes because nobody was watching that one. That’s a very important position in Florida, right? Pretty impressive if I do say so myself.
