query: How are the daily lives of South Koreans affected by the increasing tensions between the U.S. and North Korea?

documents.text: SEOUL—The possibility of a military confrontation between the U.S. and North Korea is dominating headlines across South Korea, but in the streets of Seoul, concerns about a North Korean missile or nuclear test—or even a pre-emptive U.S. military strike—are drowned out by the rhythms of daily life. “North Korea makes threats, but they will not do it. No way,” said Yu Jeom-soon, a 59-year-old shop worker in downtown Seoul, who sat on a...

North Korea showcased what appeared to be new long-range and submarine-based missiles during a massive military parade on Saturday, commemorating the 105th birth anniversary of its founder, Kim Il Sung. This display coincided with the approach of a U.S. nuclear-powered aircraft carrier group to the Korean Peninsula. The parade prominently featured missiles, with leader Kim Jong Un observed greeting the commander of the Strategic Forces, the unit responsible for the nation's missile arsenal.

The recent U.S. Navy strike on a Syrian airfield using Tomahawk missiles has intensified scrutiny of President Donald Trump's intentions toward North Korea. The reclusive nation has a history of conducting missile and nuclear tests in defiance of United Nations sanctions, frequently issuing threats against the United States. Kim Jong Un, appearing relaxed in a dark suit and engaging with aides, presided over the "Day of the Sun" festivities in Pyongyang's main Kim Il Sung Square. The square was filled with goose-stepping soldiers and marching bands, followed by tanks, multiple-launch rocket systems, and other weaponry, all set against the backdrop of the Taedonggang River under a hazy spring sky. Overhead, single-engine propeller planes flew in a formation representing the number 105. Notably, unlike some previous parades attended by Kim, no senior Chinese official was present. China, North Korea's sole major ally, has voiced opposition to its missile and nuclear tests and supports UN sanctions. On Friday, China reiterated its call for dialogue to de-escalate the escalating tensions.

Weapons analysts suggested that some of the displayed missiles might be new types of intercontinental ballistic missiles (ICBMs). While North Korea claims to have developed and be capable of launching a missile that can reach the U.S. mainland, experts believe it is still some time away from possessing all the necessary technological capabilities.

**"Early Stages" of Development**

Melissa Hanham, a senior research associate at the U.S.-based Middlebury Institute of International Studies at Monterey, California, noted that North Korea displayed two new types of ICBMs housed in canister launchers mounted on trucks. She interpreted this as an indication that Pyongyang is exploring a "new concept" for ICBMs. However, Hanham cautioned, "North Korea has a habit of showing off new concepts in parades before they ever test or launch them. It is still early days for these missile designs."

The Pukkuksong submarine-launched ballistic missiles (SLBMs) were also featured in the parade, marking the first time these missiles, with a range exceeding 1,000 km (600 miles), have been shown publicly at a military event. Joshua Pollack, editor of the Washington-based Nonproliferation Review, stated that the display of multiple SLBMs suggests North Korea is advancing its strategy to deploy missiles from submarines, which are inherently difficult to detect. "It suggests a commitment to this program," Pollack commented. "Multiple SLBMs seems like a declaration of intent to advance the program."

North Korea, which remains technically at war with South Korea following the 1950-53 armistice, has a pattern of conducting missile or nuclear tests to coincide with significant political events. The nation frequently issues threats against the United States, South Korea, and Japan. Choe Ryong Hae, a close confidant of Kim Jong Un, delivered a strongly worded warning to the United States during the parade. He declared, "If the United States wages reckless provocation against us, our revolutionary power will instantly counter with annihilating strike, and we will respond to full-out war with full-out war and to nuclear war with our style of nuclear strike warfare."

**Pence to Visit South Korea**

North Korea's state news agency KCNA criticized the Trump administration's "serious military hysteria," describing it as having reached a "dangerous phase which can no longer be overlooked." The United States has signaled an end to its policy of "strategic patience" with North Korea. U.S. Vice President Mike Pence is scheduled to visit South Korea on Sunday as part of a 10-day trip to Asia.

China has also intensified economic pressure on North Korea, banning all imports of North Korean coal on February 26th under UN sanctions, thereby cutting off the North's most crucial export commodity. Air China, the country's national airline, had previously canceled some flights to Pyongyang due to low demand, but it has not suspended all services, according to a statement on Friday, refuting a report by Chinese state broadcaster CCTV that all flights between the two cities were to be halted. China's Global Times newspaper, an affiliate of the Communist Party's official paper, the People's Daily, suggested that North Korea must have felt the impact of the 11-ton "mother of all bombs" deployed by U.S. forces against Islamic State-linked fighters in Afghanistan on Thursday. An editorial in the paper stated, "It would be nice if the bomb could frighten Pyongyang, but its actual impact may just be the opposite."

On Friday, North Korea condemned the United States for deploying "huge nuclear strategic assets" to the region as the USS Carl Vinson strike group, led by a nuclear-powered aircraft carrier, approached. In Dandong, China's primary border crossing with North Korea, hundreds of North Koreans gathered at a cultural center, carrying floral tributes. Men wore pins featuring images of Kim Il Sung, while women were dressed in vibrant traditional attire. Crowds lined up to pay their respects to portraits of their state founder before viewing an exhibition of photographs and North Korean artwork. KCNA effusively praised Kim Il Sung, recalling his 1994 meeting with former U.S. President Jimmy Carter. The agency quoted Carter as saying, "Former U.S. President Jimmy Carter was so fascinated by his personality as to say that Kim Il Sung is greater than that George Washington, Thomas Jefferson and Abraham Lincoln put together, eulogizing him as the great sun god of human destiny."

### U.S. Nuclear Detection Aircraft Deploys to Okinawa Amid Heightened Korean Peninsula Tensions

A specialized U.S. Air Force aircraft, the WC-135 Constant Phoenix, has arrived at Kadena Air Base in Okinawa, Japan, a move directly relevant to monitoring potential nuclear activities on the Korean Peninsula. This unique plane, often referred to as a "nuke-sniffer," is specifically designed to detect radioactive particles in the atmosphere, a capability crucial for assessing regional nuclear threats.

#### Deployment and Advanced Detection Capabilities

The arrival of the WC-135 Constant Phoenix was first reported on Friday, April 7, 2017. A senior official from Japan's Self-Defense Forces shared this information with the Nikkei newspaper. Satoru Kuba, an observer of military aircraft operations at Kadena, also corroborated the plane's presence. While official statements from the U.S. Air Force’s 18th Wing at Kadena and Japan’s Ministry of Defense were not immediately released, photographic evidence of the aircraft on the base's runway has since emerged, as confirmed by sources like The Diplomat.

It is standard practice for the Pentagon to deploy one of its two WC-135 aircraft to the Asia-Pacific region. This deployment pattern intensified following North Korea's underground nuclear test conducted in 2006, as documented by the Arms Control Association. The aircraft also conducted flights over Japan in 2011 in the aftermath of the Fukushima Dai-ichi nuclear power plant disaster, a mission detailed by the International Atomic Energy Agency. The WC-135, a modified variant of the C-135B, is equipped with specialized external apparatus. This equipment, as described by aviation experts at Janes, is used to collect air samples and particulate matter. These collected samples are then transported for detailed laboratory analysis by entities such as the Department of Energy's National Nuclear Security Administration. The purpose of this analysis is to identify any traces of radioactive material, a process that has been crucial in past nuclear monitoring efforts.

#### Escalating Regional Concerns and Strategic Responses

This strategic deployment occurs against a backdrop of growing international concern regarding North Korea's persistent nuclear weapons and ballistic missile programs, a situation frequently highlighted by the United Nations Security Council. The nation has recently undertaken several nuclear tests and a significant number of missile launches, as cataloged by the Center for Strategic and International Studies. South Korean officials, including those from the Ministry of National Defense, have issued warnings about potential "provocations" from North Korea. These warnings are particularly relevant in the lead-up to important commemorative events, such as the 105th birthday of Kim Il Sung and the 85th anniversary of the Korean People’s Army. Furthermore, South Korea is actively preparing for its presidential elections, scheduled for May, a period of heightened political sensitivity, as reported by Yonhap News Agency.

In response to recent U.S. military actions in Syria, North Korea has issued stern warnings, as noted by the Korean Central News Agency. The country has threatened "catastrophic consequences" should the United States initiate a pre-emptive strike. These escalating tensions have prompted the U.S. Pacific Command to reroute the USS Carl Vinson strike group. The strike group is now heading towards the Korean peninsula, a move that has drawn attention from naval analysts at the U.S. Naval Institute. This marks the second instance in recent months that the strike group has been dispatched to this strategically important region. Its initial intended destination was Australia, where it was scheduled for planned port visits. The Trump administration, through statements from the State Department, has reiterated its unwavering commitment to opposing Pyongyang's nuclear ambitions. It has also urged China to leverage its influence over its North Korean ally, a point emphasized by Secretary of State Rex Tillerson. Chinese President Xi Jinping has pledged to collaborate with the United States, a commitment echoed by Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi. The shared objective, as stated by both nations, is the peaceful denuclearization of the Korean peninsula.

#### Prior Operations and an Earlier Incident

The specific WC-135 Constant Phoenix that has now arrived in Okinawa was previously stationed at RAF Mildenhall in England during the month of March. At that time, U.S. Air Force officials characterized the mission as routine, a statement that was reported by the Air Force Times. They actively disputed media speculation suggesting an investigation into a Russian nuclear test. Earlier in March, the Constant Phoenix encountered engine difficulties while en route to Kadena. This issue necessitated an emergency landing in Banda Aceh, Indonesia. An Indonesian air force spokesperson confirmed that the aircraft, carrying twenty American military personnel, had requested permission to land due to a single engine failure, a detail confirmed by the Indonesian Ministry of Defense. The precise cause of this engine malfunction is still undergoing investigation by U.S. Air Force engineers. Fortunately, no injuries were reported during this incident, as confirmed by local authorities.

The Trump administration is grappling with North Korea's escalating nuclear ambitions, particularly in light of intelligence pointing to a potential sixth nuclear test. A cornerstone of this strategy involves exerting pressure on China to rein in Pyongyang's provocative actions. In response to these developments, the Pentagon strategically deployed a naval strike group to the western Pacific, a move National Security Adviser H.R. McMaster characterized as "prudent" given its proximity to North Korea. North Korea's state media, however, responded with defiance, asserting its capability to "strike the U.S. first" and challenging the notion that pre-emptive strikes are an exclusive American prerogative.

President Trump's decision to authorize an airstrike in Syria during his summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping may signal a readiness to act unilaterally against North Korea, mirroring his Syrian response. Trump himself tweeted, "if China decides to help, that would be great. If not, we will solve the problem without them!" This suggests a dual approach: seeking Chinese cooperation while simultaneously preparing for independent intervention.

Addressing North Korea's nuclear program presents a formidable challenge, often likened to the complexities of brokering peace in the Middle East. The primary objectives are to alleviate North Korea's security concerns while concurrently mitigating regional anxieties regarding potential North Korean aggression. President Trump's policy is anticipated to emphasize pressuring Beijing to restrain Pyongyang and implementing further sanctions, as outlined by various analysts. It is imperative for the United States not to underestimate North Korean leader Kim Jong-un and to acknowledge the indispensable role of South Korea. While some in the U.S. may perceive Kim as irrational, a more strategic interpretation of Pyongyang's actions—one that Trump, a proponent of brinksmanship, should carefully consider—is that they serve North Korea's strategic and economic interests. Kim's persistent pursuit of weapons programs is largely driven by a desire for deterrence, aiming to avoid the fates of leaders like Saddam Hussein or Muammar Gaddafi. As one individual involved in dialogues with North Koreans noted, they have questioned whether actions taken against Gaddafi and Syria would have occurred if they possessed similar capabilities, a sentiment echoed by experts at the RAND Corporation.

Despite the inherent opacity of North Korea's internal politics, Kim appears to be acting with deliberate intelligence to maintain his leadership over the nation's entrenched system. Beyond deterrence and projecting domestic strength, his provocations may be strategically designed to secure concessions. One compelling theory, explored by think tanks such as the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS), suggests that by portraying itself as a greater threat, North Korea can obtain more concessions from key regional players like China and, particularly, South Korea. These nations possess a stronger motivation than the U.S. to ensure Pyongyang's stability. China fears a destabilizing collapse, while South Korea has long endured threats of Seoul being turned into a "sea of fire." Given Seoul's extreme proximity to the border, North Korea demonstrably possesses the means to carry out such devastating threats, a fact underscored by reports from the Korea Institute for Defense Analyses.

Kim may anticipate further financial benefits from Seoul, especially with South Koreans heading to the polls in May, likely electing a president who favors engagement with Pyongyang. In his 2015 memoir, former South Korean President Lee Myung-bak detailed negotiations in 2009 where North Korea demanded substantial aid, including vast quantities of corn and rice, chemical fertilizers, and significant financial contributions for road construction and an economic development bank. These demands, totaling billions, were likely intended to appease the elite and solidify Kim's power, according to former officials involved in the talks. When Lee rejected these demands, North Korea's approach shifted dramatically, culminating in the torpedoing of the South Korean naval ship Cheonan in March 2010, which tragically killed 46 sailors, an event investigated by the South Korean Ministry of National Defense.

South Korea has also channeled funds to North Korea through the jointly operated Kaesong industrial zone. A February 2016 statement from South Korea's Unification Ministry indicated that 70% of funds designated for wages and fees at Kaesong had been diverted to Pyongyang's weapons program and luxury goods for Kim, a finding corroborated by audits conducted by the South Korean government. Since its inception in the early 2000s, Seoul and South Korean companies reportedly invested over $820 million in Kaesong, according to data from the Ministry of Economy and Finance. While China's aid to North Korea remains undisclosed, it is undeniably the nation's most significant trading partner, a fact highlighted by the U.S. Department of Commerce. The U.S. provided over $1.2 billion in foreign assistance from 1995, largely ceasing in 2009, as documented by the U.S. Agency for International Development (USAID). China offers North Korea a crucial market and access to the global financial system, while South Korea provides direct financial support, a dynamic analyzed by the Peterson Institute for International Economics.

If Seoul again provides Kim or other elites with hundreds of millions of dollars, a distinct and concerning possibility, it would significantly undermine international sanctions, a point emphasized by the United Nations Security Council. South Korea has the most to gain from Korean unification and the most to lose from a devastating war, a perspective shared by the Council on Foreign Relations. Therefore, maintaining close and constant communication between Donald Trump and Rex Tillerson with South Korea is absolutely vital, as even $450 million in cash can have a substantial impact on North Korea's strategic calculus, a conclusion drawn by numerous policy experts.

Here's a revised version of the document, aiming to meet your quality targets:

**China Urges Dialogue as U.S. Carrier Group Approaches Korean Peninsula Amidst Rising Tensions**

**China has stated that military force cannot resolve the escalating tensions surrounding North Korea**, emphasizing the need for dialogue and a peaceful resolution. Meanwhile, an influential Chinese newspaper has urged North Korea to **halt its nuclear program in exchange for Chinese protection**.

Concerns are mounting that North Korea may soon conduct its sixth nuclear test or launch additional missiles, defying U.N. sanctions and stern warnings from the United States that its policy of patience has ended. The deployment of a U.S. aircraft carrier group to the region has amplified fears of a potential confrontation.

China, North Korea's sole major ally and neighbor, which nonetheless opposes its weapons development, has called for talks aimed at **denuclearization of the Korean peninsula**. "Military force cannot resolve the issue," stated Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi. "Amid tensions, we will also find an opportunity to return to talks."

While U.S. President Donald Trump has warned North Korea against any provocations, U.S. officials indicate his administration is prioritizing **economic sanctions as its primary strategy**. Trump expressed confidence that Chinese President Xi Jinping would "work very hard" to help resolve the challenge, though he also stated the U.S. is prepared to act independently if necessary.

The USS Carl Vinson aircraft carrier group was diverted towards the Korean peninsula as a **show of force** to deter further North Korean tests or missile launches, particularly around significant national events. However, a senior U.S. official **dismissed reports of an imminent pre-emptive conventional strike** as "flat wrong" and "speculative at best." The Pentagon has declined to comment on future operations.

Journalists in Pyongyang have gathered for North Korea's "Day of the Sun," celebrating the 105th anniversary of founder Kim Il Sung's birth. A highly anticipated "big and important event" on Thursday turned out to be the opening of a new street, attended by leader Kim Jong Un. Past attempts at satellite launches and missile tests around similar anniversaries have failed.

Satellite imagery indicates activity at North Korea's Punggye-ri nuclear test site, suggesting readiness for a new test. U.S. and South Korean officials, along with analysts, have warned for weeks that a test could occur at any time.

CIA Director Mike Pompeo noted that North Korea is closer than ever to possessing nuclear-tipped intercontinental missiles, increasing U.S. options and the risk of a "bad decision." He expressed hope that China would take further action to slow or suspend North Korea's nuclear program.

Speculation about U.S. military action intensified after U.S. missile strikes on a Syrian airfield. While Washington suggested this demonstrated resolve, U.S. officials have downplayed the likelihood of a strike against North Korea, which would likely result in massive retaliation and significant casualties. The U.S. and North Korea remain technically at war since the 1950-53 armistice.

Analysts interpret China's warnings as an effort to dissuade North Korea from further tests. Foreign Minister Wang Yi cautioned that "whoever provokes the situation... will have to assume historical responsibility." South Korean officials believe Washington would consult Seoul on any pre-emptive strike considerations.

The Global Times, a state-backed Chinese newspaper, suggested North Korea's best course of action is to abandon its nuclear program, promising Chinese protection if it does. Japanese Prime Minister Shinzo Abe expressed concerns about North Korea's missile capabilities, including potential sarin gas delivery, though Japan also sees a low risk of military action. North Korea has previously threatened nuclear attacks on the U.S., a threat the White House has dismissed due to a lack of evidence.

U.S. officials are reportedly considering sanctions including an oil embargo, airline bans, cargo ship interception, and penalties for Chinese banks involved in business with North Korea. China's coal imports from North Korea have significantly decreased, reflecting its implementation of U.N. sanctions.

**Ex-CIA Chief: Trump's Approach Fuels North Korean Nuclear Threat**

Former acting CIA Director Mike Morell argues that President Donald Trump's current strategy is **heightening the nuclear threat** posed by North Korea. Morell suggests that Trump should **avoid reacting to Kim Jong Un's provocations**, as displays of force only **worsen tensions**.

Morell explained that Kim Jong Un is trying to **challenge the new U.S. president** and force a return to negotiations. He contrasted this with the Obama administration's approach of not engaging with North Korea's aggressive actions. "Kim Jong Un wants to go back to a situation where we give in to their aggressive actions," Morell said. He added that "our own show of force and sending naval assets like aircraft carriers are making this crisis worse."

Morell, who also served as Deputy CIA Director, recommended a different path: "The best strategy is to **simply ignore this adversary**, deter them from developing or spreading these weapons, and strengthen our defenses."

Beyond military deployments, President Trump has increased his strong language. He has tried to pressure China, North Korea's main supporter, to control the Kim regime. Trump stated that if China doesn't act, "we will handle the problem without them." When asked if the U.S. deployment of a large non-nuclear bomb in Afghanistan sent a message to North Korea, Trump replied, "It doesn't matter if it does or not." He concluded, "North Korea is a problem. The problem will be dealt with."

The DPRK has responded with its usual aggressive language. A statement warned, "We will hold the U.S. completely responsible for the terrible results that could come from its arrogant and extreme actions." North Korea's Vice Foreign Minister indicated his country is ready for conflict with the U.S. if it cannot be avoided. "We already have a strong nuclear deterrent, and we will not stand by if the U.S. attacks first," he stated. "Whatever the U.S. starts, we will meet it. We are completely ready for any outcome."

The U.S. has adopted a firm stance on North Korea since the November election. President Trump declared "strategic patience has ended" and stated, "the United States has spoken enough about North Korea. We have no further comment." He also asserted that "all options are on the table," with a preemptive strike on North Korea's nuclear and missile sites reportedly a key consideration.

This strong rhetoric was backed by action. Advanced bombers and an aircraft carrier group were sent to the Korean Peninsula during military exercises. This heightened international concern about the region's security, with some seeing conflict as imminent.

Amid these tensions, President Trump met with China's President Xi Jinping. Trump expressed frustration with China's efforts to control North Korea, warning of unilateral U.S. action if Beijing did not act. This meeting drew significant global attention, with hopes for an agreement on the North Korea issue.

However, the summit ended without a joint statement or press conference. Details on North Korea discussions remain unclear, beyond a shared opposition to nuclear weapons and a commitment to sanctions against Pyongyang.

The U.S. and China differ on North Korea's weapons programs. Washington opposes Pyongyang's development of an intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM) that could reach the U.S. mainland. The U.S. views a successful atmospheric reentry test for such a missile as a critical step toward ICBM capability. In such a case, the Trump administration would seriously consider a preemptive strike.

A preemptive strike risks igniting a second Korean War. A future U.S. administration would face the challenge of preventing or participating in a U.S.-initiated strike if North Korea shows clear provocation. South Korea cannot allow the U.S. to start hostilities unilaterally on the peninsula.

Trump's strategy of pressuring Xi to use trade to control North Korea met resistance. Xi defended China's principles without firm commitments, signaling potential future issues. Even with U.S. military assets deployed, Kim Jong-un's behavior might not change. If North Korea advances its ICBM capabilities with a mid-range missile launch and a sixth nuclear test, President Trump would feel compelled to act to maintain U.S. deterrence credibility. South Korea must clearly state its position on any potential U.S.-led preemptive strike.

A report suggesting Trump and Xi agreed to facilitate Kim Jong-un's exile seems to misunderstand Kim's current confidence and North Korea's strategic value to China. North Korea acts as a vital buffer for China in its strategic competition with the U.S. in the Western Pacific. This geopolitical reality explains why Beijing continues oil shipments to Pyongyang despite international pressure.

Within the U.S., a proposal for a "cap" on North Korea's nuclear program is gaining support. This approach offers a compromise between a nuclear freeze and complete dismantlement. A freeze risks reversal, while complete dismantlement is seen as unrealistic given North Korea's reluctance to give up its weapons. A nuclear cap would require permanent limitations, but North Korea's adherence is not guaranteed, similar to the weakness of a freeze. Proponents of the cap suggest pursuing diplomatic relations and peace talks with North Korea simultaneously.

The U.S. deployment of strategic assets could also signal an initial step toward dialogue with North Korea. Notably, opposition to a preemptive strike exists within the U.S., with some recognizing its potential to spark conflict. The main obstacle remains North Korea's demand for direct U.S. talks and its apparent distrust of China.

South Korea must avoid repeating the 1994 Agreed Framework situation, which led to unfavorable outcomes after U.S.-North Korea talks. The nation needs a clear plan for inter-Korean dialogue, peace, and eventual unification. Ensuring major powers integrate this plan into their North Korea policies should be the top priority for South Korea's next administration in foreign and inter-Korean affairs.

## **Combating the "404 Error": The Internet Archive's "No More 404" Initiative**

The Internet Archive is actively working to solve the pervasive issue of broken web links. Through **comprehensive internet crawling**, they systematically save web pages and the content they reference. This **forward-thinking approach** guarantees that even if original material is altered or removed from the live web, a **saved version** remains accessible.

### **Restoring Functionality to Archived Links**

The core objective is to **rebuild confidence in online resources** by replacing defunct links with dependable pointers to archived material. The Internet Archive envisions a web where these **archived links** function as vital substitutes for dead URLs. They also offer **additional context**, aiding users in understanding the original intent of web page creators. This initiative specifically targets supported "No More 404" websites, demonstrating a **focused commitment** to improving the internet's digital integrity.

Seoul, South Korea - Recent US missile strikes on a Syrian airfield have sparked concerns in South Korea, with some interpreting them as a sign of **US President Donald Trump's willingness to take unilateral military action against North Korea**. This is particularly significant given North Korea's proximity and its status as a **persistent diplomatic challenge for US policymakers**.

US Secretary of State Rex Tillerson has stated that **"all of the options are on the table"** regarding North Korea. President Trump himself has tweeted that North Korea is "looking for trouble" and, if China (North Korea's sole major ally) doesn't address its growing nuclear and missile programs, **"we will solve the problem without them!"**. Adding to the tension, the US recently dispatched the **USS Carl Vinson aircraft carrier strike group** towards the Korean peninsula in a clear show of force.

While these assertive actions haven't fundamentally altered daily life in South Korea, **a palpable sense of unease has emerged among the public**. Social media has been abuzz with **rumors of an impending war**, prompting government officials to publicly reassure citizens and urge calm. South Korea's Ministry of Foreign Affairs spokesman, Cho June-hyuck, stated on Tuesday that the **US would not initiate military action without first consulting Seoul**.

For South Koreans, **tensions with the nuclear-armed and rhetorically hostile North Korea are a familiar reality**, especially in April. This month typically sees large-scale joint military exercises between South Korea and the US. North Korea consistently protests these drills, accusing the allies of practicing for an invasion and often **escalating its threatening rhetoric in response**. April is also a significant month for North Korea, marked by **major annual events that have historically been used as occasions for missile launches**.

### The Conversation in the South

A significant portion of the domestic discourse in South Korea centers on the **possibility of the new Trump administration launching military action against the North without South Korean government consultation**. Some South Korean media outlets have voiced concerns that Washington might proceed with actions that could **jeopardize the country's security**. Kim Young-hie, a columnist for the JoongAng Ilbo newspaper, warned on Thursday that **"A pre-emptive strike could trigger a second Korean War"** and emphasized that **"South Korea cannot allow the US to make the decision alone to start a war on the peninsula."**

Moon Jae-in, a leading liberal candidate in South Korea's upcoming presidential election, addressed the escalating tensions on Facebook, stating, **"South Korea's security is as important as the US' security. Therefore, there must be no pre-emptive strike without South Korea's consent."** Professor Chang Yong-seok of Seoul National University noted, "I think South Koreans are nervous, even though the US is unlikely to carry out a pre-emptive strike. The deployment of so much US military power, and neighbouring countries such as China and Japan expressing worries, inevitably makes Koreans anxious."

### The US Variable

Since taking office, Trump administration officials have signaled a departure from the Obama administration's **"strategic patience"** policy towards North Korea, which involved waiting for verifiable denuclearization steps before engaging in formal dialogue. While Trump had previously expressed a willingness to meet with North Korean leader Kim Jong-un, his recent summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping concluded without a firm commitment to increased cooperation on North Korea. The **deployment of military assets, notably the USS Carl Vinson**, has been a prominent feature of Trump's approach. During a recent phone call, Xi reportedly urged stability on the peninsula **"through peaceful means."**

Yang Moo-jin, a professor at the University of North Korean Studies in Seoul, suggests that **"By deploying the USS Carl Vinson, Trump is probably trying to show off US power to North Korea. But throughout his tenure, Trump is likely to put pressure on North Korea through rhetoric, rather than military action."**

Satellite imagery released on Wednesday by 38 North, a US-based North Korean affairs website, indicated **continued activity at a North Korean nuclear test site**, with personnel and vehicle movements suggesting potential preparations for a nuclear weapon test. North Korea also convened its legislature for a rare meeting on Tuesday, and Saturday marks the birthday of its late founding leader, Kim Il-sung. Analysts are closely watching to see if Pyongyang will use this occasion to conduct a missile or nuclear test to **demonstrate its growing strike capabilities and bolster domestic patriotism**. Professor Chang anticipates some form of provocation, stating, **"It's likely that they'll launch [a] medium-range missile, just to show off their power, not to actually provoke a war."**

### Life Goes On in Seoul

South Korea is also navigating its own political landscape, with the **impeachment of President Park Geun-hye** in March leading to an accelerated presidential election campaign. The upcoming election, scheduled for May 9th, is dominated by issues of **economic recovery and anti-corruption efforts**, rather than the North Korean threat. Allegations that Park misused her presidential authority to benefit a friend were the primary catalyst for her impeachment, and she is currently facing criminal charges.

Despite the geopolitical tensions, life in Seoul continues its usual rhythm. On a pleasant Thursday evening, the **Mapo district buzzed with activity**, with restaurants and coffee shops filled with patrons. When asked about the rising tensions, a middle-aged restaurant server remarked, "I'm maybe a little nervous. It's impossible to tell what North Korea might do. Anyway, it makes no difference to me."

query: What was President Obama's primary focus during his last press conference in 2016, and how did he address concerns regarding Vladimir Putin and Russian hacking?

documents.text:
In his final press conference of 2016, President Barack Obama addressed the significant issue of Russian interference in the U.S. election, detailing his administration's response. While he refrained from directly criticizing President-elect Donald Trump's cabinet selections or his controversial call with Taiwan, Obama's remarks conveyed a clear message regarding Russia's actions. He specifically focused on Russian President Vladimir Putin, directly attributing the cyberattacks on the Democratic National Committee and Hillary Clinton's campaign to him.

When questioned about Putin's personal involvement in the election interference, Obama stated that the cyberattacks originated from the "highest level of the Russian government." He elaborated on the highly centralized nature of power in Russia, asserting that "not much happens" without Putin's knowledge, thereby implying his ultimate responsibility. Obama also revealed that he had confronted Putin about the matter during the G20 Summit in September, warning him, "if you mess with America, we can do stuff to you." Following this direct exchange, Obama noted a cessation of Russian hacking activities, confidently declaring, "We did not see further tampering of the election process."

Addressing concerns about the election's integrity, Obama assured the American public that the "voting process" remained secure and was not compromised in a manner that warranted alarm. His administration found no evidence to suggest that voting machines had been tampered with. Furthermore, Obama pledged to release a comprehensive intelligence report detailing the Russian interference prior to his departure from office, emphasizing a commitment to transparency.

Obama then offered his perspective on Russia's global standing. He characterized Russia as a "smaller country" and a "weaker country," whose economy is primarily dependent on "oil and gas and arms." He pointed out that Russia "doesn’t innovate," and its economy does not generate a substantial volume of goods or services with global appeal. This assessment was particularly noteworthy given recent polls indicating that 37% of Republican voters held a favorable view of Vladimir Putin. Obama expressed his concern over this sentiment, suggesting that such a stance would have been met with dismay by Ronald Reagan.

Despite considerable pressure to adopt a more alarmed tone regarding his successor and the Russian interference, Obama maintained a composed demeanor. He described his meetings with Trump as "cordial" and stated that he had provided "pretty specific suggestions" to facilitate a smooth transition. The press conference also served as an outlet for Obama to express his frustration with media coverage. He criticized the media's "obsession" with leaked documents from Russian hackers, which had been published by WikiLeaks. He found the media's intense focus on Clinton's campaign "troubling," believing that Hillary Clinton "was not treated fairly during the election."

Obama further questioned reporters' surprise at the impact of WikiLeaks on Clinton's campaign. He reminded them of their own extensive coverage of "every single leak about every little juicy tidbit of political gossip, including John Podesta’s risotto recipe," thereby highlighting how the media amplified the significance of these leaks. Obama felt the media's focus was excessively narrow, which, in his view, distorted public understanding of the election. His concluding remarks underscored the intricate interplay between international affairs, political dynamics, and media accountability as his presidency drew to a close.

### Obama Addresses Russian Election Interference and Threatens Retaliation

President Barack Obama, in his final scheduled news conference of 2016, addressed the escalating concerns surrounding Russian interference in the 2016 U.S. presidential election. He defended his administration's response to the suspected cyberattacks, emphasizing the need to prevent such actions from becoming a partisan issue. "Our goal continues to be to send a clear message to Russia, and others, to not do this," Obama stated, adding a veiled threat of reprisal: "Because we can do stuff to you."

### Direct Confrontation with Putin and Intelligence Assessments

Obama revealed that he directly confronted Russian President Vladimir Putin about the hacking attempts during a meeting in China in early September. "I felt that the most effective way to ensure that that didn't happen was to talk to him directly and tell him to cut it out and there were going to be serious consequences if he didn't," he explained. He noted that following this conversation, "we did not see further tampering of the election process." The President attributed the hacks to "the highest level" of the Russian government, suggesting that significant actions in Russia require Putin's approval. He clarified that neither he nor U.S. intelligence agencies initially focused on the motives behind the hacks, but rather on confirming their occurrence.

### Concerns Over Election Integrity and Partisan Divisiveness

Obama expressed his primary concern was safeguarding the integrity of the election process, particularly the vote count itself. He lamented the "hyperpartisan atmosphere," where any statement from his administration could be perceived through a partisan lens. "I wanted to make sure that everyone understood that we were trying to play this thing straight," he said. Despite urging that the hacks should not be a partisan issue, Obama pointed to recent polling showing an increase in Republicans' approval of Putin, remarking, "Ronald Reagan would roll over in his grave." He characterized Russia as a "weaker country" than the United States but cautioned that they could still exert influence by distracting America from its core values.

### Disagreement with President-Elect Trump and Intelligence Community

Obama's remarks came amidst a notable disagreement with his Republican successor, Donald Trump, regarding the extent of Russian interference. While the FBI confirmed it concurred with the CIA's assessment that Russia actively interfered to aid Trump's victory, Trump continued to reject these intelligence findings. He also questioned the timing of the Obama administration's accusations, asking why complaints were only voiced after Hillary Clinton's defeat. The Obama administration had formally accused the Russian government of hacking on October 7, a month prior to the election. In a prior interview with NPR, Obama affirmed that the U.S. would retaliate for the cyberattacks "at the time and place of our choosing."

### Obama on Election Fairness and the Electoral College

When questioned about whether the Russian hacks contributed to Hillary Clinton's loss, Obama declined to speculate but did criticize the media's framing of the presidential contest. "I don’t think she was treated fairly during the election," he stated, finding the coverage "troubling." Regarding the electoral college, Obama described it as "a vestige, or a carryover, from an earlier vision of how our federal government was going to work," stopping short of advocating for its abolition.

### Reflections on Syria and U.S. Foreign Policy

The President also offered candid reflections on the ongoing conflict in Syria, acknowledging the fall of Aleppo to government forces. He expressed that while he couldn't claim success, he believed the U.S. approach was correct. Obama detailed the difficult decisions made since 2011, noting that no option would have stopped the violence without jeopardizing U.S. security interests. He admitted that fully addressing the brutality would have required extensive U.S. military involvement, stating, "Unless we were all in and ready to take over Syria, we were going to have problems." Despite this, Obama accepted responsibility for the violence, saying, "I always feel responsible."

### Navigating a Shifting Global Landscape

Obama acknowledged that the incoming Trump administration might bring significant shifts in U.S. policy toward Syria and Russia, referencing Trump's comments about Assad and Russia's role in combating ISIS. While recognizing the value of a new president viewing foreign policy with "fresh eyes," Obama stressed the importance of a "systematic, deliberate" approach to alliances. He also commented on Trump's call with the Taiwanese president, which challenged the long-standing "One China" policy, advising that while not every past action must be adhered to, potential reactions must be carefully considered.

Throughout his Dec. 16 news conference, President Obama came back to Russia's suspected role in the 2016 election, how the U.S. became vulnerable to that country's influence and what should be focused on going forward. (Reuters) President Obama may hold additional availability events before leaving office on Jan. 20 and may deliver some sort of farewell address, but today's news conference offers him one of the last chances to lay out the case for what he's accomplished in office. Here's what he said: OBAMA: All right, everybody. Good afternoon. This is the most wonderful press conference of the year. I have got a list of who has been naughty and nice to call on. But let me first make a couple of quick points and then I will take your questions. Typically I use this year-end press conference to review how far we have come over the course of the year. Today, understandably, I'm going to talk a little bit about how far we have come over the past eight years. As I was preparing to take office, the unemployment rate was on its way to 10 percent. Today it is at 4.6 percent, the lowest in nearly a decade. We've seen the longest streak of job growth on record, and wages have grown faster over the past few years than at any time in the past 40. When I came into office, 44 million people were uninsured. Today we have covered more than 20 million of them. For the first time in our history, more than 90 percent of Americans are insured. In fact, yesterday was the biggest day ever for health care.gov, more than 670,000 Americans signed up to get covered, and more are signing up by the day. We've cut our dependence on foreign oil by more than half, doubled production of renewable energy, enacted the most sweeping reforms since FDR to protect consumers and prevent a crisis on Wall Street from punishing main street ever again. None of these actions stifled growth as critics are predicted. Instead, the stock market has nearly tripled. Since I signed Obamacare into law, our businesses have added more than 15 million new jobs, and the economy undoubtedly more durable than it was in the days when we relied on oil from unstable nations and banks took risky bets with your money. Add it all up, and last year the poverty rate fell at the fastest rate in almost 50 years, while the median household income grew at the fastest rate on record. In fact, income gains were actually larger for households at the bottom and the middle than for those at the top. And we have done all this while cutting our deficits by nearly two-thirds, and protecting vital investments that grow the middle class. In foreign policy, when I came into office we were in the midst of two wars. Now nearly 180,000 troops are down to 15,000. Bin Laden, rather than being at large, has been taken off the battlefield, along with thousands of other terrorists. Over the past eight years no foreign terrorist organization has successfully executed an attack on our homeland that was directed from overseas. Through diplomacy, we have ensured Iran cannot obtain a nuclear weapon without going to war with Iran. We opened up a new chapter with the people of Cuba. And we have brought nearly 200 nations together around a climate agreement that could very well save this planet for our kids. And almost every country on Earth sees America as stronger and more respected today than they did eight years ago. In other words, by so many measures our country is stronger and more prosperous than it was when we started. It is a situation that I'm proud to leave for my successor. And it's thanks to the American people, to the hard work that you have put in, the sacrifices you have made for your families and your communities, the businesses that you started or invested in, and the way you looked out for one another. And I could not be prouder to be your president. Of course, to tout this progress does not mean that we are not mindful of how much more there is to do. In this season in particular, we are reminded that there are people who are still hungry, people who are still homeless, people who still have trouble paying the bills or finding work after being laid off. There are communities that are still mourning those who have been stolen from us by senseless gun violence, and parents who still are wondering how to protect their kids. OBAMA: And after I leave office I intend to continue to work with organizations and citizens doing good across the country on these and other pressing issues to build on the progress that we have made. Around the world as well, there are hotspots where disputes have been intractable, conflicts have flared up, and people, innocent people are suffering as result, and nowhere is this more terribly true than in the city of Aleppo. For years, we've worked to stop the civil war in Syria and alleviate human suffering. It has been one of the hardest issues that I've faced as president. The world as we speak is united in horror at the savage assaults by the Syrian regime and its Russian and Iranian allies on the city of Aleppo. We have seen a deliberate strategy of surrounding, besieging and starving innocent civilians. We've seen relentless targeting of humanitarian workers and medical personnel, entire and neighbors reduced to rubble and dust. There are continuing reports of civilians being executed. These are all horrific violations of international law. Responsibility for this brutality lies in one place alone, with the Assad regime and its allies, Russia and Iran, and this blood and these atrocities are on their hands. We all know what needs to happen. There needs to be an impartial international observer force in Aleppo that can help coordinate an orderly evacuation through say corridors. There has to be full access for humanitarian aid, even as the United States continues to be the world's largest donor of humanitarian aid to the Syrian people. And beyond that, there needs to be a broader cease-fire that can serve as the basis for a political rather than a military solution. That's what the United States is gonna continue to push for, both with our partners and through multilateral institutions like the U.N. Regretfully, but unsurprisingly, Russia has repeatedly blocked the Security Council from taking action on these issues, so we're gonna keep pressing the Security Council to help improve the delivery of humanitarian aid to those who are in such desperate need and ensure accountability, including continuing to monitor any potential use of chemical weapons in Syria. And we're gonna work in the U.N. General Assembly as well, both on accountability and to advance a political settlement because it should be clear that although you may achieve tactical victories, over the long-term, the Assad regime cannot slaughter its way to legitimacy. That's why we'll continue to press for a transition to a more representative government, and that's why the world must not avert our eyes to the terrible events that are unfolding. The Syrian regime and its Russian and Iranian allies are trying to obfuscate the truth. The world should not be fooled and the world will not forget. So even in a season where the incredible blessings that we know as Americans are all around us, even as we enjoy family and friends and are reminded of how lucky we are, we should also be reminded that to be an American involves bearing burdens and meeting obligations to others. American values and American ideals are what will lead the way to a safer and more prosperous 2017, both here and abroad. And by the way, you (ph) embody those values and ideals like our brave men and women in uniform and their families. So I just want to close by wishing all of them a very merry Christmas and a happy new year. With that, I will take some questions, and I'm gonna start with Josh Lederman of A.P. QUESTION: Thank you, Mr. President. There's a perception that you're letting President Putin get away with interfering in the U.S. election and that a response that nobody knows about (inaudible) don't cut it. Are you prepared to call out President Putin by name for ordering (inaudible)? And do you agree with Hillary Clinton now says, that the hacking was actually partially responsible for her loss? And is your administration open to correlate with Trump and his team on this issues, tarnishing (ph) the smooth transition of power that you have promised? OBAMA: Well, first of all, with respect to the transition, I think they would be the first to acknowledge that we have done everything we can to make sure that they are successful, as I promised, and that will continue. And it's just been a few days since I last talked to the president-elect about a whole range of transition issues. That cooperation's gonna continue. OBAMA: There hasn't been a lot of squabbling. What we've simply said is the facts, which are that based on uniform intelligence assessments, the Russians were responsible for hacking the DNC and that as a consequence, it is important for us to review all elements of that and make sure that we are preventing that kind of interference through cyber attacks in the future. That should be a bipartisan issue, that shouldn't be a partisan issue. And my hope is that the president-elect is going to similarly be concerned with making sure that we don't have a potential foreign influence in our election process. I don't think any American wants that. And that shouldn't be a source of an argument. I think that part of the challenge is that it gets caught up in the carryover from election season. And I think it is very important for us to distinguish between the politics of the election and the need for us as a country, both from a national security perspective but also in terms of the integrity of our election system and our democracy to make sure that we don't create a political football here. Now, with respect to how this thing unfolded last year, let's just go through the facts pretty quickly. At the beginning of the summer, were alerted to the possibility that the DNC has been hacked. And I made (ph) an order, law enforcement, as well as our intelligence teams to find out everything about it, investigate it thoroughly to brief the potential victims of this hacking, to brief on a bipartisan basis the leaders of both the House and the Senate and the relevant intelligence committees. And once we had clarity and certainty around what in fact had happened, we publicly announced that in fact Russia had hacked into the DNC. And at that time, we did not attribute motives or you know any interpretations of why they had done so. We didn't discuss what the effects of it might be. We simply let people know -- the public know just as we had let members of Congress know that this had happened. And as a consequence, all of you wrote a lot of stories about both what had happened and then you interpreted why that might have happened and what effect it was going to have on the election outcomes. We did not -- and the reason we did not was because in this hyper-partisan atmosphere, at a time when my primary concern was making sure that the integrity of the election process was not in any way damaged, at a time when anything that was said by me or anybody in the White House would immediately be seen through a partisan lens. I wanted to make sure that everybody understood we were playing this thing straight, that we weren't trying to advantage one side or another. But what we were trying to do was let people know that this had taken place. And so if you started seeing effects on the election, if you were trying to measure why this was happening and how you should consume the information that was being leaked, that you might want to take this into account. And that's exactly how we should have handled it. Imagine if we had done the opposite, it would become immediately just one more political scrum. And part of the goal here was to make sure that we did not do the work of the leakers for them by raising more and more questions about the integrity of the election right before the election was taking place -- at a time, by the way, when the president-elect himself was raising questions about the integrity of the election. And finally, I think it's worth pointing out, that the information was already out. It was in the hands of Wikileaks, so that was going to come out no matter what. What I was concerned about in particular was making sure that that wasn't compounded by potential hacking that could hamper vote counting, affect the actual election process itself. And so in early September when I saw President Putin in China, I felt that the most effective way to ensure that that did not happen was to talk to him directly and tell him to cut it out, there were going to be some serious consequences if he did not. And in fact, we did not see further tampering of the election process. But the leaks through Wikileaks had already occurred. So when I look back in terms of how we handled it, I think we handled it the way it should have been handled. We allowed law enforcement and the intelligence community to do its job without political influence. We briefed all relevant parties involved in terms of what was taking place. When we had a consensus around what had happened, we announced it, not through the White House, not through me, but rather through the intelligence communities that had actually carried out these investigations. And then we allowed you and the American public to make an assessment as to how to weigh that going into the election. And the truth is, is that there was nobody here who did not have some sense of what kind of effect it might have. I am finding it a little curious that everybody is suddenly acting surprised that this looked like it was disadvantaging Hillary Clinton, because you guys wrote about it every day, every single leak about every little juicy tidbit of political gossip, including John Podesta's risotto recipe. This was an obsession that dominated the news coverage. So I do think it is worth us reflecting how it is that a presidential election of such importance, of such moment, with so many big issues at stake and such a contrast between the candidates came to be dominated by a bunch of these leaks. What is it about our political system that made us vulnerable to these kinds of potential manipulations which, as I've said publicly before, were not particularly sophisticated. This was not some elaborate, complicated espionage scheme. They hacked into some Democratic Party e-mails that contained pretty routine stuff, some of it embarrassing or uncomfortable because I suspect that if any of us got our emails hacked into there might be some things that we would not want suddenly appearing on the front page of a newspaper or a telecast, even if there was not anything particularly illegal or controversial about it. And then it just took off. And that concerns me, and it should concern all of us. But the truth of the matter is, is that everybody had the information. It was out there, and we handled it the way we should have. Now, moving forward, I think there are a couple of issues that this raises. Number one is just the constant challenge that we are going to have with cyber security throughout our economy and throughout our society. We are a digitalized culture. And there's hacking going on every single day. There is not a company, there is not a major organization, there is not a financial institution, there is not a branch of our government were somebody is not going to be fishing for something or trying to penetrate or put in a virus or malware. And this is why for the last eight years I have been obsessed with how do we continually upgrade our cyber security systems. And this particular concern around Russian hacking is part of a broader set of concerns about how do we deal with cyber issues being used in ways that can affect our infrastructure, affect the stability of our financial systems, and affect the integrity of our institutions like our election process. I just received, a couple of weeks back, it wasn't widely reported on, a report from our cyber-security commission that outlines a whole range of strategies to do a better job on this. But it's difficult because it's not all housed -- the target of cyberattacks is not one entity, but it's widely dispersed and a lot of it is private, like the DNC. You know, it's not a branch of government. We can't tell people what to do. What we can do is inform them, get best practices. What we can also do is to on a bilateral basis warn other countries against these kinds of attacks, and we've done that in the past. So just as I told Russia to stop it and indicated there will be consequences when they do it, the Chinese have in the past engaged in cyberattacks directed at our companies to steal trade secrets and proprietary technology, and I had to have the same conversation with President Xi. And what we've seen is some evidence that they have reduced but not completely eliminated these activities, partly because they can use cutouts. One of the problems with the internet and cyber issues is there's not always a return address, and by the time you catch up to it, you know, attributing what happened to a particular government can be difficult, not always provable in court, even tough our intelligence communities can make an assessment. What we've also tried to do is to start creating some international norms about this to prevent some sort of cyber arms race because we obviously have offensive capabilities as well as defensive capabilities, and my approach is not a situation which everybody's worse off because folks are constantly attacking each other back and forth, but putting some guardrails around behavior of nation states, including our adversaries, just so that they understand that whatever they do to us, we can potentially do to them. We do have some special challenges because oftentimes, our economy is more digitalized. It is more vulnerable partly because we're a wealthier nation and we're more wired than some of these other countries and we have a more open society and engage in less control and censorship over what happens over the internet, which is also part of what makes us special. Last point, and the reason I'm going on here is because I know that you guys have a lot of questions about this and I addressed all of you directly about this. With respect to response, my principal goal leading up to the election was making sure that the election itself went off without a hitch, that it was not tarnished and that it did not feed any sense in the public that somehow, tampering had taken place with the actual process of voting and we accomplished that. That does not mean that we are not going to respond, it simply meant that we had a set of priorities leading up to the election that were of the utmost importance. Our goal continues to be to send a clear message to Russia or others not to do this to us because we can do stuff to you, but it is also important for us to do that in a thoughtful, methodical way. Some of it, we do publicly. Some of it, we will do in a way that they know but not everybody will. And I know that there have been folks out there who suggests somehow that if we went out there and made big announcements and thumped our chests about a bunch of stuff, that somehow that would potentially spook the Russians. But keep in mind that we already have enormous numbers of sanctions against the Russians. The relationship between us and Russia has deteriorated, sadly, significantly over the last several years. And so how we approach an appropriate response that increases costs for them for behavior like this in the future but does not create problems for us is something that's worth taking the time to think through and figure out. And that's exactly what we've done. So, at a point in time where we've taken certain actions that we can divulge publicly, we will do so. There are times where the message will be directly received by the Russians and not publicized. And I should point out by the way, part of why the Russians have been effective on this is because they don't go around announcing what they're doing. It's not like Putin's gone around the world publicly saying, look what we did. Wasn't that clever? He denies it. So the idea that somehow public shaming is gonna be effective, I think doesn't read the -- the thought process in Russia very well. OK. QUESTION: Did Clinton lose because of the hacking? OBAMA: I'm gonna let all the political pundits in this town have a long discussion about what happened in the election. It was a fascinating election. So, you know, I'm sure there are gonna be a lot of books written about it. I've said what I think is important for the Democratic Party going forward, rather than try to parse every aspect of the election. And I -- I've said before, I couldn't be prouder of Secretary Clinton, her outstanding service and she's worked tirelessly on behalf of the American people and I don't think she was treated fairly during the election. I think the coverage of her and the issues was troubling, but having said that, what I've been most focused on -- appropriate for the fact I am not going to be a politician in about -- what is it, 32 days, 31, 34? (LAUGHTER) What I've said is that I can maybe give some counsel advice to the Democratic Party. And I think the -- the -- the thing we have to spend the most time on -- because it's the thing we have most control over -- is, how do we make sure that we're showing up in places where I think Democratic policies are needed, where they are helping, where they are making a difference, but where people feel as if they're not being heard? And where Democrats are characterized as coastal, liberal, latte- sipping, you know, politically correct, out-of-touch folks, we have to be in those communities. And I've seen that, when we are in those communities, it makes a difference. That's how I became president. I became a U.S. Senator not just because I had a strong base in Chicago, but because I was driving downstate Illinois and going to fish fries and sitting in V.F.W. Halls and talking to farmers. And I didn't win every one of their votes, but they got a sense of what I was talking about, what I cared about, that I was for working people, that I was for the middle class, that the reason I was interested in strengthening unions and raising the minimum wage and rebuilding our infrastructure and making sure that parents had decent childcare and family leave, was because my own family's history wasn't that different from theirs even if I looked a little bit different. Same thing in Iowa. And so the question is, how do we rebuild that party as a whole, so that there's not a county in any state -- I don't care how red -- where we don't have a presence and we're not making the argument, because I think we have a better argument. But that requires a lot of work. You know, it's been something that I've been able to do successfully in my own campaigns. OBAMA: It is not something I've been able to transfer to candidates in mid-terms and sort of build a sustaining organization around. That's something I would have liked to have done more of, but it's kind of hard to do when you're also dealing with a whole bunch of issues here in the White House. And that doesn't mean, though, that it can't be done, and I think there are gonna be a lot of talented folks out there, a lot of progressives who share my values, who are gonna be leading the charge in the years to come. Michelle Kosinski (ph) of CNN. QUESTION: Thank you. This week we heard Hillary Clinton talk about how she thinks that the FBI director's most recent announcement made a difference in the outcome of the election. And we also just heard in an op-ed her campaign chairman talk about something being deeply broken within the FBI. He talked about thinking that the investigation early on was lackadaisical, in his words. So what do you think about those comments? Do you think there's any truth to them? Do you think there's a danger there that they're calling into question the integrity of institutions in a similar way that Donald Trump's team has done? And the second part to that is that Donald Trump's team repeatedly -- I guess, given the indication that the investigation of the Russian hack as well as retaliation might not be such a priority once he's in office. So what do you think the risk is there? And are you going to talk to him directly about some of those comments he made? OBAMA: Well, on the latter point, as I said before, the transition from election season to governance season is not always smooth. You know, it's bumpy. There are still feelings that are raw out there. There are people who are still thinking how things unfolded. And I get all that. But when Donald Trump takes the oath of office and is sworn in as the 45th president of the United States, then he has got a different set of responsibilities and considerations. And I've said this before. I think there is a sobering process when you walk into the Oval Office. And, you know, I haven't shared previously private conversations I've had with the president-elect. I will say that they have been cordial and in some cases have involved me making some pretty specific suggestions about how to ensure that regardless of our obvious deep disagreements about policy, maybe I can transmit some thoughts about maintaining the effectiveness, integrity, cohesion of the office, our various democratic institutions, and he has listened. I can't say that he will end up implementing, but the conversations themselves have been cordial as opposed to defensive in any way. And I will always make myself available to him just as previous presidents have made themselves available to me as issues come up. With respect to the FBI, I will tell you, I've had a chance to know a lot of FBI agents. I know Director Comey. They take their job seriously. They work really hard. They help keep us safe and save a lot of lives. And it is always a challenge for law enforcement when there's an intersection between the work that they are doing and the political system. It's one of the difficulties of democracy generally. We have a system where we want our law enforcement investigators and our prosecutors to be free from politics, to be independent, to play it straight. But sometimes that involves investigations that touch on politics and particularly in this hyper-partisan environment that we've been in, everything is suspect, everything you do one way or the other. One thing that I have done is to be pretty scrupulous about not wading into investigation decisions or prosecution decisions or decisions not to prosecute. I have tried to be really strict in my own behavior about preserving the independence of law enforcement, free from my own judgments and political assessments, in some cases. And I don't know why it would stop now. Mike Dorney (ph) of Bloomberg. QUESTION: Thank you, Mr. President. On Aleppo, your views of (ph) what happens there, the responsibility of the Russian government, the Iranian government, the Assad regime (inaudible), but do you, as president of the United States, leader of the free world, feel any personal moral responsibility now at the end of your presidency for the carnage we're all watching in Aleppo, which I'm sure disturbs you (inaudible)? Secondly, also on Aleppo, you've again made clear your practical disagreements with (inaudible) and President-elect Trump has throughout his campaign, and he said again last night, that he wants to create safe zones in Syria. Do you feel like in this transition, you need to help him toward implementing that or is that not something you need to be doing? OBAMA: Mike, I always feel responsible. I felt responsible when kids were being shot by snipers. I felt responsible when millions of people had been displaced. I feel responsible for murder and slaughter that's taken place in South Sudan that's not being reported on, partly because there's not as much social media being generated from there. There are places around the world where horrible things are happening and because of my office, because I'm president of the United States, I feel responsible. I ask myself every single day, is there something I could do that would save lives and make a difference and spare some child who doesn't deserve to suffer. So that's a starting point. There's not a moment during the course of this presidency where I haven't felt some responsibility. That's true, by the way, for our own country. When I came into office and people were losing their jobs and losing their homes and losing their pensions, I felt responsible and I would go home at night and I would ask myself, was there something better that I could do or smarter that I could be that would make a difference in their lives, that would relieve their suffering and relieve their hardship. So with respect to Syria, what I have consistently done is taken the best course that I can to try to end the civil war while having also to take into account the long-term national security interests of the United States. And throughout this process, based on hours of meetings -- if you tallied it up, days and weeks of meetings -- where we went through every option in painful detail with maps and we had our military and we had our aid agencies and we had our diplomatic teams, and sometimes, we'd bring in outsiders who were critics of ours. OBAMA: Whenever we went through it, the challenge was that short of putting large numbers of U.S. troops on the ground uninvited, without any international law mandate, without sufficient support from Congress, at a time when we still had troops in Afghanistan and we still had troops in Iraq and we had just gone through over a decade of war and spent trillions of dollars and when the opposition on the ground was not cohesive enough to necessarily govern a country and you had a military superpower in Russia prepared to do whatever it took to keep its (inaudible) involved and you had a regional military power in Iran that saw their own vital strategic interests at stake and were willing to maintain that territory in the absence of consent from the Syrian government and now the Russians or the Iranians. So it may be that with Aleppo's tragic situation unfolding that in the short term, if we can get more of the tens of thousands who are still trapped there out , that so long as the world's eyes are on them and they are feeling pressure, the regime in Russia concludes that they are willing to find some arrangement, perhaps in coordination with Turkey, whereby those people can be safe. Even that will probably be temporary, but at least it solves a short-term issue that's going to arise. Unfortunately we are not there yet because right now we have Russians and Assad claiming that basically all the innocent civilians who were trapped in Aleppo are out when international organizations, humanitarian organizations who know better and who are on the ground, have said unequivocally that there are still tens of thousands who are trapped and prepared to leave under pretty much any conditions. And so right now our biggest priority is to continue to put pressure wherever we can to try to get them out. (CROSSTALK) OBAMA: Mike, I can't have too much... QUESTION: (OFF-MIKE) but do you see, responsibility notwithstanding, moving in that direction or help President-elect Trump move in that direction? OBAMA: I will help President Trump -- President-elect Trump with any advice, counsel, information that we can provide so that he, once he's sworn in, can make a decision. Between now and then, these are decisions that I have to make based on the consultations that I have with our military and the people who have been working this every single day. Peter Alexander (ph). QUESTION: Mr. President, thank you very much. Can you, given all the intelligence that we have now heard, assure the public this was once and for all a free and fair election? And specifically on Russia, do you feel any obligation now as they have been insisting that this isn't the case to show the proof, as it were? They say, put your money where your mouth is and declassify some of the intelligence and the evidence that exists. And more broadly, as it relates to Donald Trump on this very topic, are you concerned about his relationship with Vladimir Putin, especially given some of the recent cabinet picks, including his selection for secretary of state, Rex Tillerson, who toasted Putin with champagne over oil deals together? Thank you. OBAMA: I may be getting older because these multipart questions, I start losing track. (LAUGHTER) OBAMA: I can assure the public that there was not the kind of tampering with the voting process that was our concern and will continue to be of concern going forward, that the votes that were cast were counted, they were counted appropriately. We have not seen evidence of machines being tampered with, so that assurance I can provide. That doesn't mean that we find every single, you know, potential probe of every single voting machine all across the country, but we paid a lot of attention to it. We worked with state officials, et cetera, and we feel confident that that didn't occur and that the votes were cast and they were counted. And so that's on that point. What was the second one? QUESTION: Say more about declassification. OBAMA: Declassification. Look, we will provide evidence that we can safely provide, that doesn't mean, though, that it can't be done, and I think there are going to be a lot of talented folks out there, a lot of progressives who share my values who are going to be leading the charge in the years to come. Michelle Kosinski (ph) of CNN. QUESTION: Thank you. This week we heard Hillary Clinton talk about how she thinks that the FBI director's most recent announcement made a difference in the outcome of the election. And we also just heard in an op-ed her campaign chairman talk about something being deeply broken within the FBI. He talked about thinking that the investigation early on was lackadaisical, in his words. So what do you think about those comments? Do you think there's any truth to them? Do you think there's a danger there that they're calling into question the integrity of institutions in a similar way that Donald Trump's team has done? And the second part to that is that Donald Trump's team repeatedly -- I guess, given the indication that the investigation of the Russian hack as well as retaliation might not be such a priority once he's in office. And I will always make myself available to him just as previous presidents have made themselves available to me as issues come up. They help keep us safe and save a lot of lives. And it is always a challenge for law enforcement when there's an intersection between the work that they are doing and the political system.
