Thought Lauer missed an opportunity to corner Trump on his support for the war, he did press the Republican nominee on an apparent contradiction between his declaration that he would have top generals present him with a blueprint to defeat ISIS with his longstanding intimation that he already has a secret plan to do so. “I have a substantial chance of winning, but if I win I don’t want to broadcast [the plan],” Trump explained. “The fact is, we have had the worst — and you could say the dumbest — foreign policy." Trump was perhaps most emphatic in defending his past comments about Putin, despite the fodder those remarks have provided for attacks from Clinton and her allies. “I think when he calls me brilliant I’ll take the compliment, but it’s not going to get him anywhere,” Trump told Lauer, who had just listed a roster of controversial Russian moves, including Moscow’s reported culpability for the hack of the Democratic National Committee earlier this year. Trump responded that Putin has an 82 percent approval rating in Russia: “He’s been a leader far more than our president has been a leader.” And, as part of his answer to a question posed by a father who said his daughter was concerned about rising incidents of sexual assault in the military, Trump also stood by a past tweet suggesting that allowing women to serve — rather than lax enforcement of military discipline — was to blame. If Clinton appeared defensive and cautious in answering the specific, pointed questions posed to her, Trump also stumbled with the broader queries Lauer offered him. At one point, he corrected a veteran who had lost two friends to suicide about veteran suicide statistics — when she in fact had the right number, shaking her head at his retort. His defense of Putin was politically dodgy, too — the Russian leader’s American approval rating is in the single digits, according to an NBC News survey conducted even before the DNC hack that his government is widely believed to be behind. And his insistence that the generals currently serving had “been reduced to rubble” neared crossing a line in military circles because of the implication that he would replace officers who are not political appointees. Tensions boiled over between Democrats and NBC News, as well, as they questioned Lauer's questioning: "@NBCNews it is irresponsible to allow Trump to go on a 60 second rant about a military intervention that he supported w/o clarifying," tweeted Clinton's traveling press secretary, Nick Merrill. The forum landed in the wake of new NBC News/Survey Monkey polling showing that Trump holds a substantial lead over Clinton when it comes to military and veteran voters — a crucial voting bloc in states like Virginia -- 55 percent to 36 percent overall. Even so, as the two campaigns spar over the numbers of military endorsers each has, Trump’s poll numbers in the military community are lower than either Mitt Romney's in 2012 or John McCain’s in 2008 relative to Obama’s. After a summer of Clinton bombarding Trump’s fitness for the office and a stretch in which he saw his support waver after clashing with the parents of a deceased soldier, however, this week’s pitches are not just targeted at the significant military community: They are each campaign’s latest attempt to appeal to middle-class voters concerned about national security and their own safety. While Trump has gone after Clinton aggressively over her emails, she has responded by pointing out some of his policy inconsistencies on the trail and specifically identifying his ties to Russia as particularly troubling. Prior to the event, members of the Trump team spent pieces of the morning playing defense after Clinton’s super PAC, Priorities USA Action, promoted an ad that prominently featured the candidate declaring his love for war, the latest in a series of spots from both the campaign and super PAC making the case that Trump is far too inconsistent and unpredictable to sit in the Oval Office or have control of the nuclear codes. But on Wednesday, the man who once promised to “bomb the hell out of ISIS" spit the accusation right back at his opponent. “The current strategy of toppling regimes, with no plan for what to do the day after, only produces power vacuums that are filled by terrorists,” Trump said earlier in the day at a speech in Philadelphia. “Immediately after taking office, I will ask my generals to present me with a plan within 30 days to defeat and destroy ISIS." Even as he called for a massive increase in the military budget, Trump suggested he would more judicious in using force -- upending the traditional partisan battles lines on national security. “Unlike my opponent, my foreign policy will emphasize diplomacy, not destruction. Hillary Clinton’s legacy in Iraq, Libya, and Syria has produced only turmoil and suffering,” he said in Pennsylvania, skipping over his own one-time support for intervention in some of those conflicts. “Sometimes it has seemed like there wasn’t a country in the Middle East that Hillary Clinton didn’t want to invade, intervene or topple. She is trigger-happy and unstable when it comes to war,” he said.

During a "commander-in-chief forum" on NBC News on September 7th, Democratic presidential nominee Hillary Clinton and her Republican opponent Donald Trump addressed critical national security and foreign policy issues. Each candidate participated in a 30-minute segment, fielding questions on these vital topics from moderator Matt Lauer and audience members. This document provides a comprehensive review of the questionable claims made by both candidates during the event. As per our established practice, we refrain from awarding "Pinocchios" during live broadcasts.

**Hillary Clinton's Statements:**

*   **Regarding her private email server:** Clinton stated, "It was a mistake to have a personal [email] account." The central issue revolved around her use of a private server and her failure to utilize her designated State Department email account, which would have ensured the preservation of emails subject to Freedom of Information Act requests. She asserted, "Classified material has a header which says ‘top secret,’ ‘secret,’ ‘confidential.’ Nothing — and I will repeat this, and this is verified in the report by the Department of Justice — none of the emails sent or received by me had such a header." Initially, Clinton claimed, "I did not email any classified material to anyone on my email. There is no classified material." As the State Department began retroactively classifying some emails, her statement evolved to, "I’m confident that this process will prove that I never sent nor received any email that was marked classified." She has since become more specific, referencing headers indicating "top secret" and similar designations. This specificity arises because FBI Director James Comey disclosed that three emails sent to Clinton's server contained the marking "(c)" for "confidential" but lacked a formal header. The State Department indicated that two of these emails were erroneously marked. It is important to note that classification markings do not definitively render information classified, nor does their absence render it unclassified. Comey further revealed that 110 emails, separate from the 2,000 retroactively classified, were found to "contain classified information at the time they were sent or received." He also stated, "there is evidence to support a conclusion that any reasonable person in Secretary Clinton’s position, or in the position of those government employees with whom she was corresponding about these matters, should have known that an unclassified system was no place for that conversation." Therefore, Clinton's emphasis on the presence or absence of a "header" appears to be a misdirection.

*   **On her system being hacked:** Clinton claimed, "There is no evidence my system was hacked." While the FBI did not uncover evidence of Clinton's emails being compromised, their report did highlight numerous attempts to access her private server and noted its lack of expected safeguards for sensitive communications. Furthermore, an aide to Bill Clinton, utilizing the Clinton private server, was indeed hacked. The report also indicated that "hostile foreign actors" successfully infiltrated the email systems of close personal aides to Hillary Clinton, acquiring emails.

*   **On veteran suicides:** In response to a question about veteran suicide rates, Clinton stated, "Twenty — twenty suicides a day." This aligns with the updated, accurate statistic. Trump, however, reverted to the outdated and misleading figure of 22 veteran suicides per day. The discrepancy between these figures is significant. The updated statistic is derived from the most comprehensive, data-driven study on veteran suicides to date, whereas the older figure was a rough estimate based on incomplete data, a figure even its researchers cautioned against using. The Department of Veterans Affairs, in collaboration with the Department of Defense and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, conducted a study of veteran mortality records, compiling 55 million records over 35 years using military, health, and mortality data. This study found that in 2014, an average of 20 veterans died by suicide daily, representing an actual count from all 50 states. Of these suicides in 2014, 65 percent involved veterans aged 50 and older. The study also revealed that from 2001 to 2014, the suicide rate among veterans utilizing VA services increased by 8.8 percent, compared to a 38.6 percent increase among veterans who did not use VA services. It is crucial to provide context to the statistic of 20 veteran suicides per day: veterans generally exhibit higher suicide rates than the civilian population, though the degree of this difference varies across specific demographics. Clinton's presentation of the statistic lacked this essential context, rendering it less informative.

*   **On VA privatization:** Clinton asserted, "I will not let the V.A. be privatized. And I do think there is an agenda out there, supported by my opponent, to do just that. I think that would be very disastrous for our military veterans." This is a misleading assertion often made by Clinton regarding veterans' issues. She has previously referenced proposals by Concerned Veterans for America, a conservative veterans advocacy group, to reform the Department of Veterans Affairs. These proposals, developed by a bipartisan task force, would offer veterans the option of subsidized private care and establish a new, nonprofit government corporation to manage VA medical facilities. While these plans incorporate elements that open veteran healthcare to the private sector, the term "privatization" typically implies a complete transfer of government services to private industry, which does not accurately describe the group's proposal. Furthermore, Trump has stated he does not support such a privatization of the VA, as he clarified during the forum. In a July 2016 speech outlining his veterans' policy, he declared, "veterans should be guaranteed the right to choose their doctor and clinics, whether at a VA facility or at a private medical center." Trump's position is to provide veterans with the option of private healthcare if they reside far from a VA facility or face lengthy appointment wait times. This mirrors a provision in the 2014 Veterans Choice Act, a bipartisan bill enacted under President Obama in response to the VA scandal.

*   **On gun purchases by individuals on terror watch lists:** Clinton stated, "Here at home, for goodness’s sakes, we have to finally pass a law prohibiting people on the terrorist watch list from being able to buy a gun in the United States of America." While Democrats frequently highlight that individuals on the terror watch list can acquire firearms, the legislative proposals they have put forth do not automatically ban such purchases. Senator Dianne Feinstein (D-Calif.) has introduced legislation granting the attorney general the authority to determine if a suspected terrorist can purchase a firearm. Individuals subjected to a federal terrorism investigation within five years of attempting to buy a gun would be flagged in the background check system, allowing the Justice Department to review these cases. The government employs a "reasonable suspicion" standard for inclusion on the Terrorist Watchlist, which encompasses the "no fly list." Membership in a terrorist organization or inclusion on a watch list does not inherently prevent an individual from purchasing a gun. An additional disqualifying factor under federal or state law, such as a felony conviction or illegal immigration status, must be present.

*   **On the Iraq War:** Clinton correctly stated, "Now, my opponent was for the war in Iraq. He says he wasn’t. You can go back and look at the record. He supported it. He told Howard Stern he supported it." This has been extensively documented. There is no evidence that Trump opposed the Iraq War at its outset. He reiterated this false claim during the forum.

*   **On intervention in Libya:** Clinton accurately noted, "With respect to Libya, again, there’s no difference between my opponent and myself. He’s on record extensively supporting intervention in Libya, when Gadhafi was threatening to massacre his population." This is also correct, with video evidence of Trump advocating for intervention, initially highlighted by BuzzFeed.

*   **On ground troops in Iraq and Syria:** Clinton remarked, "They are not going to get ground troops. We are not putting ground troops into Iraq ever again. And we’re not putting ground troops into Syria. We’re going to defeat ISIS without committing American ground troops." This statement is perplexing given the presence of approximately 3,000 U.S. troops in Iraq at the time, serving in advisory and training capacities or on special assignments. Clinton's comment drew widespread mockery on Twitter. While she appeared to be referring to combat troops, her phrasing was imprecise. Prior to this remark, Clinton seemed to acknowledge the existing troops by stating that Arab forces engaged against the Islamic State would receive support from the United States, including "special forces, as you know, they have enablers, they have surveillance, intelligence, reconnaissance help."

*   **On troop deployments when she became Secretary of State:** Clinton stated, "When I became secretary of state, we had 200,000 troops deployed in Iraq and Afghanistan." This figure is close to accurate. Troop levels in Iraq were declining when President Obama took office, while he initiated a surge in Afghanistan, increasing troop numbers from approximately 50,000 to 100,000 by August 2010. In June 2010, there were still 88,000 troops in Iraq, down from 150,000 prior to Obama's presidency. The Congressional Research Service reported an average of 186,300 troops across both theaters in fiscal year 2009.

**Donald Trump's Statements:**

*   **On the Iraq War:** Trump asserted, "I happened to hear Hillary Clinton say that I was not against the war in Iraq. I was totally against the war in Iraq. You can look at Esquire magazine from ’04. You can look at before that." This claim is difficult to reconcile with the facts. Trump did not express opposition to the Iraq War prior to the

query: What specific technology does Gillette claim Dollar Shave Club's products infringe upon in their lawsuit?

documents.text:
**Gillette Sues Dollar Shave Club Over Razor Patent**

Gillette, a leading razor brand owned by Procter & Gamble (P&G), has filed a lawsuit against online razor retailer Dollar Shave Club. The legal action, initiated in federal court in Delaware, alleges that Dollar Shave Club's products **infringe on a 2004 Gillette patent**. This patent specifically covers a razor blade featuring a **"chromium-containing overcoat layer."** Gillette is seeking **monetary damages** and a **court order to halt the sale of these allegedly infringing razors**.

The lawsuit **does not name Dorco**, the South Korean manufacturer of Dollar Shave Club's razors, despite Dorco being an equity stakeholder in the startup. A P&G spokesman, Damon Jones, clarified that Dollar Shave Club was named because it is **"actually selling the product and bringing it to market."** Jones explained that P&G regularly conducts **"market surveillance,"** which involves testing competitor products. During a recent testing phase of Dollar Shave Club blades, P&G identified what it believed to be patent-infringing razors. This testing was prompted by press reports indicating Dollar Shave Club was **"investing new money in product development."**

A representative for Dollar Shave Club has **declined to comment** on the lawsuit. The online retailer has secured nearly **$150 million in equity investments** and was recently valued by investors at approximately **$600 million**. Dollar Shave Club gained significant traction after its **2012 launch**, which featured a viral promotional video starring CEO Michael Dubin. Since then, the company has employed a strategy of **irreverent online videos, TV commercials, and social media campaigns** to build a substantial customer base. Earlier this year, Dubin stated that the company had **over two million subscribers** receiving monthly or bi-monthly razor blade shipments. He also projected **at least $140 million in revenue for 2015**.

In response to the competitive landscape, Gillette launched its own service, **Gillette Shave Club, last year**. The company enlisted celebrities, such as Giants quarterback Eli Manning, to promote it in the lead-up to Father's Day in June.

*Correction: The patent in question pertains to a razor blade that includes an overcoat layer, not the overcoat layer itself.*

Gillette is escalating its competition with Dollar Shave Club by filing a patent infringement lawsuit. This legal move targets Dollar Shave Club's rapid ascent in the U.S. razor and blade market, a sector long dominated by Gillette. In just three years, Dollar Shave Club has secured 8% of the $3 billion market, presenting a significant challenge to Gillette's established position.

The lawsuit, lodged by Procter & Gamble's (P&G) Gillette division in Delaware, seeks to prevent Dollar Shave Club from selling razors that allegedly violate one of Gillette's patents. The complaint specifically points to a 2004 patent covering two coatings designed to enhance blade sharpness and durability. These coatings are currently utilized in Gillette's popular Mach 3, Venus, and Fusion product lines.

A P&G spokesperson, Damon Jones, indicated that the company regularly reviews competitor products and had "reason to believe" Dollar Shave Club was altering its offerings. Following an examination of Dollar Shave Club's blades, P&G asserts it discovered the alleged patent infringement. Dollar Shave Club has declined to comment on the litigation.

This legal action comes as Gillette, a company with over a century of history, faces challenges adapting to online competitors. While Gillette holds over 60% of the U.S. retail razor and blade market, its online market share is only 20%. In response, Gillette launched its own subscription service, Gillette Shave Club, last year. P&G has not released subscriber figures for this service but reported a four-percentage-point increase in its e-commerce share for blades and razors since the service's relaunch in June.

P&G has a history of employing legal strategies to counter competitive threats. In 2014, the company sued Hello Products LLC over claims that their toothpaste was "99% natural." P&G has also defended its Crest Whitestrips product against a private-label manufacturer. "We take every violation of our intellectual property seriously, and when necessary, we take legal action to defend our business as we have in the past and will continue to do in the future," Mr. Jones stated.

Dollar Shave Club gained widespread recognition in 2012 with a viral video featuring founder Michael Dubin. The company's direct-to-consumer model, offering razor cartridges for as little as $1 per month, appealed to consumers seeking alternatives to rising shaving costs. The company now reports 10 million customers purchasing blades at least every other month, with prices ranging from $1 to $9. Mr. Dubin projected $140 million in sales for the current year, encompassing other personal care items. Dollar Shave Club has raised over $90 million in venture funding, with its latest valuation at $630 million, a stark contrast to P&G's market value of $220 billion.

Gillette's subscription service employs a different approach than Dollar Shave Club's recommendation of weekly blade changes. P&G suggests that its premium razor blades can cost men approximately $5 per month, factoring in blade durability and an assumed shaving frequency of three to four times per week. The lawsuit alleges that Dollar Shave Club's razors infringe on Gillette's patent through the use of chromium to adhere an outer layer of polytetrafluoroethylene on their blades. Dollar Shave Club sources its blades from Dorco, a U.S. division of a South Korean manufacturer that also produces private-label razors for retailers like Walgreens and Dollar General Corp. Gillette's lawsuit does not name Dorco as a defendant.

Here's a revised version of the open letter, aiming to meet your quality targets:

**An Open Letter From The Thousands Of Men And Women At Gillette**

Trust in our word is as important as trust in our product – they go hand in hand. Recently, there's been a lot of discussion about Gillette: our claims, our technology, our pricing, and our marketing. Essentially, people are talking about us, the individuals who work here, and what we stand for. We want to speak to you directly about a few things that are incredibly important to us.

First and foremost, **shaving is our passion**. We don't just mean the daily act of shaving; we mean shaving as a **science**. For us, **every single detail matters**. We conduct over 60 precise quality checks, from the raw materials to the final cartridge. Each one **must pass every time**, or it will never leave our facilities. We are confident that **no other razor manufacturer globally adheres to this rigorous standard**. Our team works tirelessly every day to ensure our razors are **superior to any other product available**. Our goal is to provide you with the **absolute best shave possible**, consistently exceeding your expectations. Why is this crucial? Because we want you to start every day with the **confidence that you're using the optimal razor for the job**, without needing to worry about the correct shaving technique or experiencing irritation from inferior razors that are merely rebranded with a fancy name and website.

Secondly, we understand that being the best isn't always enough, and you've told us that **our blades can be too expensive**. We have heard you. **Effective April 1st, the majority of Gillette blade refills in the United States will be available at lower prices** at most retailers, both online and in-store. This is a **genuine price reduction**, not a temporary promotion. You will receive the **exact same high-quality products** you've come to expect – with **zero compromise on quality, period**. Please note that actual retail prices are determined solely by individual retailers, as is standard in the industry. You can find an overview of our products and pricing on our website.

Finally, we are thrilled that **over 200,000 of you have returned to Gillette** in just the past few weeks. Many of you have explored other products, and we are proud that you have come back to the **Gillette quality you know and trust**. It's a significant fact that between November 2013 and October 2015, **more than half of individuals who tried a competitor's product did not make a repeat purchase**. This trend was evident even before we launched our "Welcome Back" offer last year. While some competitors may not welcome this news, ultimately, **the quality of the shave should be the deciding factor**. We are a company with a legacy of over 115 years, built on the core values of **quality, innovation, and trust**. These are principles we have no intention of changing.

Thank you for entrusting us with your faces, every single day.

– The Faces Behind the Blades, the Men and Women of Gillette

The invention relates to improvements to razors and razor blades. A razor blade is typically formed of a suitable substrate material such as stainless steel, and a cutting edge is formed with a wedge-shaped configuration with an ultimate tip having a radius less than about 1000 angstroms, e.g., about 200-300 angstroms. Hard coatings such as diamond, amorphous diamond, diamond-like carbon-(DLC) material, nitrides, carbides, oxides or ceramics are often used to improve strength, corrosion resistance and shaving ability, maintaining needed strength while permitting thinner edges with lower cutting forces to be used. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) outer layer can be used to provide friction reduction. Interlayers of niobium or chromium containing materials can aid in improving the binding between the substrate, typically stainless steel, and hard carbon coatings, such as DLC. Examples of razor blade cutting edge structures and processes of manufacture are described in U.S. Pat. Nos. 5,295,305; 5,232,568; 4,933,058; 5,032,243; 5,497,550; 5,940,975; 5,669,144; EP 0591339; and PCT 92/03330, which are hereby incorporated by reference. In use, the ultimate tip of the edges having hard coatings and polytetrafluoroethylene outer layers can become more rounded after repeated shaves such that there is an increase in the tip radius and a generally perceived decrease in shaving performance. SUMMARY OF THE INVENTION In one aspect, the invention features, in general, a razor blade including a substrate with a cutting edge defined by a sharpened tip and adjacent facets, a layer of hard coating on the cutting edge, an overcoat layer of a chromium containing material on the layer of hard coating, and an outer layer of polytetrafluoroethylene coating on the overcoat layer. In another aspect the invention features, in general, a shaving razor including a handle and a razor head with a blade having a substrate with a cutting edge defined by a sharpened tip and adjacent facets, a layer of hard coating on the cutting edge, an overcoat layer of a chromium containing material on the layer of hard coating, and an outer layer of polytetrafluoroethylene coating on the overcoat layer. Particular embodiments of the invention may include one or more of the following features. In particular embodiments, the hard coating material can be made of carbon containing materials (e.g., diamond, amorphous diamond or DLC), nitrides, carbides, oxides or other ceramics. The hard coating layer can have a thickness less than 2,000 angstroms. The overcoat layer can be made of chromium or a chromium containing alloy compatible with polytetrafluoroethylene such as a chromium platinum alloy. The overcoat layer can be between 100 and 500 angstroms thick. The blade can include an interlayer between the substrate and the layer of hard coating. The interlayer can include niobium or a chromium containing material. The polytetrafluoroethylene can be Krytox LW1200 available from DuPont. The PTFE outer layer can be between 100 and 5000 angstroms thick. In another aspect, the invention features, in general, making a razor blade by providing a substrate with a cutting edge defined by a sharpened tip and adjacent facets, adding a layer of hard coating on the cutting edge, adding an overcoat layer of a chromium containing material on the layer of hard coating, and adding an outer layer of polytetrafluoroethylene coating over the overcoat layer. Particular embodiments of the invention may include one or more of the following features. In particular embodiments the layers can be added by physical vapor deposition (i.e., sputtering) or by chemical vapor deposition. The chromium containing layer, preferably chromium, can be sputter deposited under conditions that result in a compressively stressed coating. The sputter deposition of chromium containing materials can include applying a DC bias to the target that is more negative than −50 volts, preferably more negative than −200 volts. Alternatively an appropriate RF bias scheme can be used to achieve an equivalent chromium layer. Embodiments of the invention may include one or more of the following advantages. The use of a chromium containing overcoat layer provides improved adhesion of the polytetrafluorethylene outer layer to the hard coating layer. The razor blade has improved edge strength provided by hard coating and has reduced tip rounding with repeated shaves. Reduced tip rounding minimizes the increase in cutting force thereby maintaining excellent shaving performance. The razor blade has excellent shaving characteristics from the first shave onwards. Other features and advantages of the invention will be apparent from the following description of a particular embodiment and from the claims. DESCRIPTION OF THE DRAWINGS FIG. 1 is a vertical sectional view of a cutting edge portion of a razor blade. FIG. 2 is a perspective view of a shaving razor including the FIG. 1 razor blade. DESCRIPTION OF A PARTICULAR EMBODIMENT Referring to FIG. 1, there is shown razor blade 10 including substrate 12, interlayer 14, hard coating layer 16, overcoat layer 18, and outer layer 20. The substrate 12 is typically made of stainless steel (though other substrates can be employed) and has an ultimate edge sharpened to a tip radius of less than 1,000 angstroms, preferably 200 to 300 angstroms, and has a profile with side facets 22 at an included angle of between 15 and 30 degrees, preferably about 19 degrees, measured at 40 microns from the tip. Interlayer 14 is used to facilitate bonding of the hard coating layer to the substrate. Examples of suitable interlayer material are niobium and chromium containing material. A particular interlayer is made of niobium greater than 100 angstroms and preferably less than 500 angstroms thick. PCT 92/03330 describes use of a niobium interlayer. Hard coating layer 16 provides improved strength, corrosion resistance and shaving ability and can be made from carbon containing materials (e.g., diamond, amorphous diamond or DLC), nitrides (e.g., boron nitride, niobium nitride or titanium nitride), carbides (e.g., silicon carbide), oxides (e.g., alumina, zirconia) or other ceramic materials. The carbon containing materials can be doped with other elements, such as tungsten, titanium or chromium by including these additives, for example in the target during application by sputtering. The materials can also incorporate hydrogen, e.g., hydrogenated DLC. Preferably coating layer 16 is made of diamond, amorphous diamond or DLC. A particular embodiment includes DLC less than 2,000 angstroms, preferably less than 1,000 angstroms. DLC layers and methods of deposition are described in U.S. Pat. No. 5,232,568. As described in the “Handbook of Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) Processing,” DLC is an amorphous carbon material that exhibits many of the desirable properties of diamond but does not have the crystalline structure of diamond. Overcoat layer 18 is used to reduce the tip rounding of the hard coated edge and to facilitate bonding of the outer layer to the hard coating while still maintaining the benefits of both. Overcoat layer 18 is preferably made of chromium containing material, e.g., chromium or chromium alloys that are compatible with polytetrafluoroethylene, e.g., CrPt. A particular overcoat layer is chromium about 100-200 angstroms thick. Blade 10 has a cutting edge that has less rounding with repeated shaves than it would have without the overcoat layer. Outer layer 20 is used to provide reduced friction and includes polytetrafluoroethylene and is sometimes referred to as a telomer. A particular polytetrafluoroethylene material is Krytox LW 1200 available from DuPont. This material is a nonflammable and stable dry lubricant that consists of small particles that yield stable dispersions. It is furnished as an aqueous dispersion of 20% solids by weight and can be applied by dipping, spraying, or brushing, and can thereafter be air dried or melt coated. The layer is preferably less than 5,000 angstroms and could typically be 1,500 angstroms to 4,000 angstroms, and can be as thin as 100 angstroms, provided that a continuous coating is maintained. Provided that a continuous coating is achieved, reduced telomer coating thickness can provide improved first shave results. U.S. Pat. Nos. 5,263,256 and 5,985,459, which are hereby incorporated by reference, describe techniques which can be used to reduce the thickness of an applied telomer layer. Razor blade 10 is made generally according to the processes described in the above referenced patents. A particular embodiment includes a niobium interlayer 14, DLC hard coating layer 16, chromium overcoat layer 18, and Krytox LW1200 polytetrafluoroethylene outer coat layer 20. Chromium overcoat layer 18 is deposited to a minimum of 100 angstroms and a maximum of 500 angstroms. It is deposited by sputtering using a DC bias (more negative than −50 volts and preferably more negative than −200 volts) and pressure of about 2 millitorr argon. The increased negative bias is believed to promote a compressive stress (as opposed to a tensile stress), in the chromium overcoat layer which is believed to promote improved resistance to tip rounding while maintaining good shaving performance. Blade 10 preferably has a tip radius of about 200-400 angstroms, measured by SEM after application of overcoat layer 18 and before adding outer layer 20. Referring to FIG. 2, blade 10 can be used in shaving razor 110, which includes handle 112 and replaceable shaving cartridge 114. Cartridge 14 includes housing 116, which carries three blades 10, guard 120 and cap 122. Blades 10 are movably mounted, as described, e.g., in U.S. Pat. No. 5,918,369, which is incorporated by reference. Cartridge 114 also includes an interconnect member on which housing 116 is pivotally mounted at two arms 128. The interconnect member includes a base 127 which is replaceably connected to handle 112. Alternatively, blade 10 can be used in other razors having one, two or more than three blades, double-sided blades, and razors that do not have movable blades or pivoting heads where the cartridge is either replaceable or permanently attached to a razor handle. In use, razor blade 10 has excellent shaving characteristics from the first shave onwards. Blade 10 has improved edge strength provided by hard coating and has reduced tip rounding with repeated shaves provided by the overlayer coating while maintaining excellent shave characteristics. Other embodiments of the invention are within the scope of the appended claims.

Gillette, a subsidiary of Procter & Gamble, has initiated a patent infringement lawsuit against Dollar Shave Club. The legal action, filed in the U.S. District Court for Delaware, contends that Dollar Shave Club's products unlawfully utilize Gillette's patented blade coating technology. This advanced coating is a crucial element in the performance of Gillette's leading razor systems, including the widely recognized Mach3, Venus, and Fusion product lines. Gillette, headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts, firmly maintains its exclusive ownership of the patent rights that are the subject of this dispute. Dollar Shave Club, based in Venice, California, has acknowledged receiving the legal documents and is currently conducting a thorough examination of the claims made against it. A representative for Dollar Shave Club indicated that the company would refrain from issuing further comments on the matter for the present. Specifically, Gillette has identified a number of Dollar Shave Club's offerings, such as The Humble Twin, The 4X, and The Executive, as the particular products alleged to be infringing upon their established patent.

**Dollar Shave Club Claims No. 2 in Razor Cartridge Sales, Challenging Industry Giants**

Dollar Shave Club (DSC) has announced a significant milestone, asserting it has **surpassed Schick to capture the No. 2 position in razor cartridge volume sales** as of July. This achievement comes just over three years after the online disruptor's market debut. While this claim awaits independent verification, it generally aligns with available third-party data. If confirmed, DSC would trail only Procter & Gamble Co.'s dominant Gillette brand in dollar sales and also Schick in that metric.

### **Aggressive Marketing Fuels Explosive Growth**

The online shaving brand has experienced **phenomenal subscriber growth, doubling its base to 2.2 million in under ten months**. This surge is largely attributed to a **major national television advertising campaign launched in November**. According to founder and CEO Michael Dubin, DSC has been the **most significant spender on television advertising within the razor category since November**, outspending even Gillette. This aggressive push is further supported by a substantial **$75 million venture capital round closed in June**, earmarked to continue fueling its TV ad initiatives, following a $50 million round nine months prior.

### **Strategic Focus: Fresh Blades and Unbeatable Value**

"TV has been a winner for us and a space we're going to stay in," stated Mr. Dubin, underscoring the brand's commitment to the medium. A **new advertising campaign, rolling out this month, will highlight the distinct benefits of using fresh razor blades**. "It feels great to shave with a fresh blade," Mr. Dubin remarked. "And we feel there are millions more guys out there that have that perception and would enjoy that experience. This campaign is about finding those guys."

### **The Highly Lucrative Razor Cartridge Market**

Razor cartridges represent the **largest and most profitable segment of the shaving market**, a high-margin category within packaged goods. Dollar Shave Club reports a **16% volume share of the cartridge market for July**, based on its internal sales data. This figure is closely watched against Nielsen's annual scanner data, which indicates a 15% volume share for Schick. However, Nielsen data compiled by Deutsche Bank suggests Schick's cartridge volume share for the four weeks ending August 8th was approximately one point higher than its annual share, potentially placing the two brands in a **near tie**.

### **Competitor Perspectives and Market Dynamics**

Chris Gough, VP-investor relations at Edgewell, Schick's parent company, declined to comment on Mr. Dubin's share claim. He cited Dollar Shave Club's private status, lack of publicly audited financials, and operation within an online marketplace with limited third-party sales data. He also noted that Nielsen's coverage does not encompass the entire shaving market.

### **Third-Party Data Points to Continued Momentum**

Conversely, third-party data from Slice Intelligence, which analyzes online purchase receipts from a panel of 2 million consumers, indicates that Dollar Shave Club is **either already the No. 2 U.S. razor cartridge brand or will be imminently, even by dollar sales**. Slice data corroborates Dollar Shave Club's impressive growth trajectory, showing online sales of **$16.3 million in July**. This figure more than doubles the $7.7 million recorded in October, prior to the launch of its in-house TV campaign, and nearly triples the brand's sales rate from the previous year.

### **Schick's Performance and Market Position**

In contrast, Schick's offline unit sales declined by **6.8% year-over-year** for the four weeks ending August 8th, according to Nielsen, with dollar sales falling by 5.5%. While these figures are slightly better than the full-year results, Dollar Shave Club, which focuses on more affordable blades intended for weekly replacement, still trails Schick in cartridge dollar share by a **9% to 16% margin**, according to DSC's own Nielsen-aided estimates. Schick also maintains a lead in both volume and dollar sales when considering broader razor product categories, including disposables and handles.

### **A Remarkable Rise for a Young Upstart**

Despite these differences, Dollar Shave Club's ascent is a **notable achievement for a three-year-old company challenging an 89-year-old incumbent**. If Dollar Shave Club sustains its current monthly sales pace, it is projected to achieve **nearly $200 million in sales over the next twelve months**, according to Slice data. Even if its current annual growth rate were halved, DSC could **overtake Schick in cartridge dollar sales within a year**. A hypothetical tripling of its sales, though ambitious, could see the club surpass Schick in total dollar sales. Schick reported $222 million in cartridge sales over the past year, per Nielsen, with an additional estimated $13 million from online sales according to Slice. Schick also generated $115 million in razor handle sales and $239 million in disposable razor sales, a segment where Dollar Shave Club does not compete.

### **The Shifting Landscape of the Offline Market**

The offline razor market, while larger, is experiencing a **decline**. Nielsen estimates offline razor cartridge sales at $1.3 billion, razor handles at $427 million, and disposable razors at $1.2 billion, totaling nearly $3 billion. While disposable razor sales saw a modest 1.8% growth over the past year, sales of blades and handles each decreased by over 4%.

### **Competitive Responses and Future Challenges**

Mr. Dubin's success has not gone unnoticed by competitors. Gillette launched its "Gillette Shave Club" online sales effort in June, advertising its blades as 50% cheaper than "the other shave club." Nielsen data indicates a continued dip in Gillette's offline sales and market share in recent months, with Mr. Dubin reporting no discernible impact on his business from these ads. However, Slice data does show a **rebound in Gillette's online share**, increasing by over 4 percentage points to 18.6% from June to July, while Dollar Shave Club's share has seen a decline of over 4 percentage points since March from a peak of nearly 54%.

Coincidentally, the new Dollar Shave Club advertisements humorously address the practice of shaving with old, dirty blades to save money. Gillette is also preparing to introduce an improved men's cartridge early next year, along with new theft-resistant packaging designed to make blades more accessible from behind locked store cases – a practice that has been a recurring theme in Dollar Shave Club's advertising.

### **Emerging Competition in the Value Segment**

Dollar Shave Club also faces increasing competition in the affordable blade market, potentially from unexpected sources. Dorco, the South Korean manufacturer widely believed by industry insiders to produce DSC's blades, has been more aggressively marketing its own brand online. According to Slice, Dorco captured an **8.5% share of U.S. online sales last month** and also launched value-priced Swipe blades at Costco earlier this year.

### Kalamazoo Shootings: A Night of Unspeakable Terror and Witness Accounts

The Kalamazoo shootings, a series of horrific events that unfolded on a Saturday night, left a community in shock and mourning. **Twelve-year-old Joi Coleman** recounted a **terrifying encounter** in the parking lot of the Meadows Townhomes. She described holding a woman's hand when a man, later identified as **Jason Dalton**, approached. Dalton allegedly asked, "Have you seen Mazie?" According to Coleman, the woman offered help, but Dalton **immediately opened fire**.

Coleman vividly described the unfolding horror: "He shot her once and she was still running and then he shot her again and she fell. She was asking 'Joi, help me. Joi, help me.'" The young witness expressed her **overwhelming fear**, stating, "I knew I was going to get shot too, he didn't stop shooting." Despite the terror, Coleman and the children she was supervising managed to reach safety inside. The victim's daughter witnessed the event from a window. Police reported Dalton fired **10 rounds**, with **three bullets striking her mother**.

Another child present, Meghan Coleman, shared the **chilling words** of a young girl who ran to the window, crying, "My mom is dead! My mom is dead!" The shooter, described as "the guy," reportedly **backed up and resumed shooting**, narrowly missing. Joi's mother, who was caring for 11 children, stated that Dalton **returned to the scene four times**, with Joi recalling him pointing a silver gun directly at their house.

Neighbors Travis Gettys and Devin Fletter, who live adjacent to the Colemans, reported seeing Dalton interacting with children in the neighborhood earlier that day, about an hour before the shooting. Fletter commented, "Makes me feel like he was looking for a specific person." Gettys described the surreal experience, noting, "It's such an adrenaline rush, we were just playing video games and everything happened." Joi Coleman also remembered Dalton **initially observing them and other children** before firing. The woman who was shot was expected to recover after sustaining injuries to her legs and arm.

### Escalating Violence and Devastating Impact

Travis Gettys emphasized the **direct link between the various incidents**, stating, "At this point the connection between the three cases is the defendant. Is Mister Dalton." The violence escalated just hours after the initial shooting. Shortly after 10 p.m., **17-year-old Tyler Smith and his father, Richard Smith, were fatally shot** while looking at cars at Seelye Kia on Stadium Drive. Minutes later, **close friends Mary L. Nye, Mary Jo Nye, Judy Brown, and Barbara Hawthorne were killed** in their vehicles at the Cracker Barrel parking lot. A 14-year-old who was also in one of the cars remained on life support at Bronson Hospital.

Gettys expressed the **profound shock and grief** gripping West Michigan and the nation, stating, "This is just the most awful thing I can think of having happened." As victims and their families grapple with the senseless violence attributed to one man in the Kalamazoo community, Gettys reflected, "I never thought it would happen in my lifetime." When asked about her feelings on Sunday, Joi Coleman simply responded, **"Scared."**

### A Plea for Privacy and Healing

On Wednesday, the Kalamazoo County Sheriff's Office released a statement regarding the victim from The Meadows Apartments. The family has requested that her name and any related information **not be released**, as they are not in a condition to speak with the media. They have indicated they will inform Sheriff Richard Fuller when they are ready. The Sheriff's Office urged the media and concerned parties to **allow the family the necessary time and space** to cope with the situation and focus on recovery. The victim's condition remains serious.

**Uber Faces Scrutiny Over Driver Safety Following Kalamazoo Shootings**

In the wake of a tragic mass shooting in Kalamazoo, Michigan, ride-hailing giant Uber is once again under intense scrutiny regarding its driver safety protocols. A driver, Jason Dalton, has been charged with murder in connection with the deaths of six people. Uber, a rapidly expanding San Francisco-based company, **defended its driver screening process**, asserting that it could not have foreseen Dalton's alleged actions.

Uber maintains that its **safety procedures are robust and do not require modification**. However, some experts suggest the company needs to implement more stringent vetting for potential drivers to reassure passengers of their safety. These experts acknowledge that Dalton, who had no prior criminal record, would likely not have raised any red flags during a standard background check.

Dalton, who had been driving for Uber for less than a month, is accused of carrying out the shootings over a nearly five-hour period on Saturday night. The Kalamazoo County prosecutor indicated that Dalton **picked up Uber passengers both before and after the initial shootings**.

Uber's Chief Security Officer, Joe Sullivan, stated that Dalton passed a background check and was approved as a driver on January 25th. He had completed over 100 rides and maintained an impressive rating of 4.73 stars out of five. Sullivan emphasized that, prior to Saturday's events, Uber had **no indication of any issues with Dalton's behavior**. He further commented, "No background check would have flagged and anticipated this situation."

Despite Uber's assurances, this incident has reignited concerns about the company's security measures. Earlier this month, Uber agreed to a **$28.5 million settlement** to resolve lawsuits alleging deceptive practices regarding safety procedures and fees. Additionally, the company is facing a separate lawsuit from district attorneys in San Francisco and Los Angeles, who claim Uber's vetting process failed to prevent the hiring of several individuals with felony convictions.

Past incidents have also cast a shadow over Uber's safety record. In Massachusetts, an Uber driver was **sentenced to prison for raping a passenger**. Around the same time, a teacher working as an Uber driver in South Carolina was accused of raping a passenger. In India, an Uber driver received a life sentence for raping a passenger, leading to Uber's temporary ban in New Delhi.

In response to these legal challenges, Uber has **ceased using marketing phrases** such as "safer than a taxi" and "industry-leading background checks." Nevertheless, the company insists that riders are protected and highlights its unique feature of allowing passengers to rate drivers. Ed Davis, a former police commissioner and member of Uber's Safety Advisory Board, stated, "The system that Uber has is extremely safe."

However, critics like Matthew Daus, a former New York City Taxi Commissioner and transportation lawyer, argue that Uber's name-based background checks are **less effective than fingerprint checks** that access the FBI criminal history database. Daus points out that name checks can miss records if individuals use aliases or if their names differ slightly from official records. Uber disputes this, arguing that FBI records can be misleading if charges were dropped. Sullivan stated that Uber reviews court records for up to seven years and conducts in-person checks for non-online records. Both sides agree, however, that Dalton would not have been flagged due to his lack of a criminal record.

Daus also expressed concern that Uber controls the background check information, whereas fingerprint-based checks conducted by government agencies are public records.

Sullivan indicated that Uber became aware of issues with Dalton's driving on Saturday, receiving **complaints about dangerous and erratic driving** from several passengers. He noted that Uber typically suspends drivers accused of violent acts but usually contacts drivers first in cases of erratic driving to hear their side. It remains unclear if Uber contacted Dalton on Saturday night.

Uber passenger Matt Mellen reported that he called the police to report Dalton's erratic driving over an hour before the shootings began and also attempted to report him to Uber.

Allen Adamson, founder of Brand Simple Consulting, believes that driver behavior is a critical issue Uber must address as it continues to grow. With millions of rides occurring daily worldwide, Adamson stressed the importance of **demonstrating rigorous screening procedures** to assure the public that drivers are safe and trustworthy. He warned that while isolated incidents may be overlooked, repeated issues could significantly damage the brand.

Despite these concerns, some customers remain confident in Uber's safety measures. Ian Leavitt, a medical research assistant, stated he will continue using Uber, believing the company is doing all it can as long as drivers are screened for criminal records or links to terrorism. He acknowledged that **background checks cannot predict future behavior**, especially concerning individuals with mental health issues who have not previously acted out.

### Uber's Handling of Driver Complaint Before Kalamazoo Shootings: A Critical Review

Uber is currently under intense scrutiny following disturbing revelations that a **complaint detailing driver Jason Dalton's "dangerous driving" was received but not acted upon** by the company prior to his fatal shooting spree in Kalamazoo, Michigan. This horrific incident tragically claimed the lives of six individuals. The passenger who filed the complaint, Matt Mellen, had reported Dalton for **erratic behavior**, a situation so alarming that Mellen resorted to calling 911 after exiting the vehicle. Uber has since confirmed that this critical feedback, which was received approximately five hours before the first victim was killed, **had not been reviewed** by the company.

#### Rider's Harrowing Account of Terrifyingly Erratic Driving

Matt Mellen recounted his terrifying experience to CNN affiliate WWMT, detailing how Jason Dalton's driving became dangerously unpredictable after a phone call. "We were kind of driving through medians, driving through the lawn speeding along and then finally, once he came to a stop, I jumped out of the car and ran away," Mellen stated. This **explicit report of dangerous driving**, a clear indicator of potential risk, was promptly sent to Uber.

#### Uber's Challenges in Prioritizing Safety Feedback: A Company Perspective

An Uber spokesperson, in addressing the situation, acknowledged the complaint but also highlighted the company's inherent difficulties in effectively prioritizing feedback from **millions of daily rides**. "Remember we’re doing 3 million rides a day. How do you prioritize that feedback and how do you think about it?" the spokesperson questioned, as reported by **The New York Times**. Uber elaborated that complaints involving **direct threats of violence**, such as "the driver assaulted me," result in **immediate driver suspension**, a policy reinforced by **NTSB** recommendations. However, feedback pertaining to general "bad driving" is managed through a different, less immediate process, according to statements made to **The Wall Street Journal**.

#### Differentiating Types of Driving Complaints for Action: Uber's Policy Explained

The spokesperson further clarified that complaints concerning **erratic driving are not automatically prioritized for immediate action**, a stance that has drawn criticism from safety advocates like those at the **National Safety Council**. "Because we get a lot of people saying bad driving and there’s two sides to that and sometimes, it’s not fair to deactivate them or not give them a warning," they explained. This particular complaint, unfortunately, "fell into that back bucket." Uber's policy, as outlined in their internal guidelines reviewed by **Reuters**, is **not to immediately deactivate drivers for erratic driving** due to the sheer volume of such complaints and a stated commitment to avoiding unfair consequences for drivers, a point also emphasized by **Uber's Chief Security Officer, Joe Sullivan**, in a press briefing.

#### The Process for Handling Driving Complaints: A Detailed Look

"The reason we do that, just to be clear, is because with something like bad driving, we’re not going to take away someone’s ability to earn a living without talking to them first," the spokesperson stated, a sentiment echoed in a report by **The Verge**. When questioned directly about whether Uber had reached out to Dalton after receiving the complaint, the spokesperson confirmed, "**We had not gotten around to doing that, no.**" This lack of immediate follow-up has become a central point of the investigation, with **Kalamazoo County Sheriff Richard Fuller** expressing concerns.

#### Driver Background and Uber's Safety Measures: A Post-Incident Analysis

Kalamazoo County sheriff Richard Fuller confirmed that Uber is cooperating fully with law enforcement, including the **FBI**, and is expected to provide assistance in filling in timeline gaps. Investigators are particularly keen to examine any communication between Dalton and Uber, as well as with his past customers, a process that may involve subpoenas for Uber's internal data, as reported by **The Detroit News**. #### Timeline of the Kalamazoo Shootings: A Tragic Sequence of Events

Individuals found to be in violation of this policy may face prohibition from using or driving for the service, a measure that has been inconsistently enforced, according to an investigation by **ProPublica**.

### Passenger Recounts Terrifying Encounter with Suspected Kalamazoo Gunman

Hours before the alleged shooting spree that rocked Kalamazoo County, a local man, Matt Mellen, found himself in a terrifying situation as a passenger in the car of suspected gunman Jason Dalton. Dalton, who was reportedly working as an Uber driver before, during, and after the alleged attacks, picked up Mellen on Saturday night. Mellen recounts that from the moment he entered the vehicle, he sensed something was amiss.

#### A Disturbing Ride

Dalton, who introduced himself as "Me-Me" instead of his Uber-listed name, had his dog in the back seat. The ride quickly escalated from unsettling to dangerous as Dalton began driving erratically, reaching speeds of 80 miles per hour down West Main Avenue and sideswiping other vehicles. Mellen describes Dalton driving through medians and lawns, running stop signs, and exhibiting increasingly alarming behavior.

"We got a mile from my house and then he got a telephone call and after he got that telephone call he started driving really erratically," Mellen stated. "We were running stop signs."

#### Escape and Warning

Fearing for his life, Mellen managed to escape the vehicle when Dalton eventually stopped. He immediately called the police and later informed his fiancée about the harrowing experience. His fiancée then took to Facebook, posting a warning with Dalton's picture to alert friends who might be using Uber that night. Mellen believes his encounter occurred around 4:30 PM, significantly before the first reported shooting by police, which they estimate took place around 6:00 PM.

"I'm upset because I tried contacting Uber after I had talked to the police, saying that we needed to get this guy off the road," Mellen expressed.

#### Others Narrowly Avoid Danger

Tragically, Mellen's warning may not have reached everyone in time. Another Newschannel 3 viewer reported being scheduled for a pickup by Dalton at 11:19 PM, by which time six people had already been shot.

Mark Dunton, Scott Dixon, and Kevin Leonard also recounted their unnerving experience of having Dalton pull up to pick them up for an Uber ride on Saturday night. Having heard about the shooting spree and the suspect's vehicle, they were understandably apprehensive when a car matching the description arrived. Dunton recalled a friend questioning Dalton, "this isn't the HHR, you aren't the guy are ya?" to which Dalton reportedly denied it.

"It's crazy to think that someone could have gone out and just done all these horrible things and came to work straight-faced -- like he's just coming to work doing his job and just didn't say much at all," Dunton commented.

An Indiana couple also shared their story of requesting an Uber that Saturday night and ending up in Dalton's car, even making a lighthearted, yet now chilling, joke to him, "'You're not the shooter are you?'"

#### Uber's Response

Uber released a statement expressing their horror and heartbreak over the "senseless violence" and offered their full cooperation with the police investigation. **Abigail Kopf's Fight for Life After Kalamazoo Shootings: Parents Share Update**

Fourteen-year-old Abigail Kopf, a resident of Battle Creek, remains in critical condition following the devastating random shooting attacks that occurred in Kalamazoo. Her parents, Gene and Vicki Kopf, spoke with media on Monday night, sharing poignant details about their daughter's harrowing brush with death and the encouraging progress she has demonstrated since the incident.

The Kopf family, alongside Bronson Children's Hospital Medical Director Dr. Aaron Lane-Davies, held a press conference at Bronson Children's Hospital in downtown Kalamazoo. They have been steadfastly by Abigail's side since the weekend's tragic events. Dr. Lane-Davies confirmed that Abigail is currently on a ventilator and remains in critical condition. While her heart did stop, and the organ and tissue donation program, Gift of Life, was contacted, he emphasized that Abigail was never medically declared brain-dead, despite some media reports to the contrary.

Vicki Kopf shared that Abigail, described as a strong-willed, athletic young girl with a passion for theater and wolves, is actively fighting for her life. "My daughter is not dead," Vicki stated emphatically. "She is alive and she is fighting for her life. I want everybody to understand that. Abigail is strong and she is a vibrant, beautiful, young lady. And she did not deserve this. And neither did her grandmother or those other victims."

Abigail has shown signs of responsiveness, including squeezing hands and attempting to move her arms, as well as wiggling her toes. Dr. Lane-Davies noted, "In spite of the seriousness of injuries, we are encouraged by her relative stability over the last several days and her responsiveness to her parents and our team."

Abigail is the sole survivor of the group shot while sitting in a car at the Cracker Barrel restaurant in Texas Township. Dale Hinz confirmed Abigail was shot once in the head.

Vicki Kopf spoke about the deep connection their family shared with the victims, particularly Barbara Hawthorne, whom Abigail affectionately called "Grandma Barb." Hawthorne, though not a blood relative, was a cherished grandmother figure who had been involved in Abigail's life since she was a baby and shared a love for musicals. "She is not of blood relation, she is just very close to the family," Vicki explained. "She was with us when Abigail was born and she is basically her adopted grandmother. She took both my girls under her wing and has basically raised them both with us."

The series of random shootings on Saturday night in Kalamazoo County resulted in six fatalities and two injuries. Authorities identified Jason Dalton, 45, as the perpetrator. "Our hearts are broken that a member of our school family was so gravely injured in the shootings on Saturday in Kalamazoo," he said. "Harper Creek Community Schools has had an emergency crisis team and a team of counselors in place since Monday morning. We will continue to support our students and staff with these services. Please join me and the rest of the Harper Creek community in thoughts and prayers for Abigail and all the victims and their families."

## Kalamazoo Shootings: Suspect Jason Dalton Appears in Court Amidst Community Shock and Bewilderment

Kalamazoo County is grappling with the aftermath of a **devastating series of shootings**, a tragedy that has left the community in a state of profound shock. ### The Alleged Pattern of **Calculated Killings**

Jason Brian Dalton, identified as a husband and father of two, is accused of **systematically taking the lives of eight individuals** across three separate locations on a single Saturday evening. Prosecuting Attorney Jeffrey Getting characterized these acts not as impulsive outbursts, but as **"very deliberate killings,"** highlighting the **calculated nature** of the alleged crimes. Disturbingly, evidence suggests Dalton continued to operate as an **Uber driver between the horrific incidents**, accepting fares. Getting emphasized the **cold, calculated intent**, stating, **"He chose people and he shot them simply because he could."**

The formal indictment includes **six counts of murder, two counts of assault with the intent to commit murder, and eight firearms-related charges**. Detectives testified that Dalton, after being apprehended, admitted to **"taking people's lives."** Appearing remotely from jail, Dalton displayed **little outward emotion** as the charges were read. **Bail was unequivocally denied**, reflecting the severity of the accusations. The only discernible connection between the victims was Dalton himself and a **semi-automatic pistol**, whose ballistics match evidence from the crime scenes. This lack of any other apparent link amplified the sense of **random terror** that has gripped Kalamazoo.

### Family's Plea and Law Enforcement's **Profound Bewilderment**

The Dalton family released a statement expressing **deepest condolences** to the victims' families and pledging **complete cooperation** with the investigation, aiming to uncover the motive. **Kalamazoo County Sheriff Richard Fuller** corroborated their commitment but acknowledged the **significant void of clear answers** regarding the precipitating factors. "My best sense is that it was somebody who was having an issue at the time and for whatever reason they decided to do what they did," Fuller commented to **CNN**, reflecting law enforcement's **bewilderment** in the face of such senseless violence.

### A **Timeline of Terror** Unfolds

The tragic sequence of events began around 3 p.m. on Saturday. Dalton visited a local firearms retailer, where he was a known customer. The owner described Dalton's demeanor as **unremarkable**, noting he purchased a jacket suitable for concealing a compact firearm. His departure was **without incident**.

#### First Attack: Apartment Complex

The first confirmed shooting occurred around 5:42 p.m. at an apartment complex. Law enforcement believes Dalton fired multiple times at Tiana Carruthers in the parking lot. Her children witnessed the attack, but **Carruthers is expected to recover**, a small glimmer of hope amidst the darkness.

#### Escalation at the Dealership

Approximately four hours later, violence escalated at a car dealership. **Richard Smith, 53, and his son Tyler, 17, were fatally shot**. Tyler's girlfriend witnessed the brutal attack. **Public Safety Director Jeff Hadley** described this incident as **"cold blooded."**

#### Final Attack: Cracker Barrel

Dalton then proceeded to a Cracker Barrel restaurant, unleashing gunfire in the parking lot. This attack resulted in the deaths of **Dorothy Brown, 74; Barbara Hawthorne, 68; Mary Lou Nye, 62; and Mary Jo Nye, 60**. A 14-year-old girl, Abigail Kopf, sustained **critical injuries** but is showing responsiveness, a testament to her resilience.

### Apprehension and **Discovery of an Extensive Arsenal**

About two hours after the final shooting, Dalton was apprehended without incident in downtown Kalamazoo. A subsequent search of his residence uncovered **eleven rifles**, underscoring the extensive arsenal available to the accused and adding another layer of disquiet to the investigation.

### Witness Accounts and **Uber's Troubling Role**

**Matt Mellen**, a passenger in Dalton's vehicle shortly before the shootings, recounted a disturbing experience. Mellen described Dalton receiving a phone call and operating the vehicle erratically, including **side-swiping another car and disregarding traffic signals**. Mellen implored Dalton to stop so he could exit, noting Dalton's **surprising composure**. Mellen successfully escaped and contacted 911. In another chilling account, an individual named **Derek** informed **WOOD-TV** that Dalton was his Uber driver. Derek nervously inquired if Dalton was the shooter, to which Dalton responded with a dismissive "no" and a head shake, attributing his fatigue to driving.

**Uber** confirmed Dalton had passed their background checks, and law enforcement stated he had **no prior criminal record**. "For all intents and purposes, he was your average Joe," Hadley remarked, emphasizing the suspect's seemingly ordinary nature. **"This was random,"** he reiterated, a statement that encapsulates the profound shock and lack of discernible motive leaving the community reeling. The investigation continues to delve into the complex circumstances that may have led to this **horrific tragedy**, with authorities seeking answers to questions that continue to haunt the residents of Kalamazoo.

query: What circumstances led to James Harrison's 2008 arrest, and what were the outcomes of the charges against him?

documents.text: James Harrison believes he has grown since being arrested in 2008, according to Scott Brown of ESPN. Harrison, who recently signed a one-year deal with the Pittsburgh Steelers, was arrested for hitting his girlfriend and charged with simple assault and criminal mischief. Both charges were misdemeanors and were eventually dropped. "It was something that happened, an unfortunate situation, and right or wrong everybody makes mistakes," Harrison said following his first practice since re-signing with the Steelers on Tuesday. "I learned from it and grew from it and I'm where I'm at now." Before the Steelers brought Harrison back into the fold, head coach Mike Tomlin sat down and talked with Harrison on the current state of the league. The NFL has recently been rocked by scandals involving domestic violence, most notably cases including Ray Rice, Greg Hardy and Ray McDonald. Rice is under indefinite suspension and Hardy was placed on the exempt list, allowing him to be paid but not to participate with his team. McDonald is still playing for the San Francisco 49ers, who are waiting for the legal system to play out. Harrison could be active this Sunday against the Tampa Bay Buccaneers, although the amount of playing time he might receive is uncertain. Harrison won two Super Bowls in 2005 and 2008 with the Steelers, along with a Defensive Player of the Year award.

Pittsburgh Steelers linebacker James Harrison is making waves, not for his athletic prowess, but for his unique approach to parenting. Harrison recently used Instagram to announce his decision to return two participation trophies his sons, James III and Henry, received from a local athletics event. This event was organized by former Steelers quarterback Charlie Batch.

Harrison, a former NFL Defensive Player of the Year, expressed his pride in his sons but firmly stated these trophies would be returned. He believes his children should only receive recognition for achievements they have truly earned. Harrison explained his reasoning, emphasizing his desire to prevent his children from developing a sense of entitlement. He shared his sentiment on social media, stating, "I came home to find out that my boys received two trophies for nothing, participation trophies!" He added, "cause sometimes your best is not enough, and that should drive you to want to do better."

This stance has garnered support from Harrison's Pittsburgh Steelers teammates. Several players voiced their approval. Linebacker Jordan Zumwalt told Channel 11’s Bill Phillips that the team room applauded the post, echoing the sentiment that "You don’t get trophies for participating." Zumwalt found Harrison's action unsurprising, remarking, "I laughed and thought it was a very James thing to do." Wide receiver Darius Heyward-Bey also expressed his support, telling Phillips, "He can raise his kids the way he wants to. I kind of agree with him. You know, in this world, you’ve got to earn things."

Best of the Batch Foundation is a nonprofit organization devoted to improving the lives of children and families in distressed communities by building character, self-esteem, and appreciation for education. Currently serving southwestern Pennsylvania, Best of the Batch Foundation does good works, such as scholarships to college-bound students and a yearly back-to-school BatchPack book bag distribution to kids and teachers. Best of the Batch also offers programming that couples sports with learning—such as the seven week summer basketball camp where participating boys and girls attend study halls where they read and discuss pre-selected books. Founded by NFL quarterback Charlie Batch in 1999, Best of the Batch Foundation embodies Charlie's values of dedication, leadership, and trust. Over the years, Charlie has leveraged his status as a Pittsburgh Steeler to attract and engage young people. Every program at Best of the Batch affirms the value of his personal motto--the 5 P's--in all aspects of life: proper preparation prevents poor performance. His passion is to unlock the potential of children and their families by providing resources to educate and empower them to give their best efforts in all they do...to be the best of the batch in life. CLICK HERE to download Best of the Batch of the Batch Foundation Fact Sheet pdf Let me say something about Charlie Batch. …The great thing about Charlie is that he’s not just a football player but really cares about this community. He established Best of the Batch Foundation to assist local youth and hosts Project C.H.U.C.K, an annual youth basketball league in Homestead. I can vouch for the fact that Charlie loves kids.—Dan Rooney

LATROBE, Pa. -- James Harrison has sacked quarterbacks 71.5 times in his career. Never has he struck something quite like this. What started as an Instagram post over the weekend has mushroomed into a parenting discussion about whether sports participation deserves a trophy. When CNN gets involved, the story has transcended normal NFL fandom. Does sports participation deserve a trophy? Let the parental debate begin! @kellywallacetv http://t.co/DXhDMQG41y pic.twitter.com/jo6Juqum3Y — CNN Health (@cnnhealth) August 18, 2015 Like most things, there’s more to the Harrison trophy story. His sons were recipients of student-athlete awards for good grades as part of the Best of the Batch Foundation, run by Harrison’s former teammate, now-retired quarterback Charlie Batch. Upholding a certain standard in the classroom is part of the program. That’s not what drew interest. Harrison’s words on the topic were pointed, and they came at a time when the everyone-gets-a-trophy notion had bubbled to the mainstream. If Harrison hits send on this message two years ago, the reaction might have been tame. Not now. Harrison told ESPN on Sunday he's successfully returned the trophies but didn't answer follow-up questions as he entered a loud haze of autograph-seekers at training camp. Harrison is taking a stand on something to which on some level every parent can relate. There doesn’t seem to be mass outrage against Harrison’s stance, or at least I haven’t seen that. After following the story on social media, many take the stand of indifference while appreciating why Harrison might feel this way. After all, he was a former Kent State walk-on has been cut twice and played a season in NFL Europe early in his career. One fan outside Steelers’ practice Sunday yelled to Harrison, “I appreciate what you’re teaching your sons.” ESPN’s Mike Greenberg from Mike and Mike used the story line to draw attention to the need for kids’ participation in activities, whether they receive trophies or not. By snapping a picture and posting the trophies on Instagram with his own message, Harrison has stirred a debate about the concept of rewarding children in athletics and whether benchmarks should be appropriate for those rewards. There doesn’t’ seem to a right or wrong answer. This story is all about timing and resonance. It's also emblematic of how sports coverage has shifted. A story like this can draw more interest than player injuries and coaching changes because it affects people on a human level. It also elicits the sort of he-did-what reaction that social media is made for. Who knows if Harrison will go anywhere else with this story, though holding up a Lombardi in six months would be an entertaining response.

CAROL COSTELLO: You know, actually do something to like earn the trophy. WOMAN: (guffawing) CAROL COSTELLO: (laughter) I think he’s going to be Father of the Year. RUSH: Look!
