Super Tuesday in the 2016 presidential election is a critical moment, with states offering substantial delegate counts driving intense campaigning. **Texas is a particularly vital battleground**, presenting a massive prize of **222 delegates for Democrats** and **155 for Republicans**. This makes it a focal point for both parties.

In the Republican race, Texas Senator Ted Cruz is aiming to leverage his home-state advantage against national front-runner Donald Trump. Cruz held rallies in major Texas cities, seeking to win in his political backyard. He expressed confidence, stating, "**We are going to have a very good Super Tuesday.**" A loss in Texas could **significantly impact his presidential hopes**. Georgia follows Texas as the second-most valuable state for Republicans, with **76 delegates**. Both Trump and Marco Rubio campaigned in Georgia, recognizing its strategic importance, while Cruz focused on Texas.

Donald Trump has amassed **82 delegates**, building on recent wins. Ted Cruz has **17 delegates**, and Marco Rubio has **16**. Trump holds leads in other delegate-rich Super Tuesday states like Alabama, Georgia, Massachusetts, Oklahoma, Tennessee, and Virginia, which collectively offer about **53% of the 595 GOP delegates**. These poll averages were largely set before Trump's initial reluctance to disavow David Duke's endorsement, an issue highlighted by Rubio, who called Trump "**unelectable**." Trump has since disavowed Duke.

The demanding nature of this election cycle is evident. Ohio Governor John Kasich and Ben Carson have secured **six and four delegates, respectively**.

On the Democratic side, Hillary Clinton and Bernie Sanders are targeting key states. Clinton, fresh off a win in South Carolina, declared the campaign was entering a "**national**" phase. She focused on delegate-rich Massachusetts (**91 delegates**) and Virginia (**95 delegates**), where strong performances could grant her an **almost insurmountable delegate lead**. Clinton currently leads Sanders in delegates, **543 to 85, including superdelegates**.

Super Tuesday features contests in **11 states**, with **865 delegates at stake**, representing about **one-third of the delegates needed for nomination**. Bernie Sanders is concentrating his efforts on Minnesota and Colorado, states he believes are more receptive to his platform. At a rally in Minneapolis, Sanders stated, "**Americans don’t need crumbs, they need the whole loaf.**" He is expected to win his home state of Vermont (**16 delegates**). Minnesota (**77 delegates**) is a more significant prize. Many delegate contests are proportional, meaning Sanders will likely accrue delegates even in states he doesn't win. His strategy appears to be consolidating support on Super Tuesday and seeking a resurgence later.

Wellington, New Zealand – In a unique early start to the U.S. presidential election, Democratic Party supporters in Wellington were the first globally to cast their ballots for the crucial multi-state primaries known as "Super Tuesday." These overseas voters, registered Democrats living in Wellington, cast their votes just after midnight on Tuesday. This timing placed them nearly a full day ahead of their counterparts voting in the United States.

The voting process unfolded in a notably informal setting: the Public Bar and Eatery. A total of 28 votes were cast during this event. Senator Bernie Sanders emerged as the clear winner in New Zealand, securing 21 votes. Former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton received six votes. One ballot was unfortunately spoiled. The official confirmation of these early results is scheduled to take place later this month. This will occur at the global voter tally center, which is located in Germany.

Wellington proudly holds the distinction of being the inaugural city among 111 locations worldwide. These locations, spread across 41 countries outside the U.S., allow Democrats abroad to participate in the primary process. It is important to note that Republican voters overseas do not currently have a comparable mechanism for casting primary ballots.

As of this report, four states have already completed their primaries or caucuses. These early contests are vital for determining each party's nominee for the November presidential election. On the day of Super Tuesday itself, Democrats will be voting across 11 states and American Samoa. A significant number of delegates are at stake: 865 delegates are available. To secure the Democratic Party's nomination, a candidate needs 2,383 delegates. Democrats abroad are allocated 17 of these delegates. For Republicans, Super Tuesday involves voting in 11 states. A total of 595 delegates are available toward the 1,237 needed to clinch their party's nomination.

Kat Allikian, the chair of Democrats Abroad New Zealand, provided insight into the expatriate American community in New Zealand. She explained that this community comprises students, individuals working in specialized professions, and retirees. Allikian highlighted a significant motivator for these local voters: a strong desire to prevent a Republican victory in the White House. She expressed particular concern regarding the leading Republican candidate, Donald Trump. "The overwhelming concern of all Democrats overseas is this Trump phenomenon that is happening on the Republican side," Allikian stated. "It's mindboggling that he's running away with the nomination. Although it's not set yet."

Allikian also expressed her pride in leading the initial overseas vote. "People are really tickled that we're the very first in the world," she added, reflecting the excitement of being the first to participate.

As the calendar flips, March brings a whirlwind of states voting on the same days and in quick succession. By the middle of the month, 58 percent of the total delegates will have been awarded, and Mr. Trump could be unstoppable in getting the 1,237 needed to clinch the nomination. With the exception of Texas, the home state of Senator Ted Cruz, recent polls show Mr. Trump leading in the so-called Super Tuesday states that vote this week, including Alabama, Georgia, Massachusetts and Virginia. Though Texas has the most delegates of states voting on Tuesday, 155, they all award delegates proportionally, so that Mr. Cruz will most likely have to share the haul. The Southern states, with many evangelical Christians, have been the linchpin of Mr. Cruz’s strategy. But after Mr. Trump trounced him in South Carolina on Feb. 20, winning all 50 delegates, Mr. Cruz’s prospects are not as bright. Mr. Trump carries “enormous momentum,” Mr. Cruz admitted on Friday, and if he sweeps Super Tuesday, when more delegates are awarded than on any other day, “he could easily be unstoppable.” For Senator Marco Rubio of Florida, who ferociously challenged Mr. Trump last week in a debate and on the stump, mocking the real estate mogul’s “spray tan” and calling him a “con man,” the path forward is narrowing.

Donald Trump is poised for significant victories on Super Tuesday, strengthening his lead as the Republican front-runner and increasing pressure on his rivals. Top Republicans, including governors who held an emergency conference call to discuss Trump, anticipate he will win up to 10 states. This outcome would severely impact Texas Sen. Ted Cruz, who has focused on Southern states, and Florida Sen. Marco Rubio, who has yet to win a primary.

"If Trump wins 8 or more states on Tuesday, it would take a massive collapse for him not to be the nominee," stated Tony Fabrizio, a GOP pollster. Trump's path to the nomination has caused anxiety within the Republican establishment, with officials debating whether to endorse or oppose him.

Reflecting this concern, New Mexico Gov. Susana Martinez and Wisconsin Gov. Scott Walker, leaders of the Republican Governors Association, convened a call with fellow governors to discuss the primary's trajectory, with Trump as a central focus. During the call, Kentucky Gov. Matt Bevin asked New Jersey Gov. Chris Christie to explain his decision to endorse Trump. Christie defended his endorsement, citing his decade-long acquaintance with Trump and his confidence in Trump's leadership abilities.

The stakes are particularly high for Cruz, who had hoped to dominate Super Tuesday by appealing to conservative voters in states like Tennessee and Oklahoma. He had called March 1 the most crucial day of the primary calendar. However, with Trump's strong performance in the South, Cruz may only win his home state of Texas, a contest he has likened to the Battle of the Alamo. Trump has successfully appealed to Cruz's evangelical base, presenting himself as a devout Christian while labeling Cruz a "liar." Jerry Falwell Jr., an influential evangelical leader and Trump supporter, has recorded a robocall for the Trump campaign, criticizing Cruz for using "dirty tricks" and urging voters to choose Trump based on character over ambition.

The Cruz campaign has not yet revealed its post-Super Tuesday plans, but Cruz is expected to focus on deeply conservative states like Idaho, Louisiana, and Kansas in the following week. Rubio will concentrate on his home state of Florida, where a crucial winner-take-all primary on March 15 could determine his fate. Despite trailing Trump in polls, Rubio's team is committed to winning Florida, with top aides vowing not to lose the state. Rubio has been preparing for the Florida primary, deploying former RNC political director Clint Reed to assist his campaign there. Rubio's aides are also organizing donor meetings and a fundraising tour in South Florida.

Regardless of Tuesday's results, Cruz and Rubio's allies emphasize that Trump will still be far from securing the 1,237 delegates needed for the nomination. The 595 delegates available on Super Tuesday will be awarded proportionally, allowing Cruz and Rubio to accumulate delegates even without winning states. Trump currently holds 82 of the 133 delegates awarded so far. This suggests the primary race could extend for months.

"Donald Trump is the clear front-runner, and he’s in a very strong position," said Steve Munisteri, former Texas Republican chairman. "But it’s not over." The primary's structure favors Trump, who stands to gain from a delegate lead and a divided opposition if he wins most Super Tuesday states.

Rubio's allies are exploring a scenario where he could prevent Trump from securing a majority of delegates and then win the nomination at the convention in Cleveland. Mel Martinez, former RNC chairman and senator, acknowledged this "narrow way forward," believing that a majority of the party finds Trump "unpalatable" and would resist his nomination, potentially leading to a convention showdown. Cruz, however, dismissed this idea as an attempt by the establishment to anoint a preferred candidate.

Anti-Trump groups, like Our Principles PAC, are actively fundraising and planning ad campaigns in key March states. The PAC, led by former Mitt Romney aide Katie Packer, has secured donations from prominent Republicans, including Marlene Ricketts and Stan Hubbard. "The opposition to Trump is not going quietly into the night anytime soon," said Josh Holmes, former chief of staff to Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell. "The vast majority of Republicans are horribly dissatisfied with the current state of affairs and there is no sign whatsoever that fundraising or energy is dissipating for an alternative."

Some in Rubio's campaign are frustrated by the lack of endorsements from prominent party figures. While Mitt Romney and Jeb Bush have criticized Trump, neither has endorsed Rubio, which could unlock significant fundraising and consolidate party support. Many of Bush's former backers have shifted to Rubio, but some remain undecided. One Bush donor, Mel Sembler, stated he was focused on other issues.

However, some Bush supporters are backing Trump. Doug Ose, a former Republican congressman who fundraised for Bush, announced his endorsement of Trump, viewing him as a problem-solver and expressing dissatisfaction with Rubio's campaign tone. Ose believes Rubio could be a future leader but hopes he doesn't harm his prospects.

Super Tuesday is a key day in the U.S. presidential nominating contests, with many Americans voting. This important event gives voters a chance to influence the direction of both the Democratic and Republican parties. Leading candidates Hillary Clinton (Democrat) and Donald Trump (Republican) are seeking wins that could secure their paths to nomination. Meanwhile, Clinton's challenger Bernie Sanders, and Trump's rivals Marco Rubio and Ted Cruz, aim to keep the competition going. This guide looks at what to watch as voters in a dozen states cast their ballots.

### Super Tuesday Results and Voting

Super Tuesday results will come in throughout the evening. Early projections are expected as polls close. Georgia, Virginia, and Vermont will release initial results at 7 p.m. EST. Alabama, Tennessee, Oklahoma, and Massachusetts follow at 8 p.m. Arkansas reports at 8:30 p.m., with Texas, Minnesota, and Colorado at 9 p.m. Alaska's results are expected around midnight. States with significant early voting, like Arkansas, Georgia, Tennessee, and Texas, may have quicker results. Vermont, Bernie Sanders' home state, has a slower reporting process. Minnesota, Colorado, and Alaska, which use caucuses, might face more logistical challenges than states with primaries. Caucuses are local gatherings of party members, while primaries are state-run elections with secret ballots.

### Democratic Candidates and Strategies

For Democrats, Hillary Clinton's performance with minority voters, especially in the South, is a primary focus. If she repeats her strong support from Black voters in South Carolina (around 90%), her path to nomination will be greatly improved. Bernie Sanders needs to appeal to younger voters beyond the youngest group. In South Carolina, he only won voters under 30, which may not be enough for a decisive convention victory.

### Republican Candidates and Stakes

For Ted Cruz, Texas is crucial. After three third-place finishes, a poor Super Tuesday showing in his home state could seriously hurt his campaign. A large portion of Texas votes were cast early, with over 1.7 million voters participating. Even a Texas win doesn't guarantee Cruz all 155 Republican delegates, as they are awarded proportionally. With Donald Trump expected to gain many delegates, his rivals will look for positive signs to continue their campaigns. Can Marco Rubio show increased support without winning? Will Ted Cruz get more backing from evangelical voters, a group already supporting Trump? Can John Kasich and Ben Carson find encouraging results?

### The Southern Firewall and Beyond

With seven Southern states voting, a key question is if Hillary Clinton can build the "Southern firewall." If Bernie Sanders doesn't perform well outside his Northeastern base, it would signal major challenges for his campaign.

### General Election Signals

A significant indicator will be the election-night speeches from Hillary Clinton and Donald Trump. Observers will watch if they start focusing on a potential general election matchup. Exit poll data will offer insights into candidates' weaknesses. Will Clinton still struggle with voters prioritizing honesty? Will Trump continue to underperform with Republican voters seeking strong general election candidates, as seen against Rubio?

### Delegate Count: The Decider

Ultimately, Super Tuesday outcomes depend on the delegate count. About a quarter of all delegates are awarded today, with most states using a proportional system. Before Super Tuesday, Donald Trump had 82 delegates, Ted Cruz 18, Marco Rubio 16, John Kasich 6, and Ben Carson 4. A total of 1,237 delegates are needed for the Republican nomination. On the Democratic side, Hillary Clinton started with 546 delegates (including superdelegates), and Bernie Sanders had 87. A total of 2,383 delegates are needed to win the Democratic nomination.

This report was compiled with contributions from AP writers Stephen Ohlemacher in Washington and Will Weissert in Houston.

query: What did Ted Cruz mean by accusing Donald Trump of embodying 'New York values' during the debate?

documents.text:
### How 9/11 Redefined "New York Values" and Influenced American Conservatism

The **September 11th terrorist attacks** profoundly reshaped the national perception of "New York values." While New York City has historically leaned more liberal than the rest of the United States, the shared experience of 9/11 created a powerful, unifying moment that shifted the country's perspective. The nation witnessed the **inherent humanity and resilience of New York City** in a way that left an indelible mark.

In the immediate aftermath of 9/11, typical political divisions seemed to fade. For weeks, Americans were united in mourning, watching the news, and celebrating the **bravery of first responders and the strength of survivors**. This **shared national trauma** made it more difficult for voters, even in conservative strongholds, to view New Yorkers as fundamentally different.

Donald Trump strategically leveraged this shift. By referencing New York's spirit after 9/11, he subtly reminded conservatives that **all parts of America are valuable**. As David French of National Review observed, Trump's success against Ted Cruz highlighted a crucial point: **conservatives cannot afford to dismiss any segment of the country**. French noted that Trump, by "hearkening back to New York’s heart after 9/11, for a moment transcended politics. How easily we forget, but for weeks after the terror attacks, New York was America."

The effectiveness of Trump's "New York values" attacks may have been amplified by his skillful invocation of 9/11. Many qualities his supporters admire in him are **quintessentially New York City**: his larger-than-life personality, his **boldness**, his comfort with **self-promotion**, and his **direct, often confrontational style**. He is unafraid to be assertive, embraces conflict, and prioritizes **ruthlessness and victory**. These traits often resonate more strongly with his base than any perceived deviation from conservative ideology.

This phenomenon bears resemblance to the admiration many on the right held for Andrew Breitbart, despite his own complexities. While the Tea Party movement fostered a libertarian wing, a new, ascendant faction on the right appears less focused on traditional conservatism or libertarianism and more on what could be termed **"Breitbartian" principles**. This group often engages more in **cultural warfare** than traditional political battles, characterized by opposition to the left, Islamism, the establishment, and political correctness.

The critical question for the Republican nomination may hinge on whether this **"Breitbartian" faction is larger than the traditional evangelical and social conservative base**. Without the powerful reminder of 9/11, these latter groups might have been more critical of Trump's "New York values." The ultimate size and influence of these competing factions could **determine the outcome of Republican primaries**.

This is what Ted Cruz, who wasn’t just born out of town but out of the country, said about Donald Trump the other day: “Donald comes from New York and he embodies New York values.” And made it sound as if this was some variation of insulting Trump about his hair. DE BLASIO: CRUZ OWES AN APOLOGY FOR 'NEW YORK VALUES' INSULT Later Cruz kept digging, telling Megyn Kelly of “Fox News” that New York values weren’t Iowa values or New Hampshire values, as if he is suddenly as big an expert on values as he says he is on the Constitution. Finally on Thursday night he brought his cockeyed theories about the city to the Republican debate in South Carolina. The next time Cruz shows up here and treats the city like an ATM machine, he should make a side trip to the Bronx and explain himself there on New York values. (MARK KAUZLARICH/REUTERS) “Everybody understands that the values in New York City are socially liberal and pro-abortion and pro-gay marriage,” he said. “And focus on money and the media.” This was right before Trump turned and calmly took him apart again for insulting the city the way he has. TRUMP DEFENDS 'NEW YORK VALUES' AS CRUZ USES TERM AS INSULT Ted Cruz, who only comes here with his hand out, has decided that the most diverse city the world has ever known is filled with people who all think alike. He sounds in these moments like as slow a thinker as we have ever had run for President. If you are dumb enough to think that New York values are some sort of handicap in this presidential season, then you are as dumb and tone deaf as Jesse Jackson was calling the city “Hymietown,” as dumb as Gerald Ford was when he gave this paper the most famous front page in its history, the day he effectively told New York to drop dead. CUOMO CALLS CRUZ'S ATTACKS ON NEW YORK VALUES "OFFENSIVE" The next time Cruz shows up here he should tear himself away from his friends at Goldman Sachs and make a side trip to the Bronx and explain himself there on New York values; or to the Lower East Side, or Williamsburg, Brooklyn, or Jamaica, Queens, or 125th St. in Harlem. He should go talk to people in the Rockaways or Staten Island, the hardened survivors who bound together after Hurricane Sandy, about New York values. Or finally he should go all the way downtown, and back to September 2001. New York City became the capital of heroes on Sept. 11, 2001. (Shawn Baldwin/AP) Here is what New York values are: New York values are a young guy, a paralegal, literally giving somebody he doesn’t know the shirt off his back on a subway because winter has finally come to the city and brought freezing temperatures with it. New York values are the New York taxi driver who traveled three boroughs across four days to find the guy who had left $1,400 in his cab, so he could return the money to him. You know what that really was? It was the real life of a city that Ted Cruz knows nothing about. He is simply another tourist here, one constantly on the make. HATERS IN CHIEF:l FIND YOUR CANDIDATE BY WHAT THEY HATE The best of this city is the best of this country, and always has been. When it was hit in a way that no American city had ever been hit, it came together and rose up together in a moment as thrilling as any America had ever seen. "Well, look, he may shift in his new rallies to play 'New York, New York' because Donald comes from New York and he embodies New York values,’” Ted Cruz said of the bombastic billionaire GOP front-runner, who was born in Queens. (CHRIS KEANE/REUTERS) This wasn’t the civil rights movement, it was a different kind of movement, to lower Manhattan. That was where the city was stronger and better than it ever had been on Sept. 12, when in the words of the great Pete Hamill, the city first got to one knee, and then began to get up. We will never know how it would have gone in another American city. We just know how it went here. New York values? New York values are the ironworkers who carried their tools in backpacks and gym bags and, by God, walked over the Brooklyn Bridge on the night of Sept. 11, 2001, or all the way downtown from all the way uptown. HARRY SIEGEL: HE-MAN, REV. BACON AND NYC'S SILLY SEASON One of them I know simply said to a cop who didn’t want to let him get close to where the towers had been. And the guy said, “They need me. I cut steel.” Cruz didn't make things better for himself at the debate. Somehow he sounded even dumber about New York City as he talked about abortion and gay marriage and the rest of it, even as he applauded after Trump spoke to the greatness of the city. Cruz means what he says about New York values, clearly believes they are in opposition to the values on which he has based his campaign, whatever those actually are. Earlier on Thursday Rep. Pete King came right at Cruz like some guy he’d grown up with in Sunnyside, Queens, who needed a good slap. “Memo to Ted Cruz,” King said in a statement on Thursday. “New York values are the heroes of 9/11; the cops who fight terror; and the people you ask for campaign donations. Go back under a rock.” Cruz is a lightweight. He always has been, however well he is doing in caucuses in the heartland. He may do well there. He is just out of his class here. It is the other party that has a donkey as its mascot. But Cruz is the one who’s a career jackass. City to him: Get lost.

The Republican presidential hopefuls sharpened their attacks during a Thursday debate in North Charleston, South Carolina, marking the most intense exchanges of the campaign season. The perceived "bromance" between Donald Trump and Ted Cruz appeared to dissolve, with Trump telling CNN's Dana Bash, "I guess the bromance is over." This 2.5-hour event, sponsored by Fox Business Network, featured sharp disagreements among the seven candidates on stage.

Cruz and Trump are currently locked in a tight race for first place in Iowa, with less than three weeks until the state's caucuses. Trump, however, maintains a significant national lead. Amidst growing pressure for an establishment alternative to Trump and Cruz, sparks also flew between Marco Rubio and Chris Christie.

The highly anticipated showdown between Trump and Cruz took a few minutes to ignite, but when it did, it was impactful. Cruz forcefully countered Trump's accusations regarding his eligibility to be president, stemming from his birth in Canada – a controversy Trump has recently amplified. "Back in September, my friend Donald said he had his lawyers look at this in every which way," Cruz stated. "There was nothing to this birther issue." He added, "Since September, the Constitution hasn't changed. But the poll numbers have."

While Trump has shown animosity towards most rivals, his relationship with Cruz had been largely amicable for much of the campaign. This dynamic shifted as Iowa's polls tightened before the February 1 caucuses. Trump, aiming to erode Cruz's support in Iowa, has repeatedly questioned Cruz's status as a natural-born citizen, given his mother's U.S. citizenship. However, Cruz turned the tables on Trump, pointing out that some extreme interpretations of the citizenship requirement would deem Trump ineligible, as his mother was born in Scotland. "On the issue of citizenship, Donald, I'm not going to use your mother's birth against you," Cruz declared. Trump retorted, "Because it wouldn't work."

Cruz wasn't the only candidate put on the defensive. In the debate's closing moments, Marco Rubio sharply criticized Ted Cruz, accusing him of hiding behind conservative values. Rubio highlighted a series of issues where he claimed Cruz had "flip-flopped," including immigration and ethanol subsidies, stating, "That is not consistent conservatism. That is political calculation." When Cruz suggested Rubio was merely presenting "oppo research," Rubio retorted, "It's your record."

Trump appeared more dominant than in previous debates. In a memorable exchange, he responded to Cruz's recent accusation that Trump "embodies New York values." When asked to define this, Cruz described New Yorkers as tending towards "socially liberal" views and being focused on "money and media," suggesting "not a lot of conservatives come out of Manhattan." Trump delivered an emotional rebuttal, recalling the resilience of New Yorkers after the September 11, 2001, attacks. "I saw something that no place on Earth could have handled more beautifully, more humanely than New York," Trump stated, emphasizing the city's strength and recovery. He concluded, "We rebuilt downtown Manhattan and everybody in the world watched and everybody in the world loved New York and loved New Yorkers. And I have to tell you, that was a very insulting statement that Ted made." Cruz also faced criticism from The New York Times regarding alleged undisclosed loans during his Senate campaign. Cruz dismissed the report as a "hit piece," acknowledging "paperwork errors" but asserting that if that was the best the Times had, they "better go back to the well."

Chris Christie and Marco Rubio also clashed, each promoting their conservative credentials. Rubio accused Christie of aligning with President Barack Obama on issues like gun control, Common Core, and Planned Parenthood. Christie, in turn, criticized Rubio's past statements, suggesting he was being influenced by others, and highlighted his own executive experience as governor. "This is the difference between being a governor and being a senator... when you're a senator, what you get to do is just talk and talk and talk," Christie remarked.

Candidates also debated Trump's controversial proposal to temporarily ban Muslims from entering the U.S. Trump defended the idea as necessary to combat ISIS and avoid "political correctness." However, Jeb Bush urged Trump to reconsider, warning that the ban would hinder efforts to build a coalition with Arab nations. "All Muslims? Seriously? What kind of signal does that send to the rest of the world?" Bush questioned. Other candidates, like Kasich and Christie, expressed support for temporarily halting Syrian refugees. In response to Obama's executive actions on gun control, several candidates passionately defended Second Amendment rights. Rubio stated, "I am convinced that if this president could confiscate every gun in America, he would." Christie condemned the actions as undemocratic, calling it "not a democracy, that's a dictatorship."

Earlier in the day, Carly Fiorina stood out in a smaller debate, addressing national security, technology, and Hillary Clinton. Fiorina, whose candidacy has been marked by strong debate performances, delivered a sharp critique of Clinton, stating, "I'm not a political insider. I haven't spent my lifetime running for office. And unlike another woman in this race, I actually love spending time with my husband." She also commented on Trump's relationship with Vladimir Putin, asserting, "Despite Donald Trump's bromance with Vladimir Putin... Russia is our adversary."

Senator Ted Cruz is employing a new strategy in Iowa, aiming to criticize Donald Trump by invoking "New York values." However, Cruz has not clearly defined what these "New York values" actually are, despite claiming Iowans understand them. This mirrors his past use of vague terms like "Washington cartel," making it essential for Cruz to explain his exact meaning.

What does Cruz mean by "New York values"? Could it refer to an elite education, something Cruz himself possesses with degrees from Princeton and Harvard? Or perhaps it points to New Yorkers' embrace of diversity and tolerance, making their city a welcoming place for immigrants. This is especially relevant as Trump's views on immigration have shifted from supporting legal immigration to opposing it, even for skilled workers vital to the economy. Another interpretation is that Cruz is alluding to crass materialism. While this might be a factor, it's worth noting that Cruz also seeks donations from New York, where he might adopt a different stance on issues like abortion compared to his Iowa audiences. This suggests inconsistent messaging.

Is Cruz hinting at Trump's multiple marriages? While this attack might not be effective in Iowa, Cruz should be direct if he intends to use it. Even though divorce is less stigmatized today, Cruz needs to clearly state if he believes Trump's marital history reflects a deeper issue with his relationships with women. Cruz seems to be attempting a two-pronged approach: criticizing Trump without specific accusations or direct confrontation. Many voters might not see this as a sign of strength. Beyond these indirect criticisms, some Republicans tend to dismiss large cities and entire states as not representing "real America." Cruz's claim that New York values differ from Iowa values fits this pattern, suggesting an inability to connect with people outside their political base.

This approach is problematic, as Cruz will likely need to seek votes in major cities like Cleveland and Philadelphia. Alienating voters in these areas, whether in the primary or general election, is unwise. The idea of dismissing cities as foreign or lacking in values, and as opposed to "real" America, will likely backfire with many residents. Such language reinforces negative stereotypes of the GOP as provincial, bigoted, and exclusionary. For Cruz, it appears to be an easy way out for someone struggling to connect with ordinary people. His difficulty in social interactions within politics is noticeable. Cruz's habit of promoting himself by disparaging others, presenting himself as ideologically pure, and questioning others' motives hinders his ability to build political alliances. The nation already has a condescending and divisive president; another is not needed.

Governor Andrew Cuomo launched a strong media response Friday, directly challenging Senator Ted Cruz's recent disparaging remarks about New York. Cruz, a 2016 presidential candidate, had referenced "New York values" during a debate, characterizing the state as "socially liberal, are pro-abortion, are pro-gay marriage, focused around money and the media."

### Cuomo's Vigorous Rebuttal

Governor Cuomo articulated his strong opposition in a series of interviews. "What he said is not merely anti-New York; it is fundamentally anti-American," Cuomo stated on CNN's "New Day." He elaborated, "I find it lamentable and disturbing for all Americans, not exclusively New Yorkers. This represents the politics of division, an attempt to fracture people and indeed, this nation. Such tactics are ineffective and antithetical to our national identity."

### The Context of "New York Values"

Cruz's invocation of "New York values" arose amidst Donald Trump's questioning of Cruz's eligibility for the presidency due to his Canadian birth, despite his mother's American citizenship. Trump, a native of Queens, countered by referencing New York's resilience and response to the September 11, 2001, terrorist attacks as a testament to its values.

Governor Cuomo supported Trump's assertion on MSNBC's "Morning Joe." "That is a salient point," Cuomo remarked. "While Trump's timing may have been politically calculated, it resonated with the public. As Cruz propagates anti-New York sentiments and attempts to leverage offensive stereotypes, it's crucial to remember that 9/11 stands as a profound symbol of America's noblest qualities."

### Hypocrisy and Broad Offense

Cuomo also highlighted the apparent hypocrisy of Cruz's stance, noting the senator's history of soliciting campaign contributions in Manhattan. "This is profoundly troubling on multiple fronts and demonstrably hypocritical," he asserted on MSNBC. "To criticize New York's financial influence while simultaneously benefiting from it is disingenuous. One can only assume he will now refuse any donations from New Yorkers."

During a third interview with NY1, Cuomo further elaborated on the breadth of Cruz's perceived offenses. "He has offended the LGBTQ+ community," Cuomo stated. "He has been offensive to women. He has offended 18 million New Yorkers, and a significant congressional delegation, which is not a viable approach to governance."

### Wider Condemnation

Beyond the Governor's office, other New York entities also condemned Cruz's rhetoric. Representative Pete King (R-New York) advised the senator to "retreat." Brooklyn Borough President Eric Adams publicly denounced Cruz on Twitter. The New York Daily News featured a provocative image of the Statue of Liberty extending a middle finger towards Senator Cruz.

"New York values" is a phrase often tossed around, especially in political discourse, but what does it truly mean? As a lifelong New Yorker with roots in both upstate and downstate regions, I aim to offer a more accurate portrayal than what's often presented, particularly by those with little firsthand experience. My upbringing in Rochester and nearly a decade living in New York City have given me a deep appreciation for the state's diverse character and its iconic urban heart.

Figures like Ted Cruz, whose background is far removed from New York, often speak of these values with a superficial understanding. It's akin to judging a book by its cover or a movie by its trailer; you're only seeing a fraction of the story. Cruz, with his Texas roots and elite education, lacks the lived experience to truly grasp the complexity of New Yorkers.

Even former Mayor Michael Bloomberg, whose policies were sometimes labeled as "nanny-statism," doesn't fit the simplistic mold. While a proposal to limit large sugary drink sales contributed to this perception, it was ultimately not enacted. New York City possesses a unique character that sets it apart, a nuance often lost in broad generalizations.

When Cruz invoked Donald Trump's use of "Born in the USA" and suggested Trump would embody "New York values" and champion "New York, New York," he missed the mark. His assertion that these values differ from those in Iowa or New Hampshire overlooks the significant similarities. Much of upstate New York shares a landscape, culture, and even prevailing attitudes with New Hampshire. For instance, conservative Republicans upstate often prioritize gun ownership and show less interest in issues like same-sex marriage.

Cruz's vision of "New York values" likely stems from a narrow, stereotypical view, heavily influenced by media portrayals like "Law and Order" or a fleeting taxi ride through Times Square. This stereotype often includes a perception of liberal politics, political correctness, and regulations like the soda ban. It also encompasses the vocal opinions found in publications like the New York Post, values most prominent in wealthy Manhattan areas often used for political fundraising.

This stereotype also fails to capture Donald Trump's own background. While he is wealthy and lives in a prominent skyscraper, his early life was shaped by the outer boroughs, particularly Queens, and his father's strong business foundation. Trump's defining characteristic is an outsider's drive for success, a stark contrast to the "old money" attitudes found on the Upper East Side, where his more extravagant displays might be met with disapproval.

Most New Yorkers, like people elsewhere, are primarily focused on their personal lives and immediate concerns, not overly preoccupied with politics unless a truly widespread issue arises. Many, myself included, found Bloomberg's proposed soda limit to be a poorly conceived idea. Bloomberg himself, a quintessential New Yorker, was reportedly unbothered by such matters, a trait attributed to his wealth and, more importantly, his inherent New York identity.

The simplistic notion that New Yorkers are solely defined by toughness, rudeness, and a desire to impose their views is illogical. It's contradictory to be both gruff and overly sentimental. I'd playfully invite Mr. Cruz to spend a month living in a modest apartment in an average New York neighborhood, navigating daily life from finding a local bodega to understanding the subway and using local healthcare. If, after such an immersion, he still insists New York's values are significantly more liberal than those in Iowa City, I'll acknowledge his critique, though I won't be overly concerned.

An update from a pro-Cruz Super PAC, @KeepPromise1, suggested "NY values" prioritize financial gain over morality and self-importance over humility. This critique is like someone visiting Times Square on New Year's Eve and commenting on the perceived lack of volunteers at homeless shelters – it's based on isolated, unrepresentative observations rather than a thorough understanding.

The New York Daily News has responded forcefully to Senator Ted Cruz's critique of "New York values." In a striking front-page display, the newspaper directly challenged the Texas senator, who had previously criticized Republican front-runner Donald Trump by questioning his association with the city's distinct ethos.

The Daily News cover featured a provocative image of the Statue of Liberty extending a middle finger, accompanied by the bold headline: “Drop Dead Ted.” This powerful statement echoed the paper's iconic 1975 "Ford to City: Drop Dead" headline. The provocative cover emerged in the wake of a Republican presidential debate in South Carolina, held the night before the Iowa caucuses were set to commence the nominating process.

During the debate, Senator Cruz attempted to undermine Trump's Republican credentials by asserting, "I think most people know exactly what New York values are. Everyone understands that the values in New York City are socially liberal." In a pointed counterpoint, Daily News columnist Mike Lupica highlighted an act of profound generosity he witnessed on a Manhattan subway, where one individual offered assistance to another in need. Lupica articulated his perspective, stating, "The best of this city is the best of this country, and always has been. When it was hit in a way that no American city had ever been hit, it came together and rose up together in a moment as thrilling as any America had ever seen."

Donald Trump also delivered a sharp retort during the debate, defending the city's resilience and spirit. "We rebuilt downtown Manhattan and everybody in the world watched and everybody in the world loved New York and loved New Yorkers. And I have to tell you, that was a very insulting statement that Ted made," Trump declared. It is worth noting that the Daily News has not shied away from criticizing Trump; a previous cover addressed his controversial proposal to ban all Muslims from entering the United States. Current polling indicates Trump maintains a lead among Republican candidates, with a recent NBC News/Wall Street Journal poll showing him as the preferred choice for 33% of potential Republican primary voters, compared to 20% for Cruz.

Ted Cruz suggested that Donald Trump's persistent criticisms signal concern over his front-runner status in the presidential race, interpreting these attacks as Trump embodying "New York values." Speaking on The Howie Carr Show, Cruz addressed Trump's use of Bruce Springsteen's "Born in the USA," a song choice linked to questions about Cruz's eligibility to be president due to his Canadian birth.

Cruz posited that Trump's strategy might shift to playing "New York, New York," given Trump's origins and his embodiment of those values. The Texas senator characterized Trump as "rattled" by Cruz's growing campaign prominence, describing the attacks as "unloading at every stop with direct attacks." While initially stating he wouldn't reciprocate, Cruz subtly countered Trump on two occasions. Earlier, he told reporters that Hillary Clinton's supporters were "eager to support Donald Trump and the attacks that are being tossed my direction."

During the radio interview, Cruz echoed this sentiment, remarking on Clinton's observation that Trump was "a Democrat before he was a Republican." Cruz commented that Clinton, a partisan Democrat herself, would know how to identify Democrats, and that she and Trump know each other well. He found it noteworthy that Clinton's key supporters were amplifying Trump's attacks against him.

Cruz attributed these Democratic attacks to current polling data, which he believes shows him defeating Hillary Clinton, while Trump loses to her by a significant margin. This, he explained, likely has Clinton's supporters concerned and motivated to amplify Trump's criticisms.

Addressing Trump's comment about evangelicals not coming from Cuba, Cruz invited Trump to attend church with him. Cruz suggested that attacks on one's faith indicate nervousness about the race's trajectory. He reiterated his willingness to invite Trump to church, emphasizing that his focus remains on the campaign rather than engaging in mudslinging.

query: What prompted the backlash against Matthew and Terces Engelhart, the owners of Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre?

documents.text:
## Vegan Restaurant Founders' Animal Consumption Sparks Outrage

The founders of the well-known vegan restaurant group Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre are facing severe backlash, including death threats, after images surfaced showing Matthew and Terces Engelhart raising, slaughtering, and eating animals on their farm. This revelation has deeply angered many in the vegan community who supported their businesses.

The Engelharts, who launched the first Cafe Gratitude in 2004 and have catered to celebrities like Gwyneth Paltrow and Beyoncé, are defending their actions at Be Love Farm in Vacaville, California. They frame this shift as a personal decision driven by a commitment to "regenerative agriculture." The controversy escalated when activists shared blog posts from 2015 depicting a freezer full of beef and Matthew Engelhart eating a hamburger. These posts detailed their "transition" to meat consumption after nearly four decades of vegetarianism. Matthew Engelhart explained to the Hollywood Reporter, "We started to observe nature and what we saw is that nature doesn’t exist without animals." He views animals as making an "extreme sacrifice for humanity" and believes humans can partake in this "sacrament."

While the Engelharts' farm supplies produce to their restaurants, the news has incensed strict vegans. Online criticism has been harsh, with accusations of hypocrisy and ill will. Critics argue the Engelharts misled vegans who supported their businesses, unknowingly contributing to practices involving animal slaughter. The couple built their brand on a message of peace and love, a philosophy extended to their sister restaurant, Gracias Madre, with locations in San Francisco and Los Angeles. They have also authored books, including "Sacred Commerce: Business as a Path of Awakening." A February blog post from Be Love Farm described their move to incorporate meat products as a "necessary and important part of our growth as well as the sustainability of our farm," including images of their beef alongside pastoral scenes of their cows.

This is not the first time the Engelharts have faced public scrutiny. In 2011, they cited "aggressive lawsuits" from former employees as the reason for closing or selling eight Cafe Gratitude restaurants in the Bay Area. These lawsuits challenged tip-pooling practices and alleged unpaid overtime. Another lawsuit involved an employee claiming wrongful termination for refusing to attend a controversial self-improvement course. The Engelharts eventually settled these disputes, selling or transferring some restaurants to family members. Terces Engelhart reportedly met Matthew at a Landmark Forum seminar. Her daughter from a previous marriage now co-owns a vegan beer garden and a vegan ice cream parlor in Los Angeles. Activist Mary Cummins has suggested the Engelharts may have been influenced by Allan Savory, an ecologist who advocates for using livestock in grasslands to combat climate change, a theory that has drawn both support and opposition. The long-term impact of this controversy on the Engelhart empire, which serves many meals daily and hosts events at their farm, remains uncertain. It is unclear if patrons will continue to engage with the farm, especially its animal interactions, after learning about their meat production practices.

What a wonderful week it was on the farm. Our great friends who own Ca Momi in Napa gifted us with some of their truly delicious pizzas and profiteroles to make my recovery from a surgical procedure easier on me. I was so grateful for the added rest and kindness. I then got inspired to make my own profiteroles since we have this amazing farm cream and voila, see them in the picture above. Here I am piping them onto the parchment paper getting ready to bake them. Matthew continued his pruning of all the trees while the boys, Jaxon and Jack put mulch around the base of the trees. You can see that the almonds are in full bloom and the nectarine blossoms are coming on in their beautiful pinks! It was a big week as we begin to make the transition into our own meat products after nearly 40 years of vegetarianism. While I would clearly say we are in transition and that transition is happening deep within our beings, we know it is a necessary and important part of our own growth as well as the sustainability of our farm. Certainly a part of us wants to either deny the inevitability of death or simply not let ourselves get present to the reality of it, for our animals as well as ourselves. But we know that while we die a little bit each day as we open our hearts further to the presence of love, and as we are the caretakers of our farm animals the responsibility for their health and well being lies with us. This is clearly a longer discussion and we invite you to one of our upcoming farm dinners over the next 9 months, where we share more about this process. Here Matthew is straining off beef broth and our freezer full of pastured beef, our pastured beef. Here are the girls full of Spring clover sitting down chewing their cud in the warm Spring breeze of the midday. and introducing Jack (blonde) and Jaxon, our apprentices on the farm and a welcome youthful pair of energetic young men. With no rain in sight we have started to irrigate already, which brings me to my question for the week. How do you nourish your soul? Think about it. Have a great week, Love, Terces

The husband-and-wife owners of a trendy chain of California vegan restaurants are under attack from an angry pack of animal rights activists who discovered the restaurateurs were slaughtering animals on their farm and eating meat. Matthew and Terces Engelhart, owners of the famous celebrity-patroned Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre restaurants, have been receving death threats from activists after they started circulating blog posts from the couple’s Be Love Farm in northern California that show the couple raising cattle, according to what they told the Hollywood Reporter. “They are duping vegans to support their animal killings,” activist Chase Avior, who organized protests at the southern California restaurants, told the Hollywood Reporter. The Engleharts started the first Cafe Gratitude in San Francisco in 2004 before opening more vegan restaurants in southern California around 2011, riding the wave of consumer demand for healthy, sustainable food. The restaurants are known for being celebrity hotspots — Emma Stone, Andrew Garfield, Kerry Washington, Jay Z, and Beyonce have all been spotted enjoying their vegan dishes. Cafe Gratitude owners Matthew and Terces Engelhart are under fire after the California based vegan restaurateurs were found to have returned to eating meat. (Ashley Dentino/via YouTube) The firestorm over the owners’ carnivorism started when vigilant vegans got a hold of a 14-month old blog post stating the couple’s decision to end their 40 years of vegetarianism and begin what they call “regenerative agriculture.” The post included photos showing freezers full of beef at their farm, jars of beef stock and of Matthew Engelhart enjoying a hamburger. “We started to observe nature and what we saw is that nature doesn’t exist without animals,” Matthew Engelhart told the Hollywood Reporter last week, as an explanation for their transition. Outraged animal rights activists weren’t so forgiving of their change in diet calling it “regenerative bulls--t!” on a Facebook page dedicated to boycotting the restaurant and organizing protests. Cafe Gratitude in Venice, Calif. is a known celebrity hotspot. (Rick Loomis/LA Times via Getty Images) Protesters also left a slew of one-star Yelp reviews on the popular eatery’s site. “I’m GRATEFUL that I know they’re s--theads!” wrote one commenter. "Here's a new dish for Cafe Gratitude - I Am MURDERER!!!" another person wrote. Numerous protests have taken place outside their restaurants — with demonstrators carrying signs that called the restaurateurs “hypocrites” as leaders in the vegan community and others that said “no animal is grateful to die,” as a play on the restaurant's name. “People have taken up the mob mentality,” Matthew Engelhart bemoaned. Sign up for BREAKING NEWS Emails privacy policy Thanks for subscribing!

The owners of the most prominent vegan restaurant chain in Los Angeles were found to be raising and "harvesting" animals at their Northern California farm. Now Hollywood's vegan community is angry, and death threats have been made. "People have taken up the mob mentality," says Cafe Gratitude owner Matthew Engelhart. L.A.'s vegan vortex has angrily turned on the most prominent vegan restaurant group in town this week as word has spread that its owners are not just eating meat but raising and slaughtering animals at the working farm where they live in Northern California. Matthew and Terces Engelhart, the husband-and-wife proprietors of favored entertainment industry haunt Cafe Gratitude, tell The Hollywood Reporter they've been receiving death threats as part of a quickly growing, internet-bred campaign against them. It has also spawned a deluge of one-star reviews on their local outposts' respective Yelp pages, a boycott group on Facebook that tallies 571 members at press time and plans now underway for a protest at the Larchmont Village location on Friday at 7 p.m. "People have taken up the mob mentality," says Matthew. "It saddens me that the choices we made in the privacy of our home would lead people to feel so betrayed that it's elevated to threats on our lives. I'm very discouraged." The trouble began last week when animal rights activists discovered and then widely circulated a 14-month-old blog post written by Terces on the Engelharts' Be Love Farm website, which mixed an announcement of their transition back into a meat diet again after nearly 40 years of vegetarianism (they had been vegan since 2003) with posted pictures of strained beef broth and a freezer full of pastured beef from their own dairy cows. Matthew tells THR they have kept chickens on the farm for seven years "for eggs only," along with the cows for five years for milk, cheese and butter that's for sale. (He claims they've "harvested," or slaughtered, several cows in total and never sold the meat, only shared it with "our friends, neighbors and community.") The news has come as a shock to many vegans, who have been regular customers of the restaurants and claim the Engelharts have built their brand on not just serving vegan food but clearly wrapping themselves in the righteousness of the vegan cause — which they argue has now been undermined. "The reason we're so upset is that veganism is a belief system," says Carrie Christianson, who started the Facebook boycott group. "You are patronizing a restaurant that you think has that philosophy, and it turns out it doesn't. Vegans should know that this restaurant has a farm that slaughters animals." The Engelharts launched the first Cafe Gratitude in San Francisco in 2004. The mini-chain began colonizing Southern California in 2011 with the on-the-ground help of their manager sons from different marriages, Ryland Engelhart and Cary Mosier, beginning with a location in Larchmont Village, followed by outposts in Venice and downtown L.A.'s Arts District (as well as locations in San Diego and Newport Beach). Investors such as musician Jason Mraz have backed them. (A newer sister concept, Gracias Madre, which serves "meatless Mexican" fare, opened in 2014 in West Hollywood; their daughter, Mollie, co-owns Culver City's separate Sage Organic Vegan Bistro — which also purchases from Be Love Farm — with Woody Harrelson.) Cafe Gratitude is known for giving dishes names that are self-affirmations. The most popular include the "I Am Whole" macrobiotic sea-vegetable rice and a curried lentil assemblage called "I Am Humble." The locations have become a solid entertainment industry draw for business lunches, as well as a reliable celebrity nexus. Customers range from Beyonce Knowles and Sacha Baron Cohen to Ariana Grande and Jake Gyllenhaal. The Engelharts frame their return to "sustainable, regenerative" animal consumption as the mindful culmination of years spent laboring on their upstate farm. "We started to observe nature, and what we saw is that nature doesn't exist without animals," says Matthew. "Neither does natural farming. You know, you can't buy organic vegetables that aren't fertilized with animal residue. So that was our discovery. We aren't on a soapbox." Yet while the Engelharts are at pains to paint their decision as a private and personal rural journey separate from their public work at their urban restaurants, their critics find the reasoning specious, believing the enterprises, the figureheads and their choices are inextricably bound. "This is a vegan restaurant company that's been a leader in the vegan community," says Chase Avior, a screenwriter and director who is organizing the Friday protest. "If they're going to go around and say it's not about animal rights, it's not about speaking about the animals horrifically abused, it's just about better nurturing a plant-based lifestyle, that's something different. A lot of vegans have a right to feel betrayed by this." Still, the couple's vegan supporters think the fury is misplaced. "Boycott factory farming — something that will make a real difference to the cause," says Rainbeau Mars, author of The 21-Day SuperStar Cleanse and, along with Ryland Engelhart, a board member at Venice-based healthy-soil nonprofit Kiss the Ground. "And as for their own personal diets, would we stop supporting the Dalai Lama because he eats meat? Or stop supporting the organic farmer who grows your tomatoes because she eats meat?" After that interview, Mars wrote a long follow-up email to THR to clarify her position ("despite needing to go teach a yoga class on the helicopter pad of the Four Seasons this morning and head out of town this evening"). In it, Mars explains how she has worked in "service to greater humanity" and preaches a sort of compassion. "I feel we should support the leaders in our community that are sharing charity and compassion, health and community activism such as the founders of Cafe Gratitude and Gracias Madre," she says. "I invite us all to not point the finger at others but look at ourselves." Such thinking doesn't sit well with others who have spoken out about the Engelharts' choices at Be Love Farm, which according to the couple these days supplies only a limited amount of fruits and vegetables to the restaurants. One of them is the musician Moby, who opened his own vegan restaurant five months ago in Silver Lake called Little Pine. "I sincerely hope that they discontinue their practice of raising and killing animals for food," he wrote on Facebook. Meanwhile, Cooking Channel personality and raw food chef Jason Wrobel (his clients include Robin Wright and Jeremy Piven) penned a long open letter on his website, echoing many online sentiments: "People feel misled, deliberately lied to and that a business they've so lovingly supported for many years has lost its way. I feel that my hard-earned money has been used for purposes that are unethical, cruel and out-of-alignment with my values. And I strongly feel that a more public announcement from the company should have been made about the practices at the farm, not tucked away in some obscure blog post." For their part, the Engelharts profess to be perplexed by the dissent. "I don't think there's any organization on the planet that's done more to promote a plant-based diet than us," says Matthew. "We've moved it from a dogma to a genre. We serve 28,000 meals a week in all of our enterprises. We've done nothing but a plant-based diet at our restaurants and we're being attacked. It doesn't make sense to me." Their critics aren't satisfied, despite their record. "I eat at non-vegan restaurants all the time," says Elana Lavine, who runs the L.A. healthy lifestyle blog Klean Slate. "Just don't claim to be something you're not. They're slaughtering animals! And there's been a lack of total transparency about it on the restaurant side. It looks kind of shady, like they are hiding things. And there was always that cloud around them when it came to the cult thing." The "cult thing" has left customers leery in the past. The couple, who like to say they practice "sacred commerce," had to close their Northern California locations in 2010 in the midst of lawsuit battles, one related to a policy encouraging allegedly illegal tip-pooling and another stemming from a mandate that their employee get involved with the controversial personal-betterment organization Landmark Forum, a successor to Werner Erhard's 1970s self-help program EST, which centered on a unique style of session therapy that was recently dramatized on FX's period spy show The Americans. Both iterations have been criticized for their aggressive recruitment tactics. The Engelharts, who met while attending Landmark Forum, believe they're being held up to a dogmatic purity standard — one that, in the scheme of their contributions to veganism, is entirely unfair. "Somehow we got made mom and dad of some vegan movement," says Matthew. "We never signed up for that. It's crazy. Do [the protestors] check in on the diets of every other vegan restaurant owner? Do they check in on the diets of the owners of Whole Foods? We are baffled. And believe me there are lots of vegan enterprises where the principals are not vegan, or they have other enterprises that are not vegan." He goes on, "I am allowed to change my mind. And by the way, I never even told them what my mind was. All I told them was it's a vegan restaurant. And it still is! And it always will be!"

Chef Chandra Gilbert, a culinary force with **33 years of industry experience**, is introducing her distinctive approach to plant-based dining to Los Angeles. Her new venture, **Gracias Madre**, promises a vibrant fusion of **bold flavors** and **seasonally-conscious ingredients**. Gilbert's extensive background includes formative roles at celebrated kitchens such as Alice Waters' Café Fanny, Cowgirl Creamery, and Greens Restaurant. She has been an integral part of the Café Gratitude family since **2004**.

Her tenure with the renowned vegan restaurant group commenced as Director of Operations in the Bay Area. It was within this capacity that the seed for Gracias Madre was planted, germinating from conversations with Café Gratitude Founders Matthew and Terces Engelhart. Collaboratively, they **co-designed the restaurant's aesthetic and ambiance** and **co-created its innovative menu**. This menu was deeply rooted in the exceptional produce cultivated at the Engelhart's Be Love Farm in Vacaville.

Gracias Madre first debuted in San Francisco in **2009**, bringing Gilbert's vision to life. Located in the city's Mission District, the restaurant quickly garnered **critical acclaim** for its **lively and inventive interpretation of Mexican cuisine**. Now, with the **January 2014 launch of Gracias Madre in West Hollywood**, Gilbert is extending this celebrated culinary philosophy to the Los Angeles dining scene. The Los Angeles iteration of the menu continues to highlight the bounty from Be Love Farm, **enhanced by the freshest ingredients procured from local farmers markets**.

## Café Gratitude: A Plant-Based Culinary Experience Rooted in Well-being and Sustainability

Café Gratitude invites you to a unique plant-based dining experience, where delicious, gourmet food nourishes both your personal health and the planet's well-being. Our core philosophy centers on cultivating an environment and a menu that actively promotes wellness and environmental responsibility. We operate under a distinctive business model called “Sacred Commerce,” which emphasizes inspired service, transparent communication, and a deep appreciation for life's abundance.

Our vision is to help create a world rich in abundance, celebrating life through vibrant, flavorful food and a dedicated team. We honor the earth and ourselves by meticulously selecting the finest organic ingredients, supporting local farmers, championing sustainable agriculture, and using eco-friendly products. Every dish is prepared with intention, encouraging you to embrace our welcoming spaces and adopt a mindset of self-love, acceptance, generosity, and gratitude. Experience the joy of being nourished, cared for, and revitalized.

Chef Dreux Ellis, our Executive Chef, leads the creation and implementation of our organic, plant-based menu across all Café Gratitude locations in Southern California. Since joining the Venice team in April 2012, Chef Ellis has been instrumental in making health-conscious, flavor-packed cuisine accessible to a broader audience. He now guides the culinary direction for our restaurants in the Arts District, Larchmont Village, Venice, and San Diego, as well as the thoughtfully reimagined Gratitude restaurants in Newport Beach and Beverly Hills.

**Discover Our Locations:**

*   **Los Angeles - Larchmont Village:** 639 N Larchmont Blvd., Los Angeles, CA 90004 | (323) 580-6383 | Hours: 8 AM - 10 PM Daily
*   **San Diego:** 1980 Kettner Blvd., San Diego, CA 92101 | (619) 736-5077 | Hours: 8 AM - 10 PM Daily (Valet parking available Thurs-Sat, 5:30 PM - 11:30 PM)
*   **Newport Beach:** 1617 Westcliff Drive, Newport Beach, CA 92660 | (949) 386-8100 | Weekdays: 8 AM - 10 PM; Weekends: 8 AM - 10 PM (To-Go/Cafe Hours: 7 AM - 10 PM Daily)
*   **Arts District, Los Angeles:** 300 S Santa Fe Ave., Los Angeles, CA 90013 | (213) 929-5580 | Hours: 8 AM - 10 PM Daily
*   **Venice:** 512 Rose Ave., Venice, CA 90291 | (424) 231-8000 | Hours: 8 AM - 10 PM Daily (90-minute parking available in downstairs garage with validation)
*   **Beverly Hills:** 419 N Canon Drive, Beverly Hills, CA 90210 | (424) 389-1850 | Hours: 8 AM - 10 PM Daily (Parking information on-site)

**Connect With Us:**

Explore resources like "Kiss The Ground" to deepen your understanding of soil health and regenerative agriculture. Subscribe to our newsletter for updates. For career opportunities or general inquiries, please contact us directly.

This week was a period of significant growth and profound learning, notably marked by the highly anticipated launch of a new Cafe Gratitude location in the vibrant Arts District of downtown Los Angeles. The extensive training of 112 new employees presented a considerable challenge, yet it also served as a powerful testament to the exceptional individuals who are deeply committed to Cafe Gratitude's core mission: cultivating open hearts, expanding love, and fostering ethical livelihoods. This commitment was further underscored by the presence of a surprise guest during a training lunch, an event that brought widespread smiles and a palpable sense of joy. Beyond the professional milestones, visits to Los Angeles always offer cherished moments with my grandsons, Valentine, who is 6 months old, and Rio, who is 2 months old. The role of grandmother is a source of immeasurable joy and fulfillment.

On the farm, we celebrated an extraordinary harvest, yielding enormous heads of lettuce, a clear indicator of the land's remarkable fertility. This week also marked a deeply personal turning point: after more than four decades of vegetarianism, we prepared and consumed our first hamburgers. This significant dietary shift, while presenting its own challenges, has been profoundly illuminating. We approach this change with deep gratitude for the sacrifices made by our cattle.

Matthew's decision to reintroduce meat into his diet after forty years of strict vegetarianism is rooted in a complex understanding of non-violence that extends to the entirety of the ecological web. He posits that conventional agricultural practices, particularly plowing and monoculture farming, represent one of the planet's most destructive forces, directly contributing to species extinction and the loss of vital habitats. For instance, the production of margarine from Canadian canola necessitates plowing that devastates prairie ecosystems and endangers numerous species, a point highlighted by environmental researchers. Matthew advocates for the crucial role of well-managed grasslands, supported by herds of ruminants such as cattle and buffalo, in preserving biodiversity and preventing land degradation. This perspective aligns with findings from organizations like the Savory Institute, which champions holistic management.

Grasslands, which cover approximately 30% of the Earth's surface, are intrinsically dependent on ruminants for their health and vitality. Their absence, as documented by ecological studies, leads to land deterioration and desertification, with far-reaching consequences that can even exacerbate regional conflicts, as seen in areas like Syria and Iraq due to poor land management. In this ecological context, cattle emerge as critical partners in restoration efforts. They contribute to soil fertility, ensure essential ground cover, and play a pivotal role in reversing desertification and mitigating the impacts of climate change, a concept explored in depth by experts like Allan Savory. Matthew believes that ethically raised cattle, when integrated thoughtfully into our food system, offer a pathway to participate in a natural sacrament, acknowledging their divinely appointed role as a source of sustenance.

This profound perspective on sacrifice is eloquently articulated by the renowned writer and farmer Wendell Berry in his seminal work: "To live we must daily break the body and shed the blood of Creation. When we do it knowingly, lovingly, skillfully, reverently, it is a sacrament. When we do it ignorantly, greedily, clumsily, destructively it is a desecration." This philosophy was further solidified by my attendance at an Allan Savory workshop, where a particularly memorable dinner conversation provided deep insight into the authentic South African method of preparing "biltong," a traditional dried meat.

While veganism represents a significant and valid response to the ethical concerns surrounding animal cruelty within industrial food systems, we also acknowledge the substantial nutritional benefits derived from animals raised in healthy, humane environments. It is imperative, as emphasized by food system advocates, to bridge the disconnect between consumers and their food sources, embracing the complete cycle of life and death rather than maintaining a detached perspective. This holistic view is gaining traction among sustainable agriculture proponents.

This week has served as a powerful and humbling reminder of divine grace and the profound importance of embracing it in all aspects of our lives. As we continue to breathe and love, we reflect on our journey. Looking back to the opening of our very first Cafe Gratitude in San Francisco 11 years ago, a time when cold-brewed coffee and almond milk were considered novelties, the progress we have made to this day has been nothing short of extraordinary, a testament to the vision of founders like Matthew and Terces.

query: What specific actions led to the dismissal of Harriet Wallberg and Anders Hamsten from the Nobel Assembly?

Here's a revised version of the document, aiming to meet your quality targets:

**Science's Most Astonishing Discoveries: From Jupiter's Fury to Ancient Giants**

Prepare to be amazed by a collection of groundbreaking scientific revelations and captivating imagery. NASA has unveiled breathtaking, color-enhanced images of Jupiter's Great Red Spot, a colossal storm larger than Earth that has raged for over 350 years. Meanwhile, the Wellcome Image Awards continue to impress, showcasing a remarkably detailed 3D reconstruction of an African grey parrot's intricate blood vessel system post-euthanasia, and the delicate beauty of baby Hawaiian bobtail squid, revealing their central ink sac and light organ.

Archaeologists have unearthed the remains of 5,000-year-old Chinese individuals, believed to have been exceptionally tall and robust, with the tallest skeleton measuring an impressive 1.9 meters. In a celestial discovery, NASA has identified a staggering 75,000-mile-wide hole on the Sun's surface, a phenomenon linked to magnetic field interactions and cooler surface areas.

A significant paleontological find occurred in Surrey, where a 132-million-year-old dinosaur fossil was discovered at a factory. Paleontologists Sarah Moore and Jamie Jordan suspect it's an Iguanodon, a herbivore that stood approximately three meters tall and measured ten meters in length. On Mars, the prospect of discovering life appears increasingly remote as researchers have detected toxic chemicals on its surface, despite evidence of past water sources like the vast Echus Chasma.

Back on Earth, Yellowstone National Park continues to mesmerize with its geothermal wonders, including the Grand Prismatic Spring, the largest in the U.S., and the iconic "Old Faithful" geyser. The Wellcome Image Awards also feature a close-up of an iris clip fitted onto an eye, illustrating the implantation of an artificial intraocular lens used to treat conditions like myopia and cataracts.

In a stark reminder of global instability, the Syrian civil war has necessitated the first-ever withdrawal from the Svalbard Global Seed Vault, often called the "doomsday bank." Researchers have requested drought-resistant seeds, including wheat, barley, and grasses, due to damage to a seed bank in Aleppo. This marks the initial use of the vault, established in 2008, to compensate for disruptions in regional seed supply.

Scientists have achieved a significant breakthrough by isolating the distinct scent of human death. This research, the first of its kind, identifies the specific chemicals emitted by corpses, aiming to aid in future body recovery efforts and potentially develop synthetic scents for cadaver dogs or electronic detection devices.

Astronomers have captured an unprecedented event: a black hole consuming a star and then expelling a portion of it. This observation, tracking a Sun-sized star pulled into a supermassive black hole, revealed a high-speed flare ejected from the black hole's rim. While black holes devouring stars and emitting jets have been observed before, this is the first instance linking the ejected flare directly to the swallowed star.

A British scientist has unearthed the fossil of a dog-sized horned dinosaur in North America, dating back up to 100 million years. This jawbone fragment provides crucial evidence for an evolutionary divide between eastern and western dinosaur species on the continent during the Late Cretaceous period, when a shallow sea separated the landmasses.

Contrary to popular belief, the concept of distinct "male" and "female" brains is being challenged by a comprehensive study. While specific brain regions exhibit sex-based differences, individual brains rarely conform to exclusively "male" or "female" traits, with many characteristics being common across both sexes.

Evidence suggests that life on Earth may have emerged hundreds of millions of years earlier than previously understood, potentially as far back as 4.1 billion years ago. Analysis of graphite trapped in ancient crystals indicates that life could have begun almost instantaneously after Earth's formation, a discovery described as a "potentially transformational scientific advance."

NASA has confirmed the presence of flowing water on Mars. Scientists have long suspected that dark patches known as Recurring Slope Lineae were composed of briny water, and new findings, including the detection of hydrated salts, now prove these features are caused by liquid water.

Our planet may face an increased risk of meteor impacts, as our galaxy is observed to be ejecting comets that could potentially threaten Earth. While previously thought to be in a relatively safe period, scientists suggest that certain orbital disturbances could be more significant than anticipated, with evidence pointing to recent activity indicating passage through a meteor shower. These showers, occurring on an estimated 26-million-year cycle, coincide with mass extinction events over the past 260 million years.

In a remarkable feat of genetic engineering, Chinese scientists have created genetically-modified beagles with double the muscle mass by editing a specific gene. These "mutant" dogs are expected to possess enhanced running abilities, making them suitable for hunting and military applications. The research team aims to further develop modified dogs, including those engineered to exhibit human diseases like muscular dystrophy or Parkinson's, to better understand these conditions.

Researchers have discovered a "lost world" of Arctic dinosaurs, with a new species, Ugrunaaluk kuukpikensis, challenging existing understandings of dinosaur physiology. Professor Greg Erickson of Florida State University noted that this discovery raises intriguing questions about how these creatures survived in such an extreme environment.

The Swedish government has taken decisive action, dismissing the entire board of the prestigious Karolinska Institute. This significant move follows a damning investigation that revealed profound negligence in the hiring process of surgeon Paolo Macchiarini and his subsequent patient operations. The unfolding medical scandal, now encompassing serious accusations of scientific fraud and patient fatalities, has cast a long shadow over the institution renowned for awarding the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.

The controversial recruitment of Italian surgeon Paolo Macchiarini had already triggered a series of high-profile departures. Previously, the secretary of the Nobel Committee at the Institute and the then-chancellor resigned in February. More recently, the Institute's chairman stepped down just last week. Macchiarini himself was terminated in March. Karolinska officials stated that he had provided false information on his resume and was guilty of scientific negligence, particularly after two of his patients tragically died. Swedish prosecutors are currently investigating Macchiarini, suspecting him of gross criminal negligence that led to a patient's death. He has consistently denied any wrongdoing.

"Scandal is indeed the appropriate term," stated Minister for Higher Education and Research Helene Hellmark Knutsson during a press conference. She emphasized, "Individuals have suffered harm due to the actions of the Karolinska Institute and also the Karolinska University Hospital." The government's announcement of the board's dismissal came after the presentation of an external investigation, commissioned by the Institute itself, on Monday. The investigator's report highlighted that the management exhibited "a stunning indifference" to a substantial volume of highly negative references concerning Macchiarini during his hiring.

Minister Hellmark Knutsson further elaborated that the investigation conclusively demonstrated the Institute's violation of laws and regulations. She asserted that the institution had displayed a disregard for legal frameworks, ethical standards, and moral principles. She confirmed that once a new board is successfully recruited, all current board members who have not already resigned in the wake of the Macchiarini scandal will be replaced. The nation's University Chancellor, Harriet Wallberg, who held the leadership position at the Karolinska Institute when Macchiarini was initially hired, will also be required to relinquish her post, according to Hellmark Knutsson.
