Deputy White House Counsel Makan Delrahim earned over **$1 million** in the past year through a combination of salary, stock payouts, and a buyout from his law firm, Brownstein Hyatt Farber Schreck. His financial interests extend to being a passive investor in the movie "Trash Fire."

This reporting was conducted by Associated Press writers Julie Bykowicz, Chad Day, Stephen Braun, Jack Gillum, Michael Biesecker, Ted Bridis, Bernard Condon, and Ken Sweet.

Steve Bannon, a top adviser to President Donald Trump, earned more than half a million dollars last year from entities linked to a pair of major conservative donors, according to documents released by the White House detailing the personal finances of its officials. Bannon reported an ownership stake of $1 million or more in a data-crunching firm that worked for Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas) during the presidential race. He also took in $191,000 in consulting fees in 2016 as executive chairman of the conservative media website Breitbart News, which sided with Trump during the Republican primary. Story Continued Below The disclosure highlighted Bannon's ties to a pair of conservative political donors, Robert and Rebekah Mercer. The couple, which funded the upstart data company Cambridge Analytica, backed Cruz early on but eventually swung their allegiance to Trump. Cambridge Analytica later worked for Trump, and now it is seeking government contracts. Bannon’s disclosure form said that he has “an agreement in principle” to sell his stake in Cambridge. Bannon also reported income from the Government Accountability Institute and the film production company Glittering Steel, both of which are linked to the Mercers, who also are part owners of Breitbart. The documents released Friday night also put on public view new details about two Trump family members who have taken official White House posts: Trump's daughter Ivanka and son-in-law, Jared Kushner. Ivanka Trump’s self-branded businesses, including her clothing line, brought in more than $5 million since the beginning of 2016 and are valued at over $50 million, the documents showed. Ivanka recently assumed an official assistant-to-the-president post, so no disclosure has been released for her yet, but her husband's form also includes her assets. She is putting the Ivanka brands in a trust she will no longer manage directly, her legal advisers say. Meanwhile, Kushner resigned from positions with 266 different entities in order to begin work as a senior adviser to Trump, a senior administration official told reporters Friday. The disclosures showed the wealth of an administration filled with real estate and finance experts alongside government hands. They show former National Security Adviser Michael Flynn got paid at least $5,000 for a speaking engagement with the Kremlin-funded English language network RT -- a disclosure Flynn left out of his initial January disclosures but which he revealed in an amended document that he signed Friday. The forms also offer a small window into how much people are paid at the privately-held Trump Organization. Jason Greenblatt listed a little more than $1 million in pay from the president’s company for his work as its executive vice president and chief legal officer. Greenblatt spent a decade working in Trump Tower before joining the White House in January as Trump’s special representative for international negotiations. The forms being made public cover White House staff making more than $161,755 a year, as well as anyone who's a commissioned officer — people who hold a title like assistant or special assistant to the president. Trump's own form was not among those released Friday. He filed the disclosure twice as a candidate, in July 2015 and May 2016. He's not required by law to file again until May 2018, but aides said they have not decided whether he will file an update this year. Here are highlights from the newly-disclosed financial reports: Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump Ivanka Trump’s investment in the Trump International Hotel in Washington is valued at between $5 million and $25 million and brought her between $1 million and $5 million in income since its opening in September, the disclosure says. Meanwhile, Kushner made $4.5 million in 2016 from the company that owns the New York Observer. He's sold his stake in the firm. Kushner has sold or is selling several other operating businesses, including an insurance brokerage, a broadband firm and a window and door company. But he’s holding on to well over 100 other real estate assets, primarily buildings in the New York City area. Critics say his investments could pose a conflict of interest as he tackles a broad, almost unbounded portfolio of issues for his father-in-law. Omarosa Manigault The reality television star-turned-Trump White House communications staffer accepted a wedding dress, custom veil and other accessories -- worth an estimated $25,000 -- to appear on an episode of TLC’s “Say Yes to the Dress.” She also listed on her financial disclosures between $7,100 and $18,000 for rents or royalties surrounding her role in several television shows, including syndication of Trump’s ‘The Apprentice.’ She is also a one-third beneficiary of a trust worth between $1 million and $5 million that was established by her late fiancée Michael Clarke Duncan, an Oscar-nominated actor who died in 2012. Gary Cohn The former Goldman Sachs chief operating officer now heading the National Economic Council director reported assets worth at least $200 million and perhaps $1 billion or more, since the forms generally use broad ranges and don't even require a range for assets worth over $50 million. His income in the past year was between about $48 million and $76 million. Cohn did not include details about selling off his investment stakes in the financial disclosure, as other senior White House staff did, though it has previously been reported that he sold his shares in Goldman for approximately $285 million. Earlier this month the New York Times also reported that Cohn planned to sell a stake in one of China’s largest banks. The total stock is listed as worth over $1 million in his disclosure though the Times reported it was worth approximately $16 million. In addition to his Goldman holdings, Cohn reported stock worth as much as $250,000 in Bank of America, which could pose a conflict should the administration rewrite the financial regulatory regime put in place by Dodd-Frank. He also reported stock in Facebook and the president’s favorite social media, Twitter. Cohn also reported ownership stakes in several commercial real estate properties, technology patents, and a helicopter. Don McGahn The chief White House counsel left behind $2.4 million in pay as a Jones Day partner. At the firm, McGahn’s clients included Trump, then-Sen. Mark Kirk (R-Ill.), the National Rifle Association, the GOP data firm i360 and Aaron Schock, the former Illinois Republican congressman under indictment on federal criminal corruption charges. Stephen Miller Trump’s top policy adviser on the campaign made $124,885 in 2016, according to his financial disclosure. At the same time, he listed as an employer “Insperity PEO Services, L.P.,” which he wrote on his form is the name of his parents' real estate company, where he had an advisory, unpaid position while working on the campaign. Reed Cordish One of the Trump family’s closest friends is now serving in the White House. His financial disclosure forms show he maintains a stake in companies and holdings worth tens of millions of dollars, including casinos, real estate and restaurants in Baltimore, Kansas City, St. Louis, Richmond, Louisville, Kentucky, and Arlington, Texas. ## Trump Administration Officials Reveal Extensive Financial Holdings

The White House has released extensive financial disclosure forms for dozens of its top administration officials, revealing significant real estate investments and business entanglements among President Donald Trump's inner circle. These disclosures offer a snapshot of the wealth and financial interests held by individuals serving in key government roles.

### Kushner and Ivanka Trump's Substantial Portfolio

President Trump's son-in-law, Jared Kushner, and daughter, Ivanka Trump, are holding onto a substantial portfolio of at least $240 million in assets, including numerous real estate investments, while serving in White House positions. Kushner, a senior adviser, has resigned from approximately 260 entities and divested from 58 businesses or investments identified by lawyers as potential conflicts of interest. However, legal counsel, in consultation with the Office of Government Ethics, has determined that Kushner's real estate holdings, particularly in New York City, are unlikely to necessitate further divestment. Jamie Gorelick, an attorney involved in their ethics agreements, stated that the remaining conflicts are "pretty narrow and very manageable." Kushner began divesting from problematic assets shortly after the election, with some transactions predating the disclosure requirements. For instance, he sold his stake in a Manhattan skyscraper to a trust managed by his mother, with neither he nor Ivanka Trump holding any financial interest in it. The Kushner Companies, now managed by his relatives, are reportedly seeking investment partners for a major redevelopment of this building.

### Bannon's Financial Landscape

Steve Bannon, President Trump's chief strategist, disclosed assets ranging from $13 million to $56 million. This includes his influential political consultancy, Bannon Strategic Advisors Inc., valued at up to $25 million. Bannon also reported earning slightly less than $200,000 in the previous year as executive director of Breitbart News Network LLC before resigning to join the Trump campaign. Further disclosures show he served as vice president of the data firm Cambridge Analytica for over two years, resigning in August 2016 to manage Trump's campaign. Cambridge Analytica was a primary data provider for Senator Ted Cruz during his presidential bid. Bannon's consulting firm received over $125,000 from Cambridge last year, and he holds a stake in the firm valued between $1 million and $5 million, with an "agreement in principle" to sell this investment.

### Other High-Profile Officials' Disclosures

White House Chief of Staff Reince Priebus reported receiving over $500,000 in salary and bonuses from the Republican National Committee, which he led during the presidential primaries. He also earned at least $750,000 from equity buyout and partner-distribution income from the law firm Michael Best & Friedrich. Gary Cohn, who left a prominent role at Goldman Sachs to serve as Trump's chief economic adviser, is among the wealthiest officials. His financial disclosure indicates at least $40 million in income from Goldman Sachs-related dividends, interest, salary, and bonuses, with roughly half in stock compensation. Cohn also reported over $1 million in income from the Industrial and Commercial Bank of China; the White House has stated he is in the process of divesting from both this bank and his Goldman holdings. White House Press Secretary Sean Spicer characterized the business professionals joining the administration as "very blessed and very successful," asserting that the disclosures demonstrate their commitment to public service.

### Conflict of Interest and Divestment

These financial disclosures, mandated by law, provide a snapshot of officials' financial standing upon entering the White House. However, the specific ethics agreements detailing how potential conflicts of interest will be managed are not being publicly released, according to White House lawyers. While "certificates of divestiture" for employees seeking capital gains tax deferrals on sold assets will eventually be accessible, these detailed agreements remain private. Jared Kushner, for example, received such certificates for financial interests in several assets, including funds linked to his brother Joshua Kushner's investment firm, Thrive Capital. Kushner and Ivanka Trump have stepped away from businesses valued at least $50 million each, though they retain financial interests in many of them, similar to the President. Ivanka Trump is set to become a federal employee and will file her own disclosure later.

### Debt and International Dealings

Jared Kushner's disclosure also reveals tens of millions of dollars in bank debt incurred in 2015 and 2016, including liabilities with several international banks whose interests could intersect with the Trump administration's agenda. Kushner has as much as $25 million in liabilities to Deutsche Bank, a significant lender to Kushner family real estate ventures and a major creditor for Trump Organization-branded projects. Additionally, Kushner and his father, Charles, have liabilities up to $5 million with the U.S. unit of Israel Discount Bank. The Kushner Companies have also acknowledged "long-standing" relationships with two other Israeli banks. Financial information for Cabinet members requiring Senate confirmation has largely been available for weeks through the Office of Government Ethics. President Trump is required to file periodic financial disclosures, with his next due next year.

The Trump administration's **financial disclosures** reveal a significant concentration of **personal wealth** among its top officials, a fact that stands in contrast to the "drain the swamp" rhetoric. These documents show that key figures, including Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner, possess substantial assets. Together, Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner may hold financial assets exceeding **$740 million**. White House Chief Strategist Steve Bannon's assets are estimated at **$56 million**. Meanwhile, White House Chief of Staff Reince Priebus reported earning **$1.4 million in 2016**. A notable portion of this income, nearly half, originated from the Republican National Committee.

These disclosures highlight the **considerable financial holdings of President Trump's closest advisors**. This situation naturally raises **ethical questions** regarding these high-ranking administration members. While the White House indicated plans to release additional disclosures, those pertaining to President Trump and Vice President Mike Pence will **not be included** in this initial batch.

White House press secretary Sean Spicer defended the release of these documents. He described the disclosures as **"more accessible, more available than in history."** Spicer addressed President Trump's decision not to release his tax returns, explaining it as a matter of **"apples and oranges."** He clarified that the financial disclosures are **"required by law."** Spicer further emphasized that the administration is **"going above and beyond what has been done in the past"** to ensure public access to this information.

Some view the administration's collective wealth as a **point of pride**. Spicer suggested it should be **"celebrated."** He expressed belief that this demonstrates Trump's capacity to surround himself with **"very blessed and very successful"** individuals. He also noted that their willingness to publicly list their assets and undergo scrutiny, **"set aside a lot,"** signifies their dedication to the president's vision and agenda.

According to USA Today, the White House is preparing to release up to **180 financial disclosures** from its top staffers. These initial releases underscore the **extraordinary wealth** present within the administration. Prominent figures featured include Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner. Other notable individuals with substantial assets are Kellyanne Conway, who possesses a **multimillion-dollar portfolio**, and Gary Cohn, the former CEO of Goldman Sachs now heading Trump's National Economic Council. Dina Habib Powell, another former Goldman executive, is also among them.

Significantly, the disclosures reveal that Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner **continue to profit from businesses they departed** to join the White House. Kushner still benefits from his family's extensive real estate empire. Ivanka Trump retains an ownership stake, valued between **$5 million and $25 million**, in the Trump International Hotel located in Washington, D.C.

Additional details emerge from the disclosures. Kellyanne Conway received payments for consulting work with the Center for Medical Progress. This organization is an anti-abortion group known for its undercover videos targeting Planned Parenthood. Reince Priebus earned **three-quarters of a million dollars** from a Milwaukee law firm. He had been on leave from this firm since 2011.

The financial disclosures also cast doubt on the timeline of Steve Bannon's departure from Breitbart. While the disclosures suggest he resigned in August, aligning with his joining Trump's campaign, Breitbart CEO Larry Solov stated that Bannon resigned **"on or about Nov. 13,"** which was after the election.

The financial disclosures of Trump administration appointees reveal the significant wealth and varied income sources of individuals in top government roles. The White House released about 180 financial disclosure forms, highlighting what many call the wealthiest administration in U.S. history. This influx of personal wealth into public service prompts questions about potential conflicts of interest and the balance between private finances and public duty.

Key figures include Jared Kushner, President Trump's son-in-law and advisor, and his wife, Ivanka Trump. Their extensive 54-page disclosure details numerous assets and debts. Serving in unpaid roles, they aimed to navigate anti-nepotism rules. Kushner gained clearance in January, while Ivanka Trump announced her position later. Together, their disclosed assets exceed $200 million. Despite their government roles, they remain beneficiaries of a real estate and investment business valued up to $741 million. To manage potential conflicts, Kushner divested from dozens of businesses and resigned from over 260 positions. His legal team, with the Office of Government Ethics, determined his New York City real estate holdings likely wouldn't cause conflicts.

Ivanka Trump also holds a stake in the Trump International Hotel in Washington, D.C., valued between $5 million and $25 million. For 2016 and part of 2017, her earnings from this stake ranged from $1 million to $5 million. Her comprehensive disclosures and ethics agreements are expected as she takes on her White House role. She will retain her ownership in the Washington hotel while serving.

Other appointees' finances also draw attention. Steve Bannon, former Breitbart News executive chairman and chief strategist, reported substantial earnings and assets. The Wall Street Journal noted Bannon earned at least $1.4 million before his appointment and held assets between $10.7 million and $48.6 million. His income came from Breitbart News ($191,000), Cambridge Analytica ($125,333), and the Government Accountability Institute ($61,539). These groups are linked to conservative funders Robert and Rebekah Mercer. Bannon's largest income source, over $493,000, was from Bannon Strategic Advisors, Inc., likely an umbrella entity for fees from various interests, including Breitbart, Cambridge Analytica, and Glittering Steel, linked to the "Clinton Cash" documentary.

Gary Cohn, former director of the National Economic Council and ex-president of Goldman Sachs, is among the wealthiest appointees. Bloomberg reported Cohn held at least $254 million in assets and earned at least $48.3 million between 2016 and early 2017. His 2016 Goldman Sachs compensation included a $1.85 million salary and a $5.74 million bonus from 2015. AP and CNBC reported that with dividends and interest from Goldman-affiliated assets, Cohn's earnings from the firm reached at least $40 million. He also received over $1 million in dividends from the Industrial and Commercial Bank of China.

Michael Flynn, former national security adviser, also had financial dealings revealed. An initial disclosure on February 11 listed no payments from Russia-linked groups. Flynn resigned two days later after reports of his meeting with Russia's ambassador and misrepresentations to Vice President Pence. A later disclosure on March 31 included "speaking engagements" with RT TV, Kaspersky Government Security Solutions, Inc., and Volga-Dnepr Airlines, with payments exceeding $5,000. NPR reported these three entities paid Flynn over $50,000 collectively. His more recent disclosure shows at least $1.37 million in income, with $827,055 from his consulting firm, Flynn Intel Group LLC, which had been inactive since December.

These public financial disclosures offer a snapshot of appointees' holdings at the start of their government service. Information on later divestitures or resignations will be released separately. Some appointees were still divesting assets, a process usually completed before taking office. However, the Trump administration announced many appointees before these financial negotiations concluded. The Office of Government Ethics indicates the Trump White House has been slower than the Obama administration in submitting nominees' financial arrangements for review, attributing this to the complex and substantial portfolios of its wealthy appointees. Media estimates place the Trump Cabinet's cumulative wealth between $6 billion and $14 billion.

Federal ethics law mandates the release of these financial disclosure forms. This law requires high-ranking executive branch appointees to reveal their financial holdings and establish formal agreements with ethics officials. These agreements prevent conflicts between personal financial interests and official duties, often requiring asset sales, resignations, or recusal from matters with potential conflicts. The President and Vice President, as elected officials, file disclosures later and are exempt from many staff ethics rules.

New financial disclosures provide a granular view of the personal finances of President Trump's senior White House staff. These filings detail the wealth of approximately 180 individuals, revealing an administration populated by numerous millionaires whose collective net worth surpasses $12 billion. The disclosures also highlight specific financial details, including significant gifts received by a prominent aide and substantial compensation earned by former media professionals.

Here are seven key insights from the financial disclosures:

Omarosa Manigault, a former "Apprentice" contestant and current White House aide, received an estimated $25,000 in merchandise from Kleinfeld Bridal. This was a gift acknowledging her appearance on the TLC program "Say Yes to the Dress."

White House Chief Strategist Stephen Bannon received up to $100,000 in television show royalties. This income derived from a long-standing arrangement involving the production entity behind the 1990s series "Seinfeld" and Bannon's investment firm.

Bannon also garnered over $125,000 in consulting fees from Cambridge Analytica. He previously held the positions of vice president and secretary for the firm, which receives substantial funding from GOP benefactor Robert Mercer.

Kellyanne Conway, Counselor to the President, was compensated more than $5,000 for each of 66 distinct consulting engagements prior to her White House tenure.

Julia Hahn, a key aide to Bannon, earned over $117,000 as a reporter for Breitbart News. Additionally, she generated $74,000 as a producer for conservative radio personality Laura Ingraham. In total, Hahn reported investments valued up to $2.5 million.

KT McFarland, now serving as Trump's deputy national security adviser and formerly a Fox News analyst, reported over $125,000 in income from speaking engagements in the preceding year. These payments contribute to the millions of dollars in income and assets listed by her and her spouse on their filings.

The disclosures further illuminated the considerable wealth of Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner. They are beneficiaries of the Kushner family's real estate enterprise, valued at up to $740 million. Ivanka Trump's personal business trust, encompassing her fashion brand, is valued at over $50 million, according to Kushner's financial disclosure.

This document details the financial disclosures of several individuals associated with the Trump administration, highlighting their reported earnings, assets, and debts. These disclosures, made public as part of government ethics requirements, offer a glimpse into the financial backgrounds of key personnel.

Sebastian Gorka, who previously served as a deputy assistant to President Trump and was an editor at Breitbart News, reported receiving $38,200 in consulting fees from Breitbart. He also disclosed royalties ranging from $50,000 to $100,000 for his book, "Defeating Jihad: The Winnable War." Furthermore, Gorka indicated that he had signed a contract for a second book.

Boris Epshteyn, a prominent figure during the presidential campaign known for his role as a television spokesperson for Mr. Trump, still has outstanding college loans exceeding $50,000. These loans were taken out over a decade ago, according to his financial filing. Epshteyn recently departed his White House position under unspecified circumstances.

Jason Greenblatt, an attorney with the Trump Organization, was tasked with efforts to achieve peace in the Middle East. His financial disclosure shows he earned $1,025,000 in compensation from Mr. Trump's company in the past year.

White House Press Secretary Sean Spicer addressed the financial disclosures at a daily briefing. He characterized the wealth of individuals joining the administration as something to be celebrated. Spicer stated that the President has brought many successful individuals into his administration, particularly the White House. He emphasized that these individuals have made significant sacrifices by setting aside many of their assets to serve in government. Spicer suggested that their willingness to disclose all assets, endure public scrutiny, and relinquish substantial holdings demonstrates their commitment to the President's vision and agenda.

The release of these financial disclosures on a Friday evening aligns with a common practice in Washington D.C. of releasing large volumes of documents late in the week. This timing often occurs when the public is less likely to be actively reviewing such information, drawing a parallel to a quote from the fictional character Josh Lyman, Deputy Chief of Staff on "The West Wing," who remarked, "No one reads the paper on Saturday."

The document also lists several other individuals who have submitted disclosure forms. These include:

*   Stephen K. Bannon, Counselor to the President.
*   Thomas Bossert, Assistant to the President for Homeland Security and Counterterrorism and Deputy National Security Adviser.
*   James W. Carroll, Senior Counsel.
*   Justin Clark, Deputy Assistant to the President and Director of Intergovernmental Affairs.

query: What were Donald Trump's initial ambitions regarding the health care deal, and how did they ultimately end in failure?

documents.text:
Donald Trump's much-touted promise of a "terrific" health care deal dissolved into a brief phone call, where Speaker Paul Ryan delivered the stark reality: the effort was far from successful. The businessman president, who had campaigned on being the outsider capable of fixing a dysfunctional Washington, found himself outmatched and empty-handed in his inaugural legislative confrontation.

"We learned a lot about some very arcane rules," Trump remarked in the Oval Office following the defeat of his plan to dismantle President Obama's signature health care law. The fundamental principle of congressional majority rule proved to be the insurmountable obstacle. Despite a comfortable 22-seat majority in the House, Trump failed to secure sufficient support for the repeal and replace legislation, a central tenet of his campaign platform. This inability to advance a bill through a chamber controlled by his own party, particularly on an issue that has been a Republican priority for over half a decade, raises significant questions about Trump's vaunted dealmaking prowess and the Republican Party's legislative agenda.

"It's a black eye for the speaker and the president," commented Scott Reed, chief strategist for the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, an organization that had endorsed the proposed measure. This setback, detailed by multiple senior administration and congressional officials, was particularly galling as Trump was undermined not by Democratic opposition, but by his own inability to manage the same rebellious faction of the Republican conference that had previously ousted Speaker John Boehner. Many House Republicans, having never served in the majority under a Republican president, are still grappling with how to forge a cohesive governing coalition.

"The Republican Party is still operating as an opposition party," observed Josh Holmes, former chief of staff to Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell. He added, "If they can't break the fever…it says an enormous amount about the prospects of tax reform, infrastructure and some sort of immigration proposal."

Trump himself appeared almost relieved to pivot away from the health care battle, even as White House officials expressed concern over the long-term repercussions of what one senior official termed a "clear embarrassment for us." The president's personal distress seemed more focused on the size of his inauguration crowds, Attorney General Jeff Sessions' recusal from the Russia investigation, and the legal challenges to his travel ban executive order, rather than the legislative defeat. "No bullshit, I think he's actually pretty comfortable with the outcome. He wants to move ahead and do taxes," a different senior White House official stated.

However, tax reform, a complex undertaking that has eluded presidents and Congresses for over three decades, presents its own formidable challenges. The Republican Party had anticipated that deficit reduction from a health care package would provide a financial buffer for tax reform efforts. "Yes," Speaker Ryan conceded on Friday, "this does make tax reform more difficult."

For weeks, Trump had exhibited a seemingly disengaged approach to the health care negotiations, both in private discussions with aides and at public rallies. "Trump just wanted to get something he could sign," revealed an adviser who speaks with him frequently. "He was over it." Advisers and aides noted that he would often steer conversations away from the health care bill to other topics.

During one phone call with Ryan earlier this month, Trump reportedly expressed dissatisfaction with the bill, not due to policy specifics like Medicaid expansion, maternity coverage, deductibles, or insurance premiums, but because he disliked the term "buckets" that Ryan had used to describe components of their plan. "I don't like that word buckets. You throw trash in buckets. I don't like that word," Trump is quoted as saying, according to two individuals familiar with the conversation. He preferred the term "phases," a suggestion Ryan readily adopted. This focus on superficial details mirrored his campaign rhetoric, where he promised "repeal and replace — with something terrific." Yet, by February, Trump confided to a group of visiting governors that dismantling Obamacare would prove more intricate. "Nobody knew healthcare could be so complicated," he admitted.

In the final stages of negotiation, Trump found himself caught between the opposing ideological wings of a deeply divided House Republican conference. Concessions made to the hardline House Freedom Caucus alienated the more moderate Tuesday Group, and vice versa. The White House's arguments for the legislation were primarily political, with Press Secretary Sean Spicer urging lawmakers on Friday, hours before the bill's demise, "This is your chance." Conversely, the arguments against the bill were rooted in policy, an area where Trump appeared less comfortable.

When Trump visited Capitol Hill to advocate for the bill, lawmakers reported that his remarks were largely devoid of substantive health care discussion, focusing instead on the need to vote for it. Representative Walter Jones, R-N.C., remained opposed, stating Trump offered "not a whole lot about health care, except to vote for it." Other lawmakers indicated Trump was not conversant in the bill's specifics and primarily discussed his popularity in their districts or the potential political repercussions of opposing the measure. Ultimately, the proposed law garnered less than 20 percent public support, and the president's approval rating dipped below 40 percent.

Lacking the necessary votes, Trump employed one of his signature deal-making tactics late Thursday: the ultimatum. Trump's top lieutenants informed House Republicans that the bill was a take-it-or-leave-it proposition, representing their sole opportunity to eliminate Obamacare. In the days and hours leading up to the vote, which appeared increasingly precarious, the White House maintained high expectations. Spicer had labeled Trump "the closer." Trump pushed for a floor vote until the very last minute. Senior administration officials insisted late into Friday afternoon that they intended to hold the roll call "to make them vote against the president on live TV," as one official put it. "We are voting," this individual stated just 45 minutes before the vote was ultimately called off.

This approach echoed Trump's own "The Art of the Deal" philosophy, which advocates for boldness coupled with a willingness to retreat. "I never get too attached to one deal or one approach," Trump wrote. "For starters, I keep a lot of balls in the air because most deals fall out." While this strategy might prove effective in the real estate industry, where opportunities are abundant, it proved counterproductive in Congress, where time and political capital are finite resources, and where the consequences of one deal invariably impact subsequent negotiations.

The setback could also exacerbate internal White House friction, where rival factions have frequently clashed. Some observers noted that Trump's son-in-law and senior adviser, Jared Kushner, was in Aspen during the critical period leading up to the vote. Others placed blame on Chief of Staff Reince Priebus, who had a close relationship with Speaker Ryan and was expected to facilitate Trump's navigation of Capitol Hill. Still others faulted Chief Strategist Steve Bannon for failing to leverage his former company, Breitbart News, to support the bill or to rally hardline conservatives in Congress.

The blame game commenced even before the vote was officially withdrawn. "This is 100 percent a Ryan failure," asserted a third White House official. While Trump himself reportedly bore no ill will towards Ryan, numerous advisers and aides were reportedly criticizing the Speaker before the vote, and Ryan himself indicated he anticipated bearing the brunt of the blame. By midday Friday, the health care bill was experiencing a significant erosion of support, with the influential chairman of the House Appropriations Committee announcing his opposition.

Speaker Ryan met with Trump at the White House to apprise him of the dire outlook. Despite Trump and his top advisers' initial desire to proceed, signs of concern began to emerge. Spicer no longer used the term "the closer." GOP leadership pushed to abandon what was now perceived as a futile endeavor. Shortly after 3 p.m., Trump spoke with Ryan again, following a 45-minute conversation the previous Thursday night. "He talked to Paul Ryan for a few minutes, who said he was at least 10 and 15 votes short," one senior White House official recounted. Ryan indicated he intended to pull the vote unless Trump objected, to which Trump agreed. Ryan subsequently explained the implications to a national television audience: "We're going to be living with Obamacare for the foreseeable future."

Upon ending the call, Trump jotted down some notes and contacted reporters to present his perspective before the full White House staff had been briefed. The president's primary concern throughout the process, and even after the vote's cancellation, was the media coverage and its reflection on him, as he frequently expressed to advisers his displeasure with the televised criticism of the law.

In the Capitol basement, following the vote's cancellation, Republican lawmakers convened to discuss the path forward. The Rolling Stones song Trump often played at his rallies, "You can't always get what you want," served as an ironic soundtrack to the gathering. Tara Palmeri and Jake Sherman contributed to this report.

## Republican Healthcare Overhaul Stalls: AHCA Pulled Amidst Deep Party Divisions

Republicans faced a significant legislative setback on Friday, withdrawing their proposed American Health Care Act (AHCA) from House consideration. After weeks of intense negotiations aimed at repealing and replacing the Affordable Care Act (ACA), the party conceded failure, unable to garner sufficient internal support for the ambitious overhaul of the U.S. health insurance system. This dramatic turn of events underscored profound divisions within the Republican party and the immense difficulty of legislating complex policy areas like healthcare.

### Shifting Blame and Legislative Priorities

President Donald Trump, speaking from the Oval Office, attributed the bill's collapse to Democrats, characterizing the ACA as "the signature achievement of Barack Obama." He expressed openness to bipartisan collaboration, stating, "If [Democrats] got together with us, and got us a real healthcare bill, I’d be totally OK with that." Trump then squarely blamed Democratic leaders, declaring, "The losers are Nancy Pelosi and Chuck Schumer, because they own Obamacare. They 100% own it." While avoiding criticism of House Speaker Paul Ryan, the President pivoted from health reform discussions to the administration's next legislative focus: tax reform. "We’re probably going to start going very strongly on big tax cuts. Tax reform that will be next," he announced, adding a reflective comment, "We all learned a lot. We learned a lot about loyalty."

### The Path to Withdrawal

Earlier that day, as opposition within the Republican party solidified, Speaker Paul Ryan informed President Trump that the bill lacked the necessary votes for passage. White House Press Secretary Sean Spicer had, until that point, maintained that the vote would proceed as scheduled. He told reporters, "Has the team put everything out there, have we left everything on the field? Absolutely. But at the end of the day this isn’t a dictatorship and we’ve got to expect members to ultimately vote how they will according to what they think." However, the 3:30 PM ET deadline passed without a vote being called. Subsequently, reports indicated that President Trump had requested the bill be pulled. Minutes later, House Republicans officially announced the withdrawal of the legislation.

### Acknowledging Governing Challenges

At a subsequent press conference, Speaker Ryan candidly acknowledged the inherent difficulties of transitioning from an opposition party to a governing party. "Moving from an opposition party to a governing party comes with growing pains and, well, we’re feeling those growing pains today. I will not sugarcoat this: this is a disappointing day for us," he stated. He admitted that after nearly a decade of campaigning against the ACA, Republicans had failed to coalesce around a viable alternative. "We are going to be living with Obamacare for the foreseeable future," Ryan conceded. He explained that he had recommended the bill's withdrawal upon realizing the vote count was insufficient, while also commending President Trump's efforts, describing him as "fantastic." When pressed on how Republicans would address constituents after failing to deliver on their central campaign promise, Ryan responded with a candid, "That’s a really good question. I wish I had a better answer for you."

### Shifting Focus to Other Priorities

A reporter from *The Washington Post* later recounted a conversation with President Trump, during which the President indicated that the healthcare bill would not be revisited in the immediate future. Speaker Ryan also confirmed that Republicans would be shifting their focus to other key priorities, including securing the border, rebuilding the military, and enacting tax reform. "Now we’re going on to move on with the rest of our agenda because we have big, ambitious plans to improve people’s lives in this country," Ryan stated.

### Surprise and Internal Disagreement

Despite considerable speculation, the bill's withdrawal came as a surprise to many Republican members. An emergency House Republican Caucus meeting had been convened shortly before the scheduled vote. As this meeting commenced, the House entered a recess, with Democratic members audibly chanting "Vote, vote, vote" in an effort to pressure Republicans. Speaker Ryan then emerged to announce the bill's withdrawal in a brief statement to the press.

### Key Obstacles to Passage

A significant factor contributing to the bill's demise was the hesitation of many moderate Republicans representing swing districts. These members expressed concern over the substantial cuts to Medicaid proposed in the AHCA. The non-partisan Congressional Budget Office had estimated that these cuts would lead to approximately 24 million fewer Americans having health insurance over a ten-year period. Conversely, conservative Republicans objected to the bill for what they perceived as its retention of too much of the ACA's existing structure. Despite a concession made on Thursday to remove the federal mandate for essential health benefits, such as maternity and mental healthcare, this adjustment failed to sway enough conservative votes. Nevada Republican Mark Amodei highlighted a critical deficiency, noting a lack of discussion on a "unified Republican vision for what we should do with healthcare in the House." Paul Gosar, a member of the hard-right Freedom Caucus, attributed the setback to issues with White House staff.

### Political Ramifications and Reactions

The failure to pass the AHCA represented a major political blow to Speaker Paul Ryan, who had spearheaded the legislative effort. It also placed President Trump in a vulnerable position, having made the repeal of the "disaster" of Obamacare a cornerstone of his campaign. Despite his public persona as a skilled dealmaker, Trump's first significant legislative negotiation on Capitol Hill concluded in defeat. House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi hailed the outcome as "a great day for our country" and "a victory for the American people." Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer attributed the bill's failure to "incompetence and broken promises," delivering a sharp critique: "In my life, I have never seen an administration as incompetent as the one occupying the White House today. They can’t write policy that actually makes sense, they can’t implement the policies they do manage to write, they can’t get their stories straight, and today we’ve learned that they can’t close a deal, and they can’t count votes. So much for The Art of the Deal."

### Varied Interpretations and Future Uncertainty

Republican members offered varied interpretations of the events. Louie Gohmert of Texas, who had opposed the bill, blamed House leadership for cornering members and the President. "The president didn’t really get involved until after they created this bill and he was fighting for it," Gohmert remarked. Bradley Byrne of Alabama expressed continued willingness to support the AHCA, lamenting that House Republicans "fell just short" and praising the efforts of both Ryan and Trump. Some Republicans voiced uncertainty about the future of healthcare reform. Gohmert suggested that while he would not prioritize healthcare if he were president, as a legislator, the issue still required a correct resolution. Mark Walker, chair of the Republican Study Committee, acknowledged the setback, stating, "I can’t pretend that this is a win for us. I’m sure our friends on the left, this is a good moment for them."

President Donald Trump about the health care overhaul bill, Friday, March 24, 2017, in the Oval Office of the White House in Washington. rump says he would be willing to reopen negotiations for a health... (Associated Press) President Donald Trump about the health care overhaul bill, Friday, March 24, 2017, in the Oval Office of the White House in Washington. rump says he would be willing to reopen negotiations for a health... (Associated Press) WASHINGTON (AP) — Just two months in, Donald Trump's presidency is perilously adrift. His first major foray into legislating imploded Friday when House Republicans abandoned a White House-backed health care bill, resisting days of cajoling and arm-twisting from Trump himself. Aides who had confidently touted Trump as the deal's "closer" were left bemoaning the limits of the presidency. "At the end of the day, you can't force somebody to do something," White House spokesman Sean Spicer said. On its own, the health care bill's collapse was a stunning rejection of a new president by his own party. And for Trump, the defeat comes with an especially strong sting. The president who campaigned by promising "so much winning," has so far been beset by a steady parade of the opposite. With each setback and sidetrack, comes more concern about whether Trump, the outsider turned president, is capable of governing. "You can't just come in and steamroll everybody," said Bruce Miroff, a professor of American politics and the presidency at the State University of New York at Albany. "Most people have a modest understanding of how complicated the presidency is. They think leadership is giving orders and being bold. But the federal government is much more complicated, above all because the Constitution set it up that way." The ambitious agenda Trump vowed to quickly muscle through has now been blocked by both Congress and the courts. Whole weeks of his presidency have been consumed by crises that are often self-inflicted, including his explosive and unverified claim that President Barack Obama wiretapped his New York skyscraper. Earlier this week, the FBI director confirmed that Trump's campaign is being investigated for possible coordination with Russia during the election, an investigation that could hang over the White House for years. Trump's advisers say some of the churn is to be expected from a president with an unconventional style and little regard for Washington convention. They counter the notion of a White House in crisis by pointing to Trump's well-received nomination of Neil Gorsuch to the Supreme Court. They appeal for patience, noting that the administration is indeed in its early days. But early missteps can be difficult to overcome, particularly for a president like Trump, who took office with historically low favorability ratings and has continued to lose support since his inauguration. According to Friday's Gallup daily tracking poll, 54 percent of Americans disapprove of his performance on the job. James Thurber, who founded the Center for Congressional and Presidential Studies at American University, blamed Trump for an apparent "misunderstanding or ignorance of how the separation of powers works" that is hurting him at a time "where he should have much more success." Trump is hardly the first president to stumble through his early days in arguably one of the hardest jobs in the world. Bill Clinton's presidency got off to a chaotic start and was quickly shrouded by an ethics controversy involving the firing of employees at the White House travel office. Jimmy Carter, another Washington outsider, clashed with his own party. Richard Nixon struggled to unite a deeply divided nation. "There have been moments like this in history in terms of where the country is, in terms of the president kind of having a chaotic couple of months," said David Greenberg, a historian at Rutgers University. Still, he said Trump's challenges are exacerbated by his "complete inexperience in the political arena, his personality and style." Indeed, many of Trump's early struggles have been clearly avoidable. His courting of chaos undercuts his campaign promises to bring business efficiency to Washington. Infighting and gamesmanship among top White House aides, sometimes egged on by the president himself, consume a striking amount of attention in the West Wing. His wiretapping allegations against Obama put his advisers in the untenable position of trying to justify an allegation for which there is no evidence. His first, hastily written ban on travel to the U.S. from several countries was blocked by the courts, as was a rewrite, and judges cited his own campaign rhetoric in their rulings. The president's advisers had hoped the health care measure would give the White House a much-needed burst of momentum and prove to wary Republicans that it was worth sticking with Trump in order to accomplish something the party had sought for seven years. Hardly a policy wonk, Trump embraced the plan put forth by House Speaker Paul Ryan and promised Republican leaders he would invest his own political capital into rounding up votes. He did, spending hours working the phones with lawmakers, often early in the morning and late at night. His advisers held bowling and pizza nights for GOP lawmakers at the White House. But Ryan's decision to pull the bill Friday underscored Trump's limitations. His powers of persuasion couldn't overcome the ideological concerns of conservatives who are more popular in their home districts than Trump or the political fears of moderates worried about attaching themselves to an unpopular president. Trump, who has privately lashed out at his staff and publicly berated the media during other rough patches in his young presidency, was surprisingly sanguine in defeat. "We learned a lot about the vote-getting processes," Trump said. "For me, it's been a very interesting experience." ___ EDITOR'S NOTE: Julie Pace has covered the White House and politics for The Associated Press since 2007. Follow her at http://twitter.com/jpaceDC ___ This story has been corrected to change Wednesday to Friday in 2nd paragraph.

The Republican effort to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act (ACA) experienced a significant legislative setback this week. The proposed bill, a cornerstone promise of President Donald Trump and Speaker Paul Ryan, was ultimately withdrawn from House consideration on Friday, averting a certain defeat. Speaker Ryan subsequently acknowledged that the ACA, often referred to as "ObamaCare," would likely remain the law of the land "for the foreseeable future," indicating no immediate plans for another legislative push. This outcome represents a pivotal moment for several key political figures, with distinct winners and losers emerging from the ensuing political fallout.

**LOSERS**

**President Donald Trump:** This legislative failure represents a substantial blow to President Trump, whose campaign was built on promises of decisive action and effective deal-making. His inaugural major legislative initiative faltered, despite his direct appeals to wavering Republican members. The precise impact on his broader agenda and the potential for Republican frustration directed at him remain uncertain. While Trump projected an image of resilience, his comment regarding the ACA's persistence – "there’s not much you can do about it" – from a president commanding congressional majorities, underscored a palpable sense of powerlessness. The full ramifications of this setback will continue to unfold over time.

**Speaker Paul Ryan (R-Wis.):** The unsuccessful healthcare bill is as damaging to Speaker Ryan as it is to the President. His inability to shepherd the American Health Care Act through the House constitutes a profound embarrassment. Ryan was deeply invested in the legislation, which encountered formidable opposition from conservative members within his own party and influential interest groups. Some Trump loyalists suggest that Ryan erred strategically by prioritizing healthcare over tax reform. Conservative media figures have begun questioning his leadership capabilities. Despite receiving public support from President Trump, Ryan's defeat highlights a struggle to maintain sufficient party discipline.

**Vice President Pence, Health and Human Services Secretary Tom Price, and OMB Director Mick Mulvaney:** These former House members were anticipated to leverage their insider knowledge to influence the ultra-conservative House Freedom Caucus and other lawmakers. Mick Mulvaney, a founding member of the caucus, was particularly well-positioned to sway votes. However, their collective efforts proved insufficient to secure the necessary support, resulting in a notable political setback for each individual.

**MIXED**

**The House Freedom Caucus:** This conservative bloc successfully blocked the Republican healthcare bill, but the long-term implications of this victory remain uncertain. Under the leadership of Rep. Mark Meadows (R-N.C.), the caucus resisted presidential pressure. Their unified stance was instrumental in the bill's failure, demonstrating their considerable influence. Nevertheless, they now face the challenge of navigating the consequences of opposing a president from their own party, who enjoys strong support among many grassroots Republicans. Furthermore, their strategy of compelling leadership to propose a more accommodating alternative has not yet yielded tangible results.

**WINNERS**

**Former President Barack Obama:** The survival of his signature domestic policy, the ACA, represents a significant victory. This occurred despite unified control of the White House and Congress by those who had pledged to dismantle it. While Trump suggested the ACA would collapse on its own, he did not commit to further repeal efforts, instead indicating an openness to incremental reforms with Democratic collaboration. The ACA's continued existence solidifies Obama's presidential legacy.

**House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi (D-Calif.):** Pelosi effectively showcased remarkable party unity, a stark contrast to the evident Republican division. Not a single Democrat broke ranks to support the Republican plan, thereby creating a substantial legislative obstacle. Pelosi, a seasoned political strategist, appeared to relish the Republicans' internal struggles, famously remarking it a "Rookie’s error, Donald Trump" following the initial vote delay.

**Jared Kushner:** Trump's son-in-law and a key advisor, Kushner reportedly opposed the immediate focus on healthcare legislation. His absence from Washington for a significant portion of the week, attending a family ski trip, kept him physically and politically distanced from the contentious negotiations. Consequently, he emerged from this legislative failure relatively unscathed.

**Governors:** Several Republican governors voiced strong criticism of the House replacement plan, citing concerns regarding its potential impact on Medicaid funding. Governors such as John Kasich (Ohio), Brian Sandoval (Nevada), Asa Hutchinson (Arkansas), and Rick Snyder (Michigan) co-signed a letter to congressional leaders expressing their opposition to the bill. Their stance provided crucial political cover for lawmakers in their respective states who were hesitant to endorse the legislation.

**The AARP:** This organization, representing older Americans, actively campaigned against the ACA repeal bill. They characterized a proposed change as an "age tax," highlighted the projected decline in health insurance coverage, and framed the vote as an "accountability vote," signaling their intention to oppose lawmakers who voted against their interests. The AARP's successful advocacy efforts underscored its considerable influence in shaping public opinion and legislative outcomes.

The dramatic collapse of the American Health Care Act unfolded over a tense 24-hour period, a political crucible that White House and congressional officials described as a significant learning experience for President Trump and his senior advisors. This account draws on insights from government officials who participated in the final negotiations and spoke on the condition of anonymity.

### A Political Education

"We all learned a lot," President Trump remarked to reporters on Friday afternoon. He elaborated, "We learned a lot about loyalty, and we learned a lot about the vote-getting process." The President acknowledged, "Certainly for me, it was a very interesting experience." While initially having limited involvement in the bill's creation, Mr. Trump became increasingly invested in recent weeks. He actively promoted the legislation at rallies outside Washington and convened meetings in the West Wing with both conservative and moderate factions, whose support was deemed essential for its passage.

### Shifting Priorities and Falling Short

Despite his efforts, Mr. Trump expressed a degree of ambivalence about prioritizing healthcare reform, stating in Nashville the previous week, "I would have loved to have put it first, I’ll be honest," referring to tax reform. Nevertheless, he conveyed to his aides his belief that the healthcare measure could not succeed without his direct advocacy. By Thursday afternoon, mere hours before a scheduled vote, it became evident that the combined efforts of the President, Vice President Mike Pence, and other senior White House officials had not secured sufficient support.

### The Freedom Caucus Standoff

A pivotal meeting in Speaker Paul Ryan's Capitol office with members of the resistant Freedom Caucus highlighted the deepening divisions. Top White House officials presented the concessions made at the group's request, including the removal of federal standards for essential health benefits such as maternity and wellness care. However, Freedom Caucus members proceeded to articulate further demands, sparking frustration among Speaker Ryan and President Trump's aides.

Stephen K. Bannon, the President's chief strategist, and Mick Mulvaney, his budget director, informed the caucus that negotiations had concluded and that the President required a definitive stance – yes or no. When called upon to declare their positions, several members declined. As the tension escalated, Representative Mark Meadows of North Carolina stated he was speaking for the group and that they were not yet prepared to commit, signaling that the necessary votes would not materialize.

President Trump addressed his plan to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act, saying he will let Obamacare "explode," before taking questions from the media on March 24 at the White House. (The Washington Post) Donald Trump was elected in large part on one, loud promise: I know how to make deals these normal politicians don't. Part of that mystique — as outlined in his best-selling book “The Art of the Deal” — is the willingness to call his rival's bluff, to put his cards on the table and ask everyone else to do the same. That's what Trump did Thursday night after a postponement of the planned vote to begin the process of reforming the Affordable Care Act. House Republicans needed to put up or shut up, Trump insisted. Despite being told the votes simply weren't there, Trump pushed forward — arguing that it was now or never. It was vintage Trump, taking a gamble no other typical politician would take: Force a vote on a massive part of your legislative agenda with an uncertain outcome. Then Trump blinked. Hours after Trump huddled with Speaker Paul D. Ryan at the White House, he told The Post's Bob Costa that he had decided congressional Republicans should pull the legislation. The votes simply weren't there, and the possibility of real embarrassment on the House floor existed. Sure. But, Trump was elected as an outsider — someone who ran against the system. Why not force that system to go on the record with either their support or opposition? I regret that @POTUS @realDonaldTrump and @SpeakerRyan agreed to pull the bill. Accountability for both sides of argument useful. — Hugh Hewitt (@hughhewitt) March 24, 2017 Now begins the blame game. And there's plenty to go around. The White House was already starting to point the finger at Ryan for making health-care reform the first legislative priority of the new Republican-controlled Washington. Rank and file members were suggesting that simply not enough time was given to thinking about what was in the bill before it was offered. Establishment Republicans blamed the House Freedom Caucus for their refusal to compromise. House Speaker Paul Ryan (R-Wis.) spoke about the decision to withdraw the GOP health-care plan on March 24. "We're going to be living with Obamacare for the foreseeable future," he said. (The Washington Post) The House Freedom Caucus just single-handedly saved #Obamacare. — Adam Kinzinger (@RepKinzinger) March 24, 2017 All true! But Trump is the president of the United States. Trump ran as the only person who could solve the major problems facing the country. Trump was the one who billed himself as the dealmaker extraordinaire, the guy who had faced down people in corporate boardrooms all over the country and all over the world — and won. But when the time came to push all his chips to the middle of the table, Trump folded. Period. Beyond the spin, that is what happened here. If Trump had continued to insist that the bill be put to a vote, trust me that Ryan would have done it despite his reservations. What you are likely to hear over the next few hours and days is that Trump did so because no deal is better than a bad deal. The problem with that argument is Trump's Twitter paper trail. Friday, as the House prepared to vote, Trump tweeted that this legislation was a great chance for Republicans to make good on their campaign promises — calling the legislation a “great plan.” After seven horrible years of ObamaCare (skyrocketing premiums & deductibles, bad healthcare), this is finally your chance for a great plan! — Donald J. Trump (@realDonaldTrump) March 24, 2017 Trump will, as he always does, somehow declare victory and move on. “The beauty,” Trump told Costa, “is that they [Democrats] own Obamacare. So when it explodes, they come to us, and we make one beautiful deal for the people.” The simple fact, however, is that Trump wanted this deal, pushed for this deal, called his own party's bluff on this deal and then walked away when it appeared as though the deal wouldn't come together. Dealmaker Donald played chicken. But he lost his nerve at the last minute.

### Republican Health Care Overhaul Bill Pulled from House Floor Amidst Vote Shortage

House Speaker Paul Ryan, R-Wis., announced on Friday, March 24, 2017, that the Republican health care overhaul bill was being abruptly withdrawn from the House floor. The decision came as the bill lacked sufficient votes, and leadership sought to avoid a significant and embarrassing defeat for President Donald Trump and Republican leaders.

#### Years of Opposition Culminate in Collapse

For six years, House Republicans had passed approximately 60 bills aimed at dismantling President Barack Obama's signature health care law. However, these efforts were largely symbolic, knowing that with Obama in the White House, such measures would never become law. This time, with a Republican president and Congress, the party faced its first genuine opportunity to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act (ACA). The bill, intended to eliminate the individual mandate, expand Medicaid, and trim federal subsidies for medical expenses, represented the culmination of seven years of unsuccessful attempts by the GOP to unite behind a replacement plan.

#### Internal Divisions and External Opposition Doom the Bill

The legislation ultimately faltered due to a fractured Republican caucus. Centrist Republicans believed the bill went too far in its proposed changes, while conservatives argued it was not sufficiently aggressive in repealing the ACA. This internal division, coupled with unified Democratic opposition, proved insurmountable.

Adding to the pressure, nonpartisan congressional analysts projected that the bill would lead to 24 million people losing health coverage within a decade and increase costs for lower-income and older individuals. This assessment was echoed by opposition from various groups, including doctors, hospitals, consumer advocacy organizations, and AARP.

#### Unfulfilled Promises and Political Fallout

The failure to deliver on a central campaign promise to repeal and replace the ACA presented a significant political challenge for Republicans. For nearly a decade, candidates had pledged to dismantle Obama's law, a commitment that was a cornerstone of President Trump's presidential campaign. When asked about facing constituents after this setback, Speaker Ryan admitted, "I don't know how long it's going to take us to repeal this law," and acknowledged the difficulty of providing a satisfactory answer.

#### Celebrations and Future Uncertainty

Democrats, staunch defenders of the ACA, reacted with visible elation. House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi, D-Calif., was seen celebrating the bill's demise. The ACA, credited with extending coverage to 20 million people, mandates that insurers cover essential services and prohibits them from denying coverage to individuals with pre-existing conditions.

Top congressional Republicans acknowledged the need to shift focus to other legislative priorities. Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell, R-Ky., expressed his disappointment, stating, "Obamacare is failing the American people and I deeply appreciate the efforts of the speaker and the president to keep our promise to repeal and replace it."

Some House authors of the bill indicated a desire to move on. Rep. Greg Walden, R-Ore., chairman of the House Energy and Commerce Committee, declared, "D-O-N-E done. This bill is dead." Rep. Kevin Brady, R-Texas, chairman of the House Ways and Means Committee, stated that Republicans would now concentrate on tax reform.

Despite the immediate setback, some expressed hope for alternative solutions. Sen. Bill Cassidy, R-La., suggested that his bipartisan proposal with Sen. Susan Collins, R-Maine, remained a viable option to fulfill the promise of repealing and replacing the ACA. However, the path forward remains complex, as reforming the nation's healthcare system, which constitutes one-sixth of the U.S. economy, involves intricate economic and political considerations. Striking a balance between conservative desires for a free-market approach and the concerns of moderates about coverage losses and increased costs presents a formidable challenge.

Ironically, the bill's failure mirrored predictions made by former Speaker John Boehner, R-Ohio, who was largely ousted by the hard-right House Freedom Caucus—the same group whose opposition contributed to the current bill's collapse. Boehner had previously stated that a full repeal and replacement of the ACA was unlikely, noting that Republicans "never ever agree on health care."

Donald Trump had heard enough about policy and process. It was Thursday afternoon and members of the House Freedom Caucus were peppering the president with wonkish concerns about the American Health Care Act—the language that would leave Obamacare’s “essential health benefits” in place, the community rating provision that limited what insurers could charge certain patients, and whether the next two steps of Speaker Paul Ryan’s master plan were even feasible—when Trump decided to cut them off. "Forget about the little shit," Trump said, according to multiple sources in the room. "Let's focus on the big picture here." Story Continued Below The group of roughly 30 House conservatives, gathered around a mammoth, oval-shaped conference table in the Cabinet Room of the White House, exchanged disapproving looks. Trump wanted to emphasize the political ramifications of the bill's defeat; specifically, he said, it would derail his first-term agenda and imperil his prospects for reelection in 2020. The lawmakers nodded and said they understood. And yet they were disturbed by his dismissiveness. For many of the members, the "little shit" meant the policy details that could make or break their support for the bill—and have far-reaching implications for their constituents and the country. "We’re talking about one-fifth of our economy," a member told me afterward. Ultimately, the meeting failed to move any votes. Two Freedom Caucus members—Brian Babin and Ted Poe, both of Texas—told the president that they had switched to yes, but their decisions had already been registered with White House vote-counters prior to sitting down with Trump. (Their colleagues didn't appreciate the gesture, feeling that Babin and Poe were trying to score points with the president at their expense.) Upon returning to Capitol Hill, the Freedom Caucus gathered in a meeting room inside the Rayburn office building, discussed Trump's admonitions to them and took another vote. The tally had not changed: Of the group’s roughly three dozen members, two-thirds remained opposed, with only five or six of those saying they were "soft" in that stance. The president had been working on many of them individually in recent days, typically with what members described as "colorful" phone calls, littered with exaggerations and foul language and hilariously off-topic anecdotes. In some cases, the pressure worked. Jim Bridenstine, a Freedom Caucus member and longtime problem for the Republican leadership, agreed to back the bill after conversations with Trump and other administration officials. (It wasn't necessary to remind Bridenstine that he was a leading candidate to become NASA administrator, and would likely hurt his chances by voting against the president.) But by and large, Trump's first attempt to corral the Republican-controlled Congress—and particularly the Freedom Caucus, a rambunctious, ideologically charged collection of GOP legislators who have long refused to fall in line behind the party's leadership—failed miserably. That failure played a major role in the collapse of the American Health Care Act almost exactly 24 hours after their meeting at the White House, and now, as Trump warned, threatens to paralyze the president's first-year policy agenda and send Republicans into a damaging cycle of intra-party recrimination. By and large, Trump’s first attempt to corral the GOP Congress failed miserably and threatens to paralyze his first-year policy agenda. There will be sufficient blame to go around in the days ahead, and indeed, some Trump loyalists are already pointing the finger at Ryan and his leadership team. The speaker, without question, was clumsy in his strategic rollout and far too presumptuous about the legislation's infallibility. But for a president who pledged to break through the gridlock in Washington—and who promoted himself as a peerless dealmaker—the defeat of Trump's first major policy initiative undermines his take-charge image and emboldens his enemies in both parties. Tom Price, the new Health and Human Services secretary who previously served 12 years in Congress, had assured nervous allies recently that the difference between Ryan's speakership and that of Speaker John Boehner—who was driven from office by the intransigence of the Freedom Caucus—was that unlike his predecessor, Ryan had the backing of a strong Republican president. Mick Mulvaney, Trump's budget director and a former Freedom Caucus member himself, made similar arguments. But faced with his first major test, the president failed—on multiple occasions and on many levels. For starters, Trump kept the GOP health care bill at arm's length for more than a week, offering a smattering of favorable remarks but failing to embrace it in convincing fashion. Ryan's rivals on Capitol Hill got the message: The president was lukewarm about the legislation. According to interviews with officials in all three camps—the White House, the Republican leadership and the Freedom Caucus—conservatives saw a schism between Trump and Ryan, and seized on the perceived opening. Early last week, during a budgetary meeting at the White House, the two leaders of the Freedom Caucus—chairman Mark Meadows and former chairman Jim Jordan—kept diverting the discussion to health care, much to the annoyance of Budget Committee chairwoman Diane Black. When the meeting broke, Meadows and Jordan swiftly sought an audience with the president to discuss Ryan's bill. Trump granted them the meeting, during which the conservatives complained that Ryan was presenting them with a "binary choice"—either vote for the bill that had been introduced, or vote to preserve Obamacare—that was doing the president a disservice. Trump replied that he was open to negotiation and new ideas, and Meadows and Jordan left the White House thinking they had a powerful ally. Ryan’s team was less than thrilled at the narrative of a good cop, bad cop routine. Things went downhill quickly from there. After Meadows visited Trump at Mar-a-Lago, the president’s Florida retreat, that weekend to push for conservative changes—capping a week of futile discussions about significantly altering the House bill—Freedom Caucus members were eager to hear from Trump on Tuesday when he arrived at the Capitol. But when he rose to address the GOP conference, the president made it clear there would be no further modifications, and said he expected Republicans to rally around Ryan's bill. Then Trump made a mistake. After singling out Meadows and asking him to stand up in front of his colleagues, Trump joked that he might "come after" the Freedom Caucus boss if he didn't vote yes, and then added, with a more serious tone: "I think Mark Meadows will get on board." It was a crucial misreading of Meadows, who has been determined to please both the White House and his conservatives colleagues on the Hill. Upon assuming the chairmanship of the Freedom Caucus earlier this year, Meadows was viewed suspiciously by some of his members who worried that the North Carolina congressman is too cozy with Trump and would hesitate to defy him. Meadows campaigned extensively with Trump last fall and struck up a relationship with White House chief strategist Steve Bannon, who communicates with him almost daily by text. Meadows knew the health care fight would be viewed as a test of his independence from Trump, and the moment the president called him out, he was boxed in. "That was the biggest mistake the president could have made," one Freedom Caucus member told me. "Mark desperately wanted to get to yes, and Trump made it impossible for him. If he flipped after that he would look incredibly weak." That was the biggest mistake the president could have made. Mark desperately wanted to get to yes, and Trump made it impossible for him.” Disappointed with the episode, and with the president's apparent inflexibility, Meadows and other Freedom Caucus members back-channeled with the administration and landed what they thought was an invitation to the White House on Wednesday morning. They hoped for a meeting with Trump and an opportunity to negotiate some major policy changes directly with him. Instead, they found themselves hauled into the less-than-inspiring Executive Office Building for a pep rally with Vice President Mike Pence, chief of staff Reince Priebus, Bannon, and other members of Trump's inner circle—but not the president himself. (As an aside, it’s impossible to ignore the failure of Pence, Price and Mulvaney, three former conservative darlings while in the Congress, to sell more of their ideological brethren on this bill.) Members of the Freedom Caucus realized right away that there would be no negotiating. Pence tried to pump up the conservatives, telling them the fight was theirs to win and that they needed to help Trump and Ryan score a victory for the new administration. The plea landed on deaf ears. "Take one for the team" was a phrase repeatedly deployed; at one point, after Bannon used it, Joe Barton, a white-haired conservative from Texas, snapped back in response that Bannon was talking to them like children and he didn't appreciate it. The room filled with uncomfortable silence; Bannon backed down and the meeting went on. (Barton eventually announced his support for the legislation—one of only a few who would eventually switch positions.) After several hours, the members returned to the Capitol feeling frustrated, calling the meeting "a waste of time" and wondering if they had missed their only window for cutting a deal with Trump. That night, however, allies in the White House sent word to the Freedom Caucus that one thing they wanted—reforms to the “essential health benefits” provision under Title I of the Affordable Care Act—could be done. Excitement spread in the group, but there was also confusion; some members felt that would be a big enough concession to win their vote, while others felt it was only a step in the right direction. As they sought to clarify their internal disagreements, there was another meeting scheduled for the next morning, Thursday—this one at the White House and with the president himself. Filled with hope once again, Freedom Caucus members were once again promptly disappointed. This meeting was yet another "take one for the team" seminar. The atmosphere was friendly, and the president had the group laughing with irrelevant riffs and stories of negotiations past, but it became clear, as soon as he made the "little shit" comment, that no serious changes were going to be made, because the president didn't have sufficient command of the policy details to negotiate what would or would not be realistic for Ryan to shepherd through the House. Through charm, force of personality and sheer intimidation, Trump did move some votes into the yes column. But GOP leaders were left wondering why he didn't do more—why he didn't send tweets, travel to congressional districts, put his famed dealmaking skills to work. The answer, to Republicans on both ends of Pennsylvania Avenue, is obvious: Because he lacked familiarity with the legislation itself, and thought it was Ryan’s job to sell the specifics. “Trump is a business executive. When he tells his lieutenants to get something done, he's used to it getting done,” one senior House GOP aide told me. “He’s really not used to getting involved himself.” *** If the bill failed because Trump is a great salesman with a poor grasp of policy, it also failed because Ryan is a poor salesman with a great grasp of policy. The speaker has spent decades straddling the worlds of politics and policy, and is infinitely more comfortable operating in the latter. He has dozens of friends around town in the constellation of conservative think tanks, lobby shops, activist groups and media outlets. Knowing that health care was batting leadoff for the new, unified Republican government, it would seem a no-brainer for the speaker to spend a few days, if not a few weeks, meeting with leading voices on the right to introduce the American Health Care Act, answer their questions, accept their criticisms and, most important, preempt any attacks on the legislation itself. After all, as Democrats love to point out, Ryan had seven years to plan for this moment—first as Budget chairman, then as Ways and Means chairman, then as speaker—and if anyone on the right was ready, it ought to have been him.
