But Ryan didn’t feel such preventative measures were necessary. After days of drafting the bill in secretive locations at the Capitol—and Sen. Rand Paul, a hard-core Obamacare critic, exposing the absurdity by bringing reporters along as he hunted door-to-door for a copy—the text was leaked, and then unceremoniously released, without any clearly coordinated media strategy between the speaker's office and the White House. Conservatives around Washington, including some of Ryan’s longtime friends, were stunned. “The bill has had the worst rollout of any major piece of legislation in memory,” Rich Lowry, editor of National Review and a longtime Ryan ally, wrote in his Politico Magazine column on March 15. Back in 2013, when the so-called Gang of Eight had authored its comprehensive immigration reform bill, Sen. Marco Rubio spent weeks making the rounds and meeting with top influencers on the right, taking unlimited time to answer every question and consider every criticism. He talked to journalists, grassroots leaders and academics; he offered himself as a human sacrifice to every prominent voice in conservative talk radio, attempting to neutralize opposition to the bill before it materialized. It never became law, but Rubio did everything he could. It passed the Senate, at least, before dying a quick death in the House—and that was in large measure thanks to having a media-savvy Tea Party darling take the lead and work conservative journalists and opinion leaders. There was no such effort on Ryan’s part, and it showed. (Several allies argued he had done some outreach, but they failed to provide any specific examples.) After he unveiled the bill, leading health care experts on the right like Yuval Levin and Avik Roy trashed it as a poorly conceived mess; conservative pressure groups and their media allies immediately branded it as “Obamacare Lite.” Only then did Ryan move aggressively to mitigate the damage. He convened a group of conservative journalists in his office in mid-March and his team began tracking, and publicizing, every media appearance he made, especially promoting his interviews with conservative critics such as Sean Hannity and Laura Ingraham. But it was too little, too late. Loathing of the legislation had already reached a fever pitch and Ryan was powerless to steady the situation. The only thing he could have done to appease the far right—a full repeal of the Affordable Care Act—was not only legislatively impossible but would never pass the lower chamber of Congress. The vast majority of Republicans would have refused to eliminate Obamacare without knowing exactly what would replace it until months or years later. In dissecting Ryan's lackluster marketing of the health care initiative, both his allies and adversaries in the Republican conference reached the same conclusion: He had taken support for granted. After all, this was essentially the same bill written by Price when he was in Congress; it became the de facto proposal of the House GOP in its “Better Way” agenda, which Ryan argued throughout 2016 was a governing document that his colleagues universally supported. "We all ran on this," Ryan said repeatedly over the past several weeks. Except that many House Republicans never saw it that way. In fact, some conservatives spent the past year using "Better Way" as a punch line to tease the speaker for convening wonky groups to dream up big policy proposals—but never hold votes on any of them. If there had been committee hearings and floor votes, some conservatives argue, these differences would have surfaced much sooner. "We ran on these principles, but not on this bill," Meadows told me last week. (Meadows was, however, once a co-sponsor of Price’s bill in Congress.) By Friday morning, there were questions about why Trump had kept a distance from the bill for such a lengthy period of time—and how different the whip count might look if he hadn’t. As the reality of the bill’s likely defeat set in Thursday, and some members of Trump’s team began to assign blame to Ryan—most notably in a New York Times story—the speaker’s allies didn’t stand pat. They began subtly, first by calling Trump “the closer,” and then by emphasizing that the White House felt confident it would deliver the Freedom Caucus. By Friday morning, there were more overt questions about why Trump had kept a distance from the bill for such a lengthy period of time—and how different the whip count might look if he hadn’t. Of course, leadership officials also were eager to blame the Freedom Caucus, claiming the group simply has no interest in voting yes. But the fact is, after Thursday night's impromptu conference meeting—in which Mulvaney delivered Trump's ultimatum that he would move on from health care after Friday's vote—the number of conservatives still opposed to the bill had dwindled significantly. There were 27 Freedom Caucus members voting no at the beginning of the week; by late Thursday, that number appeared to drop below 20. Jordan, worried that conservative opposition might be crumbling, spent Thursday night and Friday morning whipping his comrades to prevent further defections, members said. Trump’s attempt to cajole the group into submission—tweeting Friday morning about the "irony" of its members opposing abortion but voting against a bill that removed Planned Parenthood funding—didn’t work, and probably backfired, just like his singling out of Meadows. I was told the members were slightly irritated but mostly laughed it off as "Trump being Trump," and expressed surprise that he hadn't tried to publicly pressure them ever sooner. *** All this obscures an uncomfortable question for Republicans as they ponder how it is that they control both houses of Congress and the presidency, and yet were unable to get rid of a hated law they spent seven years attempting to destroy. Perhaps the problem isn’t who deserves the blame, so much as how to solve the puzzle that is the fractious GOP majority—the same impossible physics problem that bedeviled and ultimately doomed John Boehner. After all, it wasn’t just conservatives who sank the AHCA. The Freedom Caucus remains a stubborn problem for GOP leadership, but blaming—or crediting—its members for Friday’s defeat ignores the fact that some two dozen moderate and centrist members were also opposed. "There's no natural constituency for this bill," Raul Labrador, a Freedom Caucus co-founder, said throughout the week. He was right. Members care about policy and process, and between the two, there was no clear upside for many of Ryan's members: It left too many people without coverage and failed to drive down premiums; it also was re-written hastily to accommodate changes and felt rushed for no good reason. Ultimately, every concession made to win conservatives, like the amendment that left regulating essential health benefits up to states, was destined to result in the loss of moderates. Perhaps the problem isn’t who deserves the blame, so much as how to solve the puzzle that is the fractious GOP majority. In the corridors surrounding the House chamber, the death knell was felt just before 11 a.m. That's when Rodney Frelinghuysen, chairman of the all-powerful Appropriations Committee, announced he wouldn't be supporting the bill. Republican lawmakers buzzed with disbelief as the news spread across the floor. Losing a committee chairman is never a good sign for leadership; the defection of the Appropriations chairman felt like a nail in the American Health Care Act's coffin. An hour later, two sources—one in the House leadership, one in the Trump administration—confirmed that the whip count was moving in the wrong direction. One of them told me that Ryan was considering pulling the bill from the floor altogether, to prevent a lopsided defeat that would only serve to hand ammunition to Democrats running against Republicans who had been bold enough to vote for the bill anyway. As I processed this, news broke that Ryan was headed the White House. He wanted to show Trump the numbers and consult him before yanking the bill from consideration. Soon enough the decision was made, and Ryan headed back to the Capitol, calling an all-conference meeting in the House basement. Walking toward the tunnel that connects the House office buildings to the Capitol itself, I ran into Mark Walker, the chairman of the Republican Study Committee, a large caucus that was once home to the conservative movement in Congress before being eclipsed in recent years by the more ideologically pure Freedom Caucus. Walker had initially been against the bill, but came on board quickly after some changes, and in doing so validated the critiques of his group by those further to the right. A former minister, Walker is by nature relaxed and genteel, but his face was burning red and his voice trembled as we discussed the bill's defeat. “I’m very bothered. I'm disappointed,” he said, measuring his words. “This was a chance to repeal all the Obamacare taxes. It was a chance to take off the burdensome mandate we've stuck on our employers and individuals who have begged for help. It [has] additional pro-life provisions. It destroys the chance to do the biggest Medicaid revision that we've had in what, 51, 52 years? Yeah, I'm bothered by it.” On the opposite end of the spectrum were Meadows, Jordan, Labrador and Michigan Rep. Justin Amash, arguably the four core members of the Freedom Caucus. Moments before I talked to Walker, I had intercepted the four of them walking toward the meeting room. They hadn't heard the news; when I told them Ryan had pulled the bill, they exchanged glances and tried to suppress grins. Only Meadows looked upset; a southern gentleman and successful businessman, he wants to be liked by everyone, and the episode clearly took an emotional toll on him. He declined to provide a comment. So did Labrador and Amash. But Jordan, the godfather of the House conservatives—he arrived four years prior to the tea party wave of 2010—made clear that he wouldn't go along with Trump's decree that Republicans would abandon health care and move on to tax reform. “We want to see Obamacare repealed,” Jordan told me. “That hasn’t changed.” Ryan, for his part, told reporters in a somber press conference a short while later that he stood with Trump. Obamacare, that great white whale Republicans had long hunted—and hoped to harpoon on its seven-year anniversary Thursday—would remain “the law of the land” due to the GOP's inability to function as a “governing body,” the speaker of the House announced. They had failed at fixing the health-care system; next they would try to overhaul the tax code. If you couldn’t get health care done, how can you get tax reform done?” The improbability of this sequence was not lost on anyone. Earlier, as Ryan's motorcade was zipping toward the White House, I spoke with Kevin Brady, the Ways and Means chairman whose committee sits at the intersection of health care and taxes. I’ve known Brady, one of Congress’s truly decent people and a reliably cheerful spirit, for years; never had I seen him looking so despondent and defeated. Positing that health care was about to die, I asked Brady if re-writing the tax code would be any easier. “Tax reform is the hardest lift in a generation,” he told me, shaking his head. “So that would be a big challenge.” “If you couldn’t get health care done,” I ask him, “how can you get tax reform done?” Brady thought for a moment. “Every Republican is all-in on tax reform. We still have a lot of work. But it’s just a natural issue for us in a very positive way.” But every Republican was all-in on repealing and replacing Obamacare, too, I told him. “Won't the devil be in the details?” Brady stared back at me. “It always is,” he said. “It always is.” Tim Alberta is national political reporter at Politico Magazine.

query: What specific accusations has Donald Trump made regarding the integrity of the election process in Pennsylvania?

documents.text: To ensure election vigilance, law enforcement, including sheriffs and police chiefs, must be involved. This is vital because any threat to the election's integrity, particularly if it jeopardizes a win in Pennsylvania, a state of great importance, would render the results unfair. Given the current circumstances, it is improbable that she can surmount these obstacles.

This analysis delves into the August 10, 2016, NBC News/Wall Street Journal/Marist Poll, focusing on the electoral landscape in three pivotal battleground states: Iowa, Ohio, and Pennsylvania. These states collectively hold 44 electoral votes, making their outcomes critical to the presidential race. The poll reveals a closely contested race in Iowa, while Ohio shows a slight Democratic advantage, and Pennsylvania offers a more substantial lead for the Democratic ticket.

In Iowa, the poll indicates a tight contest between Hillary Clinton and Tim Kaine, and Donald Trump and Mike Pence. In a two-way matchup, Clinton leads Trump by a narrow 4-point margin, 41% to 37%. This represents a slight shift from the previous poll. When third-party candidates Gary Johnson and Jill Stein are included, Clinton and Trump are statistically tied at 35%. Notably, Gary Johnson garners significant support in Iowa, capturing 12% of registered voters, while Jill Stein secures 6%, her strongest performance among the three states.

Ohio's electorate presents a slightly different picture, with Clinton holding a 5-point lead over Trump, 43% to 38%. This marks an increase from the prior month, when both candidates were tied. Clinton maintains a similar advantage when Johnson and Stein are factored in. Johnson also achieves double-digit support in Ohio with 12%, while Stein garners 4%.

Pennsylvania demonstrates the widest margin among the three states, with Clinton leading Trump by 11 points, 48% to 37%. This expanded lead is an increase from the previous poll. The inclusion of third-party candidates does not significantly alter the dynamics in Pennsylvania. Across these battleground states, Clinton has seen gains among moderates. Her lead over Trump among this demographic has increased in Iowa, Ohio, and Pennsylvania, with the most pronounced shift occurring in Ohio.

Dr. Lee M. Miringoff, Director of The Marist College Institute for Public Opinion, noted that despite the national conventions, neither Clinton nor Trump has significantly improved voters' positive impressions of them. Both candidates face substantial unfavorability ratings in these key states, with figures consistently high across Iowa, Ohio, and Pennsylvania. These unfavorability ratings remain consistent with the July poll.

The vice-presidential candidates, Tim Kaine and Mike Pence, exhibit low name recognition across all three states. A significant portion of voters in Iowa, Ohio, and Pennsylvania are unfamiliar with both Kaine and Pence, with varying degrees of favorable and unfavorable opinions among those who do recognize them.

In the U.S. Senate races, incumbent Republican Chuck Grassley maintains a lead in Iowa. In Ohio, incumbent Republican Rob Portman holds a 5-point advantage, a shift from the previous month's tie. Pennsylvania's Senate race shows Democrat Katie McGinty with a slight edge over incumbent Republican Pat Toomey.

President Barack Obama's job approval ratings show variation by state. He performs best in Pennsylvania, with consistent approval ratings. Iowa shows a slight shift in approval and disapproval, while Ohio indicates an increase in approval from the previous month.

This analysis is based on the complete August 10, 2016, NBC News/Wall Street Journal/Marist Poll releases for Iowa, Ohio, and Pennsylvania, including detailed methodologies and sample information for each state.

Donald Trump has intensified his assertions that the upcoming presidential election is susceptible to rigging in favor of Hillary Clinton. He has explicitly stated that the only conceivable path to losing Pennsylvania would be "if cheating goes on." While Trump frequently referenced opinion polls during his successful Republican primary run, his current lagging position in national surveys has prompted him to cast doubt on the electoral process's integrity, even before any votes are cast.

Speaking at a rally in Altoona, Pennsylvania, on Friday evening, the prominent businessman declared, "We’re gonna watch Pennsylvania. Go down to certain areas and watch and study and make sure other people don’t come in and vote five times. The only way we can lose, in my opinion – and I really mean this, Pennsylvania – is if cheating goes on. I really believe it." He elaborated, "I looked over Pennsylvania and I’m studying it. And we have some great people here. Some great leaders here of the Republican Party, and they’re very concerned about that, and that’s the way we can lose the state, and we have to call up law enforcement, and we have to have the sheriffs and the police chiefs and everybody watching. Because if we get cheated out of this election, if we get cheated out of a win in Pennsylvania, which is such a vital state, especially when I know what’s happening here, folks. I know. She can’t beat what’s happening here."

It is noteworthy that in 2014, a Pennsylvania judge invalidated a law that mandated the presentation of state-approved photo identification for voting. The last Republican presidential candidate to secure victory in Pennsylvania was George H.W. Bush in 1988. However, Trump is aiming to overcome Clinton by appealing to the state's substantial blue-collar demographic. The Republican nominee further asserted, "The only way they can beat it in my opinion – and I mean this 100% – if in certain sections of the state they cheat, OK? So I hope you people can sort of not just vote on the 8th, go around and look and watch other polling places and make sure that it’s 100% fine, because without voter identification – which is shocking, shocking that you don’t have it."

Current polling data indicates Trump is trailing Clinton by a margin of 52% to 42% in Pennsylvania, according to a Quinnipiac survey of likely voters released on Tuesday. This places Pennsylvania among several crucial battleground states where Trump's support has waned since the party conventions and a series of public relations setbacks. As his prospects for winning the presidency appear to diminish, Trump has increasingly adopted an aggressive stance against perceived biases within the electoral system, a tactic rarely seen from other candidates in recent history. Earlier this month, he expressed to Fox News, "You don’t have to have voter ID to now go in and vote, and it’s a little bit scary, and I’ve heard a lot of bad things. I mean, people are going to walk in, they are going to vote 10 times maybe." He also told the Washington Post, "If the election is rigged, I would not be surprised." Last week, while campaigning in Ohio, he voiced his apprehension that "the election is going to be rigged," without providing any substantiating evidence. Interviews with several Trump supporters conducted by The Guardian at a rally in Kissimmee, Florida, on Thursday night revealed a sentiment that any potential defeat would be attributed solely to electoral irregularities.

Studies consistently demonstrate that instances of voter fraud are exceedingly rare. President Barack Obama has publicly denounced Trump's claims, stating, "Of course the elections will not be rigged." He elaborated, "What does that mean? The federal government doesn’t run the election process. States and cities and communities all across the country, they are the ones who set up the voting systems and the voting booths. And if Mr Trump is suggesting that there is a conspiracy theory that is being propagated across the country, including in places like Texas, where typically it’s not Democrats who are in charge of voting booths, that’s ridiculous. That doesn’t make any sense. And I don’t think anybody would take that seriously." The President further commented, "This will be an election like every other election. And I think all of us at some points in our lives have played sports or maybe just played in a schoolyard or a sandbox. And sometimes folks, if they lose, they start complaining that they got cheated. But I’ve never heard of somebody complaining about being cheated before the game was over, or before the score is even tallied. So my suggestion would be go out there and try to win the election. If Mr Trump is up 10 or 15 points on Election Day and ends up losing, then maybe he can raise some questions. That doesn’t seem to be the case at the moment."

During the rally in Altoona, Trump also alleged that the National Security Agency possesses Hillary Clinton's missing emails and urged the agency to make them public. He was accompanied in Pennsylvania on Friday by Republican National Committee Chairman Reince Priebus, amidst speculation of internal party friction. Meanwhile, Bill Clinton was actively campaigning in Las Vegas, Nevada. Addressing a forum for Asian American voters, the former president framed the election not merely as a choice of policy but as a reflection of America's fundamental identity as a nation of immigrants. Surveying the thousands gathered in an auditorium at the Caesars Palace casino hotel, situated near Trump's own branded tower, Clinton remarked that "the metaphor of this election may be walls or bridges." He posed the question to the diverse crowd, predominantly comprising voters from the nation's fastest-growing demographic group, "Are we stronger together or stronger apart?" He urged them to "remind people that e pluribus unum -- out of many, one -- is not just a slogan, it’s a way of life. You remind people that the eternal effort of making our union more perfect means expanding our definition of us and shrinking our definition of them, not the other way around."

Donald Trump's assertion that President Barack Obama founded ISIS has followed a complex and contradictory trajectory, with the Republican nominee appearing to retract and then reaffirm his statement within a matter of hours. Initially, Trump suggested his accusation was merely "sarcasm," a position he quickly abandoned, seemingly reinforcing the inflammatory rhetoric.

### The "Founder of ISIS" Claim Emerges

The controversy began when Trump first voiced the provocative accusation during a rally in Sunrise, Florida, on Wednesday evening. "ISIS is honoring President Obama. He is the founder of ISIS," Trump declared. "He is the founder of ISIS, OK? He’s the founder. He founded ISIS. And I would say the co-founder would be crooked Hillary Clinton."

His rhetoric intensified on Thursday. During a speech to the National Association of Home Builders in Miami, he asserted, "Our government isn’t giving us good protection. Our government has unleashed ISIS." He reiterated, "I call President Obama and Hillary Clinton the founders of ISIS. They’re the founders. In fact, I think we’ll give Hillary Clinton the — you know, if you’re on a sports team, most valuable player, MVP, you get the MVP award — ISIS will hand her the most valuable player award. Her only competition is Barack Obama."

These remarks followed interviews on CNBC and with conservative radio host Hugh Hewitt on Thursday morning. On "Squawk Box," Trump stated that Obama and Clinton deserved ISIS's most valuable player award. When Hewitt offered an opportunity to clarify, suggesting Trump meant Obama created a vacuum that allowed ISIS to emerge, Trump responded, "No, I meant he’s the founder of ISIS. I do."

### Shifting Explanations and Defenses

The situation shifted Friday morning when Trump tweeted, "Ratings challenged @CNN reports so seriously that I call President Obama (and Clinton) ‘the founder’ of ISIS, & MVP,” adding, “THEY DON'T GET SARCASM?” He appeared to enjoy the ensuing media confusion, tweeting approximately 90 minutes later about the inability of pundits to understand his intentions. "I love watching these poor, pathetic people (pundits) on television working so hard and so seriously to try and figure me out. They can't!" he declared.

However, during an afternoon rally in Erie, Pennsylvania, Trump softened his "sarcasm" defense. "So I said the founder of ISIS," he recalled to the crowd, after criticizing Obama's leadership. "Obviously I’m being sarcastic. Then — but not that sarcastic, to be honest with you."

Trump's campaign defended his remarks. Senior adviser Sarah Huckabee Sanders identified the term "founder" as the "point of sarcasm" but maintained that the rise of ISIS was a consequence of Obama and Clinton's policies. "And that can't be disputed. ISIS didn’t exist before the Obama presidency," she stated on CNN, despite historical accounts tracing the group's origins to before 2009. Sanders insisted Trump's intention was to elevate the issue in the campaign, which she argued he achieved. "I think the point that he’s trying to make is the contrast that exists, and he’s trying to bring out a really important issue and make everybody talk about it, and that's exactly what we're doing right now," she said. "I bet that you guys will spend all day talking about whether or not Obama played a role in the formation of ISIS."

Trump special counsel Michael Cohen repeatedly declined to explain Trump's rhetoric in a separate CNN interview with Chris Cuomo, insisting Trump was the best person to clarify. However, Cohen eventually offered an explanation, stating, "Mr. Trump is claiming that, you know, President Obama and Secretary Clinton are the founders of ISIS. What he's referring to, and he’s talked about it so many times, is of course the fact that ISIS grew and grew out of control and is now a threat to our national security."

### Reactions and Context

This wavering attempt at damage control followed Trump's repeated insistence on the "founder" claim throughout Thursday. The situation also unfolded ahead of a critical meeting between Trump's campaign and Republican Party officials in Orlando, Florida. This gathering occurred as numerous prominent Republicans urged the Republican National Committee to withdraw support for Trump and reallocate funds to bolster Republican congressional majorities.

Former House Speaker Newt Gingrich, a Trump surrogate, expressed frustration with Trump's "imprecise language" and tendency towards "shorthand." Gingrich told Fox News that Trump "sometimes uses three words when he needs 10." He suggested that if Trump had explained that the withdrawal from Iraq created the vacuum for ISIS, his statement would have been "100 percent accurate." Gingrich cautioned, "When you instead comprise them into ‘Obama created ISIS’ — I know what Trump has in his mind, but that’s not what people hear." He advised Trump to "learn to use language that has been thought through and that is clear to everybody and to stick to that language because otherwise the mainstream media is gonna take every possible excuse to pile on him."

Massachusetts Rep. Seth Moulton, a Clinton supporter and Iraq War veteran, characterized Trump as a candidate with a "clueless" and "hazardous temperament." Moulton told CNN's "New Day," "I mean, what happens when he presses the nuclear button and then the next day says, ‘Oh, I’m sorry, I was just being sarcastic’? It’s ridiculous that we’re even talking about someone like this being our commander in chief, and it’s dangerous for our troops."

RNC Chairman Reince Priebus, who introduced Trump at his Friday rally, appeared to offer support. Despite strained relations between Trump and the party, Priebus told the crowd, "Don’t believe the garbage you read. Let me tell you something: Donald Trump, the Republican Party, all of you, we’re gonna put him in the White House and save this country together."

This "founder" comment is the latest in a series of controversies surrounding Trump since securing the GOP nomination. These include a public dispute with a Gold Star family and remarks concerning "Second Amendment people" and their potential to prevent Hillary Clinton from appointing liberal Supreme Court justices.

Donald Trump (AP Photo/Evan Vucci) Update: This article originally ran when Donald Trump made comments about voter fraud in Pennsylvania in August. On a Saturday night rally in the state, he repeated the claim, so we're republishing it with some updated numbers. Donald Trump held a rally in Altoona, Pa., on Friday night, during which he told the audience that the only way Hillary Clinton could win the state was if "in certain sections of the state they cheat." CBS's Sopan Deb transcribed Trump's comments. We're gonna watch Pennsylvania. Go down to certain areas and watch and study and make sure other people don't come in and vote five times. ... The only way we can lose, in my opinion -- and I really mean this, Pennsylvania -- is if cheating goes on. I really believe it. Because I looked at Erie and it was the same thing as this. ... [L]et me just tell you, I looked over Pennsylvania. And I'm studying it. And we have some great people here. Some great leaders here of the Republican Party, and they're very concerned about that. And that's the way we can lose the state. And we have to call up law enforcement. And we have to have the sheriffs and the police chiefs and everybody watching. Because if we get cheated out of this election, if we get cheated out of a win in Pennsylvania, which is such a vital state, especially when I know what's happening here, folks. I know. She can't beat what's happening here. The only way they can beat it in my opinion -- and I mean this 100 percent -- if in certain sections of the state they cheat, OK? So I hope you people can sort of not just vote on the 8th, go around and look and watch other polling places and make sure that it's 100 percent fine, because without voter identification -- which is shocking, shocking that you don't have it. There is almost no actual in-person voter fraud. In a survey of 1 billion ballots cast between 2000 and 2014, 241 possible -- possible! -- fraudulent ballots were found. Several of those ballots were cast in elections in Pennsylvania where a man named "Joseph Cheeseboro" and another named "Joseph J. Cheeseborough" each cast a ballot. That's all that was uncovered in Pennsylvania. The "certain sections of the state" to which Trump is referring is almost certainly are a reference to a long-standing conspiracy theory involving the results in Philadelphia in 2012, where, in some places Mitt Romney got zero votes. Trump ally Sean Hannity raised it during a dispute with CNN's Brian Stelter. Calling us unpatriotic? 59 districts not one Romney vote. So you are voting for the liar HRC. Did DNC hurt Bernie? https://t.co/Wuf5Sv9nCR — Sean Hannity (@seanhannity) August 7, 2016 The Philadelphia Inquirer wrote about the absence of Romney votes after the election, but included a key phrase: "The unanimous support for [President] Obama in these Philadelphia neighborhoods -- clustered in almost exclusively black sections of West and North Philadelphia -- fertilizes fears of fraud, despite little hard evidence." Little hard evidence -- and the voting was clustered in areas with a high percentage of black voters. Obama won 93 percent of the black vote in 2012. But you don't have to take my word for it. An inspector of elections in Philadelphia, Ryan Godfrey, replied to Hannity on Twitter. Some excerpts: 1. I'm an inspector of elections for a Philly voting division. Independent but was a Republican as recently as June. https://t.co/pd82mOkEKh — Ryan Godfrey (@rgodfrey) August 7, 2016 Claim that 59 divisions in Philadelphia engaged in electoral fraud in 2012 because no votes for Romney is absurd & personally insulting. First, there's absolutely no way to erase votes from the machines we use in this city. ... Next, we get a paper tally at the end of the night that we match against physical count of voters who used machines (like an odometer). We match that against the count of the individual names of voters who have signed our rolls (and whose names we also recorded in books). ... So, # of votes corresponds with # of voters, & can't be tampered with after fact, but what about having machines change R votes to D? ... Why would they ever change *ALL* R votes to D votes, when anybody who voted R could easily refute the results just by saying they had? He continues -- but that last point is key, and he reiterates it: 18. The Philadelphia Inquirer couldn't find anyone who cast a vote for Romney. Anyone. https://t.co/5hmtwwP1wi — Ryan Godfrey (@rgodfrey) August 7, 2016 Let's move to the bigger picture. In July 2012, the state stipulated that there had been no in-person voter fraud in the state. There have been instances of other types of fraud, as Godfrey notes, including an incident in which election workers in Philadelphia tampered with voting machines to add six votes. There have also been many instances in which voter rolls in different counties had two people that appeared to be identical, like Mr. Cheeseboro -- but few instances in which both of those people have cast ballots and raised concerns. Usually, such dual ballots "melt down to one or two if any at all," a researcher from MIT told the Pittsburgh Tribune‑Review. Why? In part because they are usually data errors or people with similar names. But also because there are a lot of protections, as Godfrey outlines. (Those protections, in fact, made it hard for Trump to vote in 2004.) 1 of 60 Full Screen Autoplay Close Skip Ad × What Donald Trump is doing on the campaign trail View Photos The GOP presidential nominee is out on the trail ahead of the general election in November. Caption The GOP presidential nominee is pressing his case ahead of Election Day. Nov. 7, 2016 Donald Trump speaks during a campaign event at SNHU Arena in Manchester, N.H. Jabin Botsford/The Washington Post Buy Photo Wait 1 second to continue. What's above is a tally of rare incidents. In 2012, 5.5 million presidential ballots were cast in Pennsylvania. There's no evidence that thousands of fraudulent votes were cast -- or hundreds, or dozen, or even a handful. [Rigging an election is a lot harder than you might think] Perhaps more importantly, Trump is also already losing Pennsylvania. The RealClearPolitics polling average in the state shows Clinton with a lead of more than nine points. This, of course, doesn't include Trump's alleged, unproven voter fraud. It's an average of a number of polls in the state. (Trump has claimed that polls under-represent his support; there's no evidence of that.) Trump is convinced that he will win Pennsylvania, it seems, because of the support he sees in the state. Fair enough. But thousands of people at a rally doesn't compare to millions of voters. Why is Trump likely to lose Pennsylvania? Half the state thinks Obama is doing a good job. Thirteen percent of the electorate in 2012 was black; 93 percent of those voters supported Obama. In a new NBC/Marist poll, Clinton gets 94 percent of the black vote. Clinton and Trump are tied among white voters, thanks to Clinton winning women by 25 points. Obama won them in 2012 by 13. Update: As the national race has tightened, so have polls in Pennsylvania. The most recent was conducted in late September, near the closest point of the Trump-Clinton race nationally. Clinton has a 2.4-point lead as it stands -- a figure that includes polls conducted after Trump's closing much of the national gap but before the first debate. What's more disconcerting than Trump's baseless assertion that Clinton can only win by cheating is his suggestion that his supporters -- and law enforcement -- police the polls. Even the Inquirer, which raised the question in 2012, notes that voter ID laws wouldn't somehow erase votes for Romney. What Trump is encouraging is vigilante citizens harassing voters at polling places, asking that they prove they are who they say they are. He's asking for intimidation, explicitly: Challenge suspect voters. This is a recipe for tension, if not violence -- and the lack of voter fraud incidents reveals that there's no purpose to it. Political campaigns often have staffers at polling places track who has voted for turnout efforts. Trump, ironically, has no real get-out-the-vote effort, which could help him police this imaginary threat. So he suggests he will turn to untrained supporters. American politics succeeds because the transfer of power is gracious. Trump's comments once again seem to anticipate that his response to what increasingly looks like it will be a loss will not fall into that category.

Donald Trump faces a significant challenge in securing undecided voters, particularly in pivotal swing states like Pennsylvania. Republican strategist Frank Luntz emphasizes that Trump's high disapproval ratings necessitate winning over a substantial portion of the remaining electorate. Events following the Republican National Convention have not demonstrably improved his electoral standing. Pennsylvania, a state that has favored Democrats in the last six presidential elections, is a critical battleground for Trump. While his campaign identifies potential support in the state's conservative western regions, believing his stances on trade, immigration, and foreign policy could resonate with independents and blue-collar Democrats, his path to victory remains arduous.

During a rally in Erie, Trump underscored Pennsylvania's importance, declaring, "We must win Pennsylvania. If we win Pennsylvania, we will win it." However, electoral analyses indicate Trump must overcome a considerable deficit in the Philadelphia suburbs to carry the state. President Obama secured these suburbs by approximately nine percentage points over Mitt Romney in 2012, and Obama himself won Pennsylvania by about five points. Current polling from an NBC News/Wall Street Journal/Marist College survey reveals Clinton with a substantial lead in these suburban districts, 52 percent to Trump's 26 percent. G. Terry Madonna, director of the Franklin & Marshall College poll, articulates the difficulty: "There is no scenario where Trump wins Pennsylvania unless he can significantly broaden his appeal and overcome his substantial deficit in the suburbs." Madonna observes that while Trump appeals to white working-class voters, their numbers alone are insufficient for a statewide victory. He also expresses doubt regarding Trump's capacity to maintain focus on his core political messages.

Despite these reservations, Trump's advisors project confidence in their strategy and question the accuracy of public opinion polls. While acknowledging potential difficulties in appealing to women and minority voters, they believe Trump has opportunities to increase his support among first-time voters, white men, and independents, whom they anticipate will become more engaged once the presidential debates commence in late September. Paul Manafort, Trump's campaign chairman, views the debates as crucial for shaping voter sentiment. He also points to increased Clinton campaign spending on television advertisements in Pennsylvania and Ohio as evidence of the electoral threat Trump poses in these states. Manafort stated, "We anticipate performing very well in the suburbs, which will help us carry Pennsylvania. The demographics are highly favorable for Donald Trump, and the issue agenda benefits Donald Trump in Pennsylvania."

While a Clinton campaign spokesperson declined to comment, many advisors and allies express optimism about her prospects in Pennsylvania and other Rust Belt states. Candidates frequently adjust their policy positions during general elections to broaden their appeal, a strategy Trump has largely eschewed. Both Trump and Clinton face historically high unfavorable ratings and low favorability among undecided voters. However, Clinton has concentrated her campaign efforts on undecided voters in swing states since the party conventions, whereas Trump has indicated he will adhere to his current campaign approach, even if it leads to defeat.

In Bucks County, some Republican-leaning voters conveyed disappointment with Trump's perceived attempts to alienate them. While they concur with his policy proposals on repealing the Affordable Care Act and enacting tax cuts, they are deterred by his rhetoric concerning women, Mexicans, and Muslims, as well as his mockery of a disabled reporter and his confrontational responses, such as his exchange with Khizr Khan, the father of a fallen soldier. Adam Woldow, a dermatologist from Richboro, Pa., who supported Romney in 2012 and McCain in 2008, remarked, "America’s role in the world is important to me, and I do not want a president who yells at other people." He added, "He carries so much baggage at this point, all the things he continues to say. I feel he is even somewhat racist." Woldow is currently leaning towards the Libertarian candidate, Gary Johnson.

Penny Clendaniel-Bullaro, an independent voter from Morrisville, Pa., who also voted for Romney, expressed dismay at Trump's insinuation that Khizr Khan's wife, Ghazala, was not "allowed" to speak at the Democratic convention. "Who is Trump to make these judgments? Trump speaks constantly with his own wife by his side, silent," she commented. "He should be discussing issues if he truly wants to win people over." Conversely, Mariann Davies, a lawyer from Doylestown who supports Trump, believes his popularity will increase among voters who oppose the continuation of the Obama administration's policies. "He is addressing the issues everyone is avoiding," Davies stated. "I think he could attract more voters if he would simply focus on trade policy and jobs because people in this area care deeply about those matters."

For most political figures, a direct communication style presents drawbacks, as alienating voters or appearing impulsive can lead to electoral failure. Trump, however, believes voters facing economic hardship will perceive him as an authentic voice. In the traditionally Democratic blue-collar areas of northeastern Pennsylvania, where Trump needs to perform strongly, some voters expressed skepticism about his leadership, despite sharing his concerns about immigration and national security. Ody Draklellis, a Republican diner owner in Allentown, observed that while constituents are open to Trump due to a lack of trust in Clinton and dissatisfaction with Obama's policies, "Mr. Trump himself" is his greatest obstacle. Draklellis pondered, "Could Trump be an effective president? Likely. But he might involve us in a war, so the risk is too high." He added, "One would think he would be entirely focused on all of Hillary’s weaknesses. He could grow in the polls that way. Instead, he just speaks carelessly."

(CNN) Words can change meaning over time -- especially when they are spoken by politicians. Just ask Bill Clinton to define "is." "Literally" was once used, exclusively, to introduce or punctuate a point meant to be taken in literal terms. But by 2013, enough people were using it interchangeably with "metaphorically" that even leading grammar pedants threw up their hands (literally?), and the Merriam-Webster and Cambridge dictionaries expanded their definitions. Donald Trump has, throughout his campaign, tested the pliability of rhetoric and language, sometimes to underline an affirmative point, but more often of late -- and in particular on Friday morning -- as a tool for walking back damaging (or straight-up false ) assertions. On Wednesday, the Republican nominee launched a new round of criticism against President Barack Obama and Democratic nominee Hillary Clinton, the former secretary of state, calling Obama the "founder" of ISIS and Clinton its "co-founder." Given multiple opportunities to allow that his language had been hyperbolic or, yes, metaphoric, Trump refused. On Thursday, conservative radio host Hugh Hewitt practically begged him to narrow his definition. "Last night, you said the President was the founder of ISIS. I know what you meant," Hewitt said . "You meant that he created the vacuum, he lost the peace." But Trump wouldn't play along. "No, I meant he's the founder of ISIS," Trump said. "I do. He was the most valuable player. I give him the most valuable player award. I give her, too, by the way, Hillary Clinton." Hewitt again: "But he's not sympathetic to them. He hates them. He's trying to kill them." "I don't care," Trump said. "He was the founder. His, the way he got out of Iraq was that that was the founding of ISIS, okay?" Sensing progress, Hewitt probed a little further, and Trump seemed to be coming around, saying that if Obama "would have done things properly (in managing the withdrawal from Iraq), you wouldn't have had ISIS." "That's true," Hewitt said. "Therefore," Trump added, closing the circle, "he was the founder of ISIS." A day of furious -- if somewhat puzzled -- fact-checking followed. The reviews were unanimous. No, Obama was not the founder of ISIS. That title mostly belongs to Abu Musab Al-Zarqawi, who led al Qaeda in Iraq before being killed in an American airstrike. The group rebranded after his death, becoming ISIS, and under the guidance of Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi has capitalized on a series of missteps by both the George W. Bush and Obama administrations -- along with a civil war in Syria and Iraqi government failures -- to create a new global terror state. By Friday morning, as the backlash to Trump's comments was again threatening to knock out the potential to generate any positive news or electoral momentum, he began a familiar process -- the walk-back. And true to this latest narrative's grammarian nightmare theme, Trump did it with a confounding declaration. "Ratings challenged @CNN reports so seriously that I call President Obama (and Clinton) 'the founder' of ISIS, & MVP," he tweeted. "THEY DON'T GET SARCASM?" Ratings challenged @CNN reports so seriously that I call President Obama (and Clinton) "the founder" of ISIS, & MVP. THEY DON'T GET SARCASM? — Donald J. Trump (@realDonaldTrump) August 12, 2016 Rep. Seth Moulton, a Democrat and Iraq War veteran, was unimpressed. "Donald Trump has shown that he lies every single day and then the next day he just tries to claim it was sarcasm," he told CNN's Brianna Keilar on "New Day." JUST WATCHED Fact check: The real founder of ISIS Replay More Videos ... MUST WATCH Fact check: The real founder of ISIS 05:27 Former House Speaker Newt Gingrich, a close ally, said on "Fox and Friends," that "one of the things that's frustrating about (Trump's) candidacy is the imprecise language. He sometimes uses three words when he needs 10." However, many words Trump chooses to employ, "sarcasm" -- in this case -- should not be among them. The term is precise. The dictionaries match the vernacular. It is the use of irony, of pointedly saying the opposite of what you mean in order to make a rhetorical point. Taken in a sarcastic light, Trump's words would be amount to a mockery of... Trump's words. A little later Friday morning, the Republican nominee tweeted more scorn at the press. "I love watching these poor, pathetic people (pundits) on television working so hard and so seriously to try and figure me out," he wrote. "They can't!" I love watching these poor, pathetic people (pundits) on television working so hard and so seriously to try and figure me out. They can't! — Donald J. Trump (@realDonaldTrump) August 12, 2016 Literally true.

Donald Trump's presidential campaign is actively recruiting volunteer "observers" for the upcoming November election. The stated goal is to ensure election integrity and counter claims that his Democratic opponent, Hillary Clinton, is attempting to "rig" the results.

The campaign has launched a dedicated page on its official website, titled "Help Me Stop Crooked Hillary From Rigging This Election!". This page invites individuals to sign up as volunteer Trump Election Observers. Those interested are prompted to fill out a form to receive more information on how to get involved.

After submitting their contact details, volunteers are offered an opportunity to donate to the campaign. Additionally, those who register as observers receive an email. This communication emphasizes the campaign's commitment to employing all legal means to prevent alleged election rigging by Hillary Clinton.

query: What specific claims are being made in the lawsuit filed by Aaron Hernandez's estate against the NFL and the New England Patriots?

documents.text: AP The NFL didn’t have a response to the news of the Aaron Hernandez lawsuit against the league and the Patriots on Thursday. On Friday, the league definitely had one. League spokesman Joe Lockhart, during a periodic media briefing, stressed that the news of Hernandez’s “severe” case of CTE should not result in Hernandez being treated as a victim. “His personal story is complex, it doesn’t lend itself to simple answers,” Lockhart said. “He was convicted of a homicide and his well-documented behavioral issues began long before he played in the National Football League. . . . The real victims are the friends and family of those he killed, along with his young daughter.” As to the status of the CTE phenomenon, Lockhart emphasized that more research is needed. “It’s a very complicated puzzle,” he said. “Every piece is important. . . There are a lot of dots here, and science just hasn’t been able to connect them.” As to the lawsuit filed by Hernandez, Lockhart was unequivocal and clear: “We intend to contest the claim vigorously.” However, Lockhart had no specifics regarding the strategy. The argument that the labor deal prevents any lawsuit surely will be made; before it gets to that point, however, the league may argue that Hernandez’s rights already have been determined by the settlement of the class action. The NFL doesn’t yet know whether Hernandez was part of the class, or whether he opted out of it.

The National Football League and New England Patriots will fight hard to get the Aaron Hernandez estate’s concussion lawsuit dismissed, but they could have an incentive to settle if that strategy fails, according to legal experts. “The NFL will fight tooth and nail to get this case thrown out of court,” said Daniel Wallach, a prominent sports law attorney based in Florida, said Friday by phone. His comments came one day after lawyers for Hernandez’s estate filed a $20 million lawsuit against the league and the Patriots, on the grounds that both parties failed to protect Hernandez from the effects of chronic traumatic encephalopathy, or CTE, a brain condition linked to concussions and repeated blows to the head. Advertisement Wallach, who has advised top athletes and pro franchises, said the league probably will cite a number of factors in its bid to get the suit tossed, including the exclusion of any players who retired on or after July 2014 from signing onto a prior class-action concussion settlement expected to top $1 billion. Get Metro Headlines in your inbox: The 10 top local news stories from metro Boston and around New England delivered daily. Sign Up Thank you for signing up! Sign up for more newsletters here Hernandez was in jail awaiting his first murder trial in July 2014, and the league could argue he fell into the retired player category because he had “no reasonable expectation” of resuming his playing career, Wallach said. In addition, he said, the league will cite the full scope of Hernandez’s football career going back to his youth Pop Warner days, as well as his violent tendencies that predated his selection by the Patriots in the 2010 draft. The team released the once-promising tight end immediately after his June 2013 arrest for murder. Michael McCann, an attorney and founding director of the Sports and Entertainment Law Institute at the University of New Hampshire School of Law, agreed that Hernandez’s estate faces challenges. Advertisement “These are difficult cases to bring, that a finding of CTE proves liability against the NFL,” he said. At the same time, McCann added, the league might be tempted to settle if it can’t get the case dismissed, to avoid turning over documents in a protracted legal battle. “It’s still a potentially threatening lawsuit to the Patriots and the NFL,” said McCann, who is also a leading expert on the Deflategate saga. “We know the Patriots are known to be private about various issues.” So is the league, Wallach said. “Five years into this overall concussion litigation, one thing the league has been very successful at is keeping its executives off the witness stand, avoiding testimony under oath, and not having any of its so-called treasure trove of documents in the public eye,” Wallach said. Advertisement But a third expert, Andrew Brandt, said he doesn’t see a settlement on the horizon. “My sense is they would not settle, as they would have a lot of defenses around Hernandez’s lifestyle and his short career in the NFL compared to other levels of football,” said Brandt, who heads the Moorad Center for the Study of Sports Law at Villanova University. Hernandez was acquitted in April on charges of killing Safiro Furtado and Daniel de Abreu in a drive-by shooting in Boston in 2012. Five days after the acquittal, the 27-year-old Hernandez hanged himself in his prison cell at Souza-Baranowski Correctional Center. He was serving a life sentence for the June 2013 killing of Odin L. Lloyd. A judge later vacated Hernandez’s first-degree murder conviction in the Lloyd case because Hernandez had not exhausted his appeals at the time of his suicide. Bristol County prosecutors are challenging that decision. The league, citing Hernandez’s criminal history, vowed Friday to vigorously defend itself against the lawsuit. Material from The New York Times was included in this report. Ben Volin and Milton J. Valencia of the Globe Staff contributed to this report.

Boston University researchers announced Thursday that former Patriots tight end Aaron Hernandez was diagnosed with a severe case of Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy (CTE). This degenerative, trauma-based brain disease has been identified in a significant number of former football players and other athletes.

Hernandez's brain was donated for examination after his death. Dr. Ann McKee, a leading expert at Boston University, conducted the analysis. She diagnosed Hernandez with Stage III CTE, a serious stage within the four-stage progression of the disease.

The examination revealed notable brain atrophy and a "severe deposition" of tau protein, which is strongly associated with CTE. This protein buildup is a hallmark of the disease.

CTE is widely believed to result from repeated head trauma. Consequently, it is frequently observed in athletes participating in contact sports such as football, hockey, and boxing, where such impacts are common. The research team has released a video detailing their findings.

Stage III CTE is often linked to the development of dementia. Aaron Hernandez died by suicide in prison in April 2017, while serving a life sentence for the 2013 murder of Odin Lloyd.

Hernandez's diagnosis places him among a growing number of former NFL players who have died by suicide and subsequently been found to have CTE. Notable examples include Junior Seau and Dave Duerson. The disease has also been associated with increased aggression and violence. Former Chiefs linebacker Jovan Belcher, for instance, murdered his girlfriend before taking his own life at the team's facility in 2012.

The **alarming discovery** of severe Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy (C.T.E.) in the brain of **Aaron Hernandez**, a former New England Patriots tight end who died at just 27, has **shocked researchers**. This finding is particularly **surprising** given that scientists have been studying the link between brain disease and the deaths of professional football players for years. Hernandez's posthumous brain examination revealed a **degenerative brain disease** so advanced that the damage was comparable to that found in players in their 60s. His family's lawyer stated that this was **"the most severe case they had ever seen in someone of Aaron's age."** C.T.E., a **progressive neurodegenerative disease**, has been identified in over 100 former N.F.L. players, with some tragically taking their own lives, according to research from Boston University.

## Aaron Hernandez's Family Alleges CTE Fueled Violent Behavior in Lawsuit

The family of former NFL player Aaron Hernandez has filed a federal lawsuit, asserting that Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy (CTE) was the root cause of his violent actions. This claim, made public on Thursday, suggests the degenerative brain disease transformed Hernandez into a "homicidal maniac." While not a medical expert, the author acknowledges that understanding CTE as a debilitating mental condition is not solely within the purview of medical professionals. The lawsuit, which seeks $20 million from the New England Patriots and the NFL, has also been characterized by some as a potential "money grab."

The timing of the lawsuit's filing coincided with a press release from Boston University's CTE Center, which revealed that Hernandez had an advanced form of the disease. CTE can only be definitively diagnosed through an autopsy. Attorney Jose Baez stated that it was "the most severe case they had ever seen in someone of Aaron’s age," noting Hernandez was only 27 at the time of his death. This development presents a significant challenge for the NFL, which has already faced considerable scrutiny regarding its handling of concussion-related injuries.

The author raises questions about whether CTE alone is responsible, proposing it may have exacerbated a pre-existing condition. Evidence suggests Hernandez's troubles began as early as 2006, following his father's death. His mother, Terri, described him as rebellious and angry, a changed person after his father's passing. By age 17, Hernandez was involved in a bar fight that resulted in a bouncer sustaining a ruptured eardrum. He was also linked to a shooting incident after a dispute over a necklace, though the witness later recanted their statement.

Despite only having a single-game suspension for a failed drug test as an official mark on his record at the time, a USA Today article referred to him as a "good kid (who) was headed down the wrong path." However, the author suggests that those familiar with the "routine criminality and covering up" during Urban Meyer's coaching tenure at the University of Florida might hold a different perspective. Reports indicate Hernandez failed multiple drug tests at Florida, which made NFL teams hesitant. Although considered a first-round talent, he was not drafted until the fourth round, with one scout citing concerns about his associates and a tendency for trouble.

The author speculates that Hernandez's attorney, Jose Baez, may seek to expand the lawsuit to include the University of Florida and the NCAA. The central legal argument, as presented, is that if CTE led to Hernandez's criminal behavior, then the Patriots and/or the Gators could be held liable. However, the author posits that Hernandez might have displayed violent tendencies irrespective of his football career, drawing a parallel to the Casey Anthony trial where, in their view, the defense strategy was a "reach." The potential for a high-profile trial involving figures such as Bill Belichick is also a possibility.

The dots are awfully close to each other, daring you to connect them. Aaron Hernandez had Stage 3 CTE when he committed suicide, after years of inexplicable violent behavior, including at least one murder. It would be simple and easy to blame football for what happened to him. Dot: The hits to the head gave him brain damage. Dot: The brain damage made him paranoid. Dot: The paranoia made him violent. Dot: The brain damage eventually made him suicidal. Maybe all the dots really do connect in this case. Maybe just some of them do. But the truth is that we don’t know, and we may never know. There is so much—so much—we still don’t know about concussions. We know repeated head trauma can ruin a life, but not every brain reacts in the same way. We know there is a direct correlation between football and CTE. And we know that the NFL’s response to this issue, for many years, was to sweep it under the rug—and when that didn’t work, to buy bigger rugs. The CTE problem in football is real, and it is enormous. But it has also led to facts-based hysteria. We take what we know about football and CTE, which is obviously significant, and we apply it to every single player, every case of dementia, every football life gone awry. And it’s just not that simple. On the surface, the Hernandez case may seem like the clearest case of football-related head trauma causing a player to literally and figuratively lose his mind. After all, nobody went crazier than Aaron Hernandez, right? I would argue the opposite: the extreme nature of Hernandez’s transformation, and the early age when it began, makes his story even foggier than most. Hernandez’s widow is suing the NFL on behalf of his daughter. The suit claims that the NFL in general and the Patriots specifically were “fully aware of the damage that could be inflicted from repetitive impact injuries and failed to disclose, treat or protect him from the dangers of such damage.” Maybe Shayanna Jenkins and her daughter Avielle will triumph in court. I’m not going to speculate on that; my colleague Michael McCann is far more qualified and capable of examining the lawsuit than I am. I will say this, though: Pinning his suicide on the NFL makes for a good headline. It may be a winning legal strategy. But it’s a hard theory for me to buy in this case. Aaron Hernandez played 40 games in the NFL. He also played 40 games at the University of Florida, and he played a lot of football before he arrived at Florida. Even if you do pin his suicide on football, can you really isolate his NFL career as the cause? Hernandez’s behavior started to change radically when he was in high school, almost immediately after his father died. He showed signs of Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder, though it was never diagnosed. He hung out with a different crowd. At times he was distant, at others charming, and he seemed to go back and forth with frightening ease. We still don’t know the full extent of his drug use. He was investigated for a double shooting in Florida in 2007—when he was a college freshman. Character concerns caused him to fall in the NFL draft. Nothing in that last paragraph can be pinned on the NFL. Nothing. Of course, the thrust of the lawsuit is that the CTE made Hernandez suicidal, not that it made him a violent criminal. But can you really separate the two that easily? Hernandez took his own life while serving a life sentence for murder. He did it just after being acquitted in another murder trial. You can come up with a bunch of theories for Hernandez’s suicide that seem sensible on their own. He couldn’t stand to live the life ahead of him. He was filled with regret, though he didn’t show it publicly. Once he was acquitted, there were no battles left to win. He knew that dying while he was appealing his conviction would technically vacate his conviction, possibly shielding his wife and daughter from any civil suits. He had CTE. Or: We don’t know. Maybe someday we will. The link between football-related head trauma and CTE is a serious issue. The NFL deserves heavy scrutiny for its actions, both past and present. We should absolutely continue that conversation, but maybe it’s best, for now, to leave Aaron Hernandez out of it.

The recent revelation that a 27-year-old former NFL player's brain resembled that of a 67-year-old, despite only playing 44 career games, serves as a stark and disturbing indicator of American football's profound impact on athlete health. This news, coinciding with the posthumous diagnosis of severe dementia in Aaron Hernandez, a 27-year-old who tragically died by suicide, compels a critical examination of the league's response to the growing crisis of Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy (CTE). While legal statements from Hernandez's attorney might confirm the obvious – that some of the former New England Patriots star's decisions were inexplicable – they powerfully remind us that erratic behavior in young, successful athletes can stem from physical causes. The central question is no longer *if* football contributes to brain degeneration, but rather *what* concrete actions will be taken to mitigate this risk.

For too long, the discussion about football and CTE has been marked by hesitation and a slow, almost reluctant, acceptance of the science. Researchers are still unraveling CTE's complexities, and definitively quantifying the cumulative effect of helmet impacts on players' brains will undoubtedly take decades. However, enough is already understood to demand immediate and decisive action. For over a decade, neurologists have meticulously documented the link between head trauma sustained by players and the development of tau protein deposits, the hallmark of CTE. Through extensive measurement of head impact forces and detailed brain tissue examination, a clear connection has been established between the sport and the premature aging of athletes' brains. Even the NFL has, to some degree, acknowledged this link. Yet, despite discussions about improved helmet technology, safer tackling techniques, and efforts to discourage players from becoming "human torpedoes," the fundamental issue – the ticking time bomb within players' helmets that can alter the minds of intelligent individuals – remains largely unaddressed. The league offers ample rhetoric and promises of brain study initiatives, but the tangible actions needed to potentially save players' lives are conspicuously absent.

In contrast, the Canadian Football League (CFL) and its players' union have recently taken a proactive step by eliminating full-contact practices during the season. CFL Commissioner Randy Ambrosie told the CBC that this decision was driven by a clear opportunity to act proactively for player safety. While this move might seem less impactful given that CFL teams already have a limited number of full-contact practices, comparable to the NFL, it sends a powerful message of commitment from the CFL and its players' union to address the root causes of the problem. This is a message the NFL, at present, does not appear poised to match.

A common misconception is that CTE is primarily caused by the spectacular, bone-jarring hits seen in games – those moments where players are violently slammed or knocked unconscious. This perception, fostered in the early days of CTE discovery and amplified by popular media like the film "Concussion," is increasingly being challenged by current research. The focus has shifted from these dramatic collisions to the seemingly less consequential, yet potentially more insidious, daily impacts occurring during practices and games. The repetitive, low-level trauma of helmets colliding, even at half-speed or during less intense game situations, can trigger cumulative damage. Each impact, though seemingly minor, contributes to a gradual erosion of brain health. Scientists now suspect that this relentless accumulation of micro-trauma, rather than isolated catastrophic hits, may be the true culprit behind the devastating neurological consequences observed in former players. This insidious, invisible damage can, over time, reach critical levels, leading to the severe cognitive and behavioral changes associated with CTE.

The NFL's reluctance to fully engage with the CTE discussion is understandable, given the significant financial implications. Litigation from former players has already cost the league close to a billion dollars in settlements. Aaron Hernandez's attorney, Jose Baez, has publicly stated his intention to sue the Patriots and the NFL on behalf of Hernandez's young daughter, highlighting the ongoing legal battles. The league's strategy often appears heavily influenced by litigation, employing extensive legal teams to navigate potential lawsuits stemming from various decisions and policies.

To be fair, the NFL does raise valid questions regarding the precise extent to which CTE impacts its players. The nature of CTE, being detectable only posthumously, presents significant challenges for scientific study. The brains available for research have often come from players like Hernandez, whose tragic circumstances and behavioral issues led to their brains being donated for examination. However, the brains of thousands of former players who have lived seemingly normal lives without significant neurological issues have not undergone the same level of scrutiny. This self-selective nature of current CTE studies could potentially lead to misleading conclusions. It is crucial to acknowledge that CTE cannot be universally blamed for every negative outcome in a player's life. Sometimes, poor choices are simply poor choices, and attributing all instances of bad behavior to a neurological condition risks oversimplification. We cannot definitively state that CTE directly caused Aaron Hernandez to commit murder in 2013, the crime for which he was sentenced to life in prison. Nevertheless, CTE is a tangible and serious condition that demands our attention.

Moving forward, a more robust approach than simply reducing full-contact practices during the season is likely necessary to effectively mitigate the risk of CTE. Perhaps football at all levels needs to consider a significant reduction, or even elimination, of contact practices altogether. It may be that the sport itself requires fundamental reinvention to prioritize player safety above all else. However, the CFL's recent action represents a positive and commendable step towards confronting a menace that has irrevocably altered the lives of countless individuals. While it may be a small stride, it is a stride nonetheless. The time has long passed for listening solely to legal counsel; it is now imperative to take many more decisive actions to protect the health and well-being of those who play the game.

CLOSE SportsPulse: USA TODAY Sports' Peter Barzilai discusses the ramifications of Aaron Hernandez's severe CTE diagnosis. USA TODAY Sports Aaron Hernandez was found to have CTE. (Photo: Mark L. Baer, USA TODAY Sports) The confirmation that former New England Patriots tight end Aaron Hernandez suffered from severe chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE) at just 27 years old, a diagnosis revealed posthumously following his suicide, has profoundly impacted the National Football League. This finding, established through an autopsy of his brain, indicates a pathology far more advanced than typically observed in individuals of his age. It directly challenges the league's established narratives and its ongoing efforts to address concerns surrounding head trauma in the sport.

The autopsy results revealed Stage 3 CTE, the second-most severe classification of this degenerative disease. Furthermore, Hernandez's brain showed signs of atrophy, with significant deterioration noted in a critical central membrane. These findings represent a substantial deviation from normal neurological development, as detailed in a lawsuit filed by Hernandez's fiancée and daughter against the NFL and the Patriots. The lawsuit specifically cites the Boston University neuropathology report, which described the "unusually severe" nature of Hernandez's CTE pathology, particularly considering his youth. This presents a significant challenge for the NFL, one that cannot be easily dismissed.

For years, the NFL has found it easier to deflect responsibility for the cognitive decline and behavioral changes seen in former players, often attributing them to factors beyond the sport, especially when players were in their later years. The league frequently cited a lack of definitive links, suggesting a combination of environmental and lifestyle influences. Even in tragic instances like those of Junior Seau and Dave Duerson, who died in their 40s and 50s due to the effects of brain damage, the NFL largely avoided direct accountability. The league consistently emphasized the need for "more research" into genetics, mental illness, and other potential contributing factors, effectively delaying definitive conclusions about football's role.

The growing body of evidence, with the number of former players diagnosed with CTE after death increasing from 20 to over 100, had minimal impact on the league's position. Even a study published in the Journal of the American Medical Association in July, which found CTE in 110 out of 111 former NFL players' brains, was met with the NFL maintaining that "many unanswered questions relating to the cause, incidence and prevalence of long-term effects of head trauma such as CTE" persisted. However, Aaron Hernandez's case, a player who died at 27 with such advanced CTE, fundamentally shifts this perspective. The NFL can no longer credibly claim ignorance or a lack of definitive evidence when a player so young exhibits such severe neurological damage.

The league dedicates substantial resources annually to assuring parents that youth football is a safe and beneficial activity. Yet, the news of Hernandez's condition is likely to heighten parental concerns, raising serious questions about the long-term consequences of participation. If a 27-year-old professional athlete, at the sport's highest level, could develop such severe CTE, what does this imply for children playing at the youth level? The lawsuit outlines the typical symptoms associated with Stage III CTE, as experienced by individuals like Hernandez, including memory loss, impaired executive function, aggression, impulsive behavior, reduced concentration, mood instability, depression, apathy, and cognitive deficits. These are not minor issues; they are life-altering conditions that can profoundly affect individuals and their families.

While an NFL spokesman stated the league had not yet reviewed the lawsuit and could not comment, it is evident that the NFL has been aware of the potential for such a situation. The league's consistent strategy has involved questioning scientific findings and delaying research, a tactic now severely undermined by the stark reality of Hernandez's case. The pursuit of knowledge regarding CTE's causes—understanding the threshold for damaging hits, the timing of maximum impact, and factors influencing individual susceptibility—is crucial. It represents the only viable path forward for the long-term viability of football. Aaron Hernandez's tragic death at such a young age marks a critical turning point in the understanding and management of CTE and repetitive head trauma. To disregard this reality is not only disingenuous but a profound act of self-deception for the NFL.

Follow USA TODAY Sports columnist Nancy Armour on Twitter @nrarmour.

query: What major executive actions is President Donald Trump planning to sign on his first day in office?

documents.text:
Donald Trump's transition team remained tight-lipped about the specifics of his first day in office, despite his extensive campaign promises. While Trump had campaigned on a detailed to-do list, his team indicated a potential shift in pace, acknowledging that complex issues requiring negotiation with Congress or foreign leaders might not be addressed immediately. However, some significant policy announcements were still anticipated in the initial days of his administration.

Transition spokesman Sean Spicer confirmed that Trump planned to issue two executive orders on trade soon. A key item on Trump's "Day One" agenda was formally declaring the U.S. intention to withdraw from the Trans-Pacific Partnership trade deal, a pact he had strongly opposed during his campaign, arguing it harmed American businesses and workers. He also pledged to initiate renegotiations of the 23-year-old North American Free Trade Agreement or withdraw from it. "I think you will see those happen very shortly," Spicer stated.

Early actions were also expected in the energy sector, with plans to roll back regulations on oil drilling and coal. Cybersecurity was another focus, with Trump having already announced his intention to request a report on the nation's cyber defenses within 90 days of taking office. Broad promises were also made to immediately dismantle President Barack Obama's immigration policies, though some of these vows might prove challenging to implement swiftly.

The president-elect viewed Monday as the commencement of his administration's work. At his first post-election press conference, Trump mentioned that while the inauguration would be an enjoyable event, his team had planned "some pretty good signings on Monday and Tuesday and Wednesday and Thursday and Friday, and then, also the next week." He was also expected to sign some paperwork on Friday, including formal Cabinet nominations requiring Senate confirmation. Executive orders, both logistical and agenda-driven, were anticipated to launch his administration.

Vice President-elect Mike Pence elaborated on the transition team's strategy, stating, "Specifically we've focused in the president-elect's direction on a Day One, Day 100 and Day 200 action plan for keeping our word to the American people and putting the president-elect's promises into practice." Pence added, "We are all ready to go to work. We can't wait to get to work for the American people."

Trump's ambitious "Day One" list included proposing a constitutional amendment for congressional term limits, implementing a federal worker hiring freeze, and initiating the removal of undocumented immigrants with criminal records. A significant promise was to "cancel every unconstitutional executive action, memorandum and order issued by President Obama." This could lead to substantial policy shifts, including the potential termination of the Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals (DACA) program, which has protected approximately 750,000 young immigrants from deportation and provided them with work permits. Trump could choose to immediately end deportation protection and revoke work permits, or halt new enrollments while allowing existing permits to remain valid until expiration.

Trump indicated that immigration enforcement would initially target criminals, a group he estimated to be between 2 million and 3 million people. He pledged to "move criminal aliens out day one" in collaboration with state, local, and federal authorities. However, this promise faced practical hurdles, including jurisdictional objections to assisting federal immigration enforcement and a shortage of jail space. The government's capacity for immigration detention was around 34,000 individuals, a number recently exceeded due to a surge of arrests at the Mexican border.

Another item on Trump's agenda was labeling China a currency manipulator. However, in a recent interview, Trump stated he would prefer to "talk to them first" rather than take immediate action on his first day.

The potential for a more measured approach might stem from the ongoing process of establishing his administration, including awaiting Cabinet confirmations and staffing federal agencies, with key positions like the chairman of the Council of Economic Advisers still unfilled. Instead of immediate policy rollouts, Trump's transition period included meetings with prominent CEOs and highlighting job creation announcements. His team had also been reviewing Obama's executive actions to identify those that could be quickly rescinded.
